Solution:
e = (velocity of separation) / (velocity of approach)

\[1 = \frac{{v - 5}}{{10}}\]

\[v = 15m/{s^{ - 1}}\]

Question: A proton is projected with a velocity 10<sup>7</sup> ms<sup>-1</sup>, at right angles to a uniform magnetic field of induction 100 mT. The time (in second) taken by the proton to traverse 90<sup>o</sup> arc is (Take, mass of proton = 165 × 10<sup>-27</sup> and charge of proton = 1.6 × 10<sup>-19</sup> C)
Solution:
Velocity of proton, $$ u = rac{{Bqr}}{m}$$
where,
r = radius of circular path.
Distance covered by proton to traverse
\[90^\circ \,arc = rac{1}{4} imes circumference\]
\[d = rac{1}{4} imes 2\pi r = rac{{\pi r}}{2}\]
Time taken by proton to cover distance d,$$
\[t = rac{{\pi r/2}}{ u } = rac{{\pi r}}{{2 u }} = rac{\pi }{2} rac{r}{{Bqr/m}} = rac{{\pi m}}{{2\,Bq}}\,\,...\left( i ight)\]

Putting, \[m = 1.65 imes {10^{ - 27}}kg,\]

\[B = 100\,\,mT = 100 imes {10^{ - 3}}T\]

and \[q = 1.6 imes {10^{ - 19}}C,in\,\,Eq.\,\,\left( i ight)\]

\[ herefore \,\,\,t = rac{{3.14 imes 1.65 imes {{10}^{ - 27}}}}{{2 imes 100 imes {{10}^{ - 3}} imes 1.6 imes {{10}^{ - 19}}}}\]

\[t = 1.62 imes {10^{ - 7}}s\]

Question: According to Einstein’s photoelectric equation, the graph between the kinetic energy of photoelectrons ejected and the frequency of incident radiations is
Solution:
According to Einstein’s photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectron ejected, $K.E = E - \phi $\[ \Rightarrow KE = hf - h{f_0}\]

If the frequency of the incident light is greater than threshold frequency then the ejection of photoelectrons starts. After that, the frequency increases kinetic energy also increases..

Question: A particle of mass $$2 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,kg$$, charge $$4 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,C$$ enters in an electric field of 5 V/m, then its kinetic energy after 10 s is
Solution:
Force on a charge placed inside an electric field given
 as
$$\eqalign{
  & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,F = qE \cr
  & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,ma = qE \Rightarrow a = qE/m \cr
  & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{{qE}}{m} \Rightarrow dv = \frac{{qE}}{m} \cr} $$
Integrating with proper limits,

$$\int_0^v {dv} = \int_0^t {\frac{{qE}}{m}} \,dt$$
Now, $$v = \frac{1}{m}\,qE\left( t \right)$$

$$\eqalign{
  & KE = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{1}{2}m\frac{{{q^2}{E^2}{t^2}}}{{{m^2}}} \cr
  & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{q^2}{E^2}{t^2}}}{m} = \frac{{{{\left( {4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)}^2} \times {5^2} \times {{10}^2}}}{{2 \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \cr
  & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 10\,\,J \cr} $$

Question: The number of the significant figures in \[11.118 \times {10^{ - 6}}V\] is
Solution: The number of significant figure is 5 as 10−6 does not affect this number.

Question: The variation of quantity A with quantity B, plotted in figure. Which of the following motions of a particle in a straight line, cannot be described from this graph:
Options: Quantity A is velocity if motion is uniform.
Quantity A is displacement if motion is uniform.
Quantity A is velocity if motion is uniformly accelerated.
Solution:
Option (a) is correct as quantity B may represent time . Here option (b) is wrong because for uniform motion , v - t graph is simply a straight line parallel to the time axis (velocity remains constant in uniform motion).
If the motion is uniform, displacement - time graph is a straight line with a positive slope. Thus , option (c) can be correct.

Also , if the motion is uniformly acceleration (acceleration is constant), this implies that velocity is increasing with time and hence, velocity - time graph is a straight line with a positive also. Thus, option (d) is correct.

The correct options are (a), (c) and (d).

Question: Electrical force between two point charges is 200 N. If we increase 10% charge on one of the charges and decrease 10% charge on the other, then electrical force between them for the same distance becomes
Solution:
Let two charges be q1 and q2 and r be the distance between them, then electrical force,
$$F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}} = 200\,\,N\,\,\,\,...\left( i \right)$$
If q1 is increased by 10%, then
$${q'_1} = \frac{{110}}{{100}}{q_1}$$
and q2 is decreased by 10%, then
$${q'_2} = \frac{{90}}{{100}}{q_2}$$
Then, electrical force between them is
$$F' = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{{q'}_2}{{q'}_2}}}{{{r^2}}}$$
$$ \Rightarrow \,F' = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{\frac{{110}}{{100}}{q_1} \times \frac{{90}}{{100}}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}$$
$$\eqalign{
  & \cr
  & \Rightarrow \,\,F' = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}} \times \frac{{99}}{{100}}\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {ii} \right) \cr} $$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$\eqalign{
  & F' = 200 \times \frac{{99}}{{100}} \cr
  & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,F' = 198\,\,N \cr} $$

Question: A body projected vertically upwards with a velocity u returns to the starting point in 4 seconds. If g = 10 m/sec<sup>2</sup>, the value of u is
Solution:
Time of flight is 4sec
Time of ascent = 2 sec

So, $v = u - gt = 0$

$u = gt = 10\left( 2 \right) = 20{\text{ m/sec}}$

Question: A solid sphere and a spherical shell both of same radius and mass roll down from rest without slipping on an inclined plane from the same height . The time taken to reach the bottom of the inclined plane is :
Solution:
Both solid sphere and hollow sphere have same mass (m) and same radius (R) Acceleration of a rolling down body is given by;
$ \Rightarrow a = \frac{{g{\text{ }}sin\theta }}{{1 + \frac{I}{{m{R^2}}}}}$

Time taken to reach the surface can be found as;

$ \Rightarrow s = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}$

$ \Rightarrow s = 0 + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}$

$ \Rightarrow t = \sqrt {\frac{{2s}}{a}} $

Moment of inertia for solid sphere, ${I_S} = \frac{2}{5}m{R^2}$

Acceleration of solid sphere;

$ \Rightarrow a = \frac{{g{\text{ }}sin\theta }}{{1 + \frac{{{I_S}}}{{m{R^2}}}}} = \frac{{g\sin \theta }}{{1 + \frac{{2m{R^2}}}{{5m{R^2}}}}}$

Time taken by solid sphere;

$ \Rightarrow {t_S} = \sqrt {\frac{{2s}}{a}} = \sqrt {2s \times \frac{{\left( {1 + \frac{{2m{R^2}}}{{5m{R^2}}}} \right)}}{{gsin{\text{ }}\theta }}} $

Moment of inertia for hollow sphere, ${I_H} = \frac{2}{3}m{R^2}$

Acceleration of hollow sphere;

$ \Rightarrow a = \frac{{g{\text{ }}sin\theta }}{{1 + \frac{{{I_H}}}{{m{R^2}}}}} = \frac{{g\sin \theta }}{{1 + \frac{{2m{R^2}}}{{3m{R^2}}}}}$

Time taken by hollow sphere;

$ \Rightarrow {t_H} = \sqrt {\frac{{2s}}{a}} = \sqrt {2s \times \frac{{\left( {1 + \frac{{2m{R^2}}}{{3m{R^2}}}} \right)}}{{gsin{\text{ }}\theta }}} $

As we can observe that time taken by hollow sphere is less than time taken by the solid sphere.

Question: A body of mass m crosses the top most point of a vertical circle with critical speed. What will be tension in string when it is horizontal-
Solution:
Critical velocity at top most point $ = \sqrt {rg} $
$\therefore $ Using conservation of energy
$T.E{._A} = T.E{._B}$
$\therefore \,\,\frac{1}{2}mv_A^2 + mg.\left( r \right) = \frac{1}{2}mv_B^2 + 0$
$\therefore \,\,\frac{1}{2}m.rg + mgr = \frac{{mv_B^2}}{2}$
$\therefore \,\,v_B^2 = 3rg$
$\therefore \,\,{v_B} = \sqrt {3rg} $
$\therefore \,\,T = \frac{{mv_B^2}}{r} = 3mg$
Hence [c]

Question: The cathode of a photoelectric cell is changed such that the work function changes from W<sub>1</sub> to W<sub>2</sub> (W<sub>2</sub>> W<sub>1</sub>). If the current before and after changes are I<sub>1</sub> and I<sub>2</sub>, all other conditions remaining unchanged, then (assuming hν > W<sub>2</sub>)
Solution:
According to the question, work function \[{W_2} > {W_1}\].
Since hν > W2 photo-electrons will be emitted
Now, the photoelectric current is independent of the work function.
The photoelectric current is directly proportional to the intensity of incident rays. Since, there is no
change in the intensity of incident rays, ${I_1} = {I_2}$.
Therefore, the current will be the same after changing the value of the work function

Question: Given $\vec A = \hat i + 2\hat j - 3\hat k.$ When a vector $\vec B$ is added to $\overrightarrow A $ , we get a unit vector along X–axis. Then, $\vec B$is
Solution:
$\overrightarrow {\text{A}} = \widehat {\text{i}} + 2\widehat {\text{j}} - 3\widehat {\text{k}}$
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\text{Let}}\,\vec B = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k\, \\
  {\text{Given}}\,\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B = \widehat i \\
   \Rightarrow (1 + x)\hat i + (2 + y)\hat j + (z - 3)\hat k = 1\hat i \\
  {\text{Since}},x,y\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,z\,{\text{components}}\,{\text{are}}\,{\text{independent}}\,{\text{to}}\,{\text{each}}\,{\text{other}}. \\
  {\text{Equating}}\,{\text{them}}\,{\text{on}}\,{\text{eighter}}\,{\text{side}}.1 + x = 1 \\
   \Rightarrow x = 0 \\
  2 + y = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow y = - 2z - 3 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow z = 3 \\
   \Rightarrow \vec B = - 2\hat j + 3\hat k \\
\end{gathered} \]

\[As,r = rac{{m u }}{{qB}}\]

Options: the magnitude of its average velocity over the interval
one–half its speed at the end of the interval
distance covered during the time interval divided by the time interval
its acceleration multiplied by the time interval

And removes with constant acceleration.

Question: A man is sitting in a boat which is floating in a pond. If the man drinks some water from the pond, the level of water in the pond will:
Solution: When a man drinks water from the pond, the water level of pond will decrease. But when the man is sitting in a boat floating in the pond and does the same, the boat sinks further and thus, the level of water in the pond remains the same.

Question: An iron block of sides 5 cm × 8 cm × 15 cm has to be pushed along the floor. The force required will be minimum when the surface in contact with ground is:
Options: Force is the same for all surfaces
Solution:
Friction force between a block and a surface is given by;
⇒ f = μmg
A block can only be moved when applied force exceeded frictional force;
⇒ F > f
So, minimum force required to move the block will be equivalent to frictional force.
We can observe that generated friction force is independent of surface area of the surface which is in contact with ground.
Thus, required minimum force will be same for all surfaces.

Question: A uniform solid sphere is rotating about its diameter at an angular velocity ꞷ . If it contracts so that its radius reduces to $\frac{1}{n}$ of its original value while the mass remains unchanged, its angular velocity becomes
Solution:
Given, Initial angular speed of the solid sphere= \[\omega \]
The  solid spheres contract so that its radius reduces to \[\frac{1}{n}\]of its original 
Value while the mass remains unchanged.
Let, 
The mass of the solid sphere = M.
The initial radius of the solid sphere \[{R_1} = R\]
\[ \Rightarrow \]The final radius of the solid sphere  \[ = {R_2} = \frac{R}{n}\]
The final angular velocity of the solid sphere \[{\omega _2}\]
As there is no external torque acting on the solid sphere, the angular momentum of the system will remain constant. 
\[ \Rightarrow \,{I_1}{\omega _1} = {I_2}{\omega _2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\frac{2}{5}M{R^2}{\omega _1} = \frac{2}{5}M{\left( {\frac{R}{n}} \right)^2}{\omega _2}\]
\[\therefore \,{\omega _2} = {n^2}{\omega _1} = {n^2}\omega .\]

Question: A small steel ball of radius $ r $ is allowed to fall under gravity through a column of a viscous liquid of coefficient of viscosity $ \eta $ . After some time the velocity of the ball attains a constant value known as terminal velocity $ {{v}_{T}} $ . The terminal velocity depends on (i) the mass of the ball $ m $ , (ii) $ \eta $ . (iii) $ ~r $ and (iv) acceleration due to gravity $ g $ . Which of the following relations is dimensionally correct?
Solution:
By substituting dimension of each quantity in RHS of option
(a), we get
 $ \left[ \frac{mg}{\eta r} \right]=\left[ \frac{M\times L{{T}^{-2}}}{M{{L}^{-1}}{{T}^{-1}}\times L} \right]=\left[ L{{T}^{-1}} \right] $
This option gives the dimension of velocity.
Hence, the correct answer is option (a).

Question:
In an AC circuit, the emf (e) and the current (i) at any instant are given respectively by
\[e = {E_0}\sin \omega t\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,i = {I_0}\sin \left( {\omega t - \phi } \right)\]
The average power in the circuit over one cycle of AC is
Solution:
Given, e = E0sinωt
and i = I0 sin(ωt - φ)
Instantaneous power, Pint = ei
= E0I0 sinωt sin(ωt - φ)
Average power over one cycle is
\[{P_{av}} = rac{1}{T}\sum olimits_0^T {{P_{\operatorname{int} }}dt} \]
\[ = rac{1}{T}\sum olimits_0^T {{E_0}{I_0}} \sin \omega t\sin \left( {\omega t - \phi } ight)dt\]
\[ = rac{1}{T} imes {E_0}{I_0}\sum olimits_0^T {\left( {{{\sin }^2}\omega t\cos \phi + \sin \phi \sin \omega t\cos \omega t} ight)dt} \]
\[\, = rac{{{E_0}{I_0}}}{T}\left[ {\cos \phi imes rac{T}{2} \pm 0} ight]\]
\[ = rac{{\left( {{E_0}{I_0}\cos \phi } ight)T/2}}{T} = rac{{{E_0}{I_0}\cos \phi }}{2}\]
where, cosφ is called the power factor of an AC circuit.

Question: An arrow is shot into air. Its range is 200 m and its time of flight is 5 s. If \[{\text{g  =  10 m/}}{{\text{s}}^{\text{2}}}\], then horizontal component of velocity and the maximum height will be respectively:
Solution:
Formula for time,
$T = \frac{{2{u_y}}}{g}$

$\therefore {u_y} = \frac{{gT}}{2}$

$ = \frac{{10 \times 5}}{2}$

$ = 25{\text{ }}m{s^{ - 1}}$

Now formula for maximum height,

$H = \frac{{{u_y}^2}}{{2g}}$

$ = \frac{{{{(25)}^2}}}{{20}}$

$ = 31.25{\text{ }}m$

Now range,

$R = {u_x} \times T$

${u_x} = \frac{R}{T}$

$ = \frac{{200}}{5}$

$ = 40{\text{ }}m{s^{ - 1}}$

Question: A 120g mass has a velocity $\vec v = \left( {2\hat i + 5\hat j} \right)m{s^{ - 1}}$at a certain instant K.E. of the body at that instant is
A candle of diameter d is floating on a liquid in a cylindrical container of diameter D (D >> d) as shown in figure. If it is burning at the rate of 2 cm/h, then the top of the candle will :
Options: fall at the rate of 1 cm/h
fall at the rate of 2 cm/h
go up at the rate of 1 cm/h
Solution:
Weight of candle = Weight of liquid displaced
$$V\rho g = V\prime \rho \prime g\prime $$

Volume $$ = \pi {\left( {\frac{d}{2}} \right)^2} \times 2L$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left( {\pi \times \frac{{{d^2}}}{4} \times 2L} \right)\rho = \left( {\pi \times \frac{{{d^2}}}{4} \times L} \right)\rho \prime $$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{\rho }{{\rho '}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Candle is burning at the rate of 2 cm/h, then after an hour, candle length is (2L−2)

$$\therefore \left( {2L - 2} \right)\rho = \left( {L - x} \right)\rho \prime $$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{\rho }{{\rho \prime }} = \frac{{L - x}}{{2\left( {L - 1} \right)}}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} = \frac{{L - x}}{{2(L - 1)}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = 1cm$$

So, in one hour it falls 1 cm. Thus, rate of fall of top of the candle is 1 cm/h.

Question: At any instant, the velocity and acceleration of a particle moving along a straight line are v and a. The speed of the particle is increasing if
Solution: For increasing speed both velocity (v) and acceleration (a) are in the same direction.

Question: In the given circuit, find the energy stored in the capacitor connected between points A and B (given $$C = 1\mu F$$)
Solution:
Potential drop is zero along an ideal conductor because it has zero resistance.
Potential at point $$A = 10\,V$$
Potential at point $$B = 0\,V$$
$$\therefore $$ Potential difference across
$$AB = 10\,V$$

Energy stored
$$ = \frac{1}{2}C{V^2} = \frac{1}{2} \times {10^{ - 6}} \times {\left( {10} \right)^2} = 50\,\mu J$$

Question: Centrifugal force is considered as pseudo force when

An observer who is moving with the particle and experiencing the force
Solution: Centrifugal force is considered when an observer moving along with the particle having circular motion.

Question: The angles which a vector \[\widehat i + \widehat j + \sqrt 2 \widehat k\] makes with X, Y and Z axes respectively are
Solution:
If a vector $\overrightarrow A = a\widehat i + b\widehat j + c\widehat k$ makes angles α, β & γ with x, y and z axes respectively then

$\cos \alpha = \frac{a}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},\,\,\cos \beta = \frac{b}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }}$ and $\cos \gamma = \frac{c}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }}$

$\therefore \,\,\,\cos \alpha = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {{\left( {\sqrt 2 } \right)}^2}} }} = \frac{1}{2}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\alpha = 60^\circ $

$\cos \beta = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {{\left( {\sqrt 2 } \right)}^2}} }} = \frac{1}{2}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\beta = 60^\circ $

$\cos \gamma = \frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {{\left( {\sqrt 2 } \right)}^2}} }} = \frac{1}{2}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\gamma = 45^\circ $

Question:
Assertion : A body with constant acceleration always moves along a straight line
Reason : A body with constant magnitude of acceleration may not speed up.
Options: Assertion is True, Reason is True; Reason is a correct explanation for Assertion
Assertion is True, Reason is True; Reason is NOT a correct explanation for Assertion
Assertion is True, Reason is False
Both Assertion and Reason are False
Solution: In projectile motion acceleration is, always constant Thus, the body with constant acceleration does not always move along a straight line. A body having constant magnitude of acceleration might not speed up as in the case of uniform circular motion

Question: Increase in pressure at one point of the enclosed liquid in equilibrium of rest is transmitted equally to all other points of liquid, illustrates :
Solution:
It is due to Pascal’s law.
In fluid mechanics, Pascal’s law is a concept that states that a pressure change occurring inside a confined in-compressible fluid is transmitted throughout the fluid such that the same change occurs everywhere.

Question: If \[\vec A + \vec B = \vec C\]and \[A = \sqrt 3 ,\,\,B = \sqrt 3 \]and C = 3, then the angle between vectors A and B is-
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow C = \overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B ;A = \sqrt 3 ,B = \sqrt 3 \,\,{\text{and}}\,\,C = 3\,\]

\[{C^2} = {A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow 9 = 3 + 3 + 2\left( 3 \right)\cos \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} = \cos \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\theta = 60^\circ \]

Question: A body of mass 0.01 kg executes simple harmonic motion (S.H.M.) about x = 0 under the influence of a force shown below : The period of the S.H.M. is
Solution:
According to standard equation,
$$\;So{\text{ }}slope{\text{ }}of{\text{ }}given{\text{ }}graph = \frac{{ - 8}}{2} = - 4 = - m{\omega ^2}\;$$

$F = - m{\omega ^2}x$

$m = 0.01{\text{ kg}}$

$ \Rightarrow {\omega ^2} = 400$

$ \Rightarrow \omega = 20$

${\text{and}}\,{\text{T}} = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega } = 0.3{\text{ sec}}$

Question: Which of the following transitions gives photon of maximum energy?
Solution:
The energy of electrons, at $n = 1$; $E = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{n^2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow E = - 13.6{\text{ eV}}$
At n = 2, then $E = - \frac{{13.6}}{4} = - 3.4{\text{ eV}}$
At $n = 6$, then $E = - \frac{{13.6}}{{36}} = - 0.37{\text{ eV}}$
Energy is absorbed for the case$n = 1{\text{ to }}n = 2$ and for the case$n = 2{\text{ to }}n = 6$
For the case $n = 2{\text{ to }}n = 1$, the energy released
$E = - 3.4 - \left( { - 13.6} \right) = 10.2{\text{ eV}}$
For the case$n = 6{\text{ to }}n = 2$, the energy released is
$E = - 0.37 - \left( { - 3.4} \right) = 3.03{\text{ eV}}$
Therefore, the photon of maximum energy is emitted when transition is from $n = 2{\text{ to }}n = 1$.

Question: The distance between charges$$5 \times {10^{ - 11}}C\,\,and\,\, - 2.7 \times {10^{ - 11}}C$$ is 0.2 m. The distance, at which third charge should be placed from second charge in order that it will not experience any force along the line joining the two charges, is
Solution:
Let a charge q is placed at a point C such that force at C is zero.
$$\eqalign{
  & {F_{{q_2}}} = {\text{force on q due to charge}}\,\,{q_2}\,\,and\,\, \cr
  & {F_{{q_1}}} = {\text{force on q due to}}\,\,{\text{charge}}\,\,{q_1}. \cr} $$
$$\eqalign{
  & As,\,{F_{{q_1}}} + {F_{{q_2}}} = 0 \Rightarrow {F_{{q_1}}} = - {F_{{q_2}}} \cr
  & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{{\left( {x + 0.2} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{q{q_2}}}{{{x^2}}} \cr
  & \Rightarrow \frac{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 11}}}}{{{{\left( {x + 0.2} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{2.7 \times {{10}^{ - 11}}}}{{{x^2}}} \cr
  & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,x = 0.556\,\,m \cr} $$

Question: A bullet of mass 0.01 kg and travelling at a speed of 500 ms<sup>–1</sup> strikes a block of 2 kg, which is suspended by a string of length 5 m. The centre of gravity of the block is found to rise through a vertical height of 0.1 m. The speed of the bullet after it emerges from the block is
Solution:
For block from initial to final position.

We can apply conservation of Mechanical energy.

\[M{E_i} = M{E_f}\]

\[\frac{1}{2}mv_1^2 = mgh\]

\[v_1^2 = mgh\]

\[H = 0.1m\]

\[v = \sqrt {2\left( {10} \right)\left( {0.1} \right)} \]

\[{v_1} = \sqrt 2 = 1.4\]

Now for collision

\[{\vec P_i} = {\vec P_f}\]

\[ \Rightarrow 0.01\left( {500} \right) = 2{v_1} + 0.01{v_2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \,5 = 2(1.4) + 0.01{v_2}\]

Simplifying this will give,

\[{v_2} = \frac{{2.2}}{{0.01}} = 220m{s^{ - 1}}\]

Question: The magnitude of magnetic induction for a current carrying toroid of uniform cross-section is
Options: maximum at the centre of cross-section
Solution:
\[B = \frac{{{\mu _0}nI}}{{2\pi r}}\]
\[\because \,\,\,a < r < b\]
Hence B is maximum at inner edge.

Question: Work done by an external agent in separating the parallel plate capacitor is
Solution: Work done is equal to the energy stored in the capacitor i.e. $\frac{1}{2}C{V^2}$

Question: The function of a moderator in a nuclear reactor is
Options: react with the nuclear fuel to release energy
provide neutrons to the fission process
slow down fast neutrons so as to have greater probability for nuclear fission to occur
Solution: Slow down fast neutrons so as to have greater probability for nuclear fission to occur.

Question:
Statement-1: When an ideal gas is taken from a given thermodynamics state A to another given thermodynamic state B by polytropic process, the change in the internal energy of the system will be the same in all processes.
Statement-2: Internal energy of the gas depends only upon its absolute temperature.
(A) Statement–I is True, Statement–II is True; Statement–II is a correct explanation for Statement–I.
(B) Statement–I is True, Statement–II is True; Statement–2 is NOT a correct explanation for Statement–I.
(C) Statement–I is True, Statement–II is False.
(D) Statement–I is False, Statement–II is True.
Solution: Statement-1 is true it is always $dU = n{C_v}dT,$whatever be the process. Statement-2 is false as internal energy depends on the charge in temperature.

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Kinetic Theory of an Ideal Gas

Question: A positive charge is fixed at the origin. An electric dipole, which is free to move and rotate, is placed on the positive x–axis. Its dipole moment is directed towards the positive x-axis. The dipole will:
Solution: -q charge is near the +Q as compare to +q, so attractive force will be greater than repulsive force. Therefore, dipole moves towards the origin.

Question: One projectile moving with velocity v in space, gets burst into 2 parts of masses in the ratio 1 : 3. The smaller part becomes stationary. What is the velocity of the other part ?
Solution:
Let the velocity of other mass = v

By principle of conservation of linear momentum

\[{\vec P_i} = {\vec P_f}\]

4 mv = m(0) + 3mv

\[ \Rightarrow \;v = \frac{4}{3}v\]

Question:
Statement–1: If a sphere of mass m moving with speed u undergoes a perfectly elastic head–on collision with another sphere of heavier mass M at rest (M > m), then direction of velocity of sphere of mass m is reversed due to collision (no external force acts on system of two spheres).
Statement–2: During a collision of spheres of unequal masses, the heavier mass exerts more force on the lighter mass in comparison to the force which lighter mass exerts on the heavier one.
Options: Both Statement-1 and Statement-2 are true and Statement-2 is the correct explanation for Statement-1.
Both Statement-1 and Statement-2 are true but Statement-2 is not the correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement-1 is true and the Statement-2 is false.
Statement-1 is false and the Statement-2 is true.
Solution:
\[mu + 0 = m{v_1} + M{v_2}\]conservation of momentum

\[u - 0 = {v_2} - {v_1}\]Las of restitution

\[{v_1} = \left( {\frac{{m - M}}{{m + M}}} \right)u\left( \to \right) = - \left( {\frac{{M - m}}{{M + m}}} \right)u\left( \leftarrow \right)\]

During collision no external force is acting. So statement 1 is true. During head on collision, the momentum will be conserved. Therefore the heavier mass will not be moving and in accordance to the

Newton law of motion, both masses will apply equal and opposite force. Therefore statement 2 is false.

Question: A charged particle moving with velocity 4 × 10<sup>6</sup> ms<sup>-1</sup> enters perpendicular to a magnetic field B = 2 Wbm<sup>-2</sup>. It moves in a circular path of radius 2 cm, then charge per unit mass is
Solution:
For a charged particle, entering perpendicular to a magnetic field will execute a circular path. Such that,
\[{F_m} = q u B\sin heta  = m{ u ^2}/r\]

\[ \Rightarrow \, rac{q}{m} = rac{ u }{{rB\,\,\sin heta }}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \, rac{q}{m} = rac{{4 imes {{10}^6}}}{{2 imes {{10}^{ - 2}} imes 2 imes \sin 90^\circ }}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \,q/m = {10^8}C\,\,k{g^{ - 1}}\]

Question: In terms of Bohr radius a<sub>0</sub>, the radius of the second Bohr orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by
Solution:
Bohr’s radius ${a_0}$.
We know that, radius $r \propto {n^2}$where, $n$ is thenumber of shells.
Therefore, the radius of the second Bohr orbit of a hydrogen atom is $4{a_0}$.

Question: The mass of a box is 2.3 kg. Two marbles of masses 2.15 g and 12.39 g are added to it. The total mass of the box to the correct number of significant figures is
Solution:
Total mass

\[ = 2.3 + 0.00215 + 0.01239 = 2.31\;kg\]

Total mass in appropriate significant figures be 2.3 kg.

Question:
The displacement of the particle from its mean position (in metre) is given by
y = 0.2 sin (10πt + 1.5π) cos (10πt + 1.5π).

The motion of the particle is
Options: periodic but not simple harmonic motion
simple harmonic motion with period of 0.1 s
simple harmonic motion with period of 0.2 s.
Solution:
$y = 0.2\sin \left( {10\pi t + 1.5\pi } \right)\cos \left( {10\pi t + 1.5\pi } \right)$
$ \Rightarrow y = 0.1\sin \left( {20\pi t + 3\pi } \right)$

This motion is SHM and $$\omega = 20\pi = \frac{{2\pi }}{T}$$

$ \Rightarrow T = 0.1{\text{ sec}}$

Question: Under what condition the magnitude of the average velocity of a particle is equal to the average speed?
Options: If motion is in a straight line
If motion is unidirectionally in a straight line
Solution: The magnitude of the average velocity of a particle is equal to the average speed if it moves along a straight line unidirectionally, that is distance equal to the displacement of the body.

Question: In the above question, the net force acting on the block of mass m is :

Solution:
Net force, acting on the block

Question: When a body is dropped from a tower, then there is an increase in its
Solution: Speed increases due to acceleration due to gravity.

Question: A spring is vibrating with frequency under same mass. If it is cut into two equal pieces and same mass is suspended by connecting them in parallel then the new frequency will be :
Solution:
For vibrating spring, $$f = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{k}{m}} $$, n is frequency.
New spring constant, $$k' = 2k$$

$$f' = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{2k}}{m}} = f\sqrt 2 $$

Question: A disc of mass 10 g is kept floating horizontally by throwing 10 marbles per second against it from below. If the mass of each marble is 5 g. What will be the velocity with which the marble is striking the disc ? Assume that the marble strikes the disc normally and rebound downwards with the same speed:
Solution:
Weight of disc acting downward \[ = Mg\]
Change in velocity of marble after rebound

\[ = v - \left( { - v} \right) = 2v\]

Change in momentum of each marble\[ = m\left( {2v} \right) = 2mv\]

Total momentum imparted by marbles per second to the disc \[ = 2mnv\]

The disc remains at rest if weight of disc acting downward is same as momentum of marbles:

\[Mg = 2mnv\]

\[ \Rightarrow v = \frac{{Mg}}{{2mn}} = \frac{{\left( {0.010} \right)\left( {9.8} \right)}}{{2\left( {0.005} \right)\left( {10} \right)}} = 0.98\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}\]

Question: Two immiscible liquids P and Q of different densities are contained in a wide U-tube as shown in fig. The heights of the two liquids above the horizontal line XX` which cuts the boundary between the liquids are HP and HQ respectively. The U-tube is transported to a planet where the acceleration of free fall is 2/3 that on the earth, where the liquids do not evaporate and where the heights of liquid (measured relative to XX`) are hP and hQ respectively. Which of the given statements is correct?
Options: The liquid levels are unchanged, i.e., h<sub>P</sub> = H<sub>P</sub> and h<sub>Q</sub> = H
Both liquid levels rise up so that h<sub>P</sub> - h<sub>Q</sub>= H<sub>P</sub> - H<sub>Q</sub>
Both liquid levels rise up so that h<sub>P</sub>/H<sub>P</sub> = h<sub>Q</sub>/H<sub>Q</sub>
The liquid P falls, and liquid Q rises such that $$\frac{{{h_P}}}{{{h_Q}}} = \frac{2}{3}\frac{{{H_P}}}{{{H_Q}}}$$
Solution:
Equating pressures at level XX’ for both columns,
$${P_0} + {H_Q}{\rho _Q}g = {P_0} + {H_P}{\rho _P}g$$

$$\frac{{{H_Q}}}{{{H_P}}} = \frac{{{\rho _P}}}{{{\rho _Q}}}{\text{ }}...(1)$$

$$( \Rightarrow H\rho = {\text{constant)}}$$

Similarly, in another planet

$${P_0} + {h_Q}{\rho _Q}\left( {g \times \frac{2}{3}} \right) = {P_0} + {h_P}{\rho _P}\left( {g \times \frac{2}{3}} \right)$$

$$\frac{{{h_Q}}}{{{h_P}}} = \frac{{{\rho _P}}}{{{\rho _Q}}}{\text{ }}...(2)$$

$$ \Rightarrow h\rho = {\text{constant}}$$

From (1) and (2), it is clear that the liquids levels are unchanged. i.e.,

$${h_P} = {\text{ }}{H_P}\,,\,{h_Q} = {\text{ }}{H_Q}$$

Question: Two stones of masses 4 kg and 8 kg are moving with velocities 4 m/s and 10 m/s towards each other due to mutual gravitational attraction. The velocity of their centre of mass will be :
Solution:
Using conservation of momentum:
\[\left( {{m_1} + {m_2}} \right)v = {m_1}{v_1} + \left( { - {m_2}{v_2}} \right)\]

\[v = \left| {\frac{{{m_1}{v_1} - {m_2}{v_2}}}{{\left( {{m_1} + {m_2}} \right)}}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{16 - 80}}{{12}}} \right| = 5.333\,m{s^{ - 1}}\]

Question: In the above question, the minimum value of coefficient of friction so that sphere may roll without slipping is :
Solution:
${f_l} = \mu N,{\text{ }}{f_l} = \mu mg\cos \theta ;$

${f_s} = \frac{2}{7}mg\sin \theta $

${f_l} \geqslant {f_s}$

$\mu mg\cos \theta \geqslant \frac{2}{7}mg\sin \theta $

${\mu _{\min }} \geqslant \frac{2}{7}\tan \theta $

Question: If a conductor is electrically neutral, then
Options: Net charge on it should be zero
Potential on it should be zero
Both charge and potential should be zero
None of these may not be zero
Solution: The net charge on the conductor should be zero, as conductor is electrically neutral. So, the potential of a neutral conductor may not be zero and it depends on the other charges available in the surrounding.

Question: A uniform rod of length L and mass 18 kg is made to rest on two measuring scale at its two ends. A uniform block of mass 2.7 kg is placed on the rod at a distance L/4 from the left end. The force experienced by the measuring scale on the right end is nearly:
Solution:
Net torque with respect to point ‘O’ is zero.

Now,

\[{m_1}g\frac{L}{4} + Mg\frac{L}{2} = {N_2}L\]

\[10 \times \left( {2.7\frac{L}{4} + 18\frac{L}{2}} \right) = {N_2}L\]

\[{N_2} = 10\left( {\frac{{2.7}}{4} + \frac{{18}}{2}} \right) = 96.75N\]

Question: Forces exerted by a uniform electric field on an electron having mass me and proton of mass mp are represented as Fe and Fp respectively are related as
Solution:
The force (F) on charge q due to electric field strength E is
$$F = qE$$
Here, E is uniform, hence $${F_p} = {F_e}.$$

Question: When a body is weighed in a liquid, the loss in its weight depends upon:
Solution: Loss in weight is equal to upthrust. Upthrust depends on weight of liquid displaced and weight of liquid displaced depends on volume of liquid displaced, density of liquid and acceleration due to gravity. So, loss in weight depends on volume of body.

Question: The binding energy per nucleon is maximum in case of
Solution:
The net binding energy of a nucleus is the difference between the nuclear attraction and the electrostatic repulsion between positively charged protons. As the atomic number of an element
increases its binding energy also increases but proton-proton repulsion also increases.
Therefore, the number of protons is less in iron $_{26}F{e^{56}}$, so it is more stable and has maximum
binding energy per nucleon, and has less proton-proton repulsion.

Question: A particle of mass $ M $ , starting from rest, undergoes uniform acceleration. If the speed acquired in time $ T $ is $ V $ , the power delivered to the particle is
Solution: Power $  = \frac{{{\rm{work}}}}{{{\rm{time}}}} = \frac{{{\rm{change}}\,\,{\rm{in}}\,\,{\rm{K}}{\rm{.E}}{\rm{.}}}}{{{\rm{time}}}} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{M{V^2}}}{T} $

Question: The numbers  $ 2.745\,\,\operatorname{and} \,\,2.735 $  on rounding off to  $ 3 $  significant figures will give
Solution:
Rounding off $ 2.745\,\,\operatorname{to} \,\,3 $ significant figures it would be $ 2.74 $
Rounding off $ 2.735\,\operatorname{to} \,3 $ significant figures it would be $ 2.74 $

Question: If the force applied is F and the velocity gained is v, then the power developed is :
Solution:
Rate of work done is called power
\[P = \frac{W}{t} = \frac{{Fs}}{t}\]
\[P = Fv\]

Question: When a ship floats on water:
Options: the mass of water displaced is equal to the mass of the ship
the mass of water displaced is lesser than the mass of the ship
the mass of water displaced is greater than the mass of the ship
Solution: Archimedes principle suggests that a body, when immersed (partially or fully) in a liquid, experiences an upward force, which is equal to the weight of the liquid displaced by it. Clearly, when a ship floats on water, the mass of water displaced is equal to the weight of the ship.

Question: Voltage in the secondary coil of a transformer does not depend upon
Options: ratio of number of turns in the two coils
Solution: Voltage in the secondary coil of a transformer does not depend upon frequency of the source.

Question: A coil of inductive reactance 31Ω has a resistance of 8Ω. It is placed in series with a condenser of capacitive reactance 25Ω. The combination is connected to an AC source of 110 V. The power factor of the circuit is
Solution:
Power factor of AC circuit is given by
\[\cos \phi = \frac{R}{Z}\]…(i)
where, R is resistance employed and Z the impedance of the circuit.
\[Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {{X_L} - {X_C}} \right)}^2}} \]…(ii)
Eqs. (i) and (ii), meet to give
\[\cos \phi = \frac{R}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {{X_L} - {X_C}} \right)}^2}} }}\]…(iii)
Given, R = 8Ω, XL = 31Ω
And XC = 25Ω
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\cos \phi = \frac{8}{{\sqrt {{{\left( 8 \right)}^2} + {{\left( {31 - 25} \right)}^2}} }} = \frac{8}{{\sqrt {64 + 36} }} = \frac{8}{{10}} = 0.80\]

Question: A boy throws a ball with a velocity V<sub>0</sub> at an angle α to the horizontal. At the same instant he starts running with uniform velocity to catch the ball before it hits the ground. To achieve this, he should run with a velocity of
Solution: Since there is no acceleration acting along the X component of the motion of the ball, the boy should move at the same speed as the X component of the ball, which is equal to \[{V_0}\cos \alpha \]

Question: The change in surface energy when a drop of surface tension T and radius R splits into 1000 droplets of radius r is :
Solution:
$$\frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3} = 1000 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}$$
$$R = 10r$$

Final surface area $$ = 1000 \times 4\pi \times \frac{{{R^2}}}{{100}} = 40\pi {R^2}$$

Work done = change in surface energy,

$$W = T\Delta A = (40 - 4)\pi {R^2}T = 36\pi {R^2}T$$

Question: The length of a rectangular sheet is 1.5 cm and breadth is 1.203 cm. The area of the face of rectangular sheet to the correct no of significant figures is:
Three amplifiers each with a voltage gain of 10 are connected in series. Then overall amplification is:
Solution:
If G is gain of one stage, then gain of n stages =
\[{G^n} = {10^3} = 1000\]

Question: In the figure shown, horizontal force ${F_1}$is applied on a block but the block does not slide. Then as the magnitude of vertical force ${F_2}$is increased from zero the block begins to slide; the correct statement is
Options: The magnitude of normal reaction on block increases
Static frictional force acting on the block increases
Maximum value of static frictional force decreases
Solution:
$N + {F_2} = mg$
$N = mg - {F_2}$
As ${F_2}$increases N will decrease
Static friction ${f_s} = {\mu _s}N = {\mu _s}\left( {mg - {F_2}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow $By increasing ${F_2},{f_s}$will decrease hence the block will slide.

Question: A body of mass 2 kg is tied at one end of a string 1 m long. The other end is fixed and the body revolves in a horizontal circle. The maximum tension which the string can withstand is 2000 N. Calculate the maximum number of revolutions per minute the body will make and its linear velocity when the string just breaks-
Solution:
We know, \[T = m{\omega ^2}r\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2000 = 2{\left( {2\pi n} \right)^2} \cdot l\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2000 = 2 \times 4{\pi ^2} \cdot {n^2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow {n^2} = \frac{{2000}}{{8 \times {\pi ^2}}} = \frac{{2000}}{{8 \times 10}}\]
\[\therefore n = 5\]
∴ No. of revolutions \[ = 5 \times 60rpm = 300rpm\]
\[\therefore v = \omega r\]
\[ \Rightarrow v = 2\pi n \cdot r = 2\pi \times 5 \times l\]
\[ \Rightarrow = 31.6m/s\]

Question: A 6 kg ball starts from rest and rolls down a rough gradual slope until it reaches a point 80 cm lower than its starting point. Then the speed of the ball is :
Solution:
By taking ball + earth as a system.
\[\therefore {W_{ext}} = \Delta K.E. + \Delta P.E.\]

\[0 = \frac{1}{2}M{v_c}^2 + \frac{1}{2}{I_c}{\omega ^2} + \left( { - Mgh} \right)\]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}M\left( {{v^2} + \frac{2}{5}{v^2}} \right) = Mgh\]

{$\because \omega = \frac{v}{R},I = \frac{2}{5}M{R^2}$ and, K.E. is translational + rotational}

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{7}{{10}}{v^2} = gh\]

\[v = \sqrt {\frac{{10\left( {0.8} \right)10}}{7}} = 3.35{\text{ m/s}}\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
Tags.Topic: Dynamics of Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis

Question: As per the diagram, a point charge +q is placed at the origin O. Work done in taking another point charge –Q from the point A [coordinates (0, a)] to another point B [coordinates (a; 0)] along the straight path AB is:
Solution:
$$U = \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r_{12}}}}$$
$${U_A} = \frac{{\left( { - Q} \right)q}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}\left( {OA} \right)}} = - \frac{{Qq}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}a}}$$$${U_B} = \frac{{\left( { - Q} \right)q}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}\left( {OB} \right)}} = - \frac{{Qq}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}a}}$$

$${W_{AB}} = {U_A} - {U_B} = - \frac{{Qq}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}a}} - \left( { - \frac{{Qq}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}a}}} \right) = 0$$

Question: In the figure, the equivalent capacitance between A and B is
Solution:

It is a balanced Wheatstone bridge.
$$ \Rightarrow \,\,{C_{AB}} = \frac{{5 \times 3}}{{5 + 3}} + \frac{{5 \times 3}}{{5 + 3}}$$
$$ \Rightarrow {C_{AB}} = 2 \times \frac{{15}}{8}\, = \frac{{30}}{8} = 3.75\,\mu F$$

Question: The vector having initial and terminal points as (2, 5, 0) and (–3, 7, 4), respectively is
Solution:
Let A and B be two points whose coordinates are given as (2, 5, 0) and (– 3, 7, 4)

$\therefore \,\,\,\overrightarrow {AB} = \left( { - 3 - 2} \right)\widehat i + \left( {7 - 5} \right)\widehat j + \left( {4 - 0} \right)\widehat k$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\overrightarrow {AB} = - 5\widehat i + 2\widehat j + 4\widehat k$

Hence, the correct option is (c).

Question: A flywheel of mass 50 kg and radius of gyration about its axis of rotation of 0.25 m is acted upon by a constant torque of 12.5 Nm. Its angular velocity at t = 5 sec is:
Solution:
Moment of inertia,
\[I = M{K^2}\]

\[ = 50{\left( {0.25} \right)^2} = \,3.125kg - {m^2}\]

\[{\text{Torque}} = I\alpha \]

\[12.5 = 3.125\left( \alpha \right)\]

\[ \Rightarrow \alpha = 4{\text{ rad/se}}{{\text{c}}^2}\]

After 5sec,

\[\omega = 0 + \alpha t = 20{\text{ rad/sec}}\]

Question: C<sup>14</sup> has half life 5700 years. At the end of 11400 years, the actual amount left is
A mass M is lowered with the help of a string by a distance x at a constant acceleration \[\frac{g}{2}\]. The magnitude of work done by the string will be:
Solution:
Tension in string by law of motion


\[mg - T = ma\]

\[{\text{T}} = mg - ma = mg - \frac{{mg}}{2} = \frac{{mg}}{2}\]

\[work{\text{ }} = T \times d \times \cos \theta \]

\[\therefore W = \frac{{mg}}{2} \times x \times \cos 180^\circ = - \frac{{mgx}}{2}\]

Question: Dynamic resistance of a diode is given by :
Solution:
Dynamic resistance is given as the ratio of change in voltage across the diode to the resulting change in current. It is also called ac resistance (Resistance offered by a diode)
\[{r_d}\]or\[{r_{ac}} = \frac{{{V_{ac}}}}{{{i_{ac}}}} = \frac{{\Delta v}}{{\Delta I}}\]
Range\[{r_d}\] is 1 to 25 ohm

Question: An object of mass 2 kg is moving with a velocity of 3 m/s and collides head on with an object B of mass 1 kg moving in the opposite direction with a velocity of 4 m/s. After collision, both objects coalesce so that they move with a common velocity v equal to
Solution:
The resultant momentum before the collision will be,

\[P = 2kg \times 3m{s^{ - 1}} + \left( { - 1kg \times 4m{s^{ - 1}}} \right) = 2kgm{s^{ - 1}}\]

Negative sign because of the opposite direction.

After the collision, the objects got stuck to each other. Therefore the mass will become,

\[1 + 2 = 3kg\]

Let us assume that the new velocity be \[v\]. The new momentum will be equivalent to the old one. That is,

\[3v = 2\]

\[\therefore v = \frac{2}{3}m{s^{ - 1}}\]

The direction will be the direction of 2kg.

Question: The (t – q) graph for a current carrying coil placed in uniform magnetic field B where t is torque magnitude on coil and q is the angle made by $$\vec A\,\,and\,\,\vec B$$.
Solution:
$\overrightarrow \tau   = \overrightarrow M  \times \overrightarrow B $

$\tau  = MB\sin \theta $

$\tau  \propto \sin \theta $

Question: The stable nucleus that has a radius half that of Fe56 is
Solution:
The radius of the nucleus, $R = {r_0}{A^{\frac{1}{3}}}$where, $A$ is the mass number.
Now, $\frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}$
Therefore, the radius half that of $F{e^{56}}$ is, ${R_1} = R$,
and ${R_2} = \frac{R}{2}$, and ${A_1} = 56$
$ \Rightarrow 2 = {\left( {\frac{{56}}{{{A_2}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}$
$\therefore {A_2} = \frac{{56}}{8} = 7$
Hence, the stable nucleus that has a radius half that of $F{e^{56}}$ is $L{i^7}$.

Question: The temperature at which the root mean square speed of a gas will be half its value at $0^\circ C$is (assume the pressure remains constant):

$T = - 204.75^\circ C$

Question: A conducting sphere of radius R is charged to a potential of V volt. Then the electric field at a distance r (> R) from the centre of the sphere would be
Solution:
Conducting sphere

If r > R

$$V = \frac{1}{{4\pi { \in _0}}}.\frac{Q}{R} = k.\frac{Q}{R}$$

$$Q = \frac{{V.R}}{k},E = k.\frac{Q}{{{r^2}}}$$

$$E = k.\frac{{VR}}{{k{r^2}}} \Rightarrow E = \frac{{VR}}{{{r^2}}}$$

Question: If $A:B = 5:3,$ then what is the value of $\left( {A + B} \right):\left( {A - B} \right)$?
Solution:
According to Componendo and dividendo rule,

If $\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right) = \left( {\frac{c}{d}} \right),$ then $\frac{{\left( {a + b} \right)}}{{\left( {a - b} \right)}} = \frac{{\left( {c + d} \right)}}{{\left( {c - d} \right)}}$

Hence,

If $A:B = 5:3$

$\therefore \,\,\,\left( {A + B} \right):\left( {A - B} \right) = \left( {5 + 3} \right):\left( {5 - 3} \right) = 8:2 = 4:1$

Question: The wavelength associated with an electron, accelerated through a potential difference of 100 V, is of the order of
Solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength associated with an electron, $\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2meV} }}$
Where, potential difference, $V = 100{\text{ V}}$, and mass of the electron, $m = 9.1 \times {10^{ - 31}}{\text{ kg}}$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}{{\sqrt {2 \times 9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 100} }}$
\[\therefore \lambda = 1.2 \times {10^{ - 10}}{\text{ }}m = 1.2\mathop A\limits^o \]

Question: What is the ratio of maximum acceleration to the maximum velocity of a simple harmonic oscillator ?
Solution:
Maximum velocity = $a\omega $
Maximum acceleration = $a{\omega ^2}$

Ratio = $\frac{{a{\omega ^2}}}{{a\omega }}\, = \,\omega $

Question: Simple pendulum is executing simple harmonic motion with time period T. If the length of the pendulum is increased by 21%, then the increase in the time period of the pendulum of the increased length is :
Solution:
$$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{\ell }{g}} $$
$$\ell ' = 1.21\ell $$

$$T' = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{\left( {1.21} \right)\ell }}{g}} = 1.12\pi \sqrt {\frac{\ell }{g}} $$

$$T' = 1.1T$$

$$\Delta T = 0.1T$$

$$\frac{{\Delta T}}{T} \times 100 = 10\% $$

Question:
A block is left from x = + A, its speed at $$\frac{A}{2}$$ is
($$\omega $$ = 2 rad/s).
Solution:
$x\, = \,A\,\sin \omega t\, = \,A\sin 2t$
$$V = A\omega \cos \omega t = 2A\cos 2t$$

$$\frac{A}{2} = A\sin 2t \Rightarrow \sin 2t = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos 2t = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}$$

$$V = 2A \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} = \sqrt 3 A$$

Question: Find the total displacement of a body in 10 s starting from rest with an acceleration of 0.4 cm/s<sup>2</sup>:
Solution:
Initial velocity \[{v_0} = 0\]
Time, t = 10 sec

Acceleration,

a = 0.4 m/s2

We know that;

\[ \Rightarrow d = {v_0}t + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow d = 0 + \frac{1}{2}\left( {0.40} \right)\left( {{{10}^2}} \right)\]

\[ \Rightarrow d = 20{\text{ cm}}\]

Question: The displacement of particle between maximum potential energy position and maximum kinetic energy position in simple harmonic motion is:

Question: Masses of 2 kg each are placed at the corners B and A of a rectangular plate ABCD as shown in the figure. A mass of 8 kg to be placed on the plate so that the centre of mass of the system should be at the centre O. Then the mass should be placed at :
Solution:
Let \[8kg\] is at \[({x_0},{y_0})\]

Let the origin at O

\[\therefore {x_{cm}} = \frac{{2\left( { - 2} \right) + 2\left( { - 2} \right) + 8 \times {x_0}}}{{2 + 2 + 8}}\]

\[0 = - 8 + 8 \times {x_0}\]

\[{x_0} = 1\]

\[{y_{cm}} = \frac{{2\left( y \right) + 2\left( { - y} \right) + 8{y_0}}}{{2 + 2 + 8}}\]

\[0 = \left( y \right) - 2y + 8{y_0}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {y_0} = 0\]

Solution:

Question: A cart of mass M is tied to one end of a massless rope of length 10 m. The other end of the rope is in the hands of a man of the same mass M. The entire system is on a smooth horizontal surface. The man is at x = 0 and the cart at x = 10 m. If the man pulls the cart by the rope, the man and the cart will meet at the point :
Solution:
In the absence of external force, the position of centre of mass remains the same therefore they will meet at their centre of mass.
\[{X_{cm}} = \frac{{{m_1}{X_1} + {m_2}{X_2}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}} = \frac{{0 + 10M}}{{2M}} = 5m\]

Question:
Assertion : The speed of whirlwind in a tornado is alarmingly high.
Reason : If no external torque acts on a body, its angular velocity remains conserved.
Options: Both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
Both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
Assertion is true but reason is false
Assertion is false and reason is also false
Solution:
In a whirlwind in a tornado, the air from nearby regions gets concentrated in a small space, thereby decreasing the value of its moment of Inertia considerably. Since,
$I\omega = {\text{constant}}$

So, due to decrease in moment of Inertia of air, its angular speed increases to a high value.

If no external torque acts, then

\[\tau = \frac{{dL}}{{dt}} = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow L = \]constant.

As, in the rotational motion, the moment of Inertia of the body can change due to the change in position of the axis of rotation. So, the angular speed may not remain conserved.

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

Question: The moment of inertia of a rod about an axis through its centre and perpendicular to it is \[\frac{1}{{12}}\,M{L^2}\] (where M is the mass and L the length of the rod). The rod is bent in the middle so that the two halves make an angle of 60°. The moment of inertia of the bent rod about the same axis would be :
Solution:
Bent rod can be treated as two rods having length $\left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)$are glued to each other at an angle of \[60^\circ \]

Thus, we need to find the moment of inertia for each rod about the axis that passes through one of its end and add them together.

Moment of inertia of a rod (length =\[\frac{L}{2}\], mass =\[\frac{M}{2}\]) about the axis that passes through its end;

\[ \Rightarrow I = \frac{{\frac{M}{2}{{\left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)}^2}}}{3}\]

Thus, total moment of inertia of the system will be;

\[ \Rightarrow {I_{net}} = {I_1} + {I_2} = \frac{{\frac{M}{2}{{\left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)}^2}}}{3} + \frac{{\frac{M}{2}{{\left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)}^2}}}{3} = \frac{{M{L^2}}}{{12}}\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

\[k = \sqrt {\frac{{{r^2}}}{2}} = \frac{5}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]

\[k = 3.54cm\]

Question: A cricket ball is thrown with a velocity of 15 m/s at an angle of 30° with the horizontal. The time of flight of the ball will be   (g = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup>)
Solution:
\[u = 15\;m/s,\theta = 30^\circ \]

Time of flight of projectile


\[T = \frac{{2u\sin \theta }}{g}\]

\[ = \frac{{2 \times 15 \times \sin 30}}{{10}} = 3 \times \frac{1}{2}\]

\[\;{\text{T}} = 1.5{\text{sec}}\]

Question: Three charges -q, +Q and -q are placed in a straight line as shown. If the total potential energy of the system is zero, then the ratio q/Q is
Solution:
Identify the pairs:

Left $-q$ and center $+Q$: $U_1 = k \frac{(-q)(Q)}{x}$

Center $+Q$ and right $-q$: $U_2 = k \frac{(Q)(-q)}{x}$

Left $-q$ and right $-q$: $U_3 = k \frac{(-q)(-q)}{2x}$

Set total energy to zero:

$$k \left[ \frac{-qQ}{x} + \frac{-qQ}{x} + \frac{q^2}{2x} \right] = 0$$

Simplify the equation:

$$\frac{-2qQ}{x} + \frac{q^2}{2x} = 0$$

Divide by $q$ and multiply by $2x$:

$$-4Q + q = 0$$

$$q = 4Q$$

Final Ratio:

$$\frac{q}{Q} = 4$$

Question: A bucket is placed in the open where the rain is falling vertically. If a wind begins to blow at double the velocity of the rain, how will the rate of filling of the bucket change?
Solution: As wind blowing will only change the direction of the drops, still the rate of filling the bucket will remain the same.

Question: An electron of mass m and charge e is accelerated from rest through a potential difference V in vacuum. The final speed will be:

Question: Two charged metallic spheres of radii r<sub>1</sub> and r<sub>2</sub> are touched and separated. What is the ratio of their surface charge density?
Solution:
Common potential:
$V = \frac{{k\left( {{Q_1} - x} \right)}}{{{r_1}}} = \frac{{k\left( {{Q_2} + x} \right)}}{{{r_2}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{Q_1} - x}}{{{Q_2} + x}} = \frac{{{r_1}}}{{{r_2}}}$
Ratio of charge densities:
$\frac{{{\sigma _1}}}{{{\sigma _2}}} = \frac{{\frac{{{Q_1} - x}}{{4\pi r_1^2}}}}{{\frac{{{Q_2} + x}}{{4\pi r_2^2}}}} = \frac{{{Q_1} - x}}{{{Q_2} + x}} \times \frac{{r_2^2}}{{r_1^2}} = \frac{{{r_1}}}{{{r_2}}} \times \frac{{r_2^2}}{{r_1^2}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{\sigma _1}}}{{{\sigma _2}}} = \frac{{{r_2}}}{{{r_1}}}$

Question: When a spring is stretched by a distance x, it exerts a force, given by \[F = \left( { - 5x - 16{x^3}} \right)N\]. The work done, when the spring is stretched from 0.1m to 0.2m is :
Solution:
Given, \[F = - 5x - 16{x^3}\]
or \[F = - \left( {5 + 16{x^2}} \right)x\]
or \[F = - kx\]
where \[k\left( { = 5 + 16{x^2}} \right)\] is force constant of spring. Therefore, work done in stretching the spring from position \[{x_1}\] to position \[{x_2}\] is
\[W = \frac{1}{2}{k_2}x_2^2 - \frac{1}{2}{k_1}x_1^2\]
We have \[{x_1} = 0.1m\,\,and\,{x_2} = 0.2m\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,W = \frac{1}{2}\left( {5 + 16{{\left( {0.2} \right)}^2}} \right){\left( {0.2} \right)^2} - \frac{1}{2}\left( {5 + 16{{\left( {0.1} \right)}^2}} \right){\left( {0.1} \right)^2}\]
\[ = 2.82 \times 4 \times {10^{ - 2}} - 2.58 \times {10^{ - 2}} = 8.7 \times {10^{ - 2}}J\]

Solution: At resonance XL = XC, hence V2 = 0.

Question: A man is walking due east at the rate of 2 kmh<sup>-1</sup>. The rain appears to him come down vertically at the rate of 2 kmh<sup>-1</sup>. The actual velocity and direction of rainfall with the vertical, respectively are
Solution:
Given, velocity of man
\[{v_m} = 2km{h^{ - 1}}\]

and velocity of rain, \[{v_r} = 2\,km{h^{ - 1}}\]


Then, actual velocity,

\[{v_{rm}} = \sqrt {v_m^2 + v_r^2} = \sqrt {{{\left( 2 \right)}^2} + {{\left( 2 \right)}^2}} \]

\[ = \sqrt {4 + 4} = 2\sqrt 2 \,km{h^{ - 1}}\]

The direction of rainfall with the horizontal (east),

\[\sin \theta = \frac{{{v_m}}}{{{v_{rm}}}} = \frac{2}{{2\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]

\[\theta = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right) \Rightarrow \theta = 45^\circ \]

Thus, direction of rainfall with the vertical is \[45^\circ .\]

Question: A body of mass 500 g is taken up an inclined plane of length 10 m and height 5 m, and then released to slide down to the bottom. The coefficient of friction between the body and the plane is 0.1. What is the amount of work done by friction in the round trip?
Solution:
Given,

\[m = 500\,gm\]

\[\mu = 0.1\]

Force of friction \[ = \mu mg\cos \theta = \frac{1}{{10}} \times \frac{{500}}{{1000}} \times \frac{{5\sqrt 3 \times 10}}{{10}}\]

Work by friction \[ = \frac{1}{{10}} \times \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{5\sqrt 3 }}{{10}} \times 10 \times 10\]

Work for Round trip is double. (Same amount)

\[W = 2 \times \frac{1}{{10}} \times \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{5\sqrt 3 }}{{10}} \times 10 \times 10 = 5\sqrt 3 J\]

Question: If charge and distance between two charges are reduced to half, then force between them will

In an L-C-R circuit R = 100 Ω. When capacitance C is removed, the current lags behind the voltage by π/3, when inductance L is removed, the current leads the voltage by π/3. The impedance of the circuit is
Solution:
When C is removed, circuit becomes R-L circuit
Hence, \[\tan \frac{\pi }{3} = \frac{{{X_L}}}{R}\] …(i)
When L is removed, circuit becomes R-C circuit.
Hence \[\tan \frac{\pi }{3} = \frac{{{X_C}}}{R}\] …(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
XL = XC, this is the condition of resonance, Z = R = 100 Ω.

Question: A train of mass 100 ton is moving up an incline of 1 in 100 at a constant speed of 36 km ph. If the friction per ton is 100 N, then power of the engine is

\[v = 36\;km/hr\]

\[ = 36 \times \frac{5}{{18}} = 10\;m/s\]

\[m = 100 \times {10^3}\;kg\]

\[F = (mg\sin \theta + f)\]

\[P = F \times v = (mg\sin \theta + f) \times v,f = 100 \times 100\]

Since, it is given that friction per ton is 100N.

= 198 kW

Question: The maximum range of a gun on horizontal terrain is 1.0 km. If g = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup>, what must be the muzzle velocity of the shell?
Solution:
Formula for maximum range is,

${R_{\max }} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{g}$

${u^2} = g \times {R_{\max }}$

Let’s convert${R_{\max }}$ from unit km into m

${R_{\max }} = 1{\text{ }}km$

$ = 1000{\text{ }}m$

Now,

${u^2} = 1000 \times 10$

$u = \sqrt {10000} $

$u = 100{\text{ }}m/s$

Question: The electric intensity outside a charged sphere of radius R at a distance r(r > R) is
Solution:
The electric intensity outside a charged sphere,
$$E = \frac{{kq}}{{{r^2}}} = \frac{{k\left( {\sigma A} \right)}}{{{r^2}}}\, = \left( {\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}} \right)\frac{{\sigma 4\pi {R^2}}}{{{r^2}}} = \frac{{\sigma {R^2}}}{{{\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}$$

Question: An air bubble of radius 10–2 m is raising up at steady rate of 2 × 10–3 m/s through a liquid of density 1.5 × 103 kg/m3. The coefficient of viscosity neglecting the density of air, will be : (g = 10 m/s2)
Solution:
Formula for viscous force,
$${F_v} = 6\pi \eta rv$$

Formula for buoyancy force,

$${F_b} = \rho g\frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}$$

Equating both forces

$$6\pi \eta rv = \rho g\frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}$$

$$\eta = \frac{{1.5 \times {{10}^3} \times 10 \times 4 \times \pi \times {{\left( {{{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)}^3}}}{{6\pi \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 3}}$$

$$\eta = 1.67 \times {10^2}$$

$$\eta = 167{\text{ }}units$$

Question: Rectifier is used to convert :
Solution: A rectifier is an electrical device which is used to convert alternating current (AC) into direct current (DC). It periodically reverses the direction of an oscillating two-directional alternating current (AC) into a single-directional direct current (DC).

Question: An L-C-R series circuit, connected to a source E, is at resonance. Then,
Options: the voltage across R is zero
the voltage across R equals applied voltage
the voltage across C is zero
the voltage across C equals applied voltage

Equation of voltage,

At resonance (series circuit),
\[{V_L} = {V_C}\,\, \Rightarrow E = {V_R}\]
i.e. whole applied voltage appeared across the resistance.

Question:

Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.

Question: The sphere A of mass m1 moves with velocity V on a frictionless horizontal surface and strikes with sphere B of mass m2 at rest. The sphere A comes back with speed V/10. Find the correct option:
Solution:
From conservation of linear momentum

\[{m_1}{v_1} + 0 = {m_1}\left( { - \frac{v}{{10}}} \right) + {m_2}{v_2}\]

\[\frac{{11{m_1}v}}{{10}} = {m_2}{v_2}\,...\left( 1 \right)\]

From newton’s law of restitution

\[{v_2} - {v_1} = e\left( {{u_1} - {u_2}} \right)\]

\[{v_2} + \frac{v}{{10}} = ev\]

\[{v_2} = \left( {e - \frac{1}{{10}}} \right)v\,\,....\left( 2 \right)\]

From (1) and (2)

\[\frac{{11{m_1}v}}{{10}} = {m_2}v\left( {e - \frac{1}{{10}}} \right)\]

\[\frac{{{m_1}}}{{{m_2}}} = \frac{{10e - 1}}{{11}}\]

since, 0 < e < 1. so \[{m_1} < {m_2}\]

Question:

Question: Two radioactive materials X<sub>1</sub> and X<sub>2</sub> have decay constants 5λ and λ respectively. If initially they have the same number of nuclei, then the ratio of the number of nuclei of X<sub>1</sub> to that of X<sub>2</sub> will be \[\frac{1}{e}\] after a time
Solution:
The number of radioactive nuclei, $N = {N_0}{e^{ - \lambda t}}$
Now, ${\lambda _1} = 5\lambda {\text{, and }}{\lambda _2} = \lambda $
Therefore, $\frac{{{N_1}}}{{{N_2}}} = {e^{ - \left( {5\lambda - \lambda } \right)t}} = {e^{ - 4\lambda t}}$
Where, $\frac{{{N_1}}}{{{N_2}}} = \frac{1}{e}$(given)
Therefore, $\frac{1}{e} = {\left( {\frac{1}{e}} \right)^{4\lambda t}}$
$\therefore 4\lambda t = 1 \Rightarrow t = \frac{1}{{4\lambda }}$

Question:

Statement - 2: An adiabatic process is not quasi-static because it is a sudden and large change of the system.
(A) Statement–I is True, Statement–II is True; Statement–II is a correct explanation for Statement–I.
(B) Statement–I is True, Statement–II is True; Statement–2 is NOT a correct explanation for Statement–I.
(C) Statement–I is True, Statement–II is False.
(D) Statement–I is False, Statement–II is True.
Solution: Statement-1 is false because quasi-static process is a infinity slow process. Statement-2 is true.

Question: Two springs of spring constants $${k_1}$$ and $${k_2}$$ are joined in series. The effective spring constant of the combination is given by :
Solution:
According to hooke’s law,
$$\frac{{{f_1}}}{{{k_1}}} = {x_1}$$

$$\frac{{{f_2}}}{{{k_2}}} = {x_2}$$

Total displacement is given by,

$$X = {x_1} + {x_2}$$

$$X = \frac{{{f_1}}}{{{k_1}}} + \frac{{{f_2}}}{{{k_2}}}$$

$$X = \frac{F}{{{k_1}}} + \frac{F}{{{k_2}}}$$

$$X = F\left( {\frac{1}{{{k_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{k_2}}}} \right)$$

$$X = F\left( {\frac{{{k_1} + {k_2}}}{{{k_1}{k_2}}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow F = \left( {\frac{{{k_1}{k_2}}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}}} \right)X$$

$$\therefore k = \frac{{{k_1}{k_2}}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}}$$

Question: A force \[\vec F = 2\hat i + 2\hat j\;N\] displace a particle through \[\vec S = 2\hat i + 2\hat k\;m\] in 16 s. The power developed by \[\vec F\] is
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow F = 2\widehat i + 2\widehat j,\overrightarrow S = 2\widehat i + 2\widehat k\] and \[\Delta t = 16\;\sec \]

Work done, \[\Delta w = \int {\overrightarrow F \cdot \overrightarrow {ds} = \overrightarrow F \cdot \overrightarrow s = 4} \]

Power \[ = \frac{{dw}}{{dt}} = \frac{{\Delta w}}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{4}{{16}} = 0.25J/s\]

Question: The length of second’s pendulum of the earth is about 1 m. What should be the length of second’s pendulum on the moon?

${g_{moon}} = \frac{1}{6}{g_{earth}}$

Question:
Assertion: The mass of positively charged body is slightly less than the mass of negatively charged body.
Reason: Loosely bounded electrons in a material and transferred on rubbing.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: A positively charged body loses electrons and a negatively charged body has excess electrons. Therefore, both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.

Question: A car travels first half the distance between two places with a speed of 30 km/h and the remaining half with a speed of 80 km/h. The average speed of the car is
Solution:
Let total distance travelled by car be ‘d’.
Time taken to travel first half distance\[\left( {\frac{d}{2}} \right)\], travelled at a speed of 30 km/h, is

\[ \Rightarrow {t_1} = \frac{d}{{60}}\]

Time taken to travel second half \[\left( {\frac{d}{2}} \right)\], travelled at a speed of 80 km/h, is \[ \Rightarrow {t_2} = \frac{d}{{160}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {\text{Average speed}} = \frac{{{\text{total distance}}}}{{{\text{total time}}}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {v_{{\text{avg}}}} = \frac{d}{{{t_1} + {t_2}}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {v_{{\text{avg}}}} = \frac{d}{{\frac{d}{{60}} + \frac{d}{{160}}}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {v_{{\text{avg}}}} = 43.6{\text{ }}km/h\]

Question: The equation of a particle executing SHM is $$\frac{{2{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} + 32x = 0$$. Then, the time period (in seconds) of the body is
Solution:
The equation of SHM is given by
$$\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} + 16x = 0$$

On comparing with standard equation

$$\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} + {\omega ^2}x = 0,$$ we get

$$\therefore {\omega ^2} = 16 \Rightarrow \omega = 4$$

$$ \Rightarrow T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega } = \frac{{2\pi }}{4} = \frac{\pi }{2}$$.

Question: Surface tension is due to

Question: The diameter of a flywheel (Disk) is 1 m. It has a mass of 20 kg. It is rotating about its axis with a speed of 120 rotations is one minute. Its angular momentum (in kg–m<sup>2</sup>/s) is :
Solution:
Angular momentum \[L = I\omega \]
\[\omega = \frac{{2\pi \times 120}}{{60}} = 4\pi {\text{ }}rad/\operatorname{s} \]

\[L = \frac{{20 \times {{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)}^2}}}{2} \times 4\pi \]

\[L = 10\pi = 10 \times 3.14 = 31.4kg{m^2}/s\]

Question: The moment of inertia of a regular circular disc of mass 0.4 kg and radius 100 cm about the axis perpendicular to the plane of the disc and passing through its centre is

Radius, r = 100 cm = 1 m
Moment of inertia of the disc

Question:
Assertion: During lightning, it is safe to sit inside the car.
Reason: The hollow structure of car provides electrostatic shielding.

Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Since cars are made of metal, the net electric field inside is always zero. So charge produced due to lightning is distributed on the outer surface of the car and finally transferred to Earth.

Question: A body of mass 2kg moves down the quadrant of circle of radius 4m. The velocity on reaching the lowest point is 8m/s. What is work done against friction? (use $g = 9.8m/{s^2}$)
Solution:
Initial velocity = 0
${W_f} = {U_{initial}} - {K_{final}}$

${W_f} = \left( {{U_i} - \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}} \right)$
${W_f} = \left( {2 \times 9.8 \times 4 - \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {8^2}} \right)$
$ = 78.4 - 64 = 14.4J$

Question: A semiconductor doped with a donor impurity is:
Solution: Donors are atoms which have more valence electrons than the atoms of the semiconductor material into which they are introduced in small quantities as an impurity or dopant. Since such donor impurities have more valence electrons than the semiconductor, a semiconductor doped with donor impurities is an n-type semiconductor.

Question: The power factor of series L-C-R circuit when at resonance is
Options: depends on values of L , C and R
Solution:
At resonance, voltage and current are in phase. Average power of circuit is given by
\[P = {E_{rms}} \times {I_{rms}} \times \cos \phi \]
where, Erms and Irms are root mean square value of voltage and current and cosφ is power factor. In a series L- C-R circuit at resonance, voltage and current being in same phase, therefore φ = 0.
Hence, power factor = cos φ = cos 0° = 1.

Question: Police is chasing a thief 50 m ahead. In 10s, distance between them reduces by 6m. What is the distance between them in 25s.?

So, distance between them is 25 s

\[ = 50 - 0.6 \times 25 = 35m\]

Unit positive charge held at the centre will be repelled by proton and attracted by electron with same force. Therefore, E cannot be zero. The assertion is wrong. Now V is scalar and E is a vector so reason is also wrong.

Question: In the reverse-biased p-n junction, the current is of the order of :
Solution: Reverse bias supports the potential barriers and no current flows across the junction due to diffusion of majority carriers. A very small reverse current may exists in the circuit of the order of microampere due to drifting of minority carriers across the junction.

Question: A body of mass M<sub>1</sub> collides elastically with another body of mass M<sub>2</sub> at rest. There is maximum transfer of energy when:
Options: same for all values of M<sub>1</sub> and M<sub>2</sub>
Solution:
For maximum transfer of energy \[{M_1} = {M_2}\] (velocity interchanged)
Velocity of \[{M_2}\] after collision,

\[{v_2} = \frac{{2u}}{{1 + \frac{{{M_2}}}{{{M_1}}}}}\]

So, \[K = \frac{1}{2}{M_2}{\left( {\frac{{2u{M_1}}}{{{M_1} + {M_2}}}} \right)^2}\]

\[K = \frac{{2M_1^2 + {M_2}{u^2}}}{{\left( {{M_1} + {M_2}} \right)}},\,putting\,\frac{{dk}}{{d{M_1}}} = 0\]

\[We\,\,get\,\,{M_1} = {M_2}\]

Question: If the momentum of a body is increased by 100% then the percentage increase of kinetic energy will be
Solution:
Relation between momentum and kinetic energy is given by $ \Rightarrow \frac{{{P^2}}}{{2m}} = K.E$
Momentum is increased by 100% so new momentum will be:
$ \Rightarrow P' = 2P$
New kinetic energy will be;
$\% $ Increase in K.E;
$ \Rightarrow \Delta K.E = \frac{{K.{E_f} - K.{E_i}}}{{K.{E_i}}} \times 100$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta K.E = \frac{{4K.E. - K.E}}{{K.E}} \times 100 = 300\% $
Thus kinetic energy will be increased by 300%.

Question: Choose the correct statement. There will be no force experienced if
Options: Two parallel wires carry current in same direction
A positive charge is projected along the axis of the solenoid
A positive charge is projected between the pole pieces of a bar magnet
Two protons move parallel to each other with same speed
Solution:
(i) Two parallel wires carrying currents in some direction attract each others.
(ii) A positive charge projected along the axis of a solenoid carrying current feels no force because in solenoid \[\vec B\] is along the axis, so \[\vec V{\text{ }} \times {\text{ }}\vec B\] is zero.
(iii) A positive charge is projected between the pole pieces of bar magnet feels a force \[f = q\left( {\vec V \times \vec B} \right)\] which in non-zero
(iv) Two protons move parallel to each other with same speed also experience force \[f = e\left( {\vec V{\text{ }} \times {\text{ }}\vec B} \right)\]

Question: The plates of a parallel plate capacitor are not exactly parallel. The surface charge density
Options: is lower at the closer end
each plate will have the same potential at every point
Solution: Being a conductor, each plate have the same potential at each point. As $$E\,\, \propto \,\,\sigma $$, and electric field being the gradient of potential $$\left( {E = \frac{{dV}}{{dr}}} \right)$$highest, where the plates are closest to each other. So, surface charge density is highest at closest end and lowest at far end. Hence, it is not uniform.

Question: Which of the following is positively charged?
Solution:
Alpha particle is a positively charged particle, it has two protons and two neutrons but have no electron to balance the positive charge of the nucleus. On the other hand, the beta particle may be a positively charged or negatively charged particle having magnitude of charge equal to that of electron.
$\gamma - $rays is a neutral particle and it cannot be deflected by the magnetic field but X-rays is an electromagnetic wave of wavelength less than $10{\text{ m}}$.

Question: When an ideal gas $\left( {\gamma  = 5/3} \right)$is heated under constant pressure, when what percentage of given heat energy will be utilised in doing external work
Solution:
$\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta W}}{{\Delta Q}} = 1 - \frac{{\Delta U}}{{\Delta Q}} = 1 - \frac{{n{C_V}dT}}{{n{C_P}dT}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta W}}{{\Delta Q}} = 1 - \frac{{{C_V}}}{{{C_P}}} = 1 - \frac{3}{5} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 = 40\% $

Question: A vector \[\vec A\] when added to the vector \[\vec B = 3\hat i + 4\hat j\]yields a resultant vector that is in the positive y direction and has a magnitude equal to that of \[\vec B\] . Find the magnitude of $\overrightarrow A $?
Solution:
Let

\[\overrightarrow A = x\widehat i + y\widehat j\]

\[\overrightarrow B = 3\widehat i + 4\widehat j \Rightarrow B = \sqrt {{3^2} + {4^2}} = 5\]

\[\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B = \overrightarrow R ;\overrightarrow R \] is in +y direction and has magnitude of $\overrightarrow B .$

\[ \Rightarrow R = 5\widehat j\]

\[\therefore \overrightarrow A = \overrightarrow R - \overrightarrow B = \widehat j - 3\widehat i\]

\[ \Rightarrow A = \sqrt {1 + 9} = \sqrt {10} \]

Question: The period of ${\sin ^2}\theta $ is
Solution:
${\sin ^2}\theta = \frac{{1 - \cos 2\theta }}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \cos 2\theta $ period is $\left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{2}} \right)$
$ = \pi $

Question: When a ball is thrown vertically upwards, it reaches a maximum height of 5m. The initial velocity of the ball was?
Solution:
The equation is given as ${v^2} - {u^2} = 2as$
Where,

v = final velocity,

u = Initial velocity,

a = acceleration due to gravity, 10 m/s2

s = h = height travelled.

Therefore, we can find u with the above information. In this case, v = 0 at the top of the path.

\[0 - {u^2} = 2 \times - 10 \times 5 = - 100m/s\]

\[{u^2} = 100\]

\[u = 10m/s\]

Hence, the initial velocity of the ball was 10 m/s.

Question: Which one of the following is not a thermodynamic co-ordinate?
Solution: In thermodynamics, each system is characterized by certain thermodynamics coordinates, such as pressure (P), temperature (T), volume (V). These thermodynamic coordinates vary from one system to another. Whereas R is a gas constant, which remains constant for any kind of thermodynamic system. Thus, R is not a thermodynamic coordinate.

Question: An object of mass 3M at rest splits into three equal fragments. Two fragments have velocities v$\hat i$ and –v$\hat j$. The velocity of third fragment will be :
Solution:
Let velocity of third particle be
Using conservation of momentum:

\[3M\left( 0 \right) = m\left( {v\widehat i} \right) + m\left( { - v\widehat j} \right) + mu\]

\[u = - \left( {v\widehat i} \right) - \left( { - v\widehat j} \right) = - v\widehat i + v\widehat j = v\left( {\widehat j - \widehat i} \right)\]

Question:
When a positively charged particle enters a uniform magnetic field with uniform velocity, its trajectory can be
(1) a straight line (2) a circle

(3) a helix
Solution:
Force on a moving charge in a magnetic field is $$q\left( {\overrightarrow v \times \overrightarrow B } \right)$$
Thus, if the particle is moving along the magnetic field, $$\overrightarrow F = 0$$

Hence the particle continues to move along the incident direction, in a straight line.

When the particle is moving perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field, the force is perpendicular to both direction of velocity and the magnetic field.
Then the force tends to move the charged particle in a plane perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field, in a circle.
If the direction of velocity has both parallel and perpendicular components to the direction magnetic field, the perpendicular component tends to move it in a circle and parallel component tends to move it along the direction of magnetic field. Hence the trajectory is a helix.

Question: A ground-state electron (in a hydrogen atom) absorb a photon of energy 3E<sub>0</sub>. How much kinetic energy does the electron now have? (\[{{\text{E}}_0}\]is the energy in the ground state of electron)
Solution:
Energy of electron in ground state = \[ - {{\text{E}}_0}\]
So total final energy = \[ - {{\text{E}}_0} + 3{{\text{E}}_0} = 2{{\text{E}}_0}\]

Question: Two solid spheres of unequal mass but same radius are released on inclined plane. They roll down without slipping. Which one will reach the ground first?
Options: Both will reach at the same time
Solution:
Acceleration of a sphere on an inclined plane:
$a = \frac{{g\sin \theta }}{{1 + \frac{{{K^2}}}{{{R^2}}}}}$

Since for both spheres, $\frac{{{K^2}}}{{{R^2}}} = 1$;

acceleration of both spheres are the same and hence, both of them will reach at the same time.

Question: In an experiment, the following observation’s were recorded: L = 2.820 m, M = 3.00 kg, l = 0.087 cm, Diameter D = 0.041 cm Taking g = 9.81 m/s2 using the formula, \[Y = \frac{{4Mg}}{{\pi {D^2}l}},\] the maximum permissible error in Y is
Solution:
\[Y = \frac{{4MgL}}{{\pi {D^2}l}}\] so maximum permissible error in

\[Y = \frac{{\Delta Y}}{Y} \times 100 = \left( {\frac{{\Delta M}}{M} + \frac{{\Delta g}}{g} + \frac{{\Delta L}}{L} + \frac{{2\Delta D}}{D} + \frac{{\Delta l}}{l}} \right) \times 100\]

\[ = \left( {\frac{1}{{300}} + \frac{1}{{9.81}} + \frac{1}{{9820}} + 2 \times \frac{1}{{41}} + \frac{1}{{87}}} \right) \times 100\]

\[ = 0.065 \times 100 = 6.5\% \]

Question: A man slides down a snow-covered hill along a curved path and falls 20 m below his initial position. The velocity in m/sec with which he finally strikes the ground is? (g = 10 m/sec<sup>2</sup>)
Solution:
The equation of motion is
\[{v^2} = {u^2} + 2gh\]

\[{v^2} = 0 + 2gh\]

\[{v^2} = \sqrt {2gh} \]

\[v = \sqrt {2 \times 10 \times 20} \]

\[v = 20m/s\]

Question: As shown in figure, a simple harmonic motion oscillator having identical four springs has time period
Solution:
As equivalent spring constant when joined in series,
$${k_s} = \frac{{k \times k}}{{k + k}} = \frac{k}{2}$$

Now, the spring are in parallel this equivalent.

Spring constant, $${k_p} = \frac{k}{2} + \frac{k}{2} = k$$

Thus, overall k does not change and time period will remain same as

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt {m/k} $$

Question: Which of the following is not the unit of surface tension?

Question: The distance travelled by a body is directly proportional to the time taken, its speed

This motion will be in a uniform motion.
Distance = Kt

where, K is some constant.

hence, speed = D/t = K = constant

(B) $$\frac{1}{2}k{A^2}{e^{ - bt/m}}$$ equals2. Amplitude

4. Total mechanical energy

Question: A body floats with (1/3) of its volume outside water and (3/4) of its volume outside another liquid. The density of the other liquid is:
Solution:
The fraction of volume of floating body immersed in a liquid is equal to relative density of body with respect to that liquid.
Since the body floats with $$\frac{1}{3}$$volume outside water and $$\frac{2}{3}$$ volume is immersed inside water. So, its density is $$\left( {\frac{2}{3}} \right)$$d, where d is density of water.

As the body floats with $$\frac{3}{4}$$ volume outside liquid, the volume immersed in liquid is $$\frac{1}{4}.$$ So, its density is $$\left( {\frac{1}{4}} \right)\,d'$$

$$\frac{1}{4}d' = \frac{{2d}}{3}$$

$$d' = \frac{{8d}}{3} = \frac{8}{3}\left( 1 \right) = \frac{8}{3}g/cc$$

Question: A mass m is revolving in a vertical circle at the end of a string of length 20 cm. By how much does the tension of the string at the lowest point exceed the tension at the top most point?
Solution:
Let A and B be the lowest point and highest point respectively

$\therefore \,$ using conservation of energy
$T.{E_A} = T.{E_B}$
$\frac{1}{2}m{v_1}^2 + 0 = \frac{1}{2}m{v_2}^2 + mg\left( {2r} \right)$
$m{v_1}^2 = m{v_2}^2 + 4mgr$
${T_A} = \frac{{m{v_1}^2}}{r} + mg$
$ = \frac{{m{v_2}^2}}{r} + \frac{{4mgr}}{r} + mg$
${T_A} = \frac{{m{v_2}^2}}{r} + 5\,mg$
${T_B} = \frac{{m{v_2}^2}}{r} - mg$
${T_A} - {T_B} = \frac{{m{v_2}^2}}{r} + 5\,mg - \frac{{m{v_2}^2}}{r} + mg$
$ = 6\,mg$
Hence [c]

Question: Two capacitors of capacitances  $3\mu {\rm{F}}$  and  ${\rm{6}}\mu {\rm{F}}$  are connected in series across a P.D. of  $ 400{\text{ volt}} $ . The P.D. across each condenser is respectively:
Solution:
${C_{e{\rm{ff}}}} = 2{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \mu {\rm{F}}$
 $ V = 400{\text{ V}} $
 $ Q = {C_{{\text{eff}}}}V = 800 \times {10^{ - 6}} = 8 \times {10^{ - 4}}{\text{ C}} $
Both capacitances are connected in series so both have same charge stored
 $ {V_1} = \frac{q}{{{C_1}}} = \frac{{800 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = \frac{{800}}{3}{\text{ V}} $
 $ {V_2} = \frac{q}{{{C_2}}} = \frac{{800 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{6 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = \frac{{400}}{3}{\text{ V}} $

Question: If force $\vec F = 5\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k$ makes a displacement of $\vec s = 6\hat i - 5\hat k,$work done by the force is:
Solution:
Work done $ = \vec F.\vec S$
$W = \left( {5\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right).\left( {6\hat i - 5\hat k} \right)$
$W = \left( {30 + 0 - 20} \right) = 10\,unit$
