Question: An AC is represented by e = 220 sin (100 πt) volt and is applied over a resistance of 110Ω. The heat produced in 7 min is
Solution:
Heat, \[H = \frac{{I_{rms}^2RT}}{J}cal\]
\[\left( {\because \,\,{I_{rms}} = \frac{{{I_0}}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{220/110}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \sqrt 2 } \right)\]
\[\therefore \,\,H = \frac{{{{\left( {\sqrt 2 } \right)}^2} \times 110 \times \left( {7 \times 60} \right)}}{{4.2}} = 22 \times {10^3}\,cal\]

Question: If maximum height and range of a projectile are same. What is the angle of projection?
Solution:
It is given that,
R = H

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{u^2}\sin 2\theta }}{g} = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{2g}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{u^2}2\sin \theta \cos \theta }}{g} = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{2g}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow 4 = \frac{{{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{\sin \theta \cos \theta }}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{\sin \theta }}{{\cos \theta }} = 4\]

\[ \Rightarrow \tan \theta = 4\]

\[\therefore \theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( 4 \right)\]

Question: Shown in the figure are the position time graph for two children going home from the school. Which of the following statements about their relative motion is true after both of them started moving? Their relative velocity (consider 1-D motion)
Solution:
Slope of a displacement time graph gives us velocity.
Here for both the children the graph are straight lines (constant slope). Hence both the children have constant velocities. But they are not equal (different slopes).
Hence relative velocity ${v_2} - {v_1} = $constant (nonzero)

Question: A p-n-p transistor is said to be in active region of operation, when:
Options: both emitter junction and collector junction are forward biased
both emitter junction and collector junction are reverse biased
emitter junction is forward biased and collector junction is reverse biased.
emitter junction is reverse biased and collector junction is forward biased.
Solution: When emitter junction is forward biased and collector junction is reverse biased then a p-n-p transistor is said to be in active region of operation.

Question: There is hole of area A at the bottom of a cylindrical vessel. Water is filled upto a height h and water flows out in t second. If water is filled to a height 4h, it will flow out in time :
Solution:
Formula of equation of motion,
$$v - u = - gt$$

$$t = \frac{u}{g}\left( {\because v = 0} \right)$$

$$t = \frac{{\sqrt {2gh} }}{g}$$

$$t = \frac{{\sqrt {2h} }}{{\sqrt g }}$$

$$t \propto \sqrt h $$

Now taking ratio of time $${t_1}$$ and time $${t_2}$$

$$\frac{{{t_1}}}{{{t_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{h_1}}}{{{h_2}}}} $$

$$\frac{{{t_1}}}{{{t_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{h}{{4h}}} $$

$$\frac{{{t_1}}}{{{t_2}}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$${t_2} = 2t$$

Question: A capacitor of 10 mF and an inductor of 1H are joined in series. An AC of 50 Hz is applied to this combination. What is the impedance of the combination?
Solution:
\[Z = \sqrt {{{\left( {\omega L - \frac{1}{{\omega c}}} \right)}^2}}  = \sqrt {{{\left( {100\pi  - \frac{1}{\pi }} \right)}^2}} \]
\[ = 100\pi  - \frac{1}{\pi }\]

\[|\vec v| = \sqrt {{{(4)}^2} + {{(3)}^2}} \]
\[ = \sqrt {16 + 9} = 5m/{\text{sec}}\]

Question: A given system undergoes a change in which work done by the system equals the decrease in its internal energy. The system must have undergone
Solution:
dQ = dU + dW
If \[\left| {dW} \right| = \left| {dU} \right|\]

Then dQ = 0. Hence process will be adiabatic.

Question: The charge deposited on $$4\,\mu F$$ capacitor in the circuit is
Solution:
Label the figure

Here, capacitors B and C are in parallel. So, their total capacitance is given by, $$4 + 2 = 6\,\mu F$$
Now, total capacity of $$6\,\mu F$$ and capacitors A are in series with respect to battery
$$\therefore \,\,\frac{1}{{{C_T}}} = \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}$$
$${C_T} = 3\,\mu F$$
Now, total charge
$$ = {C_T}V = 3 \times 12 = 36\,\mu C$$
This is the charge on A and also on combination of B and C.
Thus, voltage on combination of capacitors B and C, $$\frac{Q}{6} = \frac{{36}}{6} = 6\,V$$
Now, charge on capacitor B is given by
$${C_B} \times V = 4 \times 6 = 24\mu C = 24 \times {10^{ - 6}}C$$

Question: The centrifugal force always acts_____
Options: Outside of the plane of motion.
Solution: The centrifugal force always acts away from the center of the circle in which the body is moving. In contrast to this, the centripetal force always acts towards the center of the circle, keeping the body moving in the circle.

Question: The minimum potential difference between the base and emitter required to switch a silicon transistor ‘ON’ is approximately
Solution: To switch on the transistor the emitter-base junction of a transistor is forward biased while collector-base junction is reversed biased. The cut off voltage for silicon is 1V, so to switch on a silicon transistor a potential difference of 1 V approximately is required between the base and emitter.

Question: The unit vector perpendicular to $\widehat i - 2\widehat j + \widehat k$ and $3\widehat i + \widehat j - 2\widehat k$ is
Solution:
$\overrightarrow A \times \overrightarrow B $ is a vector ⊥ to both $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $

Now, \[\overrightarrow A \times \overrightarrow B = \left( {\widehat i - 2\widehat j + \widehat k} \right) \times \left( {3\widehat i + \widehat j - 2\widehat k} \right) = 3\widehat i + 5\widehat j + 7\widehat k\]

Now, \[\overrightarrow B = \frac{{\overrightarrow A \times \overrightarrow B }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow A \times \overrightarrow B } \right|}}\]

\[ = \frac{{3\widehat i + 5\widehat j + 7\widehat k}}{{\sqrt {{3^2} + {5^2} + {7^2}} }} = \frac{{3\widehat i + 5\widehat j + 7\widehat k}}{{\sqrt {83} }}\]

Hence, (b) is correct.

A moving particle of mass m, makes a head on elastic collision with a particle of mass 2m, which is initially at rest. The fraction of energy lost by colliding particle is :

\[{v_2} - {v_1} = u\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right)\]

On solving\[{v_1} = \frac{{ - u}}{3},\]

\[{k_f} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{m{u^2}}}{9},\,\,\,{k_i} = \frac{1}{2}m{u^2}\]

lost fraction\[\frac{{\Delta k}}{k} = \frac{8}{9}\]

\[ \Rightarrow I = 30\,\,A\]

Question: Given \[\vec R = \vec A + \vec B\] and R = A = B. The angle between \[\vec A\] and  \[\vec B\] is

Question: Energy E of a hydrogen atom with principal quantum number n is given by \[E = \frac{{ - 13.6}}{{{n^2}}}\]eV. The energy of a photon ejected when the electron jumps from n = 3 state to n = 2 state of hydrogen, is approximately
Solution:
Energy of a Hydrogen atom, $E = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{n^2}}}{\text{ eV}}$
Now, the energy of photon ejected when the electron jumps from $n = 3$to $n = 2$state of
Hydrogen, $\Delta E = {E_3} - {E_2} = - \frac{{13.6}}{9} - \left( { - \frac{{13.6}}{4}} \right)$
$\therefore \Delta E = - 1.51 + 3.4 = 1.89{\text{ eV}}$
$\therefore \Delta E \approx 1.9{\text{ eV}}$

Question:

Both Statement-1 and Statement-2 are true but Statement-2 is not the correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement-1 is true and the Statement-2 is false.
Statement-1 is false and the Statement-2 is true.
Solution:
\[{m_1}u = {m_2}v\,\,....\left( 1 \right)\]

\[eu = v\,.......\left( 2 \right)\]

using (1) and (2)

\[\frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1}}} = \frac{1}{e}\]

Question: A body of mass m is accelerated uniformly from rest to a speed v in a time T. The instantaneous power delivered to the body as a function of time, is given by
Solution:
Instantaneous power
\[P = Fv = mav\]
\[P = m\left( {\frac{v}{T}} \right)v\]
\[P = m\left( {\frac{v}{T}} \right)\left( {at} \right)\]
\[P = m\left( {\frac{v}{T}} \right)\left( {\frac{v}{T}} \right)t\]
\[P = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{{{T^2}}}t\]

Question: Under the influence of a uniform magnetic field, a charged particle moves with constant speed v in a circle of radius R. The time period of rotation of the particle
Options: depends on v and not on R
depends on R and not on v
is independent of both v and R
depends on both v and R
Solution:
$$qvB = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{R}$$
$$ \Rightarrow R = \frac{{mv}}{{Bq}}$$

$$\omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{{Bq}}{m}$$

$$T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega } = \frac{{2\pi m}}{{Bq}}$$

Question: How many significant figures will be there in the calculated solution of following problem: \[\frac{{\left( {29.2 - 20.2} \right) \times \left( {1.97 \times {{10}^5}} \right)}}{{1.39}}\]?
Solution:
Correct option is (D)

The result of addition and subtraction cannot have more digits to the right of the decimal point than either of the original numbers.

In case of division and multiplication, the result must be reported with no more significant figures as are there in the measurement with the few significant figures.

Therefore, 29.2 – 20.2 = 9.0.

Thus, the answer to this subtraction will be upto one decimal place.

Now \[\frac{{\left( {29.2 - 20.2} \right) \times \left( {1.97 \times {{10}^5}} \right)}}{{1.39}} = \frac{{\left( {9.0} \right) \times \left( {1.97 \times {{10}^5}} \right)}}{{1.39}}\]

Thus, the answer to this term will be upto 2 significant figures.

Hence, option D is correct.

Question: A solid sphere of mass M rolls without slipping on an inclined plane of inclination θ. The linear acceleration of the sphere is:
Solution:
For linear motion

$F = Ma$

$ \Rightarrow Mg\sin \theta - f = Ma{\text{ }}...(i)$

For rotational Motion

$\tau = I\alpha $

$ \Rightarrow fR = \frac{2}{5}M{R^2}\left( {\frac{a}{R}} \right){\text{ }}\left\{ {\;\because \alpha \; = \frac{a}{R}} \right\}$

$ \Rightarrow f = \frac{2}{5}Ma$

${\text{From eq}}{\text{.(i)}}$

$Mg\sin \theta = \frac{2}{5}Ma + Ma$

$ \Rightarrow Mg\sin \theta = \frac{7}{5}Ma$

$\therefore a = \frac{5}{7}g\sin \theta $

Question:
The mass of the ship is 2 × 107 kg. On applying a force of 25 × 105 N, it is displaced through 25 m. After this displacement, the ship acquires a speed of:
(Hint: a = F/m)
Solution:
Mass of ship, m = \[2 \times {10^7}kg\]
Force applied on the ship, \[F = 25 \times {10^5}N\]

Displacement of ship after applying force,

s = 25m

Initial speed of ship, u =0 m/sec

Acceleration of ship,\[a = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{{25 \times {{10}^5}}}{{2 \times {{10}^7}}} = \frac{{25}}{2} \times {10^{ - 2}}\]

We know that.

\[ \Rightarrow {v^2} = {u^2} + 2as\]

\[ \Rightarrow {v^2} = 0 + 2 \times \frac{{25}}{2} \times {10^{ - 2}} \times 25\]

\[ \Rightarrow v = 2.5{\text{ }}m/s\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Kinematic Equations for Uniformly Accelerated Motion

Question: In a gravity free space, a man of mass M standing at a height h above the floor, throws a ball of mass m straight down with a speed u. When the ball reaches the floor, the distance of the man above the floor will be :
Solution:
Time taken by ball to reach floor = \[\frac{h}{u}\]

\[{P_i} = {P_f}\]

\[O = mu - Mv\]

Speed of man

\[v = \frac{{mu}}{M}\]

\[h + vt = h + \frac{{mu}}{M} \times \frac{h}{u}\]

\[\therefore {\text{ Height of man above ground = }}h + \frac{{mh}}{M}\]

\[H = h\left( {1 + \frac{m}{M}} \right)\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

Question: The inside and outside temperature of a refrigerator are 273K and 303K respectively. Assuming that refrigerator cycle is reversible, for every joule of work done the heat delivered to the surrounding will be
Solution:
Coefficient of performance,
$\beta = \frac{{{Q_2}}}{W} = \frac{{{T_L}}}{{{T_H} - {T_L}}}$
${Q_2} = \frac{{273 \times 1}}{{303 - 273}} = \frac{{273}}{{30}} = 9J$
Heat delivered to the surroundings,
${Q_1} = {Q_2} + W$
$ = 9 + 1 = 10J$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Kinetic Theory of an Ideal Gas

Question: A metal ball of density 7800 kg/m3 is suspected to have a large number of inner cavities. It weighs 9.8 kg when weighed directly on a balance and 1.5 kg less when immersed in water. The fraction by volume of the cavities (Volume of cavity/Volume of Ball) in the metal ball is approximately:
Solution:
Method-1
$${\text{Volume of metal = }}\frac{{9.8}}{{7800}} = 1.25 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{ }}{m^3}{\text{ }}...{\text{(1)}}$$

Volume of whole ball including cavities = weight of water displaced/Density of water

Density of water $$ = 1000{\text{ }}kg/{m^3}$$

volume of whole ball $$ = 1.5{\text{ }} \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{ }}{m^3}$$

Volume of cavities = volume of ball – volume of metal

$$ = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}} - 1.25 \times {10^{ - 3}} = 0.25 \times {10^{ - 3}}{m^3}$$

$${\text{Fraction of cavity = }}\frac{{0.25 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{1.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = 0.16 = 16\% $$

Question: A stone of mass 0.1 kg tied to one end of a string 1.0 m long is revolved in a horizontal circle at the rate of $\frac{{10}}{\pi }$ revolution per second. Calculate the tension of the string ?
Solution:
$T = m{\omega ^2}1$
$ = 0.1 \times {\left( {\frac{{10}}{\pi } \times 2\pi } \right)^2} \times 1$
$ = 0.1 \times 400\,N = 40\,N$
Hence [b]

Question: A charge Q is placed at each of the opposite corners of a square. A charge q is placed at each of the other two corners. If the net electrical force on q is zero, then the q/Q equals
Solution:
Let, the side of square be$$x$$, and its diagonal be $x\sqrt 2 $.
Now, the horizontal force = vertical force
${F_1} = {F_2} = \frac{{kQq}}{{{x^2}}}$, and ${F_3} = \frac{{k{q^2}}}{{2{x^2}}}$
Now, the resultant force $F = \sqrt {F_1^2 + F_2^2} = {F_1}\sqrt 2 $ where,${F_1}$and $${F_2}$$ are perpendicular to each other.
Now equating the resultant force and ${F_3}$;
$\frac{{k{q^2}}}{{2{x^2}}} = {F_1}\sqrt 2 $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{ - k{q^2}}}{{2{x^2}}} = \frac{{kQq\sqrt 2 }}{{{x^2}}}$
$\therefore q = - 2\sqrt 2 Q$

Question: A machine gun fires 360 bullets per minute, with a velocity of 600m/s. If the power of the gun is 5.4kW, the mass of each bullet is
Solution:
\[N = 360\,,t = 60\sec \]
\[v = 600\,m/s\]
\[P = 5400\,watt\]
\[P = \frac{{\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} \times N}}{t}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 5400 = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{m \times {{\left( {600} \right)}^2} \times 360}}{{60}}\]
\[\therefore \,\,m = \frac{1}{{200}}kg = 5gm\]

Question: The angular momentum of a system of particles is not conserved :
Options: when a net external force acts upon the system
when a net external torque is acting upon the system
when a net external impulse is acting upon the system
Solution:
\[\frac{{dL}}{{dt}} = {\tau _{ext}} \ne 0\]
So, when a net external torque is acting upon the system, angular momentum will not be constant.

Question: A transformer has 1500 turns in the primary coil and 1125 turns in the secondary coil. If the voltage in the primary coil is 200 V, then the voltage in the secondary coil is
A small body of mass m slides without friction from the top of a hemispherical cup. If it leaves the surface of the cup at a vertical distance 'h' below the highest point, then-
Solution:
Using conservation of Energy at points A and B We get,

\[ \Rightarrow mgh + 0 = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow mgh = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{2}\]

\[\therefore {v^2} = 2gh\,\,\,\therefore v = \sqrt {2gh} \]
and from FBD at point B, \[N + \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = mg\cos \theta \]
When the object leaves the contact N = 0
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = mg\cos \theta \]
\[\therefore \frac{m}{r} \cdot 2gh = mg\left( {\frac{{r - h}}{r}} \right)\]
\[\therefore 2h = r - h \Rightarrow 3h = r\]
\[\therefore h = \frac{r}{3}\]

Question:
Assertion : In a plane to plane projectile motion, the angle between instantaneous velocity vector and acceleration vector can be anything between 0 to π (excluding the limiting case).
Reason : In plane to plane projectile motion, the acceleration vector is always pointing vertical downwards. (Neglect air friction).
Options: Assertion is True, Reason is True; Reason is a correct explanation for Assertion
Assertion is True, Reason is True; Reason is NOT a correct explanation for Assertion
Assertion is True, Reason is False
Both Assertion and Reason are False
Solution: Before attaining maximum height, angle is obtuse. At maximum height, the angle is perpendicular. Beyond this point, the angle is acute. At no point does this angle go beyond thin.

Question:
If work done in increasing the size of a soap film from 10 cm × 10 cm to 10 cm × 11 cm is
2 × 10–4 J. Then surface tension is
Solution:
Work done = Change in potential energy
$$W = \Delta U = T \times \Delta A$$

Surface tension acts on both faces. So,

$$\Delta {\text{A = 2}}\left[ {\left( {0.1 \times 0.11} \right) - \left( {0.1 \times 0.1} \right)} \right] = 2 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{ }}{m^2}$$

Now,

$$W = \Delta U = T \times \Delta {\text{A}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}{\text{ J}}$$

$$T = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = 0.1{\text{ }}N/m$$

Question: A transistor has α = 0.95. The change in emitter current is 100 milliampere, then the change in the collector current is :
Solution:
\[\alpha = \frac{{\Delta {I_c}}}{{\Delta {I_e}}}\]
\[\Delta {I_c} = \alpha \times \Delta {I_e} = 0.95 \times 100 = 95\,mA\]

Question: The time taken by an alternating current of 50 Hz in reaching from zero to its maximum value will be
Solution:
An alternating current is one whose magnitude changes continuously with time between zero and a maximum value and whose direction reverses periodically. The relation between frequency ( f ) and time (T ) is
\[T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{{50}} = 0.02\,s\]
It is clear from the figure, time taken to reach the maximum value is
\[\frac{T}{4} = \frac{{0.02}}{4} = 0.005\,s\]

Question: The angle between the z–axis and the vector  \[\hat i + \hat j + \sqrt 2 \,\hat k\] is
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow A = \widehat i + \widehat j + \sqrt 2 \widehat k\]

Let, \[\overrightarrow B = \widehat k,\] If angle between \[\overrightarrow A \] and z axis is θ then

\[\overrightarrow A \cdot \overrightarrow B = AB\cos \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow \sqrt 2 = \left( {\sqrt {1 + 1 + 2} } \right)\left( 1 \right)\cos \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \theta = 45^\circ \]

Question: A ball is dropped from the top of a tower in a high-speed wind. The wind exerts a steady force on the ball. The path followed by the ball will be

$x = \frac{1}{2}a{t^2};\,\,y = \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}$

are consonants

Question: A solenoid of length 50 cm and a radius of cross-section 1cm has 1000 turns of wire wound over it. If the current carried is 5 A, the magnetic field on its axis, near the centre of the solenoid is approximately (permeability of free space, $${\mu _0} = 4\pi  \times {10^{ - 7}}T - m{A^{ - 1}})$$
Solution:
The magnetic field is given by
\[B = {\mu _0}ni\]

Where, \[{\mu _0} = 4\pi  \times {10^{ - 7}}T - m{A^{ - 1}}\]

\[n = \frac{{1000}}{{50 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}},i = 5\,A\]

\[ \Rightarrow B = 4\pi  \times {10^{ - 7}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{50 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}} \times 5\]

\[\therefore B = 1.26 \times {10^{ - 2}}T\]

Question: A particle covers 150 m in 8th second starting from rest, its acceleration is
Solution:
Given, t = 8th second, u = 0 distance traveled in 8th second S1 =150m.
From equation of motion for particular time,
${S_1} = ut + \frac{1}{2}a\left( {2t - 1} \right)$
$150 = 0 + \frac{1}{2}a\left( {2 \times 8 - 1} \right) = \frac{{15a}}{2}$
Or
$a = \frac{{300}}{{15}} = 20m/{s^2}$

Question:
Statement 1 : A rigid disc rolls without slipping on a fixed rough horizontal surface with uniform angular velocity. Then the acceleration of lowest point on the disc is zero.
Statement 2 : For a rigid disc rolling without slipping on a fixed rough horizontal surface, the velocity of the lowest point on the disc is always zero.
Options: Statement–1 is True, Statement–2 is True; Statement–2 is a correct explanation for Statement–1.
Statement–1 is True, Statement–2 is True; Statement–2 is NOT a correct explanation for Statement–1.
Statement–1 is True, Statement–2 is False.
Statement–1 is False, Statement–2 is True.
Solution:
Disc is rolling on a horizontal rough surface without slipping, then lowest point of the disc always remains in contact with the horizontal surface. Thus, the tangential velocity at this point will be zero.
But if we observe this lowest point from origin of the disc, then it is rotating with an angular velocity around the circle. Thus, a centripetal force is acting on it, which means that its rotational acceleration is not zero. Whereas its tangential acceleration (Translational motion) will be zero, because lowest point always remains in contact with the surface.

Question:
Assertion. A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C is charged to a potential difference V. The battery is disconnected and then dielectric slab of dielectric constant K is inserted between it. Its potential energy decreases.
Reason. Energy stored is given by q2/2C.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Energy stored is given by q2/2C. Since battery is disconnected so charge remains constant. Since capacity with slab is K times that of air capacitance, so due to slab, capacity increases K times; where K is dielectric constant of the slab. So its potential energy decreases 1/K times.

Question: n identical capacitors each of capacitance C when connected in parallel give the effective capacitance 90<sub> </sub>μ F and when connected in series give 2.5<sub> </sub>μ F. Then, the values of n and C respectively, are
Solution:
Case I When capacitors are connected in parallel,
$$\eqalign{
  & {C_1} + {C_2} + {C_3} + ..... + {C_n} = 90\mu F \cr
  & \Rightarrow nC = 90\,\mu F\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \cr} $$
Case II When capacitors are connected in series,
$$\frac{1}{{{C_n}}} = \frac{1}{{{C_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_2}}} + ..... + \frac{1}{{{C_n}}}$$
$$ \Rightarrow \frac{C}{n} = 2.5 \Rightarrow C = 2.5n$$…(ii)
Putting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get
$$2.5{n^2} = 90$$
$$ \Rightarrow {n^2} = \frac{{90}}{{2.5}} = \frac{{900}}{{25}} = 36$$
$$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,n = 6$$
Now, putting the value of n in Eq. (ii),
We get
$$C = 2.5 \times 6 = 15 = 15\mu F$$

Question: A body of mass m strikes a stationary body of mass M and undergoes an elastic collision. After collision, m has a speed one-third its initial speed. The ratio M/m is
Solution:
\[\therefore \]Collision is perfectly elastic. Momentum conservation can be shown as,

\[mu = \frac{{mu}}{3} + Mv\]

\[Mv = \frac{2}{3}mu\]

\[ \Rightarrow v = \frac{2}{3}\left( {\frac{m}{M}} \right)u\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)\]

e\[ \times \]velocity of approach = velocity of separation

\[u = v - \frac{u}{3},\,\,v = \frac{{4u}}{3}\,......\left( 2 \right)\]

Using (1) & (2)

\[\frac{m}{M} = 2,\,\]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{M}{m} = \frac{1}{2}\]

Question: m<sub>p</sub> and m<sub>n</sub> are masses of proton and neutron respectively. An element of mass m has Z protons and N neutrons, then
Options: m may be greater than, less than or equal to Zm<sub>p</sub> + Nm<sub>n</sub>, depending on nature of element
Solution:
Mass of proton and neutrons are ${m_p}$ and ${m_n}$,
respectively. m is the mass of an element. Z and N are protons and neutrons of an element.
Now, the atomic mass of any stable nuclei is always less than the sum of the masses of the constituent particles.
Therefore, $m < \left[ {N{m_n} + Z{m_p}} \right]$

Question: If $\frac{{\sqrt 5  + x}}{{\sqrt 5  - x}} = 5.$ Find the value of x.
Solution:
$\frac{{\sqrt 5 + x}}{{\sqrt 5 - x}} = 5$

Applying Componendo and Dividendo

$\frac{{\sqrt 5 + x + \left( {\sqrt 5 - x} \right)}}{{\left( {\sqrt 5 + x} \right) - \sqrt 5 - x}} = \frac{{5 + 1}}{{5 + 1}}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{\sqrt 5 }}{x} = \frac{6}{4}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,x = \frac{{2\sqrt 5 }}{3}$

Question:
The emitter of transistor is doped the heaviest because it:
(I) acts as a supplier of charges carriers.
(II) Dissipates maximum power.
(III) has a large resistance.
(IV) has small resistance.
Which of the above statements are correct?
Solution: The reason the emitter is the most heavily doped region is because it serves to inject a large amount of charge carriers into the base, which then travels into the collector, so that switching, or amplification can occur. Doping is the process of adding impurities to a semiconductor. So by adding more impurity, more charge carriers are created which further increases the conductance and decrease resistance.

Question: A particle of mass 15 kg an initial velocity \[{\vec v_i} = \hat i - 2\,\hat j\]  m/s. It collides with another body and the impact interval is 0.1 s, resulting in a velocity \[{\vec v_f} = 6\hat i + 4\,\hat j + 5\,\hat k\]m/s after impact. The average force of impact on the particle is :
Solution:
\[{M_1} = 15kg,\,\,{\vec v_1} = \hat i - 2j\]
\[{\vec v_2} = 6\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k\]

\[\therefore \Delta \vec p = {M_1}\Delta \vec V\]

\[\Delta \vec p = {M_1}\left( {{{\vec V}_2} - {{\vec V}_1}} \right) = 15\left( {5\hat i + 6\hat j + 5\hat k} \right)\]

\[\Delta t = 0.1\]

\[\frac{{\Delta \vec p}}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{{15\left( {5\hat i + 6\hat j + 5\hat k} \right)}}{{0.1}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \vec F = 150\left( {5\hat i + 6\hat j + 5\hat k} \right)\]

Question: The energy of a photon of light is 3 eV. Then the wavelength of photon is nearly
Solution:
The energy of a photon of light,
$E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda } = 3{\text{ eV}} = 3 \times 1.602 \times {10^{ - 19}}{\text{ V}}$
Now, the wavelength of the photon, $\lambda = \frac{{hc}}{E}$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{3 \times 1.602 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}$
$\therefore \lambda = 4.138 \times {10^{ - 7}}{\text{ m}} = 413.8{\text{ nm}}$

Question: The ratio of the squares of radii of gyration of a circular disc and a circular ring of the same radius about a tangential axis in the plane of bodies is:
Solution:
Moment of inertia of a ring about axis in the plane of ring and passing through the com,
\[{\left( {{I_{cm}}} \right)_R} = \frac{{M{R^2}}}{2}\]

Moment of inertia of a disk about axis in the plane of disk and passing through the com,

\[{\left( {{I_{cm}}} \right)_D} = \frac{{M{R^2}}}{4}\]

Moment of inertia about tangential axis

\[{I_t} = {I_{cm}} + M{R^2}\]

\[{\left( {{I_R}} \right)_t} = \frac{{M{R^2}}}{2} + M{R^2} = \frac{3}{2}M{R^2} = MK_1^2\]

\[{\left( {{I_D}} \right)_t} = \frac{{M'{R^2}}}{4} + M'{R^2} = \frac{5}{4}M'{R^2} = M'K_2^2\]

\[ \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{{K_2}}}{{{K_1}}}} \right)^2} = \frac{5}{4} \times \frac{2}{3} = \frac{5}{6}\]

Question:
A long frictionless horizontal rod is set into rotation about a vertical axis passing through its centre. Two beads placed on the rod on either side of the axis, are released from rest.
The angular speed of the rod :
Options: increases with time due to work done by the beads
increases with time due to work done by centrifugal force
Solution: Due to centrifugal force, beads will move towards the ends of rods. So, moment of inertia of the system will increase and as angular momentum is conserved, angular speed of the rod will decrease.

\[ = \sqrt {g\ell } \times 2\sqrt {\frac{\ell }{g}} = 2\ell \]
\[x = 2\ell \]

Question: If a capillary tube is dipped into liquid and the levels of the liquid inside and outside are same, then the angle of contact is :
Solution:
The height h through which a liquid will rise in a capillary tube of inner radius r is given by
$$h = \frac{{2S\cos \theta }}{{r\rho g}}$$
where S is surface tension, $$\rho $$is density of the liquid and $$\theta $$ is the angle of contact. If the liquid level inside and outside of the tube are same. We have $$\cos \theta = 0$$. Since other variables cannot be zero
$$\cos \theta  = 0$$
$$\theta  = 90^\circ $$
so rise is more for the tube of smaller diameter.

Question: A particle moves in circular path with uniform speed v. The change in its velocity on rotating through $60^\circ $ is -
Solution:
Initial velocity, ${v_1} = v\hat j$
Final velocity,
${v_2} = v\cos 150\,\hat i$
$v\sin 150\,\hat j$
$\begin{gathered}
   = v\cos \left( {180^\circ - 30^\circ } \right)\hat i + v\sin \left( {180^\circ - 30^\circ } \right)\hat j \\
   = - v\cos 30^\circ \,\hat i + v\sin 30^\circ \hat j \\
\end{gathered} $
$ = - \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}v\,\hat i + \frac{v}{2}\hat j$
$\overrightarrow {\Delta v} = \overrightarrow {{v_2}} - \overrightarrow {{v_1}} $
$ = \frac{{ - \sqrt 3 }}{2}v\,\hat i + \frac{v}{2}\hat j - \hat j$
$ = \frac{{ - \sqrt 3 }}{2}v\hat i - \frac{v}{2}\hat j$
$\therefore \,\,\left| {\vec v} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{{ - \sqrt 3 }}{2}v} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{ - v}}{2}} \right)}^2}} = \sqrt {\frac{3}{4}{v^2} + \frac{{{v^2}}}{4}} $
$ = v$
Hence [c]

Question: The moment of inertia of a solid cylinder of mass M, radius R and Length L about its axis
Solution:
Consider the cylinder made up of stack of discs of radius R one over other.
Let mass of an element (disc) is dM.

\[\therefore d{I_{Disc}} = \frac{1}{2}dM{R^2}\]

\[I = \int {d{I_{Disc}} = \int {\frac{1}{2}dM{R^2} = \frac{{M{R^2}}}{2}} } \]

Question: A block of mass 60 kg just slides over a horizontal distance of 0.9 m. if the coefficient of friction between their surfaces is 0.15 then work done against friction will be
Solution:
Mass of the body, m = 60 kg
Displacement, s = 0.9 m
Coefficient of friction, μ = 0.15
Work done against friction,
W = F.s =μRs = μmgs
Where R = Normal force
= 0.15 × 60 × 9.8 × 0.9 = 79.4 J

Question:
Assertion: The electric flux emanating out and entering a closed surface are $8\,\, \times {10^3}\,\,V\,\,m$ and $2\,\, \times {10^3}\,\,V\,\,m,$ respectively. The charge enclosed by the surface is $0.053\,\,\mu C.$
Reason: According to Gauss’s theorem in electrostatics, net flux enclosed$\phi = q/{\varepsilon _o}.$
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution:
According to Gauss’s theorem in electrostatics, net flux enclosed$\phi = q/{\varepsilon _o}.$ We have
$$q = {\varepsilon _o}\phi = 8.85 \times {10^{ - 12}}\left( {8 \times {{10}^3} - 2 \times {{10}^3}} \right) = 53.10 \times {10^{ - 9}}C = 0.053\mu C.$$

Question: A comb rubbed through one’s dry hair attracts small bits of paper. This is because
Options: the atoms in the paper get polarized by the charged comb
Solution: When the comb passes through dry hair it gets electrically charged by friction. Hence, attracts small bits of paper. This is because paper gets polarized in the presence of charged comb resulting in a net force of attraction.

Question: For the path PQR in a conservative force field (fig.), the amount of work done in carrying a body from P to Q & from Q to R are 5 J & 2 J respectively. The work done in carrying the body from P to R will be –
In an L-C-R series AC circuit, if ω<sub>0</sub> is the resonant angular frequency, then the quality factor (Q-factor) is given by
Solution:
Quality factor, \[Q = \frac{{{X_L}}}{R} = \frac{{{\omega _0}L}}{R}\] (XL at resonance)
At resonance, \[{\omega _0} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {LC} }}\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,Q = \frac{L}{R} \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt {LC} }} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt {\frac{L}{C}} \]

Question: An alternating current of rms value 10 A is passed through a 12 Ω resistor. The maximum potential difference across the resistor is
Solution:
Given, Irms = 10 A
And R = 12 Ω
\[\therefore \,{E_{rms}} = {I_{rms}} \times R = 10 \times 12 = 120\,V\]
Now, maximum potential difference
\[{E_0} = {E_{rms}} \times \sqrt 2 = 120 \times \sqrt 2 = 169.68\,V\]

Question:
Assertion: If different shapes of current loops having same area are placed in uniform magnetic field such that there planes are parallel to each other, then magnetic torque is same on all loops.
Reason: τ is maximum when the plane of the loop is parallel to the field.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Consider a rectangular current carrying loop having N turns and area A, placed in a uniform magnetic field $$\vec B$$, in such a way that the normal (n) to the coil makes an angle θ with the direction of $$\vec B$$The coil experiences a torque given by $$\tau \,\, = \,\,NBIA\,\,\sin \theta $$. Vectorially, $$\vec \tau \,\, = \,\,\bar M\,\, \times \,\,\bar B\,;$$where M is magnetic moment. So$$\tau $$is maximum when θ = 90o, that is, the plane of the coil is parallel to the field. Therefore, torque $${\tau _{\max }}\,\, = \,\,NBIA$$.

Question: An inductor L, a capacitor of 20 µFand a resistor of 10 Ω are connected in series with an AC source of frequency 50 Hz. If the current is in phase with the voltage, then the inductance of the inductor is
Solution:
In an L-C-R circuit, the current and the voltage are in phase (φ = 0), when
\[\tan \phi = \frac{{\omega L - \frac{1}{{\omega C}}}}{R} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\omega L = \frac{1}{{\omega C}} \Rightarrow L = \frac{1}{{{\omega ^2}C}}\]
Here, ω = 2πf
= 2 × 3.14 × 50 s-1 = 314 s-1
And C = 20 μF = 20 × 10-6 F
\[\therefore \,L = \frac{1}{{{{\left( {314\,{s^{ - 1}}} \right)}^2} \times \left( {20 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}F} \right)}} = 0.51\,H\]

Question: Which one of the following is constant in a projectile motion?

Question: A balloon of weight w is falling vertically downward with a constant acceleration a
Solution:
FR = air resistance
$\because W = mg \Rightarrow m = \frac{W}{g}$
From F.B.D. of balloon
$\begin{gathered}
  W - {F_R} = ma \\
   \Rightarrow W - {F_R} = \frac{W}{g}a \\
   \Rightarrow {F_R} = W\left( {1 - \frac{a}{g}} \right) \\
\end{gathered} $
It is the magnitude of air resistance.

a large number of turns in the coil

Question:
Assertion: A charged particle at rest experiences no electromagnetic force.
Reason: The electric and magnetic field must be zero.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Electric force acts on moving charge as well as charge at rest; but magnetic force acts only on moving charge. So in presence of magnetic field no magnetic force acts on charge at rest.

Question: The distance between the plates of a parallel plate condenser is 8mm and P.D. 120 volts. If a 6mm thick slab of dielectric constant 6 is introduced between its plates, then
Options: The charge on the condenser will be doubled
The charge on the condenser will be reduced to half
The P.D. across the condenser will be $320\;volts$
The P.D. across the condenser will be $45\;volts$
Solution:
If nothing is said, it is considered that battery is disconnected. Hence charge remain the same
Also ${V_{air}} = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}} \times d$and ${V_{medium}} = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}(d - t + \frac{t}{k})$
⇒ $\frac{{{V_m}}}{{{V_a}}} = \frac{{(d - t + \frac{t}{k})}}{d}$ ⇒ $\frac{{{V_m}}}{{120}} = \frac{{(8 - 6 + \frac{6}{6})}}{8}$ ⇒ Vm = 45V

Question: The half life of the isotope 11Na23<sub> </sub>is 15 hrs. How much time does it take for ${\left( {\frac{7}{8}} \right)^{th}}$ of a sample of this isotope to decay
Solution:
For \[{\left( {\frac{7}{8}} \right)^{th}}\]of sample to decay, \[{\left( {\frac{1}{8}} \right)^{th}}\] should remain in undecayed
So, \[\frac{N}{{{N_0}}} = \frac{1}{8} = {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^n}\]
\[n = 3 \Rightarrow t = 3 \times 15 = 45\,hrs\]

Question: Two cylinders A and B fitted with pistons contain equal amounts of an ideal diatomic gas at 300 K. The piston of A is free to move, while that of B held fixed. The same amount of heat is given to the gas in each cylinder. Determine the greatest integral value of the ratio of change in temperature of B to the change in temperature of A.
Solution:
Process in cylinder A is isobaric and process in B is isochoric
${Q_A} = {Q_B}$
$n{C_P}d{T_A} = n{C_v}d{T_b}$
$\frac{{d{T_B}}}{{d{T_A}}} = \frac{{{C_P}}}{{{C_V}}} = \gamma = 1.4$
$\left[ {\frac{{d{T_B}}}{{d{T_A}}}} \right] = \left[ {1.4} \right] = 1$

Question: A pure semiconductor behaves as a good conductor at :

It becomes slightly conducting at Room temperature due to thermal agitation process as a result of which a few electrons in the valence band acquire energy greater than the forbidden energy gap and move to conduction band. At high temperature, semiconductor behaves as a good conductor

Question: A force of \[\left( {10\hat i - 3\hat j + 6\hat k} \right)N\] acts on a body of mass 100 g and displaces it from \[\left( {6\hat i + 5\hat j - 3\hat k} \right)m\] to \[\left( {10\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k} \right)m.\] The work done is
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow F = 10\widehat i - 3\widehat j + 6\hat k\,;\,\,{\overrightarrow r _i} = 6\widehat i + 5\widehat j - 3\hat k\,;\]

\[{\overrightarrow r _j} = 10\widehat i - 2\widehat j + 7\hat k\]

Displacement, \[\Delta \overrightarrow S = {\overrightarrow r _j} - {\overrightarrow r _i} = 4\widehat i - 7\widehat j + 10\widehat k\]

Work done, \[W = \int {\overrightarrow F \cdot \overrightarrow {ds} = \overrightarrow F \cdot \overrightarrow {\Delta s} = 40 + 21 + 60 = 121J} \]

Question: What is the voltage gain in a common-emitter amplifier, where input resistance is \[3\,\,\Omega \] and load resistance \[24\,\,\Omega \]? (Take \[\beta = 0.6\])
Solution: Given that, the input resistance is, \[R = \,3\,\,\Omega \], the load resistance is, \[{R_L} = 24\,\,\Omega \], and the transfer ratio is, \[\beta  = 0.6\]. Therefore, the voltage gain in a common emitter amplifier is, \[V = \beta  \times \frac{{{R_L}}}{R} = 0.6 \times \frac{{24}}{3} = 4.8\,\,V\].

Question: A charge of 8 mC is located at the origin. Calculate the work done in taking a small charge of –2 × 10<sup>-9</sup> C from a point P (0, 0, 3 cm) to a point Q (0, 4 cm, 0), via a point R (0, 6 cm, 9 cm).
Solution:
Charge located at the origin, q = 8 mC = 8 ×10-3 C
A small charge is moved from a point P to point R to point Q, q1 = - 2 × 10-9C
The figure given below represents all points.

Potential at point P, $${V_1} = \frac{q}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _o}{d_1}}}$$
Where d1 = 3cm = 0.03 m
Potential at point Q, $${V_2} = \frac{q}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _o}{d_2}}}$$
Where d2 = 4cm = 0.04 m
Work done by the electrostatic force does not depend on the path.
W = q1 [V2 – V1]
$$\eqalign{
  & \Rightarrow W = {q_1}\left[ {\frac{q}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _o}{d_2}}} - \frac{q}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _o}{d_1}}}} \right] \cr
  & \Rightarrow W = \frac{{{q_1}q}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _o}}}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{d_2}}} - \frac{1}{{{d_1}}}} \right] \cr} $$
Value of $$\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _o}}} = 9 \times {10^9}N{C^{ - 2}}{m^{ - 2}}$$
$$ \Rightarrow W = 9 \times {10^9} \times 8 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times ( - 2 \times {10^{ - 9}})\left[ {\frac{1}{{0.04}} - \frac{1}{{0.03}}} \right]$$
⇒ W = 1.2 J
Clearly, work done during the process is 1.27 J.

Question: In a series of successive measurements in an experiment, the readings of the period of oscillation of a simple pendulum were found to be 2.63 s, 2.56 s, 2.42 s, 2.71 s and 2.80 s. Find the time period of the simple pendulum.
Solution:
True or mean value, Tm

\[{a_m} = \frac{{{a_1} + {a_2} + {a_3} + {a_4} + .... + {a_n}}}{n}\]

\[{T_m} = \frac{{{T_1} + {T_2} + {T_3} + {T_4} + {T_5}}}{5}\]

\[ = \frac{{2.63 + 2.56 + 2.42 + 2.71 + 2.80}}{5}\]

\[{T_m} = 2.62\;s\]

Time periodTime
T12.63 s
T22.56 s
T32.42 s
T42.71 s
T52.80 s

Absolute errors in each measurement,

\[\Delta {T_n} = \left| {{T_n} - {T_m}} \right|\]

\[\Delta {T_1} = \left| {{T_1} - {T_m}} \right| = \left| {2.63 - 2.62} \right| = 0.01\;s\]

\[\Delta {T_2} = \left| {{T_2} - {T_m}} \right| = \left| {2.56 - 2.62} \right| = 0.06\;s\]

\[\Delta {T_3} = \left| {{T_3} - {T_m}} \right| = \left| {2.42 - 2.62} \right| = 0.20\;s\]

\[\Delta {T_4} = \left| {{T_4} - {T_m}} \right| = \left| {2.71 - 2.62} \right| = 0.09\;s\]

\[\Delta {T_5} = \left| {{T_5} - {T_m}} \right| = \left| {2.80 - 2.62} \right| = 0.18\;s\]

Mean absolute error of ΔTm

\[\overline {\Delta {T_m}} = \frac{{\left| {\Delta {T_1}} \right| + \left| {\Delta {T_2}} \right| + \left| {\Delta {T_3}} \right| + \left| {\Delta {T_4}} \right| + \left| {\Delta {T_5}} \right|}}{5}\]

\[\overline {\Delta {T_m}} = \frac{{0.01 + 0.06 + 0.20 + 0.09 + 0.18}}{5}\]

\[ = \frac{{0.54}}{5} = 0.108\;s\]

\[\overline {\Delta {T_m}} = 0.11\;s\]

Percentage error of ΔTm

\[{\text{Percentage}}\,\,{\text{error}} = \frac{{\overline {\Delta {T_m}} }}{{{T_m}}} \times 100 = \frac{{0.11}}{{2.62}} \times 100\]

\[T = 0.042 \times 100 = 4\% \]

Time period of simple pendulum

\[T = 2.62 \pm 4\% \]

Thus, option (A) is the correct answer.

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Accuracy, Precision of Instruments and Errors in Measurement

Question: The time period of a simple pendulum in a stationary train is T. The time period of a mass attached to a spring is also T. The train accelerates at the rate 5m/s<sup>2</sup>. If the new time periods of the pendulum and spring by T<sub>p</sub> and T<sub>s</sub>  respectively, then
Solution:
$${T_P} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{\sqrt {{g^2} + {a^2}} }}} $$
$${T_S} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{m}{k}} $$

Time Period of pendulum is inversely proportional to the root of gravity + acceleration. As the geff increased by acceleration of the train, Time period of the pendulum decreases. However, Time Period of spring is independent of gravity or acceleration of the train. Hence $${T_P} < {T_S}$$.

Question: An arc of radius r carries charge. The linear density of charge is $$\lambda $$ and the arc subtends an angle π/3 at the centre. What is electric potential at the centre
Solution:
Length of the arc = πr/3

Charge of the arc q = $$\lambda $$πr/3

$$V = \frac{{kq}}{r} = \frac{{K\pi r\lambda }}{{3r}}$$

$$V = K\lambda \frac{\pi }{3}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,V = \frac{1}{{4\pi { \in _0}}}.\frac{{\lambda \pi }}{3}$$

$$V = \frac{\lambda }{{12{ \in _0}}}$$

Solution: Relative error in measurement of length is minimum, so this measurement is most accurate.

Question: If 1gm cms−1 = x Ns, then number x is equivalent to
Solution:
\[gm - cm\;{s^{ - 1}} = {10^{ - 3}}kg \times {10^{ - 2}}m \times {s^{ - 1}}\]

\[ = {10^{ - 5}}kg \times m \times {s^{ - 1}} = {10^{ - 5}}Ns\]

Question: A parallel plate capacitor with air in between the plates has capacitance of 9μ F. The separation between the plates is d. The space between the plates is now filled with two dielectrics. One of the dielectric has dielectric constant k<sub>1</sub> = 3 with thickness d/3 and other one has dielectric constant k<sub>2</sub> = 6 and thickness 2d/3. The new capacitance will be
Solution:
$${C_{eq}} = \frac{{{C_1}{C_2}}}{{{C_1} + {C_2}}}$$
$${C_1} = {k_1}\frac{{{\varepsilon _A}A}}{{\left( {\frac{d}{3}} \right)}} = \frac{{3 \times 3{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d} = \frac{{9{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}$$
$${C_2} = {k_2}\frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{\left( {2\frac{d}{3}} \right)}} = \frac{{6 \times 3{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{2d}} = \frac{{9{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}$$
$${C_{eq}} = \frac{{81{{\left( {\frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}} \right)}^2}}}{{18\left( {\frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}} \right)}} = \frac{{81}}{{18}} \times 9 = 40.5\mu C$$

Question: A particle moves in a force field given by \[\vec F = \hat rF\left( r \right)\], where \[\hat r\] is a unit vector along the position vector,  \[\vec r,\] then which is true?
Options: The torque acting on the particle is not zero
The torque acting on the particle produces an angular acceleration in it
The angular momentum of the particle is conserved
The angular momentum of the particle increases
Solution:
Torque, \[\overrightarrow \tau = \overrightarrow r \times \overrightarrow F = 0\]\[\left( {\because \overrightarrow r \& \overrightarrow F {\text{ are parallel}}} \right)\]
Since \[\tau = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{{dL}}{{dt}} = 0 \Rightarrow L\, = {\text{constant}}{\text{.}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \]Angular momentum is conserved.

Question: The half life of radium is about 1600 years. Of 100 g of radium existing now, 25 g will remain unchanged after
Solution:
$N = {N_0} \times {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^n}$
$ \Rightarrow 25 = 100 \times \left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right) \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^n} = \frac{1}{4}$
$ \Rightarrow $ No. of half lives = n = 2
So, t = 2 × 1600 = 3200 yrs

Question:
Assertion: It is observed that if two glass rods rubbed with silk cloth are brought close to each other, they repel.
Reason: Electrification causes repulsion of same polarity.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: When a glass rod is rubbed with silk, the rod becomes positively charged while the silk becomes negatively charged. So both glass rods become positively charged and repel with each other.

Question: The motion of a particle executing SHM in one dimension is described by $$x =  - 0.3\sin \left( {t + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)$$, where x is in metre and t in second. The frequency of oscillation in Hz is
Solution:
We have, $$x = - 0.3\sin \left( {t + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)$$
Comparing with the general equation

$$x = {x_0}\sin \left( {\omega t + \phi } \right)$$

where, $${x_0}$$maximum displacement

So, $${x_0} = 0.3,\,\,\omega = 1,\,\,\phi = \frac{\pi }{4}$$.

Hence, $$2\pi f = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow f = 1/2\pi $$.

Question: As the temperature of water increases, its viscosity
Options: increases or decreases depending on the external pressure
Solution: As the temperature of liquid increases, the energy of its molecules increases which increases the movement of molecules, so the liquid becomes more fluid, thus viscosity of liquid decreases.

Question: Which of the following statement is correct for an n-type semiconductor?
Options: The donor level lies below the bottom of the conduction band
The donor level lies closely above the top of the valence band
The donor level lies at the halfway mark of the forbidden energy gap
Solution:
N-type Semi-conductor

The donor level will be only found in n-type semiconductors. When doped with pentavalent atom, then each atom of germanium will have an extra electron. The energy of these electrons will be less than the least energy of conduction band and will be greater than the highest value of energy of valence band.
Hence, the electrons from a certain energy level below donor energy level. Donor level lies closely below the bottom of the conduction band as represented in the diagram.

Solution: If two similar waves propagate one along x-axis and another along y-axis and when they superimpose each other the resultant wave motion is along a straight line.

Question: A charge particle q moves with speed 2m/s at 60° with x-axes in x-y plane. A uniform magnetic field exists along +y axes of strength 1 Tesla. The electric field required so that the charged particle moves undeflected is
Solution:
$\vec v = \hat i + \sqrt 3 \hat j$
$\vec B = \hat j$
For particle to go undeflected,
${\overrightarrow {\text{F}} _{\text{e}}} = - {\overrightarrow {\text{F}} _{\text{m}}}$
${\text{q}}\overrightarrow {\text{E}} = - {\text{q}}(\overrightarrow {\text{v}} \times \overrightarrow {\text{B}} )$
      $\overrightarrow {\text{E}} = - [(\widehat {\text{i}} + \sqrt 3 \widehat {\text{j}}) \times \widehat {\text{j}}$
$\overrightarrow {\text{E}} = - \widehat {\text{k}}$
Negative sign represents –ve z -axis.

Question: In photoelectric effect, the work function of a metal is 3.5 eV. The emitted electrons can be stopped by applying a potential of –1.2 V. Then
Options: The energy of the incident photons in 4.7 eV
the energy of the incident photons is 2.3 eV
if higher frequency photons be used, the photoelectric current will rise
when the energy of photons is 3.5 eV, the photoelectric current will be maximum
Solution:
The work function of the metal, $\phi = 3.5{\text{ eV}}$,
Stopping potential, $P = - 1.2{\text{ V}}$
Where, $K.E = \left| {P.d.(e)} \right| = 1.2{\text{ eV}}$
Now, according to Einstein’s photoelectric equation,
$K.E = E - \phi $
$ \Rightarrow K.E = \phi + hf$
$ \Rightarrow 1.2 = 3.5 + hf$
$\therefore hf = 4.7{\text{ eV}}$

Question: The radius of gyration of a body depends upon :
Solution:
Moment of inertia of a rigid body is defined as;
\[ \Rightarrow I = M{K^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow K = \sqrt {\frac{I}{M}} \]

Where, K is the radius of gyration.

We know that I depend on mass and axis of rotation

So, radius of gyration of a body depends upon the axis of rotation.

Question: A car travels half the distance with constant velocity of 40 kmph and the remaining half with a constant velocity of 60 kmph. The average velocity of the car in kmph is
Solution:
Let the total distance be 2x
Time taken in travelling the first distance x will be t1 = x/40 and time taken in travelling the second distance x will be t2 = x/60

Now the average velocity can be calculated as

V = total distance/total time

\[ = 2x/\left( {{t_1} + {t_2}} \right)\]

\[ = \frac{{2x}}{{\frac{x}{{40}} + \frac{x}{{60}}}} = 48km/hr\]

Question:
Assertion: If a proton and an electron are placed in the same uniform electric field. They experience different acceleration.
Reason: Electric force on a test charge is independent of its mass.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution:
$$F = qE$$
Electron and proton have same amount of charge so they have same magnitude of Coulomb’s force. They have different accelerations because they have different masses $\left( {a\, = \,\frac{F}{m}} \right).$
Therefore, both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.

Question: Two bodies are projected from ground with equal speed 20ms<sup>-1</sup> from the same position in the same vertical plane to have equal range but at different angles above the horizontal. If one of the angle is 30<sup>o</sup>, then the sum of their maximum heighs is (assume, g = 10ms<sup>-2</sup>)
Solution:
As range of two projectile are same, so the angle of projections must be complementary, i.e.,
\[{\theta _1} = 30^\circ \,and\,{\theta _2} = 90^\circ - {\theta _1} = 60^\circ \]

The maxiumum height of projectile is given by

\[h = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{2g}}\]

Given, \[u = 20m{s^{ - 1}}\]

\[\therefore For\,\theta = 30^\circ ,{h_1} = \frac{{{{\left( {20} \right)}^2}{{\sin }^2}30^\circ }}{{2 \times 10}}\]

\[ = \frac{{400 \times 1}}{{2 \times 10 \times 4}} = 5m\]

Similarly, for \[\theta = 60^\circ ,\]

\[{h_2} = \frac{{{{\left( {20} \right)}^2}{{\sin }^2}60^\circ }}{{2 \times 10}}\]

\[ = \frac{{400 \times 3}}{{2 \times 10 \times 4}} = 15m\]

\[\therefore \] Sum of maximum heights

\[ = {h_1} + {h_2}\]

\[ = 5 + 15\]

\[ = 20m\]

Question:
Assertion: The capacity of cylindrical capacitor is independent of its length.
Reason: The capacity of cylindrical capacitor depends only on ratio of outer to inner radius.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution:
Cylindrical capacitor consists of two concentric cylinders of radii a and b (a < b), inner cylinder is given charge + Q while outer cylinder is earthed. Common length of the cylinders is l then
\[C = \frac{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}l}}{{{{\log }_e}\left( {\frac{b}{a}} \right)}}.\]

Question: An object of mass 40 kg and having the velocity 4 m/s collides with another object mass of m = 60 kg having velocity 2 m/s. The collision is perfectly inelastic. The loss in energy is
Solution:
Let the final velocity be v.
Now applying conservation of linear momentum, we have
40 × 4 + 60 × 2 = (40 + 60)v
∴ 160 + 120 = 100v
⇒ 100 v = 280
⇒ v = 2.8m/s
Now, decrease in kinetic energy
$ = \frac{1}{2}40 \times {\left( 4 \right)^2} + \frac{1}{2}60{\left( 2 \right)^2} - \frac{1}{2}100{\left( {2.8} \right)^2}$
= 320 + 120 – 392
= 48 Joules

Question: Electric flux through a surface element $dS = 5\hat i$ placed in an electric field $E = 4\hat i + 4\hat j + 4\hat k$ is
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \phi = E \cdot dS,E = 4\widehat i + 4\widehat j + 4\widehat k \\
  {\text{and}}\;{\kern 1pt} dS = 5\widehat i \\
   \Rightarrow \phi = \left( {4\widehat i + 4\widehat j + 4\widehat k} \right) \cdot \left( {5\widehat i} \right) \\
  \phi = 20{\text{ }}units \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A system of three parallel plates each of area A are separated by distances$${d_1}$$ and $${d_2}$$. The space between them is filled with dielectric of permittivities ε <sub>1</sub> and ε <sub>2</sub>. The permittivity of free space is ε <sub>0</sub>. The equivalent capacitance of system is
Solution:
$${C_1} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _1}A}}{{{d_1}}};{C_2} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _2}A}}{{{d_2}}}$$
$${C_{eq}} = \frac{{{C_1}{C_2}}}{{{C_1} + {C_2}}} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{{{\varepsilon _1}A}}{{{d_1}}}} \right)\left( {\frac{{{\varepsilon _2}A}}{{{d_2}}}} \right)}}{{\frac{{{\varepsilon _1}A}}{{{d_1}}} + \frac{{{\varepsilon _2}A}}{{{d_2}}}}}$$
\[{C_{eq}} = \frac{{\frac{{{\varepsilon _1}{\varepsilon _2}{A^2}}}{{{d_1}{d_2}}}}}{{A\left( {\frac{{{\varepsilon _1}{d_2} + {\varepsilon _2}{d_1}}}{{{d_1}{d_2}}}} \right)}} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _1}{\varepsilon _2}A}}{{{\varepsilon _1}{d_2} + {\varepsilon _2}{d_1}}}\]

Question: A smooth table is placed horizontally and an ideal spring of spring constant k = 1000 N/m and unextended length of 0.5 m has one end fixed to its centre. The other end is attached to a mass of 5 kg which is moving in a circle with constant speed 20 m/s. Then the tension in the spring and the extension of this spring beyond its normal length are-
Solution:
Using, the condition
Centripetal force = spring force.
\[\frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = kx\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{5 \times 20 \times 20}}{{\left( {0.5 + x} \right)}} = 1000\left( x \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2 = \frac{x}{2} + {x^2}\,\,or,\,\,2{x^2} + x - 4 = 0\]
\[\therefore x = \frac{{ - 1 \pm \sqrt {1 + 4.2.4} }}{{2.2}} = \frac{{ - 1 \pm \sqrt {33} }}{4}\]
\[\therefore x = \frac{{5.74 - 1}}{4} = \frac{{4.74}}{4} = 1.19 \approx 1.2\,m\]
\[\therefore x = 1.2m - 0.5m = 0.7m\]
\[\therefore Tension\, = kx = 1000 \times 0.7 = 700N\]

Question: How much current will flow in a circuit having resistance of 109 Ω and an inductance of 0.5 H connected to an A.C. supply of 100 V and 50 Hz in series?

Assertion: The trajectory of an object moving under the same acceleration due to gravity can be straight line or a parabola depending on the initial conditions.
Reason: The shape of the trajectory of the motion of on object is determined by the acceleration alone.
Options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a true explanation of the assertion.
If both the assertion and reason are true but the reason is not the true correct the correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but reason is false.
If both the assertion and reason are false.
Solution: The shape of the trajectory of the motion of an object is not determine by the acceleration alone but also depends on the initial conditions of motion (initial position and initial velocity).

Question: Two bodies with volumes V and 2V are equalized on a balance. The larger body is then immersed in oil of density d1 = 0.9 g/cm3 while the smaller body is immersed in another liquid when it is found that the equilibrium of the balance is not disturbed. The density of the second liquid is then:
Solution:
$${V_1} = V$$

$${V_2} = {\text{ }}2V$$

Both have same mass therefore, their densities are

$${M_1} = {\text{ }}{M_2}$$

$${d_2} = \frac{1}{2} \times {d_1}$$

M2 is immersed in oil of density 0.9 g/cm3

Buoyancy force on the M2, $${B_2} = {V_2} \times 0.9 \times g$$

Let the density of liquid in which the smaller body M1 is immersed be d g/cm3

Buoyancy force on smaller body, $${B_1} = {V_1} \times d \times g$$

For the balance to remain in equilibrium, upthrusts must be equal.

$${B_2} = {B_1}$$

$${V_2} \times 0.9 \times g = {V_1} \times d \times g$$

$$d = 0.9 \times \frac{{2V}}{V} = 1.8{\text{ }}g/c{m^3}$$

a = -g = -10 m/s2 (Taking upward direction to be positive)

\[ \Rightarrow t = 1.5s\]

Question: The figure shows three situations when an electron with velocity $$\vec v$$ travels through a uniform magnetic field$$\vec B$$. In each case, what is the direction of magnetic force on the electron?
Solution:
\[ = \left( { - e} \right)\left( { - v\widehat i \times B\hat j} \right) =  - Bev\hat k\] , i.e., -ve z-axis.

Question: A body from height h is dropped. If the coefficient of restitution is e, then calculate the height achieved after one bounce.
Solution:
When body falls from height h, it reaches with velocity
\[v = \sqrt {2gh} \]

\[e = \frac{{\sqrt {2g{h_1}} }}{{\sqrt {2gh} }} = \sqrt {\frac{{{h_1}}}{h}} \]

\[{h_1} = {e^2}h\]

Question: The angle between two vectors $\overrightarrow a $ and $\overrightarrow b $ with magnitudes $\sqrt 3 $ and 4, respectively, and $\overrightarrow a .\overrightarrow b  = 2\sqrt 3 $ is
Solution:
Here, given that \[\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right| = \sqrt 3 ,\,\,\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right| = 4\] and \[\overrightarrow a \cdot \overrightarrow b = 2\sqrt 3 \]

∴ From scalar product, we know that

\[\overrightarrow a \cdot \overrightarrow b = \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|\cos \theta \]

$ \Rightarrow \,\,2\sqrt 3 = \sqrt 3 \cdot 4 \cdot \cos \theta $

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\cos \theta = \frac{{2\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt 3 \cdot 4}} = \frac{1}{2}$

$\therefore \,\,\,\theta = \frac{\pi }{3}$

Hence, the correct option is (b).

Question: Consider a current carrying loop shown in figure formed by radial lines and segments of circle whose centers are at point P. What is the magnitude of magnetic field induction at point P.
Solution:
Magnetic field at P due to straight wire =0

Due to circular wire one is outwards (of radius a) and other is inwards(of radius b)

600 means 1/6th of the whole circle.

$\therefore B = \frac{1}{6}\left[ {\frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2a}} - \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{2b}}} \right]$

$\therefore {B_{net}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}i}}{{12}}\left( {\frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{b}} \right)$

Question: A rope of length L and mass M is hanging from a rigid support. The tension in the rope at a distance x from the rigid support is:
Solution:
Mass per unit length of rope is \[ = \frac{M}{L}\]

Let the internal tension in the rope at a distance x from the rigid support is T. Now considering the F.B.D. of PB

\[T = m'g\]


m’ = mass of section PB

\[ = \frac{M}{L}\left( {L - x} \right)\]

 \[ \Rightarrow T = \frac{M}{L}\left( {L - x} \right)g \Rightarrow T = M\left( {1 - \frac{x}{L}} \right)g\]

So, option (b) is correct

Question: A parallel plate capacitor of plate area A has a charge Q. The force on each plate of the capacitor is
Solution:
Force on one plate due to another is,
$$F = QE = Q \times \frac{\sigma }{{2{\varepsilon _0}}}$$
$$Q\left( {\frac{Q}{{2A{\varepsilon _0}}}} \right) = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{2A{\varepsilon _0}}}$$

Question: ${C_V}$and ${C_P}$denote the molar specific heat capacities of gas at constant volume and constant pressure, respectively. Then,
Options: ${C_P} - {C_V}$is larger for a diatomic ideal gas than for a monoatomic ideal gas.
${C_P} + {C_V}$is larger for a diatomic ideal gas than for a monoatomic ideal gas, ${C_P}.{C_V}$is larger for a diatomic ideal gas than for a monoatomic gas
$\frac{{{C_P}}}{{{C_V}}}$is larger for a diatomic ideal gas than for a monoatomic ideal gas
${C_P} + {C_V}$is larger for a diatomic ideal gas than for a monoatomic gas
Solution:
For monoatomic gas,
${C_P} = \frac{5}{2}R\,\,and\,\,{C_V} = \frac{3}{2}R$

Question: The magnitude of the X and Y components of \[\vec A\] are 7 and 6. Also the magnitudes of X and Y components of \[\vec A + \vec B\] are 11 and 9 respectively. What is the magnitude of \[\overrightarrow B \]?
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow A = 7\widehat i + 6\widehat j\]

Let, \[\overrightarrow B = x\widehat i + y\widehat j + z\hat k\]

\[\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B = 11\widehat i + 9\widehat j\]

\[ \Rightarrow \left( {7 + x} \right)\widehat i + \left( {6 + y} \right)\widehat j + z\hat k = 11\widehat i + 9\widehat j\]

Equating Components

\[7 + x = 11 \Rightarrow x = 4\]

\[6 + y = 9 \Rightarrow y = 3\]

\[z = 0 \Rightarrow z = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow B = 4\widehat i + 3\widehat j\]

\[\therefore \,\,\,B = \sqrt {{4^2} + {3^2}} = \,5\;{\text{units}}\]

Question: Two particles of masses $${m_a}\,and\,{m_b}$$ and same charge are projected in a perpendicular magnetic field. They travel along circular paths of radius $${r_a}\,and\,{r_b}\,such\,that\,{r_a} > {r_b}.$$ Then, which is true?
Solution:
Radius of circular path,
 $${r_a} = rac{{{m_a}{ u _a}}}{{qB}}\,\,\,\,and\,\,\,\,{r_b} = rac{{{m_b}{ u _b}}}{{qB}}$$

 According to question, $${r_a} > {r_b}$$

 $$ herefore \, rac{{{m_a}{ u _a}}}{{qB}} > rac{{{m_b}{ u _b}}}{{qB}}\,\,or\,\,{m_a}{ u _a} > {m_b}{ u _b}$$

Question: A body starts from rest moves along a straight line with constant acceleration. The variation of speed V with distance s is given by graph
Solution:
Both start from rest hence velocity is zero from equation of motion, we have
\[{v^2} = {u^2} + 2as\]

Where v is final velocity, u is initial velocity, a is acceleration and t is time. Since, body starts from u = 0

\[\therefore {v^2} = 2as\]which is ground equation parabola

\[{y^2} = 4ax\]hence graph is parabola and is symmetrical along x axis.

Question: The quantities remaining constant in a collision are
Options: momentum and kinetic energy but not temperature
momentum and temperature but not kinetic energy
momentum, but neither kinetic energy nor temperature.
Solution:
Momentum but neither kinetic energy nor temperature.
Explanation: Since velocities of the colliding bodies change the K.E. will change. The shapes of the bodies at the time of collision change due to the force hence temperature will also change. Only the momentum will remain constant.

Question:
Assertion : In an elastic collision of two billiard balls, the total KE is conserved during the short time of collision of the balls (i.e., when they are in contact)
Reason : Energy spent against friction does not follow the law of conservation of energy :
Options: If both assertion and reason are correct and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
Solution:
During collision, there is a deformity of shape of the balls leading to increase in potential energy of the body, thus decreasing the kinetic energy.
Laws of conservation of energy apply to everything, including friction.

Hence, both the statements are false.

Question: A particle is attached to a vertical spring and is pulled down a distance 0.04 m below its equilibrium position and is released from rest. The initial upward acceleration of the particle is 0.30 m/s<sup>2</sup>. The period of the oscillation is
Solution:
Initial Acceleration $$ = {\omega ^2}y$$
$ \Rightarrow {\omega ^2} \times 0.04 = 0.3$

$ \Rightarrow {\omega ^2} = \frac{{30}}{4}$

$ \Rightarrow \omega = \sqrt {\frac{{15}}{2}} = \frac{{2\pi }}{T}$

$ \Rightarrow T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{2}{{15}}} = 2.29{\text{ sec}}$

Question: Small droplets of liquid are usually more spherical in shape than larger drops of the same liquid because
Options: force of surface tension is equal and opposite to the force of gravity
force of surface tension predominates the force of gravity
force of gravity predominates the force of surface tension
force of gravity and force of surface tension act in the same direction and are equal
Solution: The shape of the liquid is actually controlled by two forces viz. surface tension and gravitational force. When size of drop is small, surface tension force is greater than gravitational force, which means net force is due to surface tension.

Question: A projectile of mass 3 m explodes at the highest point of its path. It breaks into three equal parts. One part follows the original path of the projectile, the second one comes to rest. \[ \Rightarrow {X_{cm}} = \frac{{{m_1}{x_1} + {m_2}{x_2} + {m_3}{x_3}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2} + {m_3}}}\]

\[100 = \frac{{m\left( {100} \right) + m\left( {50} \right) + mx}}{{3m}}\]

\[300 = 100 + 50 + x\]

Question: Two electric charges $12{\mkern 1mu} \mu {\rm{C}}$ and $ - 6{\mkern 1mu} \mu {\rm{C}}$ are placed $20{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{cm}}$ apart in air. There will be a point P on the line joining these charges and outside the region between them, at which the electric potential is zero. The distance of P from $ - 6{\mkern 1mu} \mu {\rm{C}}$ charge is
Solution:
Point P will lie near the charge which is smaller in magnitude i.e. $ - 6{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \mu {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C}}$ . Hence potential at P

 $ V = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{\left( { - 6 \times {{10}^6}} \right)}}{x} + \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{\left( {12 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)}}{{\left( {0.2 + x} \right)}} = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0.2\,{\text{m}} $

Question:
A tank is filled with water up to height H. When a hole is made at a distance h below
the level of water. What will be horizontal range of water jet ?
Solution:
Velocity of efflux \[ = \sqrt {2gD} \]

Time taken to reach the Ground \[ = \sqrt {\frac{{2(H - D)}}{g}} \]
So, horizontal range = velocity × time
So range of the water stream \[ = \sqrt {2gD} \times \sqrt {\frac{{2(H - D)}}{g}} \]
\[x = 2\sqrt {D(H - D)} = 2\sqrt {h(H - h)} \]
As D = H – h

Question: A vessel contains an immiscible mixture of water and a liquid of density 0.8 gm/cc. A cube of side 10 cm is placed in the mixture and it is observed that the water-liquid interface is at the middle of cube height. Mass of the cube is:

Question: The potential of a large liquid drop when eight liquid drops are combined is 20 V. Then, the potential of each single drop was
Solution:
Volume of 8 drops = Volume of a big drop
$$\eqalign{
  & \therefore \left( {\frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}} \right) \times 8 = \frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3} \cr
  & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,8{r^3} = {R^3} \cr
  & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,2r = R\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( i \right) \cr} $$
According to charge conservation,
$$8q = Q\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {ii} \right)$$
Potential of one small drop,$$V' = \frac{q}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}$$
Similarly, potential of big drop,$$V = \frac{Q}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}R}}$$
$$\eqalign{
  & Now,\frac{{V'}}{V} = \frac{q}{Q} \times \frac{R}{r} \Rightarrow \frac{{V'}}{{20}} = \frac{q}{{8q}} \times \frac{{2r}}{r} \cr
  & {\text{[from Eqs}}{\text{. (i) and (ii)]}} \cr
  & \therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,V' = 5\,\,V \cr} $$

Question: A body of mass 10 kg is rotated in vertical circle of radius 4 cm at constant angular velocity of 5 rad/ sec. The maximum tension in the string is-
Solution:
The maximum tension $k$ at the bottom most point
$T = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} + mg = m{\omega ^2}r + mg$
$\begin{gathered}
   = 10 \times {\left( 5 \right)^2} \times \frac{4}{{100}} + 10 \times 10 \\
   = 10 \times \frac{{25 \times 4}}{{100}} + 100 \\
\end{gathered} $
$ = 110\,N$
Hence [c]

Question: The coefficient of mutual inductance between the primary and secondary coils of transformer is 5 H. A current of 10 A is cut-off in 0.5 s. The induced emf is
Solution:
From Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction, the induced emf e is given by
\[e = - M\frac{{\Delta i}}{{\Delta t}}\]
Given, M = 5 H, \[\Delta \]i = -10 A, \[\Delta \]t = 0.5 s
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,e = - 5 \times \frac{{ - 10}}{{0.5}} = 100V\]
\[e = 100V\]

Question: A pendulum of length 2 m left at A. When it reaches B, it loses 10% of its total energy due to air resistance. The velocity at B is:
Solution:
Length = 2m

10 % of potential equation is lost and of potential Energy is converted into K.E.


K.E. at \[{\text{B}} = \frac{{90}}{{100}} \times (mg \times h) = \frac{9}{{10}} \times m \times 9.8 \times 2\]

\[\frac{1}{2} \times m\;{{\text{v}}^2} = \frac{9}{{10}} \times m \times 9.8 \times 2\]

\[\therefore {\text{v}} = 6m/s\]

Question: A ball is thrown upwards at an angle of 60° to the horizontal. It falls on the ground at a distance of 90 m. If the ball is thrown with the same initial velocity at an angle of 30°. It will fall on the ground at a distance of:
Solution:
Here we will use formula for range,

$R = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 2\theta }}{g}$

Now let’s apply first condition R = 90 m and\[\theta = 60^\circ \], $90 = \frac{{{u^2}\sin {{120}^0}}}{g}$

Now apply second condition \[\theta = 30^\circ \],

$R = \frac{{{u^2}\sin {{60}^0}}}{g}$

Now let’s take ratio of both equations,

Remember initial velocity is same in both condition

$\frac{{90}}{R} = \frac{{\sin 120^\circ }}{{\sin 60^\circ }}$

$\frac{{90}}{R} = \frac{{\sqrt {\frac{3}{2}} }}{{\sqrt {\frac{3}{2}} }}$

$\frac{{90}}{R} = 1$

$R = 90{\text{ }}m$

Question: Water rises to a height of 30 mm in a capillary tube. If the radius of the capillary tube is made $$\frac{3}{4}th$$ of its previous value. The height to which the water will rise in the tube is:
Solution:
$${h_1}{r_1} = {h_2}{r_2}$$

$${h_2} = \frac{{30 \times 4}}{3} = 40{\text{ }}mm$$

Question: In a transistor the collector current is always less than the emitter current because:
Options: collector side is reverse biased and the emitter side is forward biased
a few electrons are lost in the base and only remaining ones reach the collector
collector being reverse biased, attracts less electrons
collector side is forward biased and emitter side is reverse biased.
Solution: The collector region is where the majority charge carriers get collected. They move from emitter to base, and base to collector. A few charge carriers get accumulated/lost in the base region. The remainder move to the collector region.

Question: The displacement of a particle along the x-axis is given by x = a sin<sup>2</sup>ωt. The motion of the particle corresponds to:
Solution:
$$x = a{\sin ^2}\omega t = a\left( {\frac{{1 - \cos 2\omega t}}{2}} \right)$$
$$x = \frac{a}{2} - \frac{a}{2}\cos 2\omega t$$

$$x - \frac{a}{2} = - \frac{a}{2}\cos 2\omega t$$

Considering $$x = \frac{a}{2}$$as mean position.

Question: The passenger move forward when train stops, due to
Solution: The passenger moves forward when the train stops, because of inertia the upper part of the body of the passenger is in the direction of initial velocity of train while the lower part stops with stop of train. Therefore, the passenger moves forward when the train stops.

Question: If the power factor changes from 1/2 to 1/4, then what is the increase in impedance in AC (keeping R constant)?
Solution:
Power factor, \[\cos \phi = \frac{R}{Z}\]
If R is constant, then \[\cos \phi \propto \frac{1}{Z}\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\frac{{{Z_1}}}{{{Z_2}}} = \frac{{\cos {\phi _2}}}{{\cos {\phi _1}}} = \frac{{1/4}}{{1/2}} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow {Z_2} = 2{Z_1}\]
$\therefore $ Percentage change
\[ = \frac{{2{Z_1} - {Z_1}}}{{{Z_1}}} \times 100 = 100\% \]

Question: The charge on capacitors in the following figure is
Solution:
The capacitors are in series, so they will have same charge. Applying Kirchhoff’s loop law,
$$ - 6 + \frac{Q}{2} + \frac{Q}{2} + 2 - 4 = 0$$
$$\therefore \,\,\,Q = 8\,C$$

Question: Given $\overrightarrow P  = 3\widehat j + 4\widehat k$ and $\overrightarrow Q  = 2\widehat j + 5\widehat k.$ The magnitude of the scalar product of these vector is
Solution:
$\overrightarrow P = 3\widehat j + 4\widehat k$ and $\overrightarrow Q = 2\widehat j + 5\widehat k$

$\overrightarrow P \cdot \overrightarrow Q = \left( {{P_x}\widehat i + {P_y}\widehat j + {P_z}\widehat k} \right).\left( {{Q_x}\widehat i + {Q_y}\widehat j + {Q_z}\widehat k} \right)$

$ = {P_x}{Q_x} + {P_y}{Q_y} + {P_z}{Q_z}$

$ = 6 + 20$

$ = 26$

Question: What is the ratio of inductive and capacitive reactance in AC circuit?
Solution:
Inductive reactance, XL = ωL Capacitive reactance,
\[{X_C} = \frac{1}{{\omega C}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{X_L}}}{{{X_C}}} = \frac{{\omega L}}{{1/\omega C}} = {\omega ^2}LC\]

Question: Find out work done to expand soap bubble to radius R = 5 cm (surface tension of water = 0.1 N/m)
What will be the temperature when the rms velocity is double of that at 300K:
Solution:
${V_{rms}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3RT}}{M}} $
${V_{rms}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3R \times 300}}{M}} $
$2{V_{rms}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3R \times {T^1}}}{M}} $
${T^1} = 1200K$

Question: The momentum of a photon of energy 1 MeV in kg m/s, will be
Solution:
The energy of the photon,
$E = 1{\text{ MeV}} = 1 \times {10^6}{\text{ eV}}$
Where, Energy, $E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }$…… (1)
Now, from de-Broglie wavelength, $\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$…. (2)
Now, put the value of equation (2) in equation (1),
Therefore, $E = pc$
$ \Rightarrow p = \frac{E}{c}$
$ \Rightarrow p = \frac{{1 \times {{10}^6} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}$
$\therefore p = 5 \times {10^{ - 22}}{\text{ kg - m/s}}$

Question: A bullet hits a block kept at rest on a smooth horizontal surface and gets embedded into it. Which of the following does not change ?
Options: gravitational potential energy of the block
Solution:
Gravitational potential energy of block does not change because no vertical displacement is possessed by block.

$\vec P$ block changes because of impulse.

$\therefore \,$KE of block also changes.

Question: The magnetic flux density B at a distance r from a long solid cylindrical wire carrying a steady current varies with r as shown in figure
Solution:
For r > R For r < R

$${\text{B}} \propto \frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{r}}}$$ $${\text{B}} \propto {\text{r}}$$

Question: 64 small drops of water having same charge and same radius are combined to form one big drop. The ratio of capacitance of big drop to small drop is
Solution:
Let the radius of each small drop be r and the radius of big drop be R. When 64 small drops of water are combined to form one big drop, then the volume remains constant.
So, the volume of 64 small drops = the volume of big drop
$$i.e.\,\,64 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3} = \frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3}$$
$$ \Rightarrow \,\,64{r^3} = {R^3}$$
$$ \Rightarrow \,\,4r = R$$
$$ \Rightarrow \,\,R = 4r\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( i \right)$$
Now, the capacitance of a spherical conductor is
$$C = 4\pi {\varepsilon _0}a$$
(a is the radius of the conductor) Now, the capacitance of small drop,
$${C_1} = 4\pi {\varepsilon _0}r\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {ii} \right)$$
and the capacitance of big drop,
$${C_2} = 4\pi {\varepsilon _0}R\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$
On putting the value of R from Eq. (i), we get
$${C_2} = 4\pi {\varepsilon _0}\left( {4r} \right)$$
$$ \Rightarrow \,\,{C_2}\, = 16\pi {\varepsilon _0}r\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {iii} \right)\,\,$$
On dividing Eq. (iii) by Eq. (ii), we get
$$\frac{{{C_2}}}{{{C_1}}} = \frac{{16\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}$$
$$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{{C_2}}}{{{C_1}}} = \frac{4}{1}$$
$$ \Rightarrow \,\,{C_2}:{C_1} = 4:1$$

Question: The volume occupied by an atom is greater than the volume of the nucleus by factor of about
Solution:
The volume is directly proportional to the cube of the radius of a sphere.
Now, the radius of an atom, ${r_{atom}} = {10^{ - 10}}{\text{ m}}$, and the radius of a nucleus is, ${r_{nucleus}} = {10^{ - 15}}{\text{ m}}$
Therefore, the ratio of the volume of the atom upon the volume of the nucleus is,
$\frac{{{V_{atom}}}}{{{V_{nucleus}}}} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{4}{3}\pi r_{atom}^3} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{4}{3}\pi r_{nucleus}^3} \right)}} = \frac{{r_{atom}^3}}{{r_{nucleus}^3}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{V_{atom}}}}{{{V_{nucleus}}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 15}}}}} \right)^3}$
$\therefore \frac{{{V_{atom}}}}{{{V_{nucleus}}}} = {10^{15}}$
Therefore, ${V_{atom}} = {V_{nucleus}} \times {10^{15}}{\text{ m}}$

Question: For a gas, if the ratio of specific heats at constant pressure P and constant volume V is g, then the value of degree of freedom is:
Solution:
Given: \[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{C_P}}}{{{C_V}}} = \gamma \]
Relation between $\gamma $ and degree of freedom (f) is given by:
$\begin{gathered}
  \gamma = 1 + \frac{2}{f} \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{2}{f} = \gamma - 1 \\
   \Rightarrow f = \frac{2}{{\gamma - 1}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Tags.Subject: Physics

Question: Consider a thin spherical shell of radius R with its centre at the origin carrying uniform positive surface charge density. The variation of the magnitude of the electric field |E (r)| and the electric potential V (r) with the distance r from the centre, is best represented by which graph?
Solution:
For a spherical shell;
Electric field;
For r < R, E = 0
For r > R, $E = \frac{{kQ}}{{{r^2}}}$
Electric potential;
For $r < R,V = \frac{{kQ}}{R} = constant$
For $r > R,V = \frac{{kQ}}{r}$
Thus, correct option would be (d).

Question: The x and y components of a force are 2 N and –3N. The force is
Solution:
x component, \[{F_x} = 2\widehat i\]

y component, \[{F_y} = - 3\widehat j\]

\[ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow F = {F_x}\widehat i + {F_y}\widehat j\]

\[ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow F = 2\widehat i - 3\widehat j\]

Question: The total energy of electron in the ground state of hydrogen atom is –13.6 eV. The kinetic energy of an electron in the first excited state is
Solution:
The total energy of electron of a Hydrogen atom at ground state, $E = - 13.6{\text{ eV}}$
Now, the energy of an electron at first excited state, ${E_1} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{n^2}}}$
For the first excited state, $n = 2$, then ${E_1} = - 3.4{\text{ eV}}$
Therefore, the kinetic energy of an electron in the first excited state, $K.E = \left| {{E_1}} \right| = 3.4{\text{ eV}}$
