Question: Which of the following is not represented in correct unit
Solution: Unit of energy will be kg-m2/sec2

Question: Two pendulums of time period 3 s and 8 s respectively starts oscillating simultaneously from two opposite extreme positions. After how much time they will be in the same phase ?
Solution:
${y_1}\, = \,A\,\sin \left( {{\omega _1}t + \pi } \right)$
$${y_2} = A\sin \left( {{\omega _2}t} \right)$$

They are in the same phase

$${\omega _1}t + \pi = {\omega _2}t$$

$$\left( {{\omega _2} - {\omega _1}} \right)t = \pi $$

$$\left( {\frac{1}{3}\, - \,\frac{1}{8}} \right)2\pi t\, = \,\pi $$

$$\frac{5}{{24}} \times 2t = 1$$

$$t = \frac{{12}}{5}\sec $$

Question: Graph: Line with negative slope
Options: particle is moving away the point of reference with uniform velocity
particle returns towards the point of reference with decreasing velocity
particle returns towards the point of reference with increasing velocity
particle returns towards the point of reference with uniform velocity
Solution: Interpretation: It represents that the particle returns towards the point of reference (negative displacement) with uniform velocity.

Question: A cylindrical block of mass m and area of cross-section A floats in a liquid of density ρ.  If it is given small vertical displacement from equilibrium, it undergoes oscillation with a time period T, then
Solution:
When the body is pushed down from equilibrium position,
${F_{restoring}} = {\text{Additional upthrust}}$

$ \Rightarrow {\text{ma = }}\left( {Ax} \right)\rho g$

$ \Rightarrow a = \frac{{A\rho g}}{m}x$

So, $\omega = \sqrt {\frac{{A\rho g}}{m}} $

$T \propto \;\frac{1}{{\sqrt \rho }}$

Question: In the figure shown below, the block is moved sideways by a distance A. The net force on the block is :
Solution:
The spring on the left of the block is elongated and that on right is compressed both exert a force on the block towards the mean position and they add up.
$${F_{eff}} = A\left( {{k_1} + {k_2}} \right)$$

Question: A cyclist is moving on a circular track of radius $80 \mathrm{~m}$ with a velocity of $72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$. He has to lean from the vertical approximately through an angle

Statement–1 : In one dimensional elastic collision between an incoming body A and body B which is at rest, mass of body B should be quite lesser than mass of body A so that body B recoils with greatest speed.
Statement–2 : Linear momentum and energy is conserved in elastic collision.
Options: Both Statement-1 and Statement-2 are true and Statement-2 is the correct explanation for Statement-1.
Both Statement-1 and Statement-2 are true but Statement-2 is not the correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement-1 is true and the Statement-2 is false.
Statement-1 is false and the Statement-2 is true.
Solution:
\[{v_2} = \frac{{2{m_1}{u_1}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}} - \frac{{\left( {{m_1} - {m_2}} \right)}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}\]
\[{u_2} = 0\]

\[{v_2} = \frac{{2{m_1}{u_1}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}},\,\,if\,{m_1} > {m_2}\,then\,{v_2} \approx \sim 2{u_1}\]

Thus, equation is derived from conservation principle of energy and momentum.

Question: A particle is moving on the X–axis. When the X– component of its acceleration is +ve and increasing
Options: X–component of its velocity must be +ve
X–component of its velocity must be –ve
Solution: Nothing definite can be said about the sign of velocity so, it may be speeding up.

Question: A particle of mass m executes simple harmonic motion with amplitude A and frequency $\upsilon $. The average kinetic energy during its motion from the position of equilibrium to the ends is
Solution:
In equilibrium position the kinetic energy will be minimum. For example if we hang a spring, when the spring reaches the ground position then spring force and the gravitational forces becomes equal and the velocity will be zero. There will be no elongation in the mean position then the kinetic energy will be maximum.
$K.{E_{avg}} = \frac{{K.{E_{\max }} + K.{E_{\min }}}}{2} = \frac{{K.{E_{\max }} + 0}}{2} = \frac{{K.{E_{\max }}}}{2}$

$ \Rightarrow K.{E_{\max }} = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$

But,

${v^2} = {\omega ^2}\left( {{A^2} - {x^2}} \right)$

$ \Rightarrow K.{E_{\max }} = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}\left( {{A^2} - 0} \right) = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}{A^2}$

$K.{E_{avg}} = \frac{1}{4}m{\omega ^2}{A^2} = \frac{1}{4} \times m{\left( {2\pi \upsilon } \right)^2}{A^2}$

$ = \frac{1}{4}m \times 4{\pi ^2}{\upsilon ^2}{A^2}$

$K.{E_{avg}} = m{\pi ^2}{\upsilon ^2}{A^2}$

Question:
Assertion : A quick collision between two bodies is more violent than a slow collision; even when the initial and final velocities are identical.
Reason : The momentum is greater in the first case.
Options: If both assertion and reason are correct and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
Solution:
Force\[{\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{Change}}\;{\text{in}}\;{\text{momentum}}}}{{{\text{time}}\;{\text{taken}}}}\]
The initial and final velocities are the same in both cases. Hence, change in momentum is the same. The only deciding factor is time taken.

When lesser time is taken, the force is larger (though the impulse remains same). Thus, quicker collisions seem more violent as more force is exerted.

Assertion is true, but reason is false (Force in first case is greater, not momentum)

Question: Two samples A and B of same gas have equal volumes and pressures. The gas in sample A is expanded isothermally to double its volume and the gas in sample B is expanded to double its volume adiabatically. If work done by the gas is same in two processes, then value of $\gamma $for the gas is best given by
Solution:
${W_A} = {W_B}$
$nRT\ln \frac{{2{V_0}}}{{{V_0}}} = \frac{{nRdT}}{{1 - \gamma }}$
$R\frac{{{P_0}{V_0}}}{R}\ln 2 = \frac{{{P_1}{V_1} - {P_2}{V_2}}}{{\gamma - 1}}$
${P_0}{V_0}\ln 2 = \frac{{{P_0}{V_0} - {P_2}\left( {2{V_0}} \right)}}{{\gamma - 1}}\,\,\,....\left( i \right)$
$\because {P_0}V_0^\gamma = {P_2}{\left( {2{V_0}} \right)^\gamma }$
${P_2} = {2^{ - \gamma }}{P_0}\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( {ii} \right)$
Put value of equation (ii) in equation (i)
${P_0}{V_0}\ln 2 = \frac{{{P_0}{V_0} - {2^{ - \gamma }}{P_0}\left( {2{V_0}} \right)}}{{\gamma - 1}}$
${P_0}{V_0}\ln 2 = \frac{{{P_0}{V_0}\left( {1 - {2^{ - \gamma + 1}}} \right)}}{{\gamma - 1}}$
$1 - {2^{ - \left( {\gamma - 1} \right)}} = \left( {\gamma - 1} \right)\ln 2$

Question: A boat 3 m long and 2 m wide is floating in a lake. When a man climbs over it, it sinks 1 cm further into water. The mass of the man is:
Solution:
Boat length $$ = 3{\text{ }}m$$
Breadth $$ = 2m$$

Boat sink depth $$ = 1{\text{ }}cm = {10^{ - 2}}{\text{ }}m$$

mass of man = volume of water displaced × density of water $$ = \left( {3 \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right) \times {10^3} = 60kg$$

Question:
Statement – 1: If no external force acts on a system of particles, then the centre of mass will not move in any direction.
Statement – 2: If net external force is zero, then the linear momentum of the system remains constant.
Options: Both Statement-1 and Statement-2 are true and Statement-2 is the correct explanation for Statement-1.
Both Statement-1 and Statement-2 are true but Statement-2 is not the correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement-1 is true and the Statement-2 is false.
Statement-1 is false and the Statement-2 is true.
Solution:
\[{\vec F_{ext}} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{{d\vec p}}{{dt}} = 0 \Rightarrow \vec P = {\text{constant}}\]
\[{{\vec F}_{ext}} = m{{\vec a}_{cm}} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow {\vec a_{cm}} = 0 \Rightarrow {\vec V_{cm}} = {\text{constant}}\]

Question: A solid sphere of radius R is placed on a smooth horizontal surface. A horizontal force F is applied at height h from the lowest point. For the maximum acceleration of the centre of mass :
Options: the acceleration will be same whatever h may be
Solution:
We know the Newton’s law;
$ \Rightarrow F = ma$

$ \Rightarrow a = \frac{F}{m}$

Thus, linear acceleration of the sphere only depends on the externally applied force (F) and its mass (m).

Thus, linear acceleration of sphere will be same for any height h.

Question: The graph between velocity and position for a damped oscillation will be:
Solution: In a damped oscillation, amplitude will decrease over time. Hence the graph will be spiral.

Question: A uniform rod of length 1.6 m and mass 20 kg is free to rotate in a vertical plane about A. The rod initially in horizontal position is released. The initial angular acceleration of the rod is :
Solution:
Moment of inertia of a rod of length l about the axis passing through its one end is given by;

$ \Rightarrow I = \frac{{m{l^2}}}{3}$

When this rod is released from its initial horizontal position a gravitational force (mg) is acting at its middle point in the downward direction, which generate torque;

$ \Rightarrow \tau = mg \times \frac{l}{2}$

We know the relation between torque and angular acceleration as;

$ \Rightarrow \tau = I{\text{ }}\alpha $

$ \Rightarrow mg \times \frac{l}{2} = \frac{{m{l^2}}}{3} \times \alpha $

$ \Rightarrow \alpha = \frac{{3g}}{{2l}} = \frac{{30}}{{2 \times 1.6}} = 9.38rad/{s^2}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
Tags.Topic: Dynamics of Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis

Question: Three identical charges are placed at the corners of an equilateral triangle. If the force between any two charges is F, then the net force on each will be
Solution:
Net force on each charge will be
$$\eqalign{
  & \,\,\,\,\,{F_{net}} = \sqrt {{F^2} + {F^2} + 2{F^2}\,\,\cos \,\,{{60}^o}} \cr
  & \Rightarrow {F_{net}} = \sqrt {2{F^2} + 2{F^2} \times \frac{1}{2}} \cr
  & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \sqrt {2{F^2} + {F^2}} \cr
  & \Rightarrow {F_{net}} = \sqrt {3{F^2}} = \sqrt 3 F \cr} $$

Question: An apple gives 21kJ energy to a boy. How much height he can climb by using this energy. If his efficiency is 28% ? (mass of boy 40kg)
Solution:
Here Energy of one apple = 21kj
= \[21 \times {10^3}J\]
Efficiency of the boy \[ = 28\% = 0.28\]
Mass of the boy \[m = 40kg\]
Here the actual energy consumed by the boy is given as \[ = 0.28 \times 21000 = 5880J\] ...(1)
and the energy consumed by the boy in climbing h meter height is given by
\[ = m\,g\,h = 40 \times 9.8 \times h\,J\] ….(2)
Equating equations (1) and (2) we get
\[40 \times 9.8 \times h = 5880J\]
\[h = \frac{{5880}}{{40 \times 9.8}} = 15m\]

Question: Consider two containers A and B containing identical gases at the same pressure, volume, and temperature. The gas in container A is compressed to half of its original volume isothermally while the gas in container B is compressed to half of its original value adiabatically. The ratio of final pressure of gas in B to that of gas in A is:
Solution:
For isothermal compression of gas, A
PiVi = PfVf

\[ \Rightarrow {{\text{P}}_{\text{f}}} = \frac{{{{\text{P}}_{\text{f}}}{{\text{V}}_{\text{f}}}}}{{\left( {\frac{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{i}}}}}{2}} \right)}} = {\text{2}}{{\text{P}}_{\text{i}}}\]

For adiabatic compression of gas B

\[{P_i}V_i^\gamma = {P_f}V_f^\gamma \]

\[ \Rightarrow {P_f} = \frac{{{P_f}V_i^\gamma }}{{{{\left( {\frac{{{V_i}}}{2}} \right)}^\gamma }}} = {2^\gamma }{P_i}\]

Hence, \[\frac{{{{\left( {{P_f}} \right)}_B}}}{{{{\left( {{P_f}} \right)}_A}}} = \frac{{{2^\gamma }{P_i}}}{{2{P_i}}}\]

= \[{2^{\gamma -1}}\]

Tags.Topic: Kinetic Theory of an Ideal Gas

Question: In step-up transformer, relation between number of turns in primary (N<sub>p</sub>) and number of turns in secondary (N<sub>s</sub>) coils is
Solution:
In step-up transformer, number of turns in primary coil is less than the number of turns in secondary coil.
i.e., \[\frac{{{N_s}}}{{{N_p}}} > 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,{N_s} > {N_p}\]

Question:
The mass of proton is 1.0073 u and that of neutron is 1.0087 u (u = atomic mass unit) The binding energy of 2He4 is (mass of helium nucleus = 4.0015 u)
Given, mass of helium nucleus 4.0015 u.
Solution:
Mass of proton, ${m_p} = 1.0073{\text{ u}}$, and mass of the neutron, ${m_n} = 1.0087{\text{ u}}$
Mass of the nucleus, $m = 4.0015{\text{ u}}$
Now, the total mass of ${}_2^4He$is,
$\Delta m = \left[ {2\left( {{m_p} + {m_n}} \right) - m} \right]$ because helium has two neutrons and two protons.
Therefore,
$\Delta m = \left[ {2\left( {1.0073 + 1.0087} \right) - 4.0015} \right] = 0.0305$
Now, the binding energy of Helium is,
$BE = 931\Delta m = 931 \times 0.0305$
(where,$1amu = u = 931{\text{ MeV}}$)
$\therefore BE = 28.3955{\text{ MeV}}$

Question: Given that the surface tension of soap solution is 30 × 10–3 N/m. The diameter of a soap bubble increases from 1 mm to 5 mm. The work done is :
Solution:
In case of liquid, work done is,
$$W = T \times 2 \times \left( {{\text{change in area}}} \right)$$

$$W = 30 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 2 \times \left( {4\pi r_2^2 - 4\pi r_1^2} \right)$$

$$W = 30 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 2 \times 4\pi \left( {{{2.5}^2} - {{0.5}^2}} \right) \times {10^{ - 6}}$$

$$W = 4.521 \times {10^{ - 6}}{\text{ }}J\, = 4.5 \times {10^{ - 6}}J$$

Question: The current gain of a transistor in common base mode is 0.995. The current gain of the same transistor in common emitter mode is :
Solution:
Current gain in the common emitter mode,
$\begin{gathered}
   = \frac{\alpha }{{1 - \alpha }} \\
   = \frac{{0.995}}{{1 - 0.995}} \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{{0.995}}{{0.005}} = 199 \\
\end{gathered} $

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: Semiconductor Electronics : Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits

Question:
Assertion: We generally used concave poles in a moving coil galvanometer.
Reason: Plane of coil always remains parallel to the direction of the magnetic field.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: We generally used concave poles, in a moving coil galvanometer to produce radial magnetic field. Plane of coil always remains parallel to the direction of the magnetic field. In this case, maximum and constant torque acts on coil which is the principle of galvanometer.

Question: The largest mass (m) that can be moved by a flowing river depends on velocity (v), density $\left( \rho  \right)$ of river water and acceleration due to gravity (g). The correct relation is
Solution:
Given, m = mass = [M], v = velocity $ = \left[ {L{T^{ - 1}}} \right],\rho = $density $ = \left[ {M{L^{ - 3}}} \right],g = $acceleration due to gravity $ = \left[ {L{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$
By substituting, the dimension of each quantity we can check the accuracy of the formula
$m = K\frac{{\rho {v^6}}}{{{g^3}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \left[ M \right] = \frac{{\left[ {M{L^{ - 3}}} \right]{{\left[ {L{T^{ - 1}}} \right]}^6}}}{{{{\left[ {L{T^{ - 2}}} \right]}^3}}}$
$ = \left[ M \right]$
L.H.S = R.H.S. i.e., the above formula is correct.

Question: A rubber band of length \[\ell \] has a stone of mass m tied to its one end. It is whirled with speed v so that the stone describes a horizontal circular path. The tension T in the rubber band is –
Solution:
On moving length of the rubber band increases.

∴ Let new length \[ = \ell + \Delta \ell \]
\[\therefore T = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{{\ell + \Delta \ell }}\]
As, \[\ell + \Delta \ell > \ell \,\,and\,\,T \propto \frac{\ell }{{length}}\]
So, \[{T_{\ell + \Delta \ell }} < {T_\ell }\]
\[\therefore T < \frac{{m{v^2}}}{\ell }\]

Question: Average power in a L-C-R circuit depends upon
Solution:
The average power of an L-C-R circuit,
Pav = Vrms irms = cosφ
Hence, the average power depends upon current, emf and phase difference.

Question:
Assertion : Two stones are simultaneously projected from level ground from the same point with same speeds but different angles horizontally. Both stones move in the same vertical plane. Then the two stones may collide in mid-air.
Reason : For two stones projected simultaneously from the same point with same speed at different angles horizontally, their trajectories may intersect at some point.
Options: Assertion is True, Reason is True; Reason is a correct explanation for Assertion
Assertion is True, Reason is True; Reason is NOT a correct explanation for Assertion
Assertion is True, Reason is False
Both Assertion and Reason are False

Question:
Assertion. When the space between the metallic plates is completely filled with conducting slab the capacitance of parallel plate capacitor becomes infinite.
Reason. If electric field inside a conductor is zero, then this results potential difference between the two plates become zero.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Since capacity is given by charge per unit potential difference. Now potential difference between the plates of conductor is zero so capacity becomes infinite.

Question: Four capacitors of equal capacitance have an equivalent capacitance C<sub>1</sub> when connected in series and an equivalent capacitance C<sub>2</sub> when connected in parallel. The ratio $$\frac{{{C_1}}}{{{C_2}}}$$ is
Solution:
For series combination of capacitance is given by
$$\frac{1}{{{C_1}}} = \frac{1}{C} + \frac{1}{C} + \frac{1}{C} + \frac{1}{C}$$
$$\frac{1}{{{C_1}}} = \frac{4}{C}$$
$$ \Rightarrow \,\,{C_1} = \frac{C}{4}$$
Now, parallel combination of capacitances is given by
$${C_2} = C + C + C + C = 4C$$
Now, $$\frac{{{C_1}}}{{{C_2}}} = \frac{C}{4} \times \frac{1}{{4C}} = \frac{1}{{16}}$$

Question: Given $\theta $ is the angle between \[\vec A\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec B\]. Then is \[|\hat A \times \hat B|\]equal to
Solution:
\[\left| {\overrightarrow A \times \overrightarrow B } \right| = AB\sin \theta \]

\[\left| {\hat A \times \hat B} \right| = \left( 1 \right)\left( 1 \right)\sin \theta = \sin \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {\because \,\,\,\left| {\hat A} \right| = \left| {\hat B} \right| = 1} \right)\]

Question: Maximum horizontal range of a projectile is 100 m. The maximum height attained by it will be :
Solution:
When $\theta = 45^\circ $ then range will be maximum,

${R_{\max }} = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 2\theta }}{g}$

${R_{\max }} = \frac{{{u^2}\sin \left( {90^\circ } \right)}}{g}$

${R_{\max }} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{g}$

$ = 100\,m$

${H_{\max }} = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{2g}}$

$ = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\left( {45^\circ } \right)}}{{2g}}$

$ = \frac{{{u^2}}}{g} \times \frac{{{{\sin }^2}\left( {45^\circ } \right)}}{2}$

$ = \frac{{{u^2}}}{g} \times \frac{1}{4}$

$ = {R_{\max }} \times \frac{1}{4}$

$ = \frac{{100}}{4}$

$ = 25\,m$

In above equation, we replaced ${R_{\max }}$ in place of $\frac{{{u^2}}}{g}$.

Question: A river is flowing west to east with a speed of 5 m min<sup>-1</sup>. A man can swim in still water with a velocity 10 m min<sup>-1</sup>. In which direction should the man swim so as to take the shortest possible path to go to the south?
Solution:
Let the swimmer swims at an angle \[\theta \] with the vertical.
From figure, \[\sin \theta = \frac{{{v_r}}}{{{v_m}}}\]


where, vr is velocity of river vm is velocity of man.

Given, \[{v_r} = 5m\,{\min ^{ - 1}}\]

\[{v_m} = 10m\,{\min ^{ - 1}}\]

\[\therefore \sin \theta = \frac{5}{{10}} = \frac{1}{2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \theta = 30^\circ \]

The component \[10\sin \theta \]swimmer’s velocity will cancel the velocity of river and the swimmer takes the shortest distance to the south. Therefore, direction is 30o east of south.

Question: A thin uniform circular disc of mass M and radius R is rotating in a horizontal plane about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to the plane with angular velocity ω. Another disc of same mass but half the radius is gently placed over it coaxially. The angular speed of the composite disc will be :
Solution:
Moment of inertia of rotating disc, \[{I_1} = \frac{1}{2}M{R^2}\]
Moment of inertia of second disc,

\[{I_2} = \frac{1}{2}M{\left( {\frac{R}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{{M{R^2}}}{8}\]

Taking both the disc as a system \[{\overrightarrow \tau _{ext}} = 0\]

\[\therefore {\overrightarrow L _i} = {\overrightarrow L _f}\]

\[\therefore {I_i}{\omega _i} = \left( {{I_1} + {I_2}} \right){\omega _{new}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{M{R^2}}}{2}\omega = \frac{{M{R^2}}}{2}\left\{ {{\omega _{new}}} \right\}\left( {1 + \frac{1}{4}} \right)\]

\[ \Rightarrow {\omega _{new}} = \frac{4}{5}\omega \]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
Tags.Topic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis

Question: A body of mass $ m $ is moving towards east and another body of equal mass is moving towards north. If after collision both stick together, their speed after collision should be
Solution:
Let the two bodies are moving with same velocity along X-axis and Y-axis, then momentum of the system before collision is $ {P_i} = mv\,\,\hat i + mv\,\hat j $
Let the system moves with velocity $ v' $ after collision, then final momentum of the system is
 $ {P_f} = {\text{ }}2{\text{ }}mv' $ $ \ldots {\text{ }}\left( {\text{2}} \right) $
According to law of conservation of momentum, $ {P_i} = {\text{ }}{P_f} $
 $ \Rightarrow \,2mv' = m|v\hat i + v\hat j| $
 $ \Rightarrow \,\,2mv' = m\sqrt {{v^2} + {v^2}} $
 $ \left[ {\because \,\,|v\hat i + v\hat j| = \sqrt {{v^2} + {v^2}} } \right] $
 $ \Rightarrow \,\,\,v'\, = \frac{{\sqrt 2 v}}{2} = \frac{v}{{\sqrt 2 }} $

Question: P and Q are two identical masses at rest suspended by an inextensible string passing over a smooth frictionless pulley. Mass P is given a downward push with a speed v as shown in figure. It collides with the floor and rebounds. What happens immediately after collision?

P and Q both move upwards with different speeds
P moves upwards & Q moves downwards with equal speed

Question: The nuclei of which one of the following pairs of nuclei are isotones?
Solution:
The nuclei which have the same number of neutrons but the different atomic number and mass number are called Isotones.
Now, according to question;
${}_{34}^{74}Se \Leftrightarrow N = A - Z = 74 - 34 = 40,$
${}_{31}^{71}Ga \Leftrightarrow N = 71 - 31 = 40$
${}_{42}^{92}Mo \Leftrightarrow N = 92 - 42 = 50,$
${}_{40}^{92}Zr \Leftrightarrow N = 92 - 40 = 52$
${}_{38}^{84}Sr \Leftrightarrow N = 84 - 38 = 46,$
${}_{38}^{86}Sr \Leftrightarrow N = 86 - 38 = 48$
${}_{20}^{40}Ca \Leftrightarrow N = 40 - 20 = 20,$
${}_{16}^{32}S \Leftrightarrow N = 32 - 16 = 16$
Therefore, ${}_{34}^{74}Se,{}_{31}^{71}Ga$ have the same number of neutrons, so this pair of nuclei is Isotones.

Tags.Topic: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus

Question: Perfect gas is one whose molecules
Options: Strongly attract or repel one another
Neither attract or repel one another

Question: If \[\left[ {\frac{{\sqrt {\left( {5x + 3} \right)}  + \sqrt {\left( {5x - 3} \right)} }}{{\sqrt {\left( {5x + 3} \right)}  - \sqrt {\left( {5x - 3} \right)} }}} \right] = \frac{5}{3},\] then find the value of 25x.
Solution:
\[\left[ {\frac{{\sqrt {\left( {5x + 3} \right)} + \sqrt {\left( {5x - 3} \right)} }}{{\sqrt {\left( {5x + 3} \right)} - \sqrt {\left( {5x - 3} \right)} }}} \right] = \frac{5}{3}\]

Applying componendo and dividendo we get

\[\frac{{\left\{ {2\sqrt {\left( {5x + 3} \right)} } \right\}}}{{\left\{ {2\sqrt {\left( {5x - 3} \right)} } \right\}}} = \frac{{\left( {5 + 3} \right)}}{{\left( {5 - 3} \right)}}\]

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{\sqrt {\left( {5x + 3} \right)} }}{{\sqrt {\left( {5x - 3} \right)} }} = \frac{8}{2} = 4$

Squaring both side we get

$\frac{{\left( {5x + 3} \right)}}{{\left( {5x - 3} \right)}} = 16$

Again applying componendo and dividendo we get

$\frac{{10x}}{6} = \frac{{17}}{{15}}$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,x = \frac{{17}}{{25}}$

$\therefore \,\,\,25x = 17$

Question: The electric potential at any point x, y, z in metres is given by V = 3x2. The electric field at a point (2, 0, 1) is
Doubly ionised helium atoms and hydrogen ions are accelerated from rest through the same potential drop. The ratio of the final velocities of the helium and the hydrogen ions is
Solution:
Let, Mass of Hydrogen ion, mH, and mass of Helium ion, mHe = 4mH
Let, charge on Hydrogen ion, qH, and Helium ion qHe = 2qH
Therefore, the kinetic energy of the particle,
$K.E = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = qV$
$\therefore v \propto \sqrt {\frac{q}{m}} $
Now, the ratio of the final velocities of the Helium and Hydrogen ions, $\frac{{{v_{He}}}}{{{v_H}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{q_{He}}}}{{{m_{He}}}} \cdot \frac{{{m_H}}}{{{q_H}}}} $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{v_{He}}}}{{{v_H}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{2{q_H}}}{{4{m_H}}} \cdot \frac{{{m_H}}}{{{q_H}}}} $
$\therefore \frac{{{v_{He}}}}{{{v_H}}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}$

Tags.Subject: Physics

Question: Moment of inertia of a uniform annular disc of internal radius r and external radius R and mass M about an axis through its centre and perpendicular to its plane is:
Solution:
I of given disc = I of disc of radius R - I of disc of radius r

\[{\text{I = }}\frac{1}{2}\rho \left( {\pi {R^2}} \right){R^2} - \frac{1}{2}\rho \left( {\pi {r^2}} \right){r^2}\]

Also,

\[M = \rho \left( {\pi {R^2} - \pi {r^2}} \right)\]

\[ \Rightarrow I = \frac{1}{2}\rho \pi \left\{ {{R^4} - {r^4}} \right\} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{M}{{\left( {{R^2} - {r^2}} \right)}}\left( {{R^4} - {r^4}} \right)\]

\[ = \frac{1}{2}M\left( {{R^2} + {r^2}} \right)\]

Question: The slope of velocity – time graph for motion with uniform velocity is equal to
Solution: As the velocity is constant, the acceleration will be zero, thus slope will be zero for a velocity- time graph.

Question: A simple pendulum of length $l$ has a maximum angular displacement $$\theta $$. The maximum kinetic energy of the bob of mass m will be:
Solution:
$$K{E_{\max }} = {\text{work done}}$$

$$K{E_{\max }} = mgh = mg\left( {l - l\cos \theta } \right) = mgl\left( {1 - \cos \theta } \right)$$

Question: A bullet is fired and gets embedded in a block kept on the table. If table is frictionless then :
Solution: As the collision is inelastic hence K.E. and P.E. are not conserved. But since no external force is applied to system hence momentum is conserved.

Question: The block is released from rest at the top of a frictionless inclined plane 16 m long. It reaches the bottom 4 sec later. The acceleration of the block on the incline is :
Solution:
Acceleration of both the blocks will be the same on the inclined plane
Let it be ‘a’

So, according to the equation of motion

\[\frac{1}{2} \times a \times {4^2} = 16\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,a = 2m/{s^2}\]

Question: The expression for displacement of an object in SHM is x = A cos ($$\omega $$t). The potential energy at $$t = \frac{T}{4}$$ is :

$x\, = \,A\,\cos \omega t$
$${\text{at }}t = \frac{T}{4},x = A\cos \left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{T} \times \frac{T}{4}} \right) = 0$$

Potential energy $${\text{ = }}\frac{1}{2}k{x^2} = 0$$

Question: The position of centre of mass of a system consisting of two particles of masses m<sub>1</sub> and m<sub>2</sub> separated by a distance L apart, from m<sub>1</sub> will be :
Solution:
\[{X_{cm}} = \frac{{{m_1}{x_1} + {m_2}{x_2}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {X_{cm}} = \frac{{{m_1}\left( 0 \right) + {m_2}\left( L \right)}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {X_{cm}} = \left( {\frac{{{m_2}L}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}} \right)\]

Question:
Which of these are correct?
I. In forward biasing, holes from p-side crosses junction and reach n-side.
II. In forward biasing, electrons from n-side crosses junction and reach p-side.
III. In n-side, holes are minority charge carriers.
IV. In p-side, electrons are minority charge carriers.
Solution:
\[ \bullet \to \]Electron
O\[ \to \]holes
In N-side, holes are minority charge carriers and electrons are majority charge carriers. In P side, electron are minority charge carrier and holes are majority charge carriers. In forward biasing holes go from P to n and ${e^ - }$from n to P.

Question: Two bullets are fired with horizontal velocities of 50 m/s and 100 m/s from two guns placed at a height of 19.6 m. Which bullet will strike the ground first?
Solution: Since both bullets have the same force being applied on their Y component of motion (as both are at the same height), both will strike the ground at the same time.

Question: Pendulum after some time becomes slow in motion and finally stops due to
Solution: When the body is made to oscillate, some kind of frictional force is present like friction because of air. This frictional force opposes the motion of oscillating body, when it is oscillating from equilibrium position or it returns towards the equilibrium position therefore, the energy given to the oscillating body in the initial displacement which is converted slowly but continuously into heat in doing work against friction. Because of this energy dissipation the amplitude of oscillation goes on decreasing and finally stops.

Question: A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of 20 ms<sup>-1</sup> from the top of a multi storey building. The height of the point where the ball is thrown 25 m from the ground. How high will the ball rise from the ground? Take g = 10 ms<sup>-2</sup>.
Solution:
Here

\[u = 20m{s^{ - 2}}\]

Height it will travel will be corresponding to the distance h (from the tower) when its velocity along y will become zero, that is

v = 0

We have

\[{v^2} = {u^2} - 2gh\]

\[ \Rightarrow {u^2} - 2gh = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow h = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}} = \frac{{{{\left( {20} \right)}^2}}}{{2 \times 10}} = 20m\]

Height from the ground \[ = 20 + 25 = 45m\]

Question: The surface density of charge on a conductor situated in air is 2 × 10<sup>4</sup> C/m<sup>2</sup>. The electric field intensity at a point very near to its surface is

Question: Two springs have force constants \[{k_A}\] and \[{k_B}\] such that \[{k_B} = 2{k_A}.\] The four ends of the springs are stretched by the same force. If energy stored in spring A is E, then energy stored in spring B is :
Solution:
Let E’ be the energy stored in spring B. As force is constant:
\[F = kx \Rightarrow x = \frac{F}{k}\]
\[E = \frac{1}{2}k{x^2} = \frac{1}{2}k{\left( {\frac{F}{k}} \right)^2} = \frac{{{F^2}}}{{2k}}\]
\[E \propto \frac{1}{k}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{E}{{E'}} = \frac{{{k_B}}}{{{k_A}}} \\
    \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\frac{E}{{E'}} = \frac{{2{k_A}}}{{{k_A}}}\]
\[E' = \frac{E}{2}\]

\[\left( {{{\text{V}}_{{\text{avg}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{2}}{{\text{v}}_{\text{1}}}{{\text{v}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{v}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{v}}_{\text{2}}}}}} \right)\]

Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: The rate of decrease of electric field is different in the two cases. In case of a point charge, it decreases as 1/r<sup>2</sup> but in the case of electric dipole it decreases more rapidly, as\[E \propto \frac{1}{{{r^3}}}.\]

Question: What is the value of inductance L for which the current is a maximum in a series L-C-R circuit with C = 10µF and ω = 1000 s<sup>-1</sup> ?
Options: Cannot be calculated unless R is known
Solution:
Current in L-C-R series circuit is given by
\[i = \frac{V}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {{X_L} - {X_C}} \right)}^2}} }}\]
where, V is rms value of current, R is resistance, XL is inductive reactance and XC is capacitive reactance. For current to be maximum, denominator should be minimum which can be done, if XL = XC. This happens in resonance state of the circuit,
i.e. \[\omega L = \frac{1}{{\omega C}} \Rightarrow L = \frac{1}{{{\omega ^2}C}}\]…(i)
Given, \[\omega = 1000\,{s^{ - 1}}\]
And \[C = 10\mu F = 10 \times {10^{ - 6}}F.\]
Hence,
\[L = \frac{1}{{{{\left( {1000} \right)}^2} \times 10 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = 0.1\,H = 100\,mH\]

Question:
Assertion: The graph of the intensity of electric field (E) inside a uniformly charged non-conducting sphere with the distance r from the centre of the sphere is a straight line passing through the origin.
Reason: Inside a uniformly charged non-conducting sphere, the electric field at a distance r is from the centre is given by $E\,\, = \,\,\frac{{KQ}}{{{R^3}}}r.$
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Electric field inside solid non-conducting sphere is $${E_{in}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}.\frac{{Qr}}{{{R^3}}}\, = \,\frac{{\rho r}}{{3{\varepsilon _0}}}\left\{ {{E_{in}}\, \propto \,r} \right\}.$$ So graph is a straight line.

Question:
Assertion: Path of a projectile, seen from another projectile is straight line
Reason: If relative acceleration is zero then relative velocity will be constant
Options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true and R is not correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
Solution: Yes, both the statements are correct. Path of a projectile seen from another projectile will be a straight line, as their relative acceleration is zero.

Question: Consider a non-spherical conductor shown in the figure which is given a certain amount of positive charge. The charge distributes itself on the surface such that the charge densities are σ1, σ2 and σ3 at the region 1, 2 and 3 respectively. Then
Solution:
Charge density is inversely proportional to radius of curvature, i.e.
Charge density$$ \propto \frac{1}{{{\text{Radius of curvature}}}}$$
$${R_3} > {R_1} > {R_2}$$
Therefore, as shown in figure,
$${\sigma _2} > {\sigma _1} > {\sigma _3}$$

Question: A good lubricant should have
Solution: A good lubricant should have high viscosity. The boundary of the lubrication between moving parts will be higher for a lubricant with higher viscosity.

Question: If the forward voltage in a semiconductor diode is doubled, the width of depletion layer :
Solution: Since voltage increases, current passing through depletion layer also increases. This means electrons are flowing more easily through the depletion layer. This is possible only if the depletion layer decreases in width.

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: Semiconductor Electronics : Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits

Question: A particle is given an initial speed u inside a smooth spherical shell of radius R = 1m that it is just able to complete the circle. Acceleration of the particle when its velocity is vertical is
Solution:
At highest point,

$N + mg = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}$
For minimum v, N = o
$\therefore v = \sqrt {rg} = \sqrt {10} m/s$
When its velocity is vertical
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = mg \cdot R + m \cdot {u^2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}$
$\therefore {v^2} = gR + {u^2} = 2gR + gR$
$\therefore v = \sqrt {3gR} $
$\therefore {a_r} = \frac{{{v^2}}}{R} = \frac{{3gR}}{R} = 3g$
and ${a_t} = g$
$\therefore \left| {\vec a} \right| = \sqrt {a_r^2 + a_t^2} = \sqrt {{{\left( {3g} \right)}^2} + {g^2}} $
$ = g\sqrt {10} $
Hence, (a) is correct answer

Question: If reading of an ammeter is 10 A, then the peak value of current is
Solution:
Ammeter reads the root mean square value of current (irms) which is related to the peak value of current (i0) by the relation
\[{i_{rms}} = \frac{{{i_0}}}{{\sqrt 2 }}\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,{i_0} = \sqrt 2 \times {i_{rms}}\]
\[ = \sqrt 2 \times 10A = 10\sqrt 2 \,A\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Average, Peak and RMS Value of Alternating Current/Voltage

Question: An electron (q = 1.6 ´ 10–19C) is moving at right angle to the uniform magnetic field 3.534 ´ 10–5T. The time taken by the electron to complete a circular orbit is
Solution:
$T = \frac{{2\pi r}}{v}$ and $r = \frac{{mv}}{{qB}}$
$ = \frac{{2\pi m}}{{qB}} = \frac{{2\pi  \times 9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 3.5 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} = 1\mu s$

Question: In L-C-R series A.C. circuit, the phase angle between current and voltage is
Solution:
In L.C.R series AC circuit the phase angle between
current and the voltage is,
 \[{V_L} - {V_C} = i\left( {{X_L} - {X_C}} \right),\,\tan \phi = \frac{{{X_L} - {X_C}}}{R}\]
If \[{V_L} > {V_C}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 0 < \phi < \frac{\pi }{2}\]
If \[{V_L} < {V_C}\]
\[ \Rightarrow - \frac{\pi }{2} < \phi < 0\]
\[\therefore \left| \phi \right| \in \left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]\]

Question: In a parallel plate capacitor, the separation between the plates is 3 mm with air between them. Now, 1 mm thick layer of a material of dielectric constant 2 is introduced between the plates due to which the capacity increases. In order to bring its capacity of the original value, the separation between the plates must be made
Solution:
Dielectric constant,
$$C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{(d - t) + \frac{t}{k}}}$$
$$\frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{3} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{d - 1 + \frac{1}{2}}}$$
$$ \Rightarrow \,\,d = 3.5\,mm$$

Question: Half-life period of a radioactive substance is 6 h. After 24 h activity is 0.01 μC, what was the initial activity?
Solution:
The half-decay time, $n = \frac{t}{T}$
$ \Rightarrow n = \frac{{24}}{6} = 4$
Now, the half-decay activity of the radioactivity
substance, $\frac{A}{{{A_0}}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^n}$
Where, final decay activity is, $A = 0.01{\text{ }}\mu {\text{C}}$
Therefore, $0.01 = {A_0}{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^4}$
$\therefore {A_0} = 0.16{\text{ }}\mu {\text{C}}$

Question: In thermodynamic process which of the following statements is not true?
Options: In an adiabatic process the system is insulated from the surroundings
In an isochoric process pressure remains constant
In an isothermal process the temperature remains constant
Solution:
In an adiabatic process the system is insulated from its surroundings.
In an isochoric process volume remains constant.
In an isothermal process the temperature remains constant.
In an adiabatic process\[P{V^\gamma }\]is made constant.

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Kinetic Theory of an Ideal Gas

Question: The power factor in a circuit connected to an AC power supply has a value which is
Options: unity when the circuit contains only inductance
unity when the circuit contains only resistance
zero when the circuit contains an ideal resistance only
unity when the circuit contains an ideal capacitance only
Solution:
Power factor, \[\cos \phi = \frac{R}{Z}\]
When circuit contains only resistance,
Then Z = R
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\cos \phi = \frac{R}{R} = 1\]

Question: If \[\overrightarrow A  \cdot \overrightarrow B  = AB,\] then the angle between \[\vec A\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec B\] is
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow A \cdot \overrightarrow B = AB\cos \theta \]

(θ = angle between A and B)

\[ \Rightarrow AB\,\cos \theta = AB \Rightarrow \cos \theta = 1\]

\[ \Rightarrow \theta = 0^\circ \]

Question: The motion of a particle along a straight line is described by equation: x = 8 + 12t – t<sup>3</sup> where x is in metre and t in second. The magnitude of the acceleration when its velocity becomes zero, is
Solution:
\[x = 8 + 12t - {t^3}\]
\[v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 12 - 3{t^2}\]

\[v = 0\]

\[12 - 3{t^2} = 0\]

\[t = 2s\]

\[a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = - 6t\]

\[a = - 6 \times 2 = - 12\]

Retardation is \[12{\text{ m}}{{\text{s}}^{{\text{ - 2}}}}\]

Question: In a two block system an initial velocity v0 with respect to ground is given to block A :
Options: the momentum of block A is not conserved
the momentum of system of block A and B is conserved
the increase in momentum of B is equal to the decrease in momentum of block A
Solution:
Clearly net force on the complete system=0

\[{\vec F_{system}} = 0\]

\[\therefore {\vec P_{system}} = {\text{conserved}}\]

\[{\vec P_A} \ne {\text{conserved}}\]

Because on A net external force is not zero.

Question: A winding wire which is used to frame a solenoid can bear a maximum 10 A current. If length of solenoid is 80 cm and its cross-sectional radius is 3 cm, then required length of winding wire is (Take, B = 0.2 T)
Solution:
$$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}Ni}}{l},\,\,{\text{where N = total number}}$$of turns, l = length of the solenoid.
\[ \Rightarrow 0.2 = \frac{{4\pi  \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times N \times 10}}{{0.8}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow N = \frac{{4 \times {{10}^4}}}{\pi }\]

Since, N turns are made from the winding wire, so length of the wire
\[\left( L \right) = 2\pi r \times N\]
\[[\because \,\,2\pi r = \,\,{\text{length of each turns]}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,L\, = \,2\pi  \times 3 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times \frac{{4 \times {{10}^4}}}{\pi } = 2.4 \times {10^3}m\]

Question: A cyclotron can accelerate
Solution: Cyclotron can accelerate α - particles (positive charge particle).

Question: A projectile is projected with initial velocity\[(6\hat i + 8\hat j)m/\sec .\] If g = 10 ms–2, then horizontal range is
Solution:
\[u\cos \theta = 6,\;\;\;{\kern 1pt} u\sin \theta = 8\]

\[R = \frac{{2(u\cos \theta )\cdot(u\sin \theta )}}{g} = \frac{{2 \times 6 \times 8}}{{10}} = 9.6\;m\]

Question: A particle of mass 1 mg has the same wavelength as an electron moving with a velocity of 3 × 10<sup>6</sup> ms<sup>–1</sup>. The velocity of the particle is (mass of electron = 9.1 × 10<sup>–31</sup> kg)
Solution:
The wavelength of the particle$ = $ the wavelength of an electron Therefore, ${\lambda _{particle}} = {\lambda _{electron}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{h}{{{m_p}{v_p}}} = \frac{h}{{{m_e}{v_e}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {m_p}{v_p} = {m_e}{v_e}$
Now, the velocity of the particle, ${v_p} = \frac{{{m_e}{v_e}}}{{{m_p}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_p} = \frac{{9.31 \times {{10}^{ - 31}} \times 3 \times {{10}^6}}}{{{{10}^{ - 6}}}}$
$\therefore {v_p} = 2.7 \times {10^{ - 18}}{\text{ m/s}}$

Question: A n-p-n transistor power amplifier in CE configuration gives
Solution:
npn transistor amplifier gives both current and voltage amplification
Current gain $ = \frac{{\Delta {i_c}}}{{\Delta {i_b}}}$
Voltage gain $ = \beta \times \frac{{{R_0}}}{{{R_{in}}}}$

Question: Which of the following is not a perfectly inelastic collision ?
Options: A man jumps on a cart
A bullet embedded in a block
Solution: Since two glass balls colliding will lead to both breaking apart, they do not form one single body. Hence the collision is not perfectly inelastic.

Question: Volume of gas become four times if
Options: Temperature become four times at constant pressure
Temperature become one fourth at constant pressure
Temperature becomes two times at constant pressure
Temperature becomes half at constant pressure
Solution: $V \propto T$(as constant pressure)

Tags.Topic: Kinetic Theory of an Ideal Gas

Question:
A charged particle with velocity $$v = x\hat i + y\hat j$$ moves in a magnetic field $$B = y\hat i + x\hat j.$$ Magnitude of the force acting on the particle is F. The correct option for F is
I. No force will act on particle, if x = y

II. Force will act along Y-axis, if y < x

III. Force is proportional to $$\left( {{x^2} - {y^2}} \right),\,if\,x > y$$

IV. Force is proportional to $$\left( {{x^2} + {y^2}} \right),\,if\,y > x$$
Solution:
\[v = x\hat i + y\hat j,B = y\hat i + x\hat j\]

\[As,\,\,F = q\left( {v \times B} \right)\]

\[F = q\left[ {\left( {x\hat i + y\hat j} \right) \times \left( {y\hat i + x\hat j} \right)} \right]\]

\[ = q\left[ {{x^2}\left( {\hat k} \right) - {y^2}\left( {\hat k} \right)} \right]\]

\[ = q\left( {{x^2} - {y^2}} \right)\hat k\]

If \[x = y \Rightarrow F = 0\,\]

Also, \[x > y\]

\[ \Rightarrow F \propto \left( {{x^2} - {y^2}} \right)\]

Question: A 500 mC charge is at the centre of a square of side 10 cm. Find the work done in moving the charge of 10 μC between two diagonally opposite points on the square.
Solution:
The points A and B are equidistant from the centre of square where charge q = 500 μC is located ; therefore, points A and B are at the same potential i.e.,
VA = VB. Work done in moving charge q0 = 10mC from A to B is W = q0 (VB - VA) = 0

Question: Which of the following will have the dimensions of time
Solution:
We know that \[\left[ V \right] = \left[ {iR} \right] = \left[ {L\frac{{di}}{{dt}}} \right]\]

or \[\left[ i \right]\left[ R \right] = \frac{{\left[ L \right]\left[ i \right]}}{{\left[ t \right]}}\]

Thus, \[\left[ {\frac{L}{R}} \right] = \left[ t \right] = T\]

Question: The magnitude of the centripetal force acting on a body of mass m executing uniform motion in a circle of radius r with speed v is
Solution:
\[F = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right]\]
This force depends upon mass, velocity, and radius of circular path
Mass = [M]
Velocity = [LT-1]
radius = [L]
\[F = \left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right] = k{\left[ M \right]^a}{\left[ {L{T^{ - 1}}} \right]^b}{\left[ L \right]^c}\]
On comparing the powers on both sides
a = 1
b = 2
c = -1
\[F = k{\left[ M \right]^1}{\left[ {L{T^{ - 1}}} \right]^2}{\left[ L \right]^{ - 1}}\]
\[F = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}\]

Question: Which of the following velocity-time graphs shows a realistic situation for a body in motion?
Solution:
In real situations, a body can’t have more than one velocity at a particular time. To find the correct velocity-time graph, we draw a vertical line parallel to the velocity axis and eliminate the options in which the vertical line cuts the graph more than once. Time factor can also be taken into consideration. Time cannot go back.
Thus option (b) is correct in which a particle can’t have multiple velocities at any given instance.

Question: Taking into account the significant figures, what is the value of 9.99 m + 0.0099 m
Solution: \[9.99\;m + 0.0099\;m = 9.9999\;m = 10.00\;m\] (In proper significant figures).

Question: If the present units of length, time and mass (m, s, kg) are changed to 100m, 100s and \[\frac{1}{{10}}kg\] then
Options: The new unit of velocity is increased 10 times
The new unit of force is decreased \[\frac{1}{{1000}}\] times
The new unit of energy is increased 10 times
The new unit of pressure is increased 1000 times
Solution:
Unit of velocity = m/sec; in new system

\[ = \frac{{100\;m}}{{100\;\sec }} = \frac{m}{{\sec }}\left( {{\text{same}}} \right)\]

Unit of force \[ = \frac{{kg \times m}}{{{{\sec }^2}}};\] in new system

\[ = \frac{1}{{10}}kg \times \frac{{100\;m}}{{100\;\sec \times 100\sec }} = \frac{1}{{1000}}\frac{{kg \times m}}{{{{\sec }^2}}}\]

Unit of energy \[ = \frac{{kg \times {m^2}}}{{{{\sec }^2}}};\] in new system

\[ = \frac{1}{{10}}kg \times \frac{{100\;m \times 100\;m}}{{100\;\sec \times 100\;\sec }} = \frac{1}{{10}}\frac{{kg \times {m^2}}}{{{{\sec }^2}}}\]

Unit of pressure \[ = \frac{{kg}}{{m \times {{\sec }^2}}};\] in new system

\[ = \frac{1}{{10}}kg \times \frac{1}{{100\;m}} \times \frac{1}{{100\;\sec \times 100\;\sec }} = {10^{ - 7}}\frac{{kg}}{{m \times {{\sec }^2}}}\]

Question: A ball is dropped from a bridge 10 m high. After the first ball has fallen for 1 s, a second ball is thrown straight down after it, what must the initial velocity of the second ball be, so that both the balls hit the surface on water at the same time? (Use g = 9.8 m/s<sup>–2</sup>)
Solution:
Height of the bridge from river, h = 10 m
Time taken by first ball to hit the river:

$ \Rightarrow 10 = \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}$

$ \Rightarrow t = \sqrt {\frac{{2 \times 10}}{{9.8}}} = 1.5{\text{ }}s$

Both the balls hit the river at the same time, and the second ball is released with some initial velocity u after 1 sec.

Thus, second ball will take time,$t' = t - 1 = 0.5{\text{ }}s$

$ \Rightarrow 10 = u \times 0.5 + \frac{1}{2}g{(0.5)^2}$

$ \Rightarrow 10 = 0.5u + 1.2$

$ \Rightarrow u = 17.6{\text{ }}m/s$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Kinematic Equations for Uniformly Accelerated Motion

Question: A period of oscillation of a simple pendulum is T in a stationary lift. If the lift moves upwards with acceleration of 8g, the period will :
Solution:
$$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{\ell }{{g + a}}} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{\ell }{{g + 8g}}} = \left( {2\pi \sqrt {\frac{\ell }{g}} } \right)\frac{1}{3} = \frac{T}{3}$$
$${\text{Also}},\,\,T \propto \sqrt {{{\frac{1}{g}}_{eff}}} \,\,\, \Rightarrow {\text{If }}{g_{eff}}{\text{ increases, T decreases}}$$

Question: A body moves over one fourth of a circular arc in a circle of radius R. The distance travelled will be respectively:
Solution: Distance = arc \[AB = \frac{{2\pi R}}{4} = \frac{{\pi R}}{2}\]

Question: A proton is about 1840 times heavier than an electron. When it is accelerated by a potential difference of 1 kV, its kinetic energy will be:
Solution: $$KE = $$Relative charge $$ \times $$ accelerating potential $$ = 1e \times 1kV = $$$$1\,keV$$

Question:
Assertion : The velocity of a body at the bottom of an inclined plane of given height is more when it slides down the plane, compared to, when it is rolling down the same plane.
Reason : In rolling down a body acquires both kinetic energy of translation and rotation.

(A) If Statement–I is true, Statement–II is true; Statement–II is the correct explanation for Statement–I.

(B) If Statement–I is true, Statement–II is true; Statement–II is not a correct explanation for Statement–I.

(C) If Statement–I is true; Statement–II is false.

(D) If Statement–I is false; Statement–II is true.
Solution: The whole potential energy is transformed into kinetic energy by sliding down. When rolling down, some component of the potential energy is converted into rotational kinetic energy. The obtained linear velocity is thus smaller.

Tags.Chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
Tags.Topic: Dynamics of Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis

Question:
Consider the following statements and select the incorrect statements.
I. The magnitude of instantaneous velocity of a particle is equal to its instantaneous speed.

II. The magnitude of the average velocity in an interval is equal to its average speed in that interval.

III. It is possible to have a situation in which the speed of the particle is never zero but the average speed in an interval is zero.

IV. It is possible to have a situation in which the instantaneous speed of the particle is zero but the average speed is not zero.
Solution: The magnitude of average velocity is not always equal to average speed, it's a special case. Both speed and average speed can never be zero.

Question: The variation of the acceleration a of the particle executing S.H.M. with displacement x is as shown in the figure.
Solution:
$\overrightarrow a = - {\omega ^2}\overrightarrow x $
$\overrightarrow a \propto - \overrightarrow x $

Question: Four bodies of masses 2,3,5 and 8 kg are placed at the four corners of a square of side 2 m. The position of CM will be:
Solution:
Com from the mass 2 kg

\[{X_{cm}} = \frac{{2(0) + 2(3) + 5(2) + 8(0)}}{{2 + 3 + 5 + 8}}\]

\[{X_{cm}} = \frac{{16}}{{18}} = \frac{8}{9}\]

\[{y_{cm}} = \frac{{2(0) + 3(0) + 5(2) + 8(2)}}{{18}}\]

\[{y_{cm}} = \frac{{26}}{{18}} = \frac{{13}}{9}\]

∴ Co-ordinate of Com from all the corner will be \[\left( {\frac{8}{9},\;\frac{{13}}{9}} \right)\]

Question: Water is flowing through two horizontal pipes of different diameters which are connected together. The diameter of the two pipes are 3 cm and 6 cm respectively. If the speed of water in the narrower tube is 4 m/s. Then, the speed of water in the wider tube is :
Solution:
$${\text{Radius of tube 1 }}\,\,{R_1} = \left( {\frac{3}{2}} \right)cm$$
$${\text{Radius of tube 2 }}\,\,{R_2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3cm$$

Acc. to equation of continuity

$${A_1}{v_1} = {A_2}{v_2}$$

$$\pi {R_1}^2{v_1} = \pi {R_2}^2{v_2}$$

$${\left( {\frac{3}{2}} \right)^2} \times 4 = {\left( 3 \right)^2} \times {v_2}$$

$${v_2} = 1{\text{ }}m/s$$

Question: What is the angular momentum of a body whose rotational kinetic energy is 10 J, if the angular momentum vector coincides with the axis of rotation and its moment of inertia is 8 g–cm<sup>2</sup>:
Solution:
\[K.E = \frac{{{L^{^2}}}}{{2I}}\]
\[{L^{^2}} = 10 \times 2 \times 8 \times {10^{ - 7}}\]

\[L = 4 \times {10^{ - 3}}kg - {m^2}/s\]

Energy required to break one bond in DNA is approximately:

Question: Which of the following shows greenhouse effect?

Question: In L-C-R resonant circuit, what is the phase angle φ ?
Solution:
In an L-C-R circuit, phase angle is related as \[\cos \phi = \frac{R}{Z}\]
Where, \[Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {{X_L} - {X_C}} \right)}^2}} \]
When circuit is resonant,
\[{X_L} = {X_C}\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\,Z = R\]
So, \[\cos \phi = \frac{R}{R} = 1,\,\,\phi = 0^\circ \]

Question: From a certain height, two bodies are projected horizontally with velocities 10 m/s and 20 m/s. They hit the ground in t<sub>1</sub> and t<sub>2</sub> seconds. Then
Solution:
\[h = {u_y}t + \frac{1}{2}g{t^2},{u_y} < 0\]
\[{\text{t}} = \sqrt {\frac{{2\;{\text{h}}}}{{\;{\text{g}}}}} \] (same h for both)

\[\therefore {{\text{t}}_1} = {{\text{t}}_2}\]

Question: A container of height 1 m is filled with water. Find the gauge pressure difference between points A & B:

We know that pressure difference,
$$\Delta P = \rho gh$$

$$\Delta P = {10^3} \times 10 \times 1$$

$$\Delta P = 10000{\text{ }}Pa$$

Question:
Consider the following statements about the blocks shown in the diagram that are being pushed by a constant force on a frictionless table

A. All blocks move with the same acceleration

Question: A body of mass 15 kg is dropped into the water. If the apparent weight of the body is 107 N. Then the applied thrust is: (g = 9.8 ms-2)

Weight - apparent weight = upthrust

$$147 - 107 = 40N.$$

Question: Parallel plate capacitor is constructed using three different dielectric materials as shown in the figure. The parallel plates, across which a potential difference is applied of area A m2 and separated by a distance d metre. The capacitance across A and B is
Solution:
Here, $${C_2} = \frac{{{K_2}{\varepsilon _0}{A_2}}}{{d/2}},$$
$${C_2} = \frac{{{K_2}{\varepsilon _0}A/2}}{{d/2}} = \frac{{{K_2}{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}$$
$${C_3} = \frac{{{K_3}{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}$$
Now, $$\frac{1}{{{C_{2 - 3}}}} = \frac{d}{{{K_2}{\varepsilon _0}A}} + \frac{d}{{{K_3}{\varepsilon _0}A}}$$
 $$ = \frac{{d{K_3} + d{K_2}}}{{{K_2}{K_3}{\varepsilon _0}A}} = \frac{{d\left( {{K_2} + {K_3}} \right)}}{{{K_2}{K_3}{\varepsilon _0}A}}$$
$${C_{2 - 3}} = \frac{{{K_2}{K_3}{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{d\left( {{K_2} + {K_3}} \right)}}$$
Again, $${C_1} = \frac{{{K_1}{\varepsilon _0}A/2}}{d} = \frac{{{K_1}{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{2d}}$$
Now combining C1 and C2-3,
$${C_1} + {C_{2 - 3}} = \frac{{{K_1}{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{2d}} + \frac{{{K_2}{K_3}{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{d\left( {{K_2} + {K_3}} \right)}}\, = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}\left( {\frac{{{K_1}}}{2} + \frac{{{K_2}{K_3}}}{{{K_2} + {K_3}}}} \right)$$

Question: A circular turn table of radius 0.5 m has a smooth diametrical groove as shown in fig. A ball of mass 90 g is placed inside the groove along with a spring of spring constant 102 N/cm. One end of the spring is tied to the edge and other end to the ball. The ball is at a distance of 0.1 m from the centre when the turn table is at rest. On rotating the turn table with a constant angular velocity of 102 rad-sec–1 the ball moves away from the initial position by a distance nearly equal to-
Solution:
Centripetal force = kx
\[\therefore m{\omega ^2}r = kx\]
\[\therefore \frac{{90}}{{1000}} \times {10^2} \times {10^2}\left( {0.1 + x} \right) = {10^2} \times {10^2} \times x\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{9}{{1000}} + \frac{{9x}}{{100}} = x\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{9}{{1000}} = x - \frac{{9x}}{{100}} \Rightarrow x = \frac{{0.9}}{{91}} \approx {10^{ - 2}}m\]

Question: An AC source of frequency 50 Hz is connected in series to an inductance of 0.5 H and resistance of 157Ω. The phase difference between current and voltage is
Solution:
\[\tan \phi = \frac{{{X_L}}}{R} = \frac{{\omega L}}{R} = \frac{{2\pi fL}}{R}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\tan \phi = \frac{{2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 0.5}}{{157}} = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\phi = 45^\circ \]

Question: A particle has a displacement of 2 units along the x-axis, 1 unit along the y-axis and 2 units along the z-axis. Then the resultant displacement of the particle is
Solution:
Displacement: \[2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k\]
Resultant displacement \[ = \sqrt {{2^2} + {1^2} + {2^2}} = 3\,units\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Position Vector, Displacement, And Motion In Two Dimensions

Question: A sample of radioactive elements contains 4 × 10<sup>10</sup> active nuclei. If half-life of element is 10 days, then the number of decayed nuclei after 30 days is
Solution:
The half-decay time, $n = \frac{t}{T}$$ \Rightarrow n = \frac{{30}}{{10}} = 3$
Now, the number of nuclei decayed, $\frac{N}{{{N_0}}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^n}$
Where, the initial number of radioactive nuclei,
${N_0} = 4 \times {10^{10}}$
Therefore, $N = 4 \times {10^{10}}{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^3}$
$ \Rightarrow N = 5 \times {10^9}$
Now, the number of decayed nuclei after $30$ days
is, $ = {N_0} - N = 4 \times {10^{10}} - 5 \times {10^9}$
$\therefore = 3.5 \times {10^{10}}$ decayed nuclei.

Question:
Statement -1: Internal energy change is zero if the temp is constant, irrespective of the process being cyclic or non-cyclic.
Statement -2: $dU = n{C_v}dT$for all process and is independent of path.
(A) Statement–I is True, Statement–II is True; Statement–II is a correct explanation for Statement–I.
(B) Statement–I is True, Statement–II is True; Statement–2 is NOT a correct explanation for Statement–I.
(C) Statement–I is True, Statement–II is False.
(D) Statement–I is False, Statement–II is True.
Solution: $dU = n{C_v}dT$ is valid for all process. If T = constant then dT = 0.

Question: Determine the time in which the smaller block reaches other end of bigger block in the figure
Solution:
Kinetic friction \[{f_k} = {\mu _k}N\]

\[ = 0.3 \times 2 \times 10 = 6{\text{N}}\]

Acceleration of upper block \[ = {{\text{a}}_{\text{1}}}\]

\[ = \frac{{10 - 6}}{2} = 2{\text{m}}/{{\text{s}}^2}\]


\[{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}} = \] acceleration of lower block

\[ = \frac{6}{8} = \frac{3}{4}{\text{m}}/{{\text{s}}^2}\]

\[{{\text{a}}_{1,2}} = {{\text{a}}_1} - {{\text{a}}_2} = 2 - \frac{3}{4} = \frac{5}{4}{\text{m}}/{{\text{s}}^2}\]

\[{S_{{\text{rel}}\;}} = {u_{{\text{rel}}\;}}t + \frac{1}{2}{a_{{\text{rel}}\;}}{t^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow 3 = 0 \times {\text{t}} + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{4} \times {{\text{t}}^2} \Rightarrow {{\text{t}}^2} = \frac{{24}}{5} \Rightarrow {\text{t}} = \sqrt {\frac{{24}}{5}} \]

\[{\text{T}} = 2.19{\text{s}}\]

So, option (c) is correct.

Question: A hollow iron ball (A) and a solid iron ball (B) and cricket ball (C) are dropped from the same height. Which among the three balls reaches the ground first? Assuming there is no resistance due to air.
Options: All the three balls reach ground simultaneously
Solution: Since all the balls have zero velocity initially and they experience equal acceleration due to gravity and travel equal distance. Thus, all the balls take equal time to reach the ground.

Tags.Topic: Kinematic Equations for Uniformly Accelerated Motion

Question: Number of ejected photoelectron increases with increase
Solution: The number of ejected photoelectrons increases with an increase in the intensity of light, because the photoelectric current is directly proportional to the intensity of the incident light. The intensity of incident light is equal to the energy per unit time per unit area. Hence, keeping the frequency constant and increasing the intensity of incident light will increase the number of ejected electrons.

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

Question: A ball falling freely from a height of 4.9 m/s, hits a horizontal surface. If e = 3/4 ,then the ball will hit the surface, for second time after : [Use g = 9.8 m/s2]
Solution:
\[{v^2} = 2gh = 2(9.8)(4.9) = {9.8^2}\]
\[v = 9.8\]

\[u = ev = \frac{3}{4}(9.8)\]

\[T = \]time to collide with ground \[{2^{nd}}\]time

\[ = \frac{u}{g} + \frac{u}{g} = \frac{{2u}}{g} = 2\left( {\frac{3}{4}} \right)\left( {\frac{{9.8}}{{9.8}}} \right) = \frac{3}{2}s = 1.5s\]

Question: A current carrying wire in the neighbourhood produces
Solution: A current carrying conductor, such as a metal wire, will produce a magnetic field around it because of the motion of charge within the wire itself. This motion produces or sets up a magnetic field around the wire in the form of concentric circles.

Question: A force acting on a test charge between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor is F. If one of the plates is removed, then the force on the same test charge will be
Solution:
E in the region between the plates = σ /ε 0
Force acting on test charge F = q0E
If one plate is removed (E’) = σ /2ε 0
New force.
F’ = q0E’
⇒ $$F' = \frac{{{q_0}\sigma }}{{2{\varepsilon _0}}} \Rightarrow F' = \frac{{{q_0}E}}{2} \Rightarrow F' = \frac{F}{2}$$

Question: The expression for the motion of  block in damped oscillation can be represented as :
Solution:
Considering equilibrium along y direction.

$$ma = - \left( {kx + bv} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow ma + kx + bv = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow m\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} + b\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + kx = 0$$

Question: A body moves 6 m north, 8 m south. What is its resultant displacement from initial position (only magnitude)?
Solution: Displacement = (8-6) = 2 m

Question: In the cyclic process shown in the figure, the work done by the gas in one cycle is
Solution: Work done = Area under curve \[ = \frac{{6{P_1} \times 3{V_1}}}{2} = 9PV\]

Question: A gun fires a bullet of mass 50 g with a velocity of 30 m/s. Due to this, the gun is pushed back with a velocity of 1 m/s, then the mass of the gun is :
Solution:
Using conservation of momentum:
\[{M_{gun}} = \frac{{{M_{bullet}}{v_{bullet}}}}{{{v_{gun}}}} = \frac{{\left( {0.05} \right)\left( {30} \right)}}{1} = 1.5{\text{kg}}\]

Question: If two soaps bubbles of equal radii r coalesce, then the radius of curvature of interface between two bubbles will be:
Solution:
Radius of curvature of interface between two bubbles is calculated by
$$\frac{1}{r} = \frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}$$

$$\frac{1}{r} = \frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{R}$$

$$r = \infty $$

Therefore, if two soap bubbles of equal radius R Coalesce then the radius of curvature of interface between two bubbles will be infinity.

Question: An electron moving with kinetic energy 6.6 × 10<sup>–14</sup> J enters a magnetic field 4 × 10<sup>–3</sup> T at right angle to it. The radius of its circular path will be nearest to
Solution:
$$E = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = 6.6 \times {10^{ - 14}}$$
$$\frac{1}{2}\frac{{{m^2}{v^2}}}{m} = 6.6 \times {10^{ - 14}}$$
$${m^2}{v^2} = 2m\left( {6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 14}}} \right)$$
\[mv = \sqrt {2m\left( {6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 14}}} \right)}  = \sqrt {2\left( {9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}} \right)\left( {6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 14}}} \right)} \]
r/ Now, $$\frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = Bqv$$
\[r = \frac{{mv}}{{Bq}} = \frac{{\sqrt {2\left( {9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}} \right)\left( {6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 14}}} \right)} }}{{4\left( {{{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)1.6\left( {{{10}^{ - 19}}} \right)}} = 50\,cm\]

Question: If a particle under S.H.M. has time period 0.1 sec and amplitude 2 × 10<sup>–3</sup> m. It has maximum velocity
Solution:
Max. Velocity of $${\text{ }}SHM = A\omega $$

$ \Rightarrow {V_{\max }} = A \times \frac{{2\pi }}{T}$

$ = 2 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times \frac{{2\pi }}{{0.1}} = \frac{\pi }{{25}}m/s$

Question: The value of the multiplication 3.124 × 4.576 correct to three significant figures is
Solution: \[3.124 \times 4.576 = 14.295 = 14.3\] (Correct to three significant figures).

Question:
The velocity–displacement curve for an object moving along a straight line is shown in the figure below.

At which of the points marked, the object is speeding up?
Solution:
Since v is positive, so a should above be positive for speeding up.
\[a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{{dv}}{{ds}}.\frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = v\frac{{dv}}{{ds}} = vx\]

Object is speeding up where the slope of the curve is positive.

Question: A mass m is performing linear simple harmonic motion, then which of the following graph represents correctly the variation of acceleration a corresponding to linear velocity v?
Solution:
\[x = \;A\;\sin \;\omega t\]

\[v = \;\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \;\omega A\;\cos \;\omega t\]

\[ \Rightarrow \;{v^2} = \;{\omega ^2}\,{A^2}\;{\cos ^2}\,\omega t\;...\;(1)\]

\[a = \;\frac{{dv}}{{at}} = - {\omega ^2}\,A\;\sin \;\omega t\]

\[ \Rightarrow \;{a^2} = {\omega ^4}\;{A^2}\;{\sin ^2}\;\omega t\;\;...(2)\]

\[{\omega ^2}{v^2} + \;{a^2} = {\omega ^4}\;{A^2}\,{\cos ^2}\omega t + \;{\omega ^4}{A^2}\,{\sin ^2}\,\omega t\]

\[ \Rightarrow \;\;{\omega ^2}{v^2} + {a^2} = \;{\omega ^4}\,{A^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \;\;ky + \;x\; = \;C\]

Question: The total vertical distance covered by a freely falling body in a given time is directly proportional to
Options: square of acceleration due to gravity
product of the time and acceleration due to gravity
Solution:
From the second equation of motion, the total vertical distance covered by a freely falling body in a given time is directly proportional to square of time.
\[S = 0 + \frac{1}{2}g{\left( t \right)^2} = \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}\]

Question: If ks and kp respectively are effective spring constant in series and parallel combination of spring as shown in figure, find $$\frac{{{k_s}}}{{{k_p}}}$$
Solution:
In Series : $${k_S} = \frac{{{k_1}{k_2}}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}} = \frac{{2{k^2}}}{{3k}} = \frac{{2k}}{3}$$
In Parallel : $${k_P} = {k_1} + {k_2} = 3k$$

$$\frac{{{k_S}}}{{{k_P}}} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{2}{3}k} \right)}}{{3k}} = \frac{2}{9}$$

Question: A particle moves along the X–axis from x<sub>i</sub> to x<sub>f</sub>. Of the following values of the initial and final coordinates, which results in the displacement with the largest magnitude?
Solution:
Displacement \[ = {x_f} - {x_i}\]
Option a) = \[|6 - 4| = 2m\]

Option b) \[| - 8 + 4| = 4m\]

Option c) \[| - 2 - 4| = 6m\]

Option d) \[|4 + 4| = 8m\]

Question: A child weighing 25 kg slides down a rope hanging from a branch of a tall tree. If the force of friction acting against him is 200 N, the acceleration of child is $\left( {g = 10m/{s^2}} \right)$
Solution:
$mg - {f_s} = ma$
$250 - 200 = 25a$
$a = 2m/{s^2}$

Question: A viscous fluid is flowing through a cylindrical tube. The velocity distribution of the fluid is best represented by the diagram
Solution: Velocity of the fluid at the middle of the pipe is more since the shear stress at the middle layer is comparatively less here. Hence, representation in (c) is correct.

Question: Two persons A and B having masses 50 kg and 60 kg respectively are sitting at the two extremes to a 4 m long boat of mass 40 kg kept on smooth ice. If they come to the middle of the boat then how (approximately) far does the boat move on the ice during the process:
Solution:
COM of the system does not move.

\[{m_1}\Delta {x_1} + {m_2}\Delta {x_2} + {m_3}\Delta {x_3} = 0\]

\[50\left( {2 - x} \right) + 60\left( { - x - 2} \right) + 40\left( { - x} \right) = 0\]

\[100 - 50x - 60x - 120 - 40x = 0\]

\[150x = - 20,\,x = \frac{{ - 20}}{{150}} \approx 13.33cm\]

Tags.Chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

Question: By what percentage should the pressure of a given mass of a gas be increased so as to decrease its volume by 10% at a constant temperature?

A beaker containing water weighs 100 g. It is placed on the pan of a balance and a piece of metal weighing 70 g and having a volume of 10 cm3 is placed inside the water in the beaker. The weight of the beaker and the metal would be:
Solution: As metal is inside the water, density of metal is greater than density of water. So, net weight = weight of water + weight of metal = 170 g

Question: A particle is projected so as to just move along a vertical circle of radius r. The ratio of the tension in the string when the particle is at the lowest and highest point on the circle is -
Solution:
As the particle is just able to complete the vertical circle
${T_{{\text{highest}}\,{\text{point}}}} = 0$
$\therefore \,$ Ratio is infinite
Hence [d]

Question:
Assertion : A ladder is more apt to slip when you are high up on it than when you just begin to climb.
Reason : At the high up on a ladder, the torque is large and on climbing up the torque is small.
Options: If Statement–I is true, Statement–II is true; Statement–II is the correct explanation for Statement–I.
If Statement–I is true, Statement–II is true; Statement–II is not a correct explanation for Statement–I.
If Statement–I is true; Statement–II is false.
If Statement–I is false; Statement–II is true.
Solution: Because of its weight at the point of contact between the ladder and the floor, when an individual is high up on the ladder, a huge torque is generated. Whereas the torque is low as he begins going up. For this reason, when one is high up on it, the ladder is more capable of sliding.

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

Question:
Assertion : Displacement of a body may be zero when distance travelled by it is not zero.
Reason: The displacement is the longest distance between initial and final position.
Options: Assertion is correct, reason is correct; reason is a correct explanation for assertion.
Assertion is correct, reason is correct; reason is not a correct explanation for assertion
Assertion is correct, reason is incorrect
Assertion is incorrect, reason is correct

Question: Two forces ${\vec F_1}\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,{\vec F_2}$ are acting at right angles to each other. Then their resultant has a magnitude
Solution:
Resultant of 2 vectors,
${\text{R}} = \sqrt {{\text{F}}_1^2 + {\text{F}}_2^2 + 2\;{{\text{F}}_1}\;{{\text{F}}_2}\cos \theta } $
⸪ vectors are acting at 90 ,cos θ = 0
$ \Rightarrow {\text{R}} = \sqrt {{\text{F}}_1^2 + {\text{F}}_2^2} $

Question: Which of the following is proportional to the inverse square of distance r.
Options: The potential at a distance r from an isolated point charge
Electric field at distance r from an isolated point charge
The force per unit length between two thin straight charged conductors separated by distance r
Electrostatic force between two large charged bodies kept at small separation apart.
Solution: The electric field is proportional to the inverse square of the distance r from an isolated point charge. Suppose a point charge (Q) produces an electric field at a point P, that is at a distance r from it, then, electric field of the point charge is given by$$E = \frac{{kQ}}{{{r^2}}}$$

Question:
Assertion: Electric lines of force never cross each other.
Reason: Electric fields at a point superimpose to give one resultant electric field.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: If electric lines of forces cross each other, then the electric field at the point of intersection will have two directions simultaneously; this is not possible physically. Since electric field is vector quantity so for resultant field principle of superposition is applied.

Question: What is not true for equipotential surface for uniform electric field?
Options: Electric field lines are perpendicular to equipotential surface
Solution: A surface with constant value of potential at all the point is known as equipotential surface. Along x-axis for a uniform electric field, the equipotential surface are planes normal to the x-axis, i.e., parallel to the yz-plane. This cannot be accomplished from a spherical surface.

Question: A wheel of radius 1 meter rolls forward a revolution on a horizontal ground. The magnitude of the displacement of the point of the wheel initially in contact with the ground is
Solution: Displacement will be zero as initial and final positions are the same.

Question: A coconut of mass 1.0 kg falls to earth from a height of 10 m. The kinetic energy of the coconut, when it is 4 m above ground is:
Solution:
Height of fall \[ = 10 - 4 = 6m\]

\[{v^2} = {u^2} + 2as\;\;\;{\kern 1pt} \therefore {v^2} = 0 + 2 \times g \times {\text{H}}\]

\[v = \sqrt {2gH} \;\;\;{\kern 1pt} = \sqrt {2 \times 9.8 \times 6} \]

\[{\text{KE}} = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times (2 \times 9.8 \times 6)\]

\[ = 9.8 \times 6\;\;\;{\kern 1pt} = 58.8\;{\text{J}}\]

Question: A motor drives a body along a straight line with a constant force. The power P delivered by the motor must vary with time t according to :
Solution:
We know that;
Power, $P = Fv$
F = constant
So acceleration will also be constant, a
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,P = F.v\]
\[ \Rightarrow P = F.\,at\]
\[ \Rightarrow P \propto t\]

Question: A force F1 acts on a particle accelerating it from rest to a velocity v. Force F1 is then replaced by F2 which decelerates the particle to rest.
Options: F<sub>1</sub> must be equal to F<sub>2</sub>
F<sub>1</sub> may be equal to F<sub>2</sub>
F<sub>1</sub> must be unequal to F<sub>2</sub>

Question: If a transformer of an audio amplifier has output impedance 8000 Ω and the speaker has input impedance of 8 Ω, the primary and secondary turns of this transformer connected between the output of amplifier and to loudspeaker should have the ratio
Solution:
From Faraday’s law, the induced emf across primary and secondary is
\[{e_p} = - {N_p}\frac{{\Delta \phi }}{{\Delta t'}}\]
\[{e_s} = - {N_s}\frac{{\Delta \phi }}{{\Delta t}}\]
Also, e = iR
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{{R_p}}}{{{R_s}}} = \frac{{{N_p}}}{{{N_s}}}\]
Given, Rs = 8000Ω, Rp = 8Ω
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\frac{{{N_s}}}{{{N_p}}} = \frac{{{R_s}}}{{{R_p}}} = \frac{{8000}}{8} = \frac{{1000}}{1} = 1000:1\]

Question: If an AC produces same heat as that produced by a steady current of 4 A, then peak value of current is
Solution:
The peak value of current (I0) is
Given by, \[{I_0} = \sqrt 2 {I_{rms}}\]
where, Irms is known as root mean square value of current.
Given, Irms = 4 A
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\;{I_0} = \sqrt 2 \times 4 = 5.6\,A\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Average, Peak and RMS Value of Alternating Current/Voltage

Question: A projectile is thrown at an angle of 40° with the horizontal and its range is R<sub>1</sub>. Another projectile is thrown at an angle 40° with the vertical and its range is R<sub>2</sub>. What is the relation between R<sub>1 </sub>and R<sub>2</sub>?
Solution:
\[{R_1} = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 80^\circ }}{{2\;g}}\]

\[{R_2} = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 100^\circ }}{{2\;g}}\]

\[\sin 80^\circ = \sin 100^\circ \]

\[\therefore {{\text{R}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{R}}_{\text{2}}}\]

Question: The liquid in the capillary tube will rise, if the angle of contact is
Solution:
Ascent formula:
$$h = \frac{{2T\cos \theta }}{{r\rho g}}$$
Only if $$\theta $$ is acute, $$\cos \,\theta $$ will be positive, meaning the height will be positive and liquid will rise. If $$\theta $$ is obtuse, $$\cos \,\theta $$ will be negative, meaning the height will be negative and liquid will fall inside the capillary. This means the level of liquid in the capillary will be lesser than the vessel. This happens in case of mercury only if $$\theta = 90^\circ \,\cos \theta $$ will be 0 , meaning the height will be zero and liquid will not rise.

Question: The value of current gain α of a transistor is 0.98. The value of β will be:
Solution:
\[\alpha = \frac{{{I_c}}}{{{I_e}}}\]
\[\beta = \frac{{{I_c}}}{{{I_b}}}\]
\[{I_e} = {I_c} + {I_b}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{I_e}}}{{{I_c}}} = 1 + \frac{{{I_b}}}{{{I_c}}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\alpha } = 1 + \frac{1}{\beta }\]
\[ \Rightarrow \beta = \frac{\alpha }{{1 - \alpha }} = \frac{{0.98}}{{1 - 0.98}} = 49\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: Semiconductor Electronics : Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits

Question: A uniform rod of mass m and length L lies radially on a disc rotating with angular speed ω in a horizontal plane about its axis. The rod does not slip on the disc and the centre of the rod is at a distance R from the centre of the disc. Then the kinetic energy of the rod is:

Question: If the gases have initial temperature 300K and they are mixed in an adiabatic container having the same volume as the previous containers. Now the temperature of the mixture is T and pressure is P. Then
Solution:
For gas A, ${P_A}V_A^{5/3} = {P_1}V_A^{5/3}$
\[{P_1} = {P_A}\]

Similarly, for Gas B, ${P_2} = {P_B}$

$P = {P_1} + {P_2} = {P_A} + {P_B}$

$P > {P_A},P > {P_B}$

For Gas A,

${V_A}T_A^{\gamma - 1} = {V_A}T_1^{\gamma - 1}$

${T_1} = {T_A}$

Similarly, for Gas B, ${T_2} = {T_B}$

$\because {T_A} = {T_B} = 300K$

So, $T = {T_1} = {T_2} = 300K$

Question: In circular motion, the centripetal acceleration is given by-
Solution: $${\vec a_R} = \vec \omega \times \vec v$$

Question: Three capacitors of 8μ F, 8 μ F, and 4 μ F are connected in parallel across a source of P.D. 120 volt. The total charge on effective capacitor will be
Solution:
Ceq = 8 + 8 + 4 = 20 μ F ; Qtotal = Ceq. V
⇒ Qtotal = 20 × 10-6 × 120 = 24 × 10-4 C

Question: Figure shows a body of mass m moving with a uniform speed v along a circle of radius r. The magnitude of change in velocity in going from A to B is
Solution:
Mass of the body m, radius r , moving with uniform speed v .

Velocity at point A :
\[ \Rightarrow {\vec V_A} = V\hat j\]
Velocity at point B:
\[ \Rightarrow {\vec V_B} = - V\hat i\]
Therefore, change in velocity \[ = {\vec V_B} - {\vec V_A}\]
\[ = - V\hat i - V\hat j\]
Magnitude\[\sqrt {{{\left( { - V} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - V} \right)}^2}} = \sqrt 2 V\,or\,V\sqrt 2 \]

Question: The value of λ for which the vectors \[3\widehat i - 6\widehat j + \widehat k\] and \[2\widehat i - 4\widehat j + \lambda \widehat k\] are parallel is
Solution:
Let

$\overrightarrow a = 3\widehat i - 6\widehat j + \widehat k$

$\overrightarrow b = 2\widehat i - 4\widehat j + \lambda \widehat k$

Since the given vectors are parallel,

∴ Angle between them is 0°

So $\overrightarrow a \cdot \overrightarrow b = \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|\cos 0$

\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\left( {3\widehat i - 6\widehat j + \widehat k} \right) \cdot \left( {2\widehat i - 4\widehat j + \lambda \widehat k} \right)\]

\[ = \left| {3\widehat i - 6\widehat j + \widehat k} \right|\left| {2\widehat i - 4\widehat j + \lambda \widehat k} \right|\]

$6 + 24 + \lambda = \sqrt {9 + 36 + 1} \cdot \sqrt {4 + 16 + {\lambda ^2}} $

$30 + \lambda = \sqrt {46} \cdot \sqrt {20 + {\lambda ^2}} $

Squaring both sides, we get

$900 + {\lambda ^2} + 60\lambda = 46\left( {20 + {\lambda ^2}} \right)$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,900 + {\lambda ^2} + 60\lambda = 920 + 46{\lambda ^2}$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,{\lambda ^2} - 46{\lambda ^2} + 60\lambda + 900 - 920 = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\, - 45{\lambda ^2} + 60\lambda - 20 = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,9{\lambda ^2} - 12\lambda + 4 = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,{\left( {3\lambda - 2} \right)^2} = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,3\lambda - 2 = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,3\lambda = 2$

$\therefore \,\,\,\lambda = \frac{2}{3}$

Alternate method:

Let $\overrightarrow a = {a_1}\widehat i + {a_2}\widehat j + {a_3}\widehat k$ and $\overrightarrow b = {b_1}\widehat i + {b_2}\widehat j + {b_3}\widehat k$

If $\overrightarrow a \parallel \overrightarrow b $

$\therefore \,\,\,\frac{{{a_1}}}{{{b_1}}} = \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{b_2}}} = \frac{{{a_3}}}{{{b_3}}}$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{3}{2} = \frac{{ - 6}}{{ - 4}} = \frac{1}{\lambda }\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{1}{\lambda } = \frac{3}{2}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\lambda = \frac{2}{3}$
