Question: Given a metallic uniformly charged sphere. The radius of the sphere is increased keeping its potential same. What is the effect on the value of the electric field intensity at its surface?
Solution:
Electric potential V = $\frac{{kQ}}{R}$
V = constant
Electric Field (E) = $\frac{{kQ}}{{{R^2}}} = \frac{{kQ}}{R} \times \frac{1}{R}$
E = $\frac{{kV}}{R}$
Since radius is increased therefore
Electric field decreases.

Question: If $3x - 5y = 2x + y,$ find the value of $\frac{{\left( {3x + 5y} \right)}}{{\left( {3x - 5y} \right)}}$?
Solution:
$ \Rightarrow \,\,3x - 5y = 2x + y$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,3x - 2x = 5y + y$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,x = 6y$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{x}{y} = \frac{6}{1}$

Multiplying both side by 3 and dividing by 5 we get,

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{3x}}{{5y}} = \frac{{18}}{5}$

Using componendo and dividendo we get

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{\left( {3x + 5y} \right)}}{{\left( {3x - 5y} \right)}} = \frac{{\left( {18 + 5} \right)}}{{\left( {18 - 5} \right)}}$

$\therefore \,\,\,\frac{{\left( {3x + 5y} \right)}}{{\left( {3x - 5y} \right)}} = \frac{{23}}{{13}}$

Question: The difference between two principal specific heats of nitrogen is 300 J kg<sup>-1</sup> K<sup>-1</sup> and ratio of the two specific heats is 1.4. c<sub>p</sub> is
Solution:
${{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}{\text{ - }}{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}{\text{ = 300}}$(as it is in J/kg. k so it is principal
specific heat.)
$$\frac{{{{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}}}{{{{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}}}{\text{ = 1}}{\text{.4}}$$
$${{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}{\text{ - }}\frac{{{{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}}}{{{\text{1}}{\text{.4}}}}{\text{ = 300}}$$
$${{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}} = \frac{{{\text{300}} \times {\text{1}}{\text{.4}}}}{{{\text{0}}{\text{.4}}}}{\text{ = 1050 }}J{\text{ }}k{g^{ - 1}}{K^{ - 1}}$$

Solution:

De-Broglie wavelength, $\lambda = \frac{h}{{mv}}$

$ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{h}{{\sqrt {2m{K_B}T} }}$

Question: Tesla is the unit of
Solution: The tesla (symbol T) is the derived SI unit of magnetic flux density, which represents the strength of a magnetic field. One tesla represents one weber per square meter.

Question: When another conductor is brought near a charged conductor, its capacity increases. This is because
Solution: Potential decreases because another conductor has a negative charge. It attracts the positive charge from the first conductor and this leads to the reduction in potential. So, more charge can be given to the first conductor. Now, if we earth the outer side of the second conductor, then the positive charge on this side will go to Earth. Due to this, the first conductor will be able to hold an even more positive charge. This is the principle of a capacitor.

Question: If the binding energy per nucleon in <sub>3</sub>Li<sup>7</sup> and <sub>2</sub>He<sup>4</sup> nuclei are respectively 5.60 MeV and 7.06 MeV, then the energy of proton in the reaction ${}_3^7Li + p \to 2He_2^4$ is
Solution:
The binding energy of Lithium is $5.6{\text{ MeV}}$, and Helium is $7.06{\text{ MeV}}$.
The number of nucleons in Lithium is $7$, and Helium is $4$.
The energy of proton in this reaction ${}_3^7Li + p \to 2He_2^4$ is
Therefore, proton energy $ = $ Binding energy of
Helium atom$ - $Binding energy of Lithium atom
$ \Rightarrow {E_p} = \left( {2 \times 4 \times 7.06} \right) - \left( {7 \times 5.6} \right)$
$\therefore {E_p} = 17.28{\text{ MeV}}$

Question: A particle of mass m moving along the x-axis has a potential energy U(x) = a + bx<sup>2</sup> where a and b are positive constants. It will execute simple harmonic motion with a frequency determined by the value of
Solution:
$U\, = \,a + \,b{x^2}$
$$F = - \frac{{dU}}{{dx}}$$

$$F = - \left[ {0 + 2bx} \right]$$

$$F = - 2bx$$

$$K = 2b$$

$$\omega = \sqrt {\frac{K}{m}} = \sqrt {\frac{{2b}}{m}} $$

$$f = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{2b}}{m}} $$

Question: A body of mass m moving with velocity u collides inelastically with a stationary body of mass 2 m. The speed of the system after collision, is :
Solution:
Using conservation of momentum:
\[v = \left| {\frac{{{m_1}{v_1} - {m_2}{v_2}}}{{\left( {{m_1} + {m_2}} \right)}}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{mu - 0}}{{3m}}} \right| = \frac{u}{3}\]

Question: A body is projected at an angle of 30° to the horizontal with a speed of 30 m/s. What is the angle with the horizontal after 1.5 seconds? Take g = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup>
Solution:
\[{v_y} = {{\text{u}}_y} - {\text{gt}} = 30\sin 30^\circ - 10\left( {\frac{3}{2}} \right)\]

\[ = \frac{{30}}{2} - \frac{{30}}{2} \Rightarrow {v_y} = 0\]

\[\therefore \tan \alpha = \frac{{{v_y}}}{{{v_x}}} = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow \alpha = 0^\circ \]

Question: A vertical U-tube of uniform cross section contains water upto a height of 20 cm. Calculate the time period of the oscillation of water when it is disturbed
Solution:
The length of the liquid column
$$L = 2 \times 20 = 40cm$$

Time period, $$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{L}{{2g}}} $$

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{40}}{{2 \times 980}}} $$

$$T = \frac{{2\pi }}{7} = 0.898 \simeq 0.9s$$

Question:

Reason: Electric field lines of force can pass through the conductor.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Since conductor cannot hold net charge inside it, so electric field intensity within a conductor is always zero. Therefore, electric field lines of force cannot pass through the conductor.

Question: A particle is moving along the X–axis whose acceleration is given by a = 3x – 4, where x is the location of the particle. At t = 0, the particle is at rest at x = 4/3. The distance travelled by the particle in 5 s is
Solution:
Given \[a = 3x - 4\]
Also, at t = 0, u = 0; \[x = \frac{4}{3}\]

So, at t = 0,

\[a = 3x - 4 = 3\left( {\frac{4}{3}} \right) - 4 = 0\]

Clearly, at any time t,

\[v\left( t \right) = u + at = 0 + 0 = 0\]

As the particle is at rest and a = 0 hence it remains at rest.

Question: A positive acceleration always corresponds to the speeding up and a negative acceleration always corresponds to the speeding down.
Options: False only when motion is uniform.
Solution:
For speeding up,

a > 0, v > 0 and a < 0, v < 0

For speeding down,

a > 0, v < 0 and a < 0, v > 0

Basically when \[\vec v\] and \[\vec a\] are in same direction, body speeds up and when \[\vec v\] and \[\vec a\] are in opposite direction body slows down.

Question: A string of negligible thickness is wrapped several times around a cylinder kept on a rough horizontal surface. A man standing at a distance d from the cylinder holds one end of the string and pulls the cylinder towards him. There is no slipping anywhere. The length of the string passed through the hand of the man while the cylinder reaches his hands is:
Solution:
Since there is no slipping anywhere if the velocity of center of cylinder is v, then velocity of top point (and thread) is 2v
$ \Rightarrow $Distance travelled by center $ = vt = d$

$ \Rightarrow $Distance travelled by top point or string$ = 2vt = l$

$\therefore \frac{l}{d} = 2 \Rightarrow l = 2d$

Question: Figure shows a uniform rod of length \[l\] and mass M which is pivoted at end A such that it can rotate in a vertical plane. Torque is only provided by gravitational force

\[\alpha = \frac{{Mg\left( {\frac{l}{2}} \right)\cos \theta }}{{\frac{{M{l^2}}}{3}}}\]

\[\alpha = \frac{3}{{2l}}g\cos \theta \]

Question: Find the value of a for which ${\text{m}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}$is a root of equation. $a{m^2} + (\sqrt 3  - \sqrt 2 ){\text{m}} - 1 = 0$
Solution:
Put
$\begin{gathered}
  {\text{m}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{{\text{a}}}{3} + (\sqrt 3 - \sqrt 2 )\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} - 1 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{a}} + (3 - \sqrt 6 ) - 3 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow a = \sqrt 6 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Three equal charges each +Q, placed at the corners of an equilateral triangle of side a. What will be the force on any charge $$\left( {k = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}} \right)$$
Solution:
Suppose net force is to be calculated on the charge which is kept at A. Two charges kept at B and C are applying force on that particular charge, with direction as shown in the figure.

$$Since\;\;{F_B} = {F_C} = F = k\;\frac{{{Q^2}}}{{{a^2}}}$$

$$So,\;\;{F_{net}} = \sqrt {F_B^2 + F_C^2 + 2\,{F_B}{F_C}\cos 60} $$

$${F_{net}} = \sqrt 3 \,F = \frac{{\sqrt 3 \,k{Q^2}}}{{{a^2}}}$$

Question:
If a body of mass \[200g\] falls from a height \[200m\] and its total potential energy is converted into kinetic energy, at the point of contact of the body with the surface, then decrease in potential energy of the body at the contact is :
\[\left( {g = 10\,m/{s^2}} \right)\]
Solution:
Decrease in potential energy is equals to gain in kinetic energy
\[KE = mgh\]
\[K.E = \frac{{20}}{{1000}}\left( {10} \right)\left( {200} \right)\]
\[K.E = 400J\]

Question: A 100 V, AC source of frequency 500 Hz is connected to an L-C-R circuit with L = 8.1 mH, C = 12 5. µF, R = 10 Ω all connected in series as shown in figure. What is the quality factor of circuit?
Solution:
The Q-factor of series resonant circuit is given as
\[Q = \frac{{Voltage\,across\,L\,or\,C}}{{Applied\,voltage\,\left( {voltage\,across\,R} \right)}}\]
\[ = \frac{{\left( {{\omega _r}L} \right)i}}{{Ri}} = \frac{{{\omega _r}L}}{R}\]
Here, L = 8.1 mH, C = 12.5μF,
R = 10 Ω, f = 500 Hz
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,\,Q = \frac{{{\omega _r}L}}{R} = \frac{{2\pi fL}}{R} = \frac{{2 \times \pi \times 500 \times 8.1 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{10}} = 2.5434\]

Question: A body of mass 0.4 kg is whirled in a vertical circle making 2 rev/sec. If the radius of the circle is 2 m, then tension in the string when the body is at the top of the circle, is\[\left[ {use\,\,g = 9.81\,m/{s^2}} \right]\]
Solution:
Mass of the body, m = 0.4 kg ,
angular velocity ω = 2 rev/sec = 12.56 rad/sec .
The radius of the circle r = 2 m Now, Tension in the string, when the body is at the top of the circle, \[T = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} - mg\]
Where, \[v = \omega r\]
Then, \[T = m{\omega ^2}r - mg\]
\[ \Rightarrow T = 0.4{\left( {12.56} \right)^2} \times 2 - \left( {0.4 \times 9.81} \right) = 122.27N\]

Question: Same current is flowing in two alternating circuits. The first circuit contains only inductance and the other contains only a capacitor. If the frequency of the emf of AC is increased, the effect on the value of the current will be
Options: increases in the first circuit and decreases in the other
decreases in the first circuit and increases in the second circuit
Solution:
We know that current in purely inductive circuit and purely capacitive circuit is given by respectively,
\[{I_L} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{{\omega L}}\sin \left( {\omega t - \pi /2} \right)\]
And \[{I_C} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{{1/\omega C}}\sin \left( {\omega t + \pi /2} \right)\]
\[ = {E_0}\sin \omega t\left( {\omega t + \pi /2} \right) \times \omega C\]
Here, ω is the angular frequency of the alternating current. Thus, current will decrease in the first circuit and increase in second circuit.

Question: The speed of a projectile at the highest point becomes \[\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\] times its initial speed. The horizontal range of the projectile will be
Solution:
$u\cos \theta = \frac{u}{{\sqrt 2 }}\,\, \Rightarrow \theta = 45^\circ $

$\therefore R = \frac{{{u^2}\sin (2 \times 45^\circ )}}{g} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{g}$

Question: $ A $ and $ B $ are two infinitely long straight parallel conductors. $ C $ is another straight conductor of length $ 1\,{\rm{m}} $ kept parallel to $ A $ and $ B $ as shown in the figure. Then the force experienced by $ C $ is
Solution:
Conductor $ A $ attracts conductor $ C $ towards $ A $
 $ \therefore $ Force towards $ A $

 $ = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }}\frac{{{I_1}{I_2}l}}{r} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }}\frac{{2 \times 3 \times 1}}{{0.05}} = \frac{{3{\mu _0}}}{\pi } \times 20 $ $ = \frac{{60 \times 4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{\pi } = 240 \times {10^{ - 7}}{\rm{N}} $

Again force towards $ B = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }} \times \frac{{3 \times 4 \times 1}}{{0 \cdot 08}} = \frac{{3{\mu _0}}}{\pi } \times 25 $ $ = \frac{{3 \times 4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{\pi } \times 25 = 300 \times {10^{ - 7}}{\rm{N}} $

 $ \therefore $ Resultant force towards $ B = \left( {300 - 240} \right){10^{ - 7}}N $ $ = 0.6 \times {10^{ - 5}}{\rm{N}} $

Question: The potential energy of a molecule increases when it is brought to the surface from the interior of a liquid because
Options: at the free liquid surface gravitational potential energy is more
work has to be done to move a molecule to the surface against the repulsive component of the inter molecular forces
work has to be done to move a molecule to the surface against the attraction from other molecules
the temperature of the liquid surface is always more than that of the interior of the liquid
Solution:
When molecules go on to the surface of the liquid, work needs to be done to remove cohesive forces or attraction forces between molecules of the liquid.
This work stored in the molecules as potential energy,

So, when they came to the surface the potential energy will increase.

Question: For a transistor, the common base current amplification factor is 0.8. The transistor is connected in common-emitter configuration. The change in the collector current when the base current changes by 6 mA is :
Solution:
Given that, the current amplification factor is, $\alpha = 0.8$, and the base current is, ${I_B} = 6{\text{ mA}}$.
Therefore, the current amplification factor for common emitter configuration is, $\beta = \frac{\alpha }{{1 - \alpha }}$
$ \Rightarrow \beta = \frac{{0.8}}{{1 - 0.8}} = 4$
 Hence, the change in collector current is,
$\Delta {I_C} = \beta \times \Delta {I_B} = 4 \times 6 = 24{\text{ mA}}$

Tags.Chapter: Semiconductor Electronics : Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits

Question:
Assertion: A transformer is used to increase voltage and current both at same time.
Reason: In a transformer we can increase power also.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: For an ideal transfer, assuming no power loss, we have ${V_P}\,\,{I_P}\,\, = \,\,{V_S}{I_S}.$ Therefore, if we gain in voltage we lose current and vice versa. Since voltage in transformer increases at the cost of current so no increase in power is possible

Question:

Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: The field of magnet of galvanometer produces strong magnetic field with the help of horse shoe magnet as compared to Earth’s magnetic field (order of 1 Gauss). So working of moving coil galvanometer is not effected by Earth’s magnetic field.

Question:
Assertion: Magnetic field lines can be entirely confined within the core of a toroid.
Reason: Magnetic field lines can be entirely confined within a straight solenoid.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: In toroid, magnetic field is uniform but in solenoid magnetic field is non-uniform. In toroid, wire is wound over its core in circular form, so magnetic field lines always form closed loops. But in solenoid, wire wound in rectangular form so closed loop is not formed.

Question:
Assertion. A charged capacitor is disconnected from a battery. Now if its plates are separated farther, the potential energy will fall.
Reason. Energy stored in a capacitor is equal to the work done in separating plates.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution:
Battery is disconnected from the capacitor.
So, Q = constant
Energy $$ = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{2C}} = \frac{{{Q^2}d}}{{2{\varepsilon _0}A}}$$
$$ \Rightarrow Energy\, \propto d$$

Question: The component of vector \[\vec A = {a_x}\hat i + {a_y}\hat j + {a_z}\hat k\] along the direction of \[\left( {\hat i - \hat j} \right)\] is
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow A = {a_x}\widehat i + {a_y}\widehat j + {a_z}\widehat k,\overrightarrow B = \widehat i - \widehat j\]

Component of \[\overrightarrow A \] along \[\overrightarrow B = A\cos \theta = \overrightarrow A \cdot \left( {\widehat B} \right)\]

\[ = \left( {{a_x}\widehat i + {a_y}\widehat j + {a_z}\widehat k} \right) \cdot \frac{{\left( {\widehat i - \widehat j} \right)}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{\left( {{a_x} - {a_y}} \right)}}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]

circular path is:

At the highest point, particle makes an almost perfect circle.

${v_x} = u\cos \theta ;$

$a = \frac{{{v_x}^2}}{R};\,\,R = \frac{{{v_x}^2}}{a} = \frac{{{{\left( {u\cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{g}$

Question: Circular loop of a wire and a long straight wire carry currents \[{I_c}\] and \[{I_e}\], respectively as shown in figure. Assuming that these are placed in the same plane. The magnetic fields will be zero at the centre of the loop when the separation \[H\] is
Solution: \[\frac{{{\mu _0}{I_c}}}{{2R}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_e}}}{{2\pi H}}\,\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\,H = \frac{{{I_e}R}}{{\pi {I_c}}}\]

Question: If $\left( {4a + 9b} \right) \times \left( {4c - 9d} \right) = \left( {4a - 9b} \right)\left( {4c + 9d} \right),$ then the value of a : b can be equal to:
Solution:
Here we have:

$\left( {4a + 9b} \right) \times \left( {4c - 9d} \right) = \left( {4a - 9b} \right)\left( {4c + 9d} \right).$ In other words we can say that: $\frac{{\left( {4a + 9b} \right)}}{{\left( {4a - 9b} \right)}} = \frac{{\left( {4c + 9d} \right)}}{{\left( {4c - 9d} \right)}}$

Hence we have:

$\frac{{\left\{ {\left( {4a + 9b} \right) + \left( {4a - 9b} \right)} \right\}}}{{\left\{ {\left( {4a + 9b} \right) - \left( {4a - 9b} \right)} \right\}}} = \frac{{\left\{ {\left( {4c + 9d} \right) + \left( {4c - 9d} \right)} \right\}}}{{\left\{ {\left( {4c + 9d} \right) - \left( {4c - 9d} \right)} \right\}}}$

[By componendo and dividendo]

In other words we have: $\frac{{8a}}{{18b}} = \frac{{8c}}{{18d}}.$

or we may writer: $\frac{a}{b} = \frac{c}{d}$ i.e. $a:b::c:d.$

Hence $a:b = c:d$ and the correct option here is (A) $c:d.$ Let us see another example below.

Question: A body is projected horizontally with speed 20 m/s from the top of a tower. What will be the displacement of the body after 5 sec?
Solution:
\[x = {{\text{u}}_x}{\text{t}} = 20 \times 5 = 100\;{\text{m}}\]

\[y = {{\text{u}}_y}{\text{t}} - \frac{1}{2}{\text{g}}{{\text{t}}^2} = 0 - \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 25 = - 125\;{\text{m}}\]

\[{\text{S}} = \sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} = \sqrt {{{100}^2} + {{125}^2}} \approx 160\;{\text{m}}\]

Question: Which of the following situations is possible?
Options: An object can have acceleration, but constant velocity.
The velocity of an object may be zero but acceleration is not zero.
Distance and the magnitude of displacement are equal for motion in a plane.
Average speed and the magnitude of average velocity are always equal for motion in a plane.
Solution: The word acceleration implies a change in the object’s velocity. One example of this is vertical particle projection. When an object is thrown upwards, the maximum height velocity of the body is zero, but the constant acceleration due to gravity is still working.

Question: The magnitude of the impulse developed by a mass of 0.2 kg which changes its velocity from \[5\,\hat i - 3\,\hat j + 7\,\hat k\]  m/s to \[2\,\hat i + 3\,\hat j + \hat k\]m/s is:
Solution:
Impulse =$\Delta \overrightarrow p $
\[ = m\left( {{{\vec v}_2} - {{\vec v}_1}} \right)\]

\[ = 0.2\left[ {\left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j + \hat k} \right) - \left( {5\hat i - 3\hat j + 7\hat k} \right)} \right]\]

\[ = 0.2\left( { - 3\hat i + 6\hat j - 6\hat k} \right)\]

\[\therefore \]Magnitude of impulse

\[{\text{ = 0}}{\text{.2}}\sqrt {9 + 36 + 36} \]

\[ = 0.2\sqrt {81} = 1.8{\text{N - s}}\]

Question: A conducting rod of length l and mass m is moving down a smooth inclined plane of inclination θ with constant velocity v. A current i is flowing in the conductor in a direction perpendicular to paper inwards. A vertically upwards magnetic field B exists in space. Then, magnitude of magnetic field B is
Solution:
The situation can be shown as

Hence, from figure,

$${F_m}\cos \theta = mg\sin \theta $$ …(i)

Also, $${F_m} = Bil$$ …(ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$B = \frac{{mg}}{{il}}\tan \theta $$

Question: The net force between two nucleons 1 fm apart is F<sub>1</sub> if both are protons, F<sub>2</sub> if both are neutrons, and F<sub>3</sub> if one is a neutron and the other is a proton.
Solution:
Nuclear force is a very short range force and it is independent of the charge on the nucleons. So the attractive nuclear force is same for any pair of nucleons.
The electrostatics force is a long range force and it acts only between charge particles i.e. protons and protons will repel each other.
So force between protons is Repulsive (electrostatic) as well as attractive (Nuclear) while that between 2 neutrons and between 1 neutron and 1 Proton is only nuclear(attractive).
So, ${{\text{F}}_{\text{1}}}\,{\text{ < }}\,\,{{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{F}}_{\text{3}}}$

Question: The electrostatic potential of a uniformly charged thin spherical shell of charge Q and radius R at a distance r from the centre is
Options: $$\frac{Q}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}$$ for points outside and $$\frac{Q}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}R}}$$ for points on surface of the sphere
$$\frac{Q}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}$$ for both points inside and outside the shell
zero for points outside and $$\frac{Q}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}$$ for points inside the shell
zero for both points inside and outside the shell
Solution:
If charge on a conducting sphere of radius R is Q, then potential outside the sphere,
$${V_{out}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{Q}{r}$$
At the surface of sphere,$${V_s} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{Q}{R}$$

Question: A particle of mass m moving towards the east with speed v collides with another particle of the same mass and same speed v moving towards the north. If the two particles stick to each other, the new particle of mass 2m will have a speed of
Solution:
By conservation of linear momentum

\[{\vec P_i} = {\vec P_f}\]

\[mv\hat i + mv\hat j = 2m{\vec V_0}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {\vec V_0} = \frac{v}{2}\hat i + \frac{v}{2}\hat j\]

\[\therefore \left| {{{\vec V}_0}} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{v}{2}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{v}{2}} \right)}^2}} \]

\[\left| {{{\vec V}_0}} \right| = \frac{v}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]

Question: The centripetal force (F) acting on an object that is rotating in a circular path depends upon the mass of object (m), radius of the circular path (r), and velocity (v) of the object. Derive the formula for the centripetal force.
Solution:
Let \[F = k{m^a}{r^b}{v^c}\]

Then,

\[\left[ F \right] = \left[ k \right]\left[ {{m^a}} \right]\left[ {{r^b}} \right]\left[ {{v^c}} \right]\]

\[\left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right] = \left[ {{M^0}{L^0}{T^0}} \right]\left[ {{M^a}} \right]\left[ {{L^b}} \right]\left[ {{L^c}{T^{ - c}}} \right]\]

\[\left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}} \right] = \left[ {{M^a}} \right]\left[ {{L^{b + c}}} \right]\left[ {{T^{ - c}}} \right]\]

On comparing, we get

\[a = 1,\,\,c = 2,\,\,b + c = 1 \to b = - 1\]

So,

\[F = km{v^2}{r^{ - 1}}\]

Question: The following graph can be interpreted as:
Options: Particle is moving but the time is constant
Both particle and time are constant
Particle falling from the top of a building
Solution:
Interpretation: It represents that a particle is changing its position but time does not change, it means the particle possesses infinite velocity.
This situation is practically not possible.

Question: Two simple pendulums of lengths 1.44 m and 1 m start swimming together. After how many vibrations will they again start swinging together?
Solution:
Given, $${\ell _1} = 1.44m,\,\,{\ell _2} = 1\,m$$
Now, the ratio of time periods of two pendulums, $$\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = \frac{{\sqrt {1.44} }}{{\sqrt 1 }} = 1.2$$.

Now, the ratio is positive integer.

So, 1.2 would be the integers after multiplied (atleast) by 5.

Thus, required answer will be 5 oscillations.

Question: A parallel plate capacitor with a slab of dielectric constant 3 filling the whole space between the plates is charged to certain potential and isolated. Then the slab is drawn out and another slab of equal thickness but dielectric constant 2 is introduced between the plates. The ratio of the energy stored in the capacitor later to that stored initially is
Solution:
Energy stored, $$U = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{Q^2}}}{C}$$
When the capacitor is charged using battery and isolated, the charge stored will remain the same. Therefore,
$$\frac{{{U_1}}}{{{U_2}}} = \frac{{{C_2}}}{{{C_1}}} = \frac{{{k_2}}}{{{k_1}}} = \frac{2}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{{{U_2}}}{{{U_1}}} = \frac{3}{2}$$

Question: A force of 2.25 N acts on a charge of 15 x 10<sup>-4</sup>C placed at point in field. Calculate the intensity of electric field at that point.
If a particle has negative velocity and negative acceleration, its speed
Solution: As the velocity is a vector quantity, both its magnitude and direction has to be considered. If a particle possesses negative acceleration and negative velocity, then it is moving in the opposite direction of its motion. That is, the speed of the particle increases with increasing time, but in the opposite direction.

Question: A particle executes simple harmonic motion with an angular frequency of 3.5 rad/s and maximum acceleration 7.5 m/s<sup>2</sup>. The amplitude of oscillation will be :
Solution:
$${\omega ^2}x = a$$, a is acceleration.
$$ \Rightarrow {\left( {3.5} \right)^2}x = 7.5$$$$ \Rightarrow x = 0.61\,m$$

Thus, amplitude is 0.61m.

Question: Hair of shaving brush cling together when it is removed from water due to
Solution: When the brush is removed from water hairs of the brush cling together as the water that gets attached to the hairs tries to minimize its surface area (in order to have minimum surface energy) thus creating tension and that this tension is known as surface tension

Question: Four identical rods of length L are joined end to end to form a square. The mass of each rod is M. The moment of inertia of the square about the median line is:
Solution:
\[{I_{total}} = {I_1} + {I_2} + {I_3} + {I_4}\]

\[ = M{\left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)^2} + \frac{{M{L^2}}}{{12}} + \frac{{M{L^2}}}{{12}} + M{\left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)^2}\]

\[ = \frac{{M{L^2}}}{2} + \frac{{M{L^2}}}{6} = \frac{2}{3}M{L^2}\]

$(\therefore {I_2}\& {I_4}$ are for rods, axis, passing through center and for 1 and 3 each particle is at distance $\frac{L}{2})$

Question:
Assertion: The Coulomb force is strongest force in the universe.
Reason: The Coulomb force is stronger than the nuclear force inside nucleus.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Among all four basic forces of nature (Gravitational, Electromagnetic, Strong Nuclear and Weak Nuclear), strong nuclear force is much stronger than electrostatic force (Coulomb’s force). Moreover, it is the strongest of all the four fundamental forces in nature.

Question: Rn decays into Po by emitting an α –particle with half life of 4 days. A sample contains 6.4×10<sup>10</sup> atoms of Rn. After 12 days, the number of atoms of Rn left in the sample will be
Solution:
$Rn\xrightarrow[{2He}]{4}Po\left( {{T_{1/2}} = 4days} \right)$
In 12 days No. of half lives = 3
So, $N = {N_0} \times {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^3}$
$ = 6.4 \times {10^{10}} \times \frac{1}{8} = 0.8 \times {10^{10}}$

Solution: Unit of speed is m/s, so dimension is LT–1

Question: The average magnetic energy density of an electromagnetic wave of wavelength $\lambda $ travelling in free space is given by
Solution:
Energy density of an electromagnetic wave

\[U = \frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _0}{E^2} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{B^2}}}{{{\mu _0}}}\]

So, the magnetic energy density is \[{\frac{{{B^2}}}{{{\mu _0}}}}\]

Question:
Assertion: Quality factor (Q-factor) of series resonant circuit is a unitless quantity
Reason: It defines sharpness of i-V curve at resonance in LCR series circuit.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution:
It defines sharpness of $i - V$ curve at resonance when Q-factor is large, the sharpness of resonance curve is more and vice-versa. Q-factor is also defined as follows,
\[Q - factor\,\, = \,\,2\pi \, \times \frac{{Max.\,\,energy\,\,stored}}{{Energy\,\,dissipation}}\]
\[ = \,\,\frac{{2\pi }}{T}\, \times \,\frac{{Max.\,\,energy\,\,stored}}{{Mean\,\,power\,\,dissipated}}\]
\[ = \,\,\frac{{\operatorname{Re} sonant\,\,frequency}}{{Band\,\,width}}\,\, = \,\,\frac{{{\omega _o}}}{{\Delta \omega }};\,\,that\,\,is,\,\,unitless.\]

Inversely proportional to the radius of the sphere
Directly proportional to the square of the radius of the sphere
Inversely proportional to the square of the radius of the sphere
Solution: Surface charge density = $\frac{Q}{{4\pi {R^2}}}$

Question: A spring of spring constant 8N/cm has an extension of 5cm. The minimum work done in joule in increasing the extension from 5cm to 15cm is
Which of the following is NOT true when a reverse bias is applied to a p-n junction?
Options: Electrons flow from the p to the n side.
Holes flow from the p to the n side.
the electric field in the depletion zone increases
The potential difference across the depletion zone increases
Solution: While reverse biasing, the movement of minority charge carriers will only be present. So, Holes flow from p to n side is a wrong statement. The holes will be flowing from n top side. Therefore, electrons will be flowing from p to n side. The electric field in the depletion zone increases.

Question: If a player catches a ball of mass 200 g moving with the velocity 40 ms<sup>–1</sup>, then he experiences a force of (time taken to catch the ball is 0.1 s)

Question: A police jeep is chasing with velocity of 45 kmh<sup>−1</sup>, a thief in another jeep moving with velocity 153 kmh<sup>−1</sup>. Police fires a bullet with muzzle velocity of 180 kmh<sup>−1</sup>. The velocity with which bullet will strike the car of the thief, is
Solution:
${v_{b,P}} = 180,\,\,{v_P} = 45$

${V_b} = {V_{b,P}} + {V_P} = 225$

${v_{b,T}} = {v_b} - {v_T}$

${v_{b,T}} = 75\,\,{\text{km/hr}}$

$ = 20\,{\text{m/s}}$

Question: The time of flight of a projectile on an upward inclined plane depends upon
Options: angle of inclination of the plane
the value of acceleration due to gravity
Solution:
Time of flight (T) \[ = \frac{{2u\sin \left( {\theta - \alpha } \right)}}{{g\cos \alpha }}\]

where,

‘u’ is initial velocity of the body.

\['\theta '\]is the angle of projectile.

\['\alpha '\]is the angle of inclination.

‘g’ acceleration due to gravity

Question: A cylindrical vessel is filled with a liquid up to a height H. A small hole is made in the vessel at a distance y below, the liquid surface as shown in figure. The liquid emerging from the hole strike the ground at distance x
Options: x is equal if hole is at depth y or H – y
Solution:
We know that

$$Range = x = 2\sqrt {\left( {H - y} \right)y} $$

We can see from this formula that for depth h or H-y value of x will be same and for y = H/2 range will be maximum.

Question: A mass of 2.9 kg, is suspended from a string of length 50 cm, and is at rest. Another body of mass 100 gm is moving horizontally with a velocity of 150 m/sec, strikes and sticks to it. What is the tension in the string when it makes an angle of 60<sup>o</sup> with the vertical
Solution:
Applying principle of conservation of momentum,

${m_1}{v_1} + {m_2}{v_2} = \left( {{m_1} + {m_2}} \right)v$
$\therefore 0.1 \times 150 + 0 = 3 \times v$
$ \Rightarrow 15 = 3v \Rightarrow v = \frac{{15}}{3} = 5m/s$

OM = OB cos 60o
$ = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4}m.$
$\therefore AM + OA - OM$
$ = \frac{1}{4}hg$
$\therefore \Delta PE = mg \cdot \frac{1}{4}$
$\therefore \frac{1}{2}m \times {\left( 5 \right)^2} = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + mg \cdot \frac{1}{4}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{25}}{2} = \frac{{10}}{4} + \frac{v}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow {v^2} = 20$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = \frac{{3 \times 20}}{{\frac{1}{2}}} = 120$
$mg\cos 60^\circ = 3 \times 10 \times \frac{1}{2} = 15N$
$\therefore T = 15 + 120N$
= 135N

Question: A car while travelling at a speed of 72 km/hr. Passes through a curved portion of road in the form of an arc of a radius 10 m. If the mass of the car is 500 kg the reaction on the car at the lowest point P is
Solution:
At the lowest point,

\[72km/hr = 72 \times \frac{5}{{18}}m/s\]
\[ = \,20\,m/s\]
\[N = mg + \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}\]
\[ = 500 \times 10 + 500 \times \frac{{{{\left( {20} \right)}^2}}}{{10}}\]
\[ = 5000 + 20000\]
\[ = 25000 = 25KN\]

Question: The mass of a lift is 2000 kg. When the tension in the supporting cable is 28000 N, then its acceleration is
Solution:
Let’s set the equilibrium condition

T = F + W

T = ma + mg

$a = \frac{{T - mg}}{m}$

$a = \frac{{28000 - 2000 \times 10}}{{2000}}$

a = 4ms-2

Acceleration is positive so lift will go upward.

Question: A nucleus moving with a velocity \[\vec v\]emits an α–particle. Let the velocities of the α–particle and the remaining nucleus be \[{\vec v_1}\,\,and\,\,{\vec v_2}\] and their masses be m<sub>1</sub> and m<sub>2</sub>.
Options: \[\vec v,{\vec v_1}\,\,and\,\,{\vec v_2}\]must be parallel to each other.
None of the two of \[\vec v,{\vec v_1}\,\,and\,\,{\vec v_2}\] should be parallel to each other.
\[{\vec v_1} + {\vec v_2}\] must be parallel to\[\vec v\].
\[{m_1}{\vec v_1} + {m_2}{\vec v_2}\] must be parallel to\[\vec v\].
Solution:
From conservation of linear momentum
\[\left( {{m_1} + {m_2}} \right)\vec v = {m_1}{\vec v_1} + {m_2}{\vec v_2}\]

Question: A disc of radius r rolls on a horizontal ground with linear acceleration a and angular acceleration \[\alpha \] as shown in figure. The magnitude of acceleration of point P shown in figure at an instant when its linear velocity is v and angular velocity is \[\omega \] will be:

Question: A capacitor is charged by a battery and the energy stored is U. The battery is now removed and the separation distance between the plates is doubled. The energy stored now is
Solution:
Energy stored, $$U = \frac{1}{2}qV$$
As the distance d is increased between the two plates.
Now, stored energy,
$$U' = \frac{1}{2}qV' = \frac{1}{2}q\left( {\frac{q}{C}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{q^2}d}}{{2{\varepsilon _0}A}}$$
$$ \Rightarrow U'\, \propto d,$$as the separation distance d is doubled the energy stored also becomes 2 times, i.e. $$U' = 2U$$

Question: In the given figure, the loop is fixed but straight wire can move. The straight wire will
Solution:
If we consider a small element dL (shown in figure), the magnetic force on the considered element is towards the straight wire.

Thus, the wire move towards the loop.

Question: What is the value of capacitance, if the thin metallic plate is introduced between two parallel plates of area A and separated at distance d?
Solution:
When a metallic slab is inserted between the plates, capacitance,
$$C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{d - t}}$$
Here, t = 0 as plate is very thin.
$$\therefore \,\,\,C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}$$

Question: Radioactive element decays to form a stable nuclide, then the rate of decay of reactant \[\left( {\frac{{dN}}{{dt}}} \right)\] will vary with time (t) as shown in figure
Solution:
\[\frac{{dN}}{{dt}} = - \lambda N\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{dN}}{{dt}} = - \lambda {N_0}{e^{ - \lambda t}}\]

Question: When there is no heat change from surrounding in a system, then the process is
Solution: When there is no exchange of heat between system and its surroundings, then its internal energy only gets converted into work. This process happens so fast, that there is no time to exchange heat. This process is known as adiabatic process.

Question: A block of mass 1 kg is projected from the lowest point up along the inclined plane. If g = 10 ms-2 , the retardation experienced by the block is

A particle moves along a straight line such that its displacement at any time t is given by
s = 3t3 + 7t2 + 14t + 5. The acceleration of the particle at t = 1s is:
Solution:
\[s = 3{t^3} + 7{t^2} + 14t + 5\]
Using \[v = \frac{{ds}}{{dt}}\& a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}}\]

$v = \frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = 9{t^2} + 14t + 14$

$a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 18t + 14$

$a\left( {1\sec } \right) = 18 + 14 = 32{\text{ }}m/{s^2}$

Question: A mass m with velocity u strikes a wall normally and returns with the same speed. What is magnitude of the change in momentum of the body when it returns

Question: A meter stick is held vertically with one end on the floor and is the other end is allowed to fall. Assuming that the end on the floor of the stick does not slip, the velocity of the other end when it hits the floor, will be:

\[mg\frac{L}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{m{L^2}}}{3}{\left( {\frac{v}{L}} \right)^2}\]

\[v = \sqrt {3gL} = 5.4m/s\]

Question: A charge particle is projected in the magnetic field of $\left( {6\widehat i - 5\widehat j} \right) \times {10^{ - 3}}T$. The acceleration of the particle is found to be $\left( {a\widehat i + 6\widehat j} \right) \times {10^{ - 6}}m{s^{ - 2}}$. The value of a is

The acceleration of particle and magnetic field are perpendicular to each other.
$\left( {6\widehat i - 5\widehat j} \right) \times {10^{ - 3}}$$.\left( {a\widehat i + 6\widehat j} \right) \times {10^{ - 6}} = 0$

$a = 5$

Question: A body is thrown vertically up from the ground. It reaches the maximum height of 100 m in 5 sec. After what time it will reach the ground from the maximum height position
Solution:
Use time of ascent = time of descent
During ascent,

$v = u + at \Rightarrow 0 = u - 10(5) \Rightarrow u = 50{\text{ m/s}}$

${\text{(}}\because v = 0{\text{ at maximum height)}}$

During descent,

$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2} \Rightarrow 100 = 0(t) + \frac{{10}}{2}{t^2}$

${\text{(}}\because u = 0{\text{ at maximum height)}}$

$ \Rightarrow {t^2} = \frac{{200}}{{10}} = 20 \Rightarrow t = 4.47{\text{ sec}}$

Clearly, the most appropriate answer is 5 sec.

Question: A piece of ice floats in a vessel with water, above which a layer of lighter oil is poured. When the whole of ice melts, which one of the following statements will be true?
Options: The level of the interface will remain the same.
The total level of the liquids in the vessel will rise.
The total level of the liquids in the vessel will fall while the level of interface will go up.
The level of interface as well as the total level of liquids in the vessel will fall
Solution:
We know that: $$\rho _{water}^{}{\text{ > }}\rho _{ice}^{}$$

Clearly, when ice (submerged inside water) melts,

$${v_{water}}(final){\text{ > }}{v_{water}}(initial)$$

$$(\because {m_{ice}}(initial){\text{ = }}{m_{ice}}(final))$$

Therefore, the level of water (interface) will rise.

Similarly, as $$\rho _{oil}^{}{\text{ < }}\rho _{ice}^{}$$; when ice (submerged inside oil) melts,

$${v_{water}}(final){\text{ < }}{v_{ice}}(initial)$$

$$(\because {m_{ice}}(initial){\text{ = }}{m_{ice}}(final))$$

Therefore, the level of oil will fall. Thus, the total level of the liquids in the vessel will fall while the level of interface will go up.

Question: An aeroplane flying at 100 m/sec dives in a vertical plane along the circle of radius 200 m. The mass of the pilot is 75 kg. The force exerted by the pilot on his seat when the aeroplane is at the maximum height
Solution:

$ = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} - mg$

$ = 3000\,N = 300\,kg\,wt$
Hence [a]

Question: A diver in a swimming pool bends his head before diving because it :
Solution:
Let\[I\omega \]is the initial angular momentum.
After diver bend, the inertia will decrease, so the new initial angular momentum will be, \[I'\omega '\]

\[I\omega = I'\omega ' \Rightarrow \omega ' = \frac{{I\omega }}{{I'}}\]

\[\omega ' > \omega \]

Question: If vectors $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $ have an angle θ between them, then value of \[\left| {\widehat A - \widehat B} \right|\]will be :
Solution:
If angle between $\vec A\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec B\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,\theta $, then between \[\vec A\,\,{\text{and}} - \vec B\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,180^\circ - \theta \]
$|\hat A - \hat B| = \sqrt {{A^2} + {B^2} + 2\hat A\hat B\cos \left( {180^\circ - \theta } \right)} $
$ = \sqrt {1 + 1 + 2\left( { - \cos \theta } \right)} $
$ = \sqrt {2(1 - \cos \theta )} $
$ = \sqrt {2\left( {2{{\sin }^2}\frac{\theta }{2}} \right)} \left( {\because \cos 2\theta = 1 - 2{{\sin }^2}\theta } \right)$
$ = 2\sin \frac{\theta }{2}$

\[ \Rightarrow {\text{h}} = \frac{{7{{(20)}^2}}}{{10 \times 9.8}} = 28.6{\text{m}}\]

Question:
As given in the figure, a series circuit connected across a 200 V, 60 Hz line consists of a capacitor of capacitive reactance 30Ω, a non-inductive resistor of 44 Ω and a coil of inductive reactance 90Ω and resistance 36Ω.

The power dissipated in the coil is
Solution:
XL = 90 Ω, R2 36 Ω, R1 = 44Ω
XC = 30Ω, V = 200 V
And total resistance,
\[R = {R_1} + {R_2} = 44 + 36 = 80\Omega \]
\[Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {{X_L} - {X_C}} \right)}^2}} \]
\[ = \sqrt {{{\left( {80} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {60} \right)}^2}} = \sqrt {6400 + 3600} = 100\]
Current, \[I = \frac{V}{Z} = \frac{{200}}{{100}} = 2A\]
Power dissipated in the coil,
\[{P_{av}} = {I^2}R = {\left( 2 \right)^2} \times = \,144W\]

Question: A body is thrown horizontally with a velocity \[\sqrt {2gh} \]from the top of a tower of height h. It strikes the level ground through the foot of the tower at a distance x from the tower. The value of x is

\[t = \sqrt {\frac{{2\;h}}{{\;g}}} \]

\[x = {\text{ ut }};\]

\[u = \sqrt {2gh} \]

\[x = \sqrt {2gh \times \frac{{2\;h}}{{\;g}}} = 2\;h\]

Question: The vector $\widehat i + x\widehat j + 3\widehat k$ is rotated through an angle θ and doubled in magnitude, then it becomes $4\widehat i + \left( {4x - 2} \right)\widehat j + 2\widehat k.$ The possible positive value of x is
Solution:
$\sqrt {{4^2} + {{\left( {4x - 2} \right)}^2} + {2^2}} = 2\sqrt {{1^2} + {x^2} + {3^2}} $

On squaring, $16{x^2} - 16x + 24 - 4\left( {{x^2} + 10} \right)$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,3{x^2} - 4x - 4 = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\left( {3x + 2} \right)\left( {x - 2} \right) = 0$

$\therefore \,\,\,x = 2$ is positive value

Question: Position vector of a particle moving in xy plane at time t is\[\overrightarrow {\text{r}}  = {\text{a}}(1 - \cos \omega t)\widehat {\text{i}} + a\sin \omega t\widehat {\text{j}}\]  the path of the particle is:
Options: a circle of radius a and centre at (a, 0)
a circle of radius a and centre at (0, 0)
neither a circle nor an ellipse
Solution:
\[x = a(1 - \cos \omega t) \Rightarrow \cos \omega t = 1 - \frac{x}{a}\]

\[y = a\sin \omega t\quad \Rightarrow \sin \omega t = \frac{y}{a}\]

\[{\sin ^2}\omega t + {\cos ^2}\omega t = 1 = {\left( {1 - \frac{x}{a}} \right)^2} + {\left( {\frac{y}{a}} \right)^2}\]

\[{(x - a)^2} + {y^2} = {a^2}\]

This is a circle with centre $\left( {a,0} \right)$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Position Vector, Displacement, And Motion In Two Dimensions

Question:
If a sample of metal weighs 210 g in air, 180 g in water and 120 g in a liquid:
(i) RD of metal is 3

(ii) RD of metal is 7

(iii) RD of liquid is 3

(iv) RD of liquid is (1/3)
Solution:
Relative density of Metal $${\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{Weight in air}}}}{{{\text{change in weight of body in water}}}}$$
$$R.D. = \frac{{210}}{{210 - 180}} = 7$$

$${\text{Density of metal = 7 }}g/c{m^3}$$

Change in weight in liquid = upthrust in liquid

$$ = {V_{solid}} \times {\rho _\ell } \times g$$

$$\Delta w \propto {\rho _\ell }$$

$$\frac{{\Delta {w_\ell }}}{{\Delta {w_w}}} = \frac{{{\rho _\ell }}}{{{\rho _w}}}$$

$${\rho _\ell } = \frac{{\Delta {w_\ell }}}{{\Delta {w_w}}}{\rho _w}$$

$${\rho _\ell } = \frac{{210 - 120}}{{210 - 180}} \times (1)$$

$${\rho _\ell } = 3{\text{ }}g/c{m^3}$$

Question: A refrigerator is to maintain eatables kept inside at $9^\circ C$, calculate the coefficient of performance?
Solution:
Temperature inside the refrigerator,
${T_1} = 9^\circ C = 282K$

Room temperature, ${T_2} = 36^\circ C = 309K$

Coefficient of performance $ = {T_1}/{T_2} - {T_1}$

$ = 282/\left( {309 - 282} \right) = 10.44$

Therefore, the coefficient of performance of the given refrigerator 10.44

Question: Two masses mA and mB moving with velocities vA and vB in opposite directions collide elastically. After that the masses mA and mB move with velocity vB and vA respectively. Then the ratio $\frac{{{m_A}}}{{{m_B}}}$is :
Solution:
Using Newton’s law of collision,
\[\frac{{{v_B} - {v_A}}}{{{v_A} - {v_B}}} = - e\]

\[e = 1\]

i.e., Perfectly elastic collision.

It is true that two colliding bodies exchange their velocities after perfectly elastic head-on colliding which implies that the bodies are identical in nature, or have the same masses.

\[{m_1} = {m_2} = m\]

Since the two bodies exchange velocities, therefore,

\[{m_A} = {m_B}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{m_A}}}{{{m_B}}} = 1\]

Question: The magnetic field of a given length of wire carrying a current for a single turn circular coil at centre is B, then its value for two turns for the same wire when same current passing through it is
Solution:
$$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}nI}}{a}$$
a is radius and n is number of turns

Total length of coil is L, so in 1 turn, radius is $$a = \frac{L}{{2\pi }}$$

If no of turns becomes twice, then

$$4\pi r = L$$⇒$$r = \frac{L}{{4\pi }} = \frac{a}{2}$$

$$B' = \frac{{{\mu _0}2I}}{{\frac{a}{2}}} = \frac{{4{\mu _0}nI}}{a} = 4B$$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Magnetic Field Due to Current Element

Question: A sphere is accelerated upwards with the help of a cord whose breaking strength is five times its weight. The maximum acceleration with which the sphere can move up without cord breaking is
Solution:
When sphere is accelerated up with constant acceleration ‘a’ then

\[T - mg = ma\]

\[T = m\left( {g + a} \right)\]

\[\because {T_{\max }} = 5g \Rightarrow 5mg = m\left( {g + a} \right)\]

\[ \Rightarrow a = 5g - g \Rightarrow a = 4g\]

So, option (a) is correct.

Question: A force of $\left( {10\hat i - 3\hat j + 6\hat k} \right)N$acts on a body of 5 kg and displaces it form $A\left( {6\hat i + 5\hat j - 3\hat k} \right)m$ to $B\left( {10\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k} \right)m.$The work done is
Solution:
\[\vec F = \left( {10\hat i - 3\hat j + 6\hat k} \right)N\]
${\vec r_1} = 6\hat i + 5\hat j - 3\hat k,{\vec r_2} = 10\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k$
$\Delta \vec r = {\vec r_2} - {\vec r_1} = 4\hat i - 7\hat j + 10\hat k$
$W = \vec F.\Delta \vec r = \left( {10\hat i - 3\hat j + 6\hat k} \right).\left( {4\hat i - 7\hat j + 10\hat k} \right)$
$ = 40 + 21 + 60 = 121\,J$

Question: Curie is a unit of

Question: For the P-V diagram shown, for path $1 \to 2 \to 3,$100J of heat is given to the system and 40J of work is done by the system. For path $1 \to 4 \to 3,$the work done by system is 10J. Then
Options: For $3 \to 1$the heat is rejected and equal to 85J, For $1 \to 4$the work done by system is 10J, ${U_3} - {U_1} = 60J$
For $3 \to 1$the heat is rejected and equal to 90J
For $1 \to 4$the work done by system is 35J
For $1 \to 4$the heat given to system is 20J
Solution:
In process $1 \to 2 \to 3$

${W_{1 - 2 - 3}} = {W_{1 - 2}} + {W_{2 - 3}}$
${Q_{1 - 2 - 3}} = {U_{1 - 2 - 3}} + {W_{1 - 2 - 3}}$
$100 = {U_{1 - 2 - 3}} + \left( {{W_{1 - 2}} + {W_{2 - 3}}} \right)$
$100 = {U_{1 - 2 - 3}} + \left( {0 + 40} \right)$
${U_{1 - 2 - 3}} = {U_{1 - 2}} = 60J$
${U_3} - {U_1} = 60J$option a is correct.
In process $1 \to 4 \to 3$
${W_{1 - 4 - 3}} = {W_{1 - 4}} = 10J$
In process $3 \to 1$
${W_{3 - 1}} = $Area under 3 – 1
\[\begin{gathered}
   = \left( {{\text{Area}}\,{\text{under}}\,1 - 4} \right) + \\
  \,\,\,\,\left( {\frac{{\left( {{\text{Area}}\,{\text{under}}\,2 - 3} \right) - \left( {{\text{Area}}\,{\text{under}}\,1 - 4} \right)}}{2}} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]
$ = 10 + \frac{{40 - 10}}{2} = 25J$
${Q_{3 \to 1}} = {U_{3 \to 1}} + {W_{3 \to 1}} = - 60 - 25 = - 85J$

Question: An electric dipole consists of two opposite charges each 0.05µC separated by 30 mm. The dipole is placed in a uniform external electric field of$${10^6}N{C^{ - 1}}.$$ The maximum torque exerted by the field on the dipole is
Solution:
$$\eqalign{
  & Given,q = 0.05\mu C = 5 \times {10^{ - 8}}C, \cr
  & 2a = 30\,\,mm = 0.03\,\,and\,\,E = {10^6}N{C^{ - 1}} \cr} $$
Torque acting on an electric dipole placed in an uniform electric field,
$$\tau = pE\,\,\sin \theta $$

For maximum torque, $\theta = 90^\circ $

$$\therefore \,\,{\tau _{\max }} = pE = E\left( {q \times 2a} \right)$$

$$ = {10^6} \times 5 \times {10^{ - 8}} \times 0.03\, = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,\,N - m$$

Question: The resultant of two forces at right angle is 5 N. When the angle between them is 120°, the resultant is \[\sqrt {13} .\] Then the forces are
Solution:
\[{R^2} = {A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos \theta \]

\[{5^2} = {A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos 90^\circ \]

\[{A^2} + {B^2} = 25\]

\[{\left( {\sqrt {13} } \right)^2} = {A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos 120^\circ \]

\[13 = 25 - AB\]

\[AB = 12\]

\[{A^2} + {\left( {\frac{{12}}{A}} \right)^2} = 25\]

\[{A^2} + \frac{{144}}{{{A^2}}} = 25\]

\[{A^4} - 25{A^2} + 144 = 0\]

\[A = 3N\]

\[B = 4N\]

Question: Each side of a cube is measured to be 5.402 cm. The total surface area and the volume of the cube in appropriate significant figures are:

\[ = 6 \times {\left( {5.402} \right)^2} = 175.09\;c{m^2} = 175.1\;c{m^2}\]

Total volume \[ = {\left( {5.402} \right)^3} = 157.64\;c{m^3} = 157.6\;c{m^3}\]

(Upto correct number of significant figure).

Question: An object moving along a straight line has a constant acceleration of 4 m/s<sup>2</sup>. The position–time graph for this object has a slope that
Options: first increases and then decreases with time
Solution: Since acceleration is constant, Velocity<sub> </sub>increases<sub>; </sub>slope<sub> </sub>of position-time graph will keep on increasing with time.

Question: The drag force on the vane is directly proportional to:
Solution: The drag force is proportional to the velocity.

Question: A Body moves 6 m north and 2 m south, what is its resultant displacement from initial position?
Solution: Displacement = 6 – 2 = 4 m

Question: An electron is travelling along the x-direction. It encounters a magnetic field in the y-direction. Its subsequent motion will be
Options: A circle in the x-z plane
A circle in the y-z plane
A circle in the x-y plane
Solution:
The correct option is B A circle in the x-z plane
Given, $\vec v = v\hat i$
$\vec B = B\hat j$

Magnetic force on the charge particle is,
$\vec F = q\left( {\vec v \times \vec B} \right) = - e\left[ {v\hat i \times B\hat j} \right]$
$\vec F = evB\left( { - \hat k} \right)$
Since force is in -z axis, it will force ${e^ - }$ to move in circle in x-z plane.

Question:
Statement–1: A quick collision between two bodies is more violent than slow collision, even when initial and final velocities are identical.
Statement–2: The rate of change of momentum determines that the impulsive force is small or large.
Options: Both Statement-1 and Statement-2 are true and Statement-2 is the correct explanation for Statement-1.
Both Statement-1 and Statement-2 are true but Statement-2 is not the correct explanation forStatement 1.
Statement-1 is true and the Statement-2 is false.
Statement-1 is false and the Statement-2 is true.
Solution:
${\text{Force}} = \frac{{{\text{change}}\,\,{\text{in}}\,\,{\text{momentum}}}}{{{\text{time}}\,\,{\text{taken}}}}$, where momentum

$p = mv$

$ \Rightarrow p \propto v$

Momentum is directly proportional to its velocity so, the rate of change of momentum determines whether the impulsive force is small or large.

Therefore, a quick collision between two bodies is more violent than slow collision.

Question: If M be the mass of the charged particle, which enters with velocity v normal to the magnetic field B, it will revolve with angular speed given by ?
Solution:
Here, the
Centripetal force is balanced by magnetic force. The centripetal force acting on the charged particle is

$$\frac{{M{v^2}}}{R}$$. Here R is the path radius.

The magnetic force acting on the charged particle equals $$qvB$$. Note that the angle between the velocity

and magnetic field is $${90^o}$$.

On equating the two values we obtain $$R = \frac{{Mv}}{{qB}}$$ ​

$\omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{{vqB}}{{Mv}} = \frac{{qB}}{M}$

Question:
According to Bohr’s theory of hydrogen atom, for the electron in the nth allowed orbit the
I. linear momentum is proportional to 1/n.
II. radius is proportional to n.
III. kinetic energy is proportional to 1/n2 .
IV. angular momentum is proportional to n.
Choose the correct option from the codes given below
Solution:
According to Bohr’s theory of hydrogen atom, we know that,
Angular momentum, \[mvr = \frac{{nh}}{{2\pi }}\]
\[ \Rightarrow mv = \frac{{nh}}{{2\pi r}}\]
Linear momentum, \[P = mv = \frac{{nh}}{{2\pi r}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow p \propto \frac{n}{r}\]
\[As,r \propto {n^2}\]
\[Hence,{\mkern 1mu} P \propto \frac{1}{n}\]
We know that, kinetic energy
\[KE \propto \frac{1}{r}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} and{\mkern 1mu} r \propto {n^2}\]
\[Hence,\,KE \propto \frac{1}{{{n^2}}}\]

Question: A water tank standing on the floor has two small holes punched in the vertical wall one above the other. The holes are 2.4 cm and 7.6 cm above the floor. If the jets of water from the holes hit the floor at the same point, then the height of water in the tank is :
Solution:
Since Range $$ = 2\sqrt {h\left( {H - h} \right)} $$
$${h_1} = 2.4$$

$${h_2} = 7.6$$

$${R_1} = {R_2}$$

$$2\sqrt {\left( {H - {h_1}} \right){h_1}} = 2\sqrt {\left( {H - {h_2}} \right){h_2}} $$

$$\left( {H - {h_1}} \right){h_1} = \left( {H - {h_2}} \right){h_2}$$

$$\left( {H - 2.4} \right)\frac{{2.4}}{{7.6}} = \left( {H - 7.6} \right)$$

$$0.31H - 0.75 = H - 7.6$$

$$6.85 = 0.69H \Rightarrow H = \frac{{6.85}}{{0.69}} \approx 10{\text{ cm}}$$

Question:
Block A and B are resting on a smooth horizontal surface given equal speeds of 2 m/s in opposite sense as shown in the figure. At t = 0, the position of block are shown, then the coordinates of centre of mass at
t = 3 s will be :
Solution:
Initial coordinate of COM
\[{X_{cm}} = \frac{{\left( 4 \right)\left( 0 \right) + \left( 4 \right)\left( {4.5} \right)}}{{4 + 4}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow {X_{cm}} = \frac{{4.5}}{2} = 2.25\]
\[ \Rightarrow \] coordinate of COM = (2.25, 0)
Now,
Momentum of system
\[\vec P = 4\left( 2 \right) - 4\left( 2 \right) = 0\]
\[\therefore {M_{system}}{\vec V_{cm}} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow {\vec V_{cm}} = 0\]
Hence the COM of the system is at rest.
\[\therefore \] coordinate of COM always remain (2.25, 0)

Question: From the top of a tower, a particle is thrown vertically downwards with a velocity of 10 m/s. The ratio of the distances, covered by it in the 3<sup>rd</sup> and 2<sup>nd</sup> seconds of the motion is (Take g = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup>)
Solution:
\[{{\text{S}}_{{n^{th}}}} = {\text{u}} + \frac{{\text{a}}}{2}(2{\text{n}} - 1)\]

${S_3} = 10 + 5(2 \times 3 - 1) = 35$

${S_2} = 10 + 5(2 \times 2 - 1) = 25$

$ \Rightarrow {S_3}:{S_2} = 7:5$

Question: The dimensions of Planck’s constant are same as
Solution:
We know that \[E = hv\]

\[h = \frac{E}{v} = \frac{{\left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{T^{ - 1}}} \right]}} = \left[ {M{L^2}{T^{ - 1}}} \right]\]

Question: A ball thrown upward reaches a height and comes back downward. Out of the following statements, which one is true for displacement?
Options: It varies continuously but never changes the sign
It varies continuously with being maximum in the beginning, in the beginning and being zero at the top.
It remains constant throughout the course of the journey
It only changes the sign when the ball is at the top
Solution:
Statement (a) is true for displacement as it varies from starting
Statement (b) is true for velocity as it is maximum at the time of projecting the ball and becomes zero at the highest point i.e. top.

Statement (c) is true for acceleration as it is always constant throughout the course of the journey and is equal to ‘g’.

Question: Two light springs of force constants $${k_1}$$ and $${k_2}$$ and a block of mass m are in one line. AB on a smooth horizontal table such that one end of each spring is fixed to a required support and other end is attached to block of mass m kg as shown in figure. The frequency of vibration is :
Solution:
$${K_{effective}} = {k_1} + {k_2}$$
$$f = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{\left( {{k_1} + {k_2}} \right)}}{m}} $$

Question: The collision of two balls of equal mass takes place at the origin of coordinates. Before collision, the components of velocities are (v<sub>x</sub> = 50 cm/s, v<sub>y</sub> = 0) and (v<sub>x</sub> = –40 cm/s and v<sub>y</sub> = 30 cm/s). The first ball comes to rest after a collision. The velocity components (v<sub>x</sub> and v<sub>y</sub> respectively) of the second ball are :
Solution:
\[{\vec v_1} = 50\hat i\,m/s\]
\[{\vec v_2} = - 40\hat i + 30\hat j\,cm/s\]
Finaly 1st ball comes to rest.
Let velocity of 2nd ball be
\[{\vec v'_2}\]
\[\therefore {\vec P_i} = {\vec P_f}\]
\[m{\vec v_1} + m{\vec v_2} = m{\vec v'_2}\]
\[m\left( {50\hat i} \right) + m\left( { - 40\hat i + 30\hat j} \right) = m{\vec v'_2}\]
\[10\hat i + 30\hat j = {\vec v'_2}\]
\[\therefore {v_x} = 10cm/s\]
\[{v_y} = 30cm/s\]

Question:
The pendulum hanging from the ceiling of a railway carriage makes angle 30° with
the vertical, when it is accelerating. The acceleration of the carriage is:
Solution:
considering equilibrium of ‘m’ w.r.t. ‘O’

\[\begin{gathered}

  T\cos \theta = mg & & ...(1) \\

  T\sin \theta = ma & & ....(2) \\

   \Rightarrow \,\frac{{T\sin \theta }}{{T\cos \theta }} = \frac{{ma}}{{mg}} \\

\end{gathered} \]

\[\begin{gathered}

   \Rightarrow \,\,\tan \theta = \frac{a}{g} \\

   \Rightarrow \,\tan {30^o} = \frac{a}{g}\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} = \frac{a}{g} \\

   \Rightarrow \,a = \frac{g}{{\sqrt 3 }} \\

\end{gathered} \]

Question: A perfect gas is filled at same temperature in three containers of volumes V, V/2 and V/4. If the respective masses of the gas are M, M/2 and M/4, the pressures exerted on the walls of three containers are
Options: Unequal and maximum in the container of the smallest volume
Unequal and maximum in the container of the largest volume
Equal in all the three cases
Solution:
$PV = nRT$
For 1st container$P{V_1} = \frac{{MRT}}{{{M_0}}}$
For 2nd container ${P_2}\frac{V}{2} = \frac{{MRT}}{{2{M_0}}}$
For 3rd container ${P_3}\frac{V}{4} = \frac{{MRT}}{{4{M_0}}}$
Hence ${P_1} = {P_2} = {P_3}$

Question: A block of metal (density 7 g/cc) of size 5 cm × 5 cm × 5 cm is weighed completely submerged in water. What will be its apparent weight (density of water = 1 g/cc)?
Solution:
$${\text{Density of water  =  }}1{\text{ }}g/c{m^3}\left( {{\rho _w}} \right)$$
$${\text{Density of metal  =  7 }}g/c{m^3}\left( {{\rho _m}} \right)$$
Buoyant force
$${F_B} = {\rho _w}Vg$$
Apparent weight
$$W = mg - {F_B}$$
$$ = \left( {{\rho _m} - {\rho _w}} \right)Vg$$
$$ = \left( {7 - 1} \right) \times 5 \times 5 \times 5g$$
$$W = \left( {6 \times 5 \times 5 \times 5} \right)g$$

Question: A string can bear a maximum tension of 100 Newton without breaking. A body of mass 1 kg is attached to one end of 1 m length of thin string and it is revolved in a horizontal plane. The maximum linear velocity which can be imparted to the body without breaking the string, will be -
Solution:
Breaking tension $ = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}$
$100\,N = \frac{{1 \times {v^2}}}{1}$
$\therefore \,\,v = \sqrt {100} \,\,{\text{m/s}}$
$ = 10\,\,{\text{m/s}}$
Hence [a]

Question:
Statement 1 : A uniform thin rod of length L is hinged about one of its ends and is free to rotate about the hinge without friction. Neglect the effect of gravity. A force F is applied at a distance x from the hinge on the rod such that force always is perpendicular to the rod. As the value of x is increased from zero to L, the component of reaction by hinge on the rod perpendicular to length of rod increases.
Statement 2 : Under the conditions given in statement 1 as x is increased from zero to L, the angular acceleration of rod increases.
Options: Statement–1 is True, Statement–2 is True; Statement–2 is a correct explanation for Statement–1.
Statement–1 is True, Statement–2 is True; Statement–2 is NOT a correct explanation for Statement–1.
Statement–1 is True, Statement–2 is False.
Statement–1 is False, Statement–2 is True.
Solution:
As shown in the above diagram, reaction force by the hinge which is perpendicular to rod will balance applied force (F).

\[ \Rightarrow \sum {F_y} = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow {R_2} + \left( { - F} \right) = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow {R_2} = F\]

Hence reaction force will be equal in magnitude of F and will be in opposite direction and doesn’t depend upon x (distance of applied force F from hinge).

Force F will tend to generate torque as;

\[ \Rightarrow x \times F = I\alpha \]

Thus, as we increase x, angular acceleration will also increase.

Question: What is the numerical value of vector $3\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k?$

An electron moves through a small distance in a uniform electric field. The magnitude of electric field is 2 × 104 NC-1. Now, if the direction of field is reversed keeping the magnitude same and a proton moves through the same distance, then which of the following options is correct?
Options: The time of fall will be more in case of electron
The time of fall will be more in case of proton
The time of fall will be same in both cases
The time of fall will be independent of charge
Solution:
The time required to fall through distance $$d$$can be derived as:
$$d = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{qE}}{m}} \right){t^2}$$$$ \Rightarrow $$$$t = \sqrt {\frac{{2dm}}{{qE}}} $$

Since $${t^2} \propto m$$, a proton takes more time.

Question: When power is drawn from the secondary coil of the transformer, the dynamic resistance
Solution: When power is drawn from the secondary coil of the transformer, its dynamic resistance increases. This is because the flux of secondary coil reduces the flux of core and thus the back emf decreases. This decreases the current in secondary coil.

Question: Two Simple Harmonic Motions of angular frequency 100rads<sup>1</sup> and 1000 rad s<sup>-1</sup>have the same displacement amplitude. The ratio of their maximum accelerations is:
Solution:
amax = \[ - {\omega ^2}A\]

\[\omega \] = angular frequency

A = amplitude

Now,

\[\omega \]1 = 100 rad/sec, \[\omega \]2 = 1000 rad/sec

A1 = A2 = A

\[{a_{1\max }} = - \omega _1^2{A_1} = - {\left( {100} \right)^2}A = - {10^4}A\]

\[{a_{2\max }} = - \omega _2^2{A_2} = - {\left( {1000} \right)^2}A = - {10^6}A\]

Question:
Out of the following functions representing motion of a particle which represents SHM ?
(1) y = sin ωt – cos ωt

(2) y = sin3ωt

(3) $$y = 5\cos \left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{4} - 3\omega t} \right)$$

(4)$$y = 1 + \omega t + {\omega ^2}{t^2}$$
Solution:
$$\left( 1 \right)y = \sin \omega t - \cos \omega t = \sqrt 2 \left[ {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\sin \omega t - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\cos \omega t} \right]$$
$$ = \sqrt 2 \sin \left( {\omega t - \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)$$

SHM with period $$T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }$$

$$\left( 2 \right)y = {\sin ^3}\omega t = \frac{1}{4}\left[ {3\sin \omega t - \sin 3\omega t} \right]$$

Periodic with $$T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }$$, but not an SHM

$$\left( 3 \right)y = 5\cos \left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{4} - 3\omega t} \right) = 5\cos \left( {3\omega t - \frac{{3\pi }}{4}} \right)$$

SHM with period $$T = \frac{{2\pi }}{{3\omega }}$$

$$\left( 4 \right)y = 1 + \omega t + {\omega ^2}{t^2}\,\,\,\,v = \omega + 2{\omega ^2}t\,\,\,\,a = 2{\omega ^2}$$

Non-periodic motion Only (1) and (3) are SHM

Question: The charge given to any conductor resides on its outer surface, because
Options: The free charge tends to be in its minimum potential energy state
The free charge tends to be in its minimum kinetic energy state
The free charge tends to be in its maximum potential energy state
The free charge tends to be in its maximum kinetic energy state
Solution: Charge given to a conductor is uniformly distributed at its outer surface to acquire its minimum potential energy state.

Question: An alternating voltage V = V<sub>0</sub> sin ωt is applied across a circuit. As a result, the current I = I<sub>0</sub> sin (ωt – π/2) flows in it. The power consumed in the circuit per cycle is
Solution:
Given, V = V0 sin ωt
And I = I0 sin (ωt – π/2)
It is obvious that current lags behind the voltage by π/2 in phase.
∴ Power consumed in the circuit,
\[P = \frac{1}{2}{V_0}{I_0}\cos \phi \]
But \[\phi = \pi /2 = 90^\circ \]
\[\therefore \,\,\,P = \frac{1}{2}{V_0}{I_0}\cos 90^\circ = 0\]\[\left( {\because \,\,\cos 90^\circ = 0} \right)\]

Then ratio V/v is

Question: Find the value of λ such that the vectors $\overrightarrow a  = 2\widehat i + \lambda \widehat j + \widehat k$ and $\overrightarrow b  = \widehat i + 2\widehat j + 3\widehat k$ are orthogonal
Solution:
Given that $\overrightarrow a = 2\widehat i + \lambda \widehat j + \widehat k$ and $\overrightarrow b = \widehat i + 2\widehat j + 3\widehat k$

Since $\overrightarrow a $ and $\overrightarrow b $ are orthogonal

$\therefore \,\,\,\overrightarrow a \cdot \overrightarrow b = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\left( {2\widehat i + \lambda \widehat j + \widehat k} \right) \cdot \left( {\widehat i + 2\widehat j + 3\widehat k} \right) = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,2 + 2\lambda + 3 = 0$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,5 + 2\lambda = 0\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\lambda = \frac{{ - 5}}{2}$

Hence, the correct option is (d).

Question: The impedance of a circuit, when a resistance R and an inductor of inductance L are connected in series in an AC circuit of frequency f, is
Solution:
In L-R circuit,
Impedance, \[Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + X_L^2} \]
Here, \[{X_L} = \omega L = 2\pi fL\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + 4{\pi ^2}{f^2}{L^2}} \]

Question: Figure shows a hemisphere of radius 4R. A ball of radius R is released from position P. It rolls without slipping along the inner surface of the hemisphere. Linear speed of its centre of mass when the ball is at position Q is :

\[\frac{1}{2}M{v^2} + \frac{1}{5}M{v^2} = Mg\left( {3R} \right)\]

\[ \Rightarrow v = \sqrt {\frac{{30gR}}{7}} \]

Question: A particle of mass 2g and charge $1\mu C$is held at a distance of 1m from a fixed charge 1mC. If the particle is released it will be repelled. The speed of particle when it is at a distance of 10m from the fixed charge is:
Solution:
Potential at 1m from the charge
$${V_A} = \frac{{K \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{1} = K \times {10^{ - 3}}$$

Potential at 10m from the charge $${V_B} = \frac{{K \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{10}} = K \times {10^{ - 4}}$$

Potential difference

$${V_A} - {V_B} = K\left( {{{10}^{ - 3}} - {{10}^{ - 4}}} \right)$$
Velocity at 10 m is $$v$$:
$$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \left( {{V_A} - {V_B}} \right)q$$

$$\frac{1}{2}\left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right){v^2} = K \times {10^{ - 6}}\left( {1 - \frac{1}{{10}}} \right) \times {10^{ - 3}}$$

$${v^2} = K \times \frac{9}{{10}} \times {10^{ - 6}} = 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{9}{{10}} \times {10^{ - 6}} = 8100$$

Thus,$$v = 90\,m{s^{ - 1}}$$

Question: A ball is being rotated in a circle of radius 5 m with constant tangential velocity of 20 m/s. A stone is also being rotated in a circle of radius 4 m with a constant tangential velocity of 16 m/s. Which one of the following choices is true about both the circular motions?
Options: Angular velocity of ball > angular velocity of stone
Angular velocity of stone > angular velocity of ball
Solution:
Angular velocity = Tangential velocity/Radius.
When we put in the values and calculate the angular velocities for each of the circular motions, we see that both the angular velocities are equal to each other and the value is 4 rad/s.

Question: A particle A of mass m initially at rest slides down a height of 1.25 m on a frictionless ramp, collides with and sticks to an identical particle B of mass m at rest as shown in the figure. Then particles A and B together collide elastically with particle C of mass 2 m at rest. The speed of particle A after the collision with particle B would be : (g = 10 m/s2)
Solution:
For velocity of A just before collides with B
\[mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv_A^2 = {v_A} = \sqrt {2gh} = \sqrt {2 \times 10 \times 1 \times 1.25} = 5\]

Conservation of linear momentum

\[m{v_A} + 0 = \left( {2m} \right)v \Rightarrow v = \frac{{{v_A}}}{2} = 2.5m/s\]

Question: A closed loop PQRS carrying a current is placed in a uniform magnetic field. If the magnetic forces on segments PS, SR and RQ are F1, F2 and F3 respectively and are in the plane of the paper and along the directions shown, the force on the segment QP is
Solution:
$${F_{Horizontal}} = {F_3} - {F_1}$$ $${F_{Vertical}} = {F_2}$$
Since net force is zero, Force along QP is equal to the resultant, but in opposite direction.

So, $${F_{QP}} = {F_{\operatorname{Re} s}} = \sqrt {{{\left( {{F_3} - {F_1}} \right)}^2} + {F_2}^2} $$

Question: The capacitance (C) of a conductor or condenser is defined as
Options: ratio of charge to its potential
ratio of potential to the charge
Work done per unit charge on it
work done to circulate unit positive charge through complete circuit
Solution: The capacitance of a conductor or a condenser is defined as the ratio of charge to its potential. Mathematically, this is given by C = Q/V where, Q is the charge and V is the potential.

Question: An electric field is spread uniformly in Y-axis. Consider a point A as origin point. The coordinates of point B are equal to (0, 2) m. The coordinates of point C are (2, 0) m. At points A B, and C, electric potentials are$${V_A},{V_B}\,and\,\,{V_C},$$ respectively. From the following options, which is correct?

Question: If $\frac{{\left( {6x + 4y} \right)}}{{\left( {6x - 4y} \right)}} = \frac{8}{6}$ then what is the value of $\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{y^2}}}$?
Solution:
Given:

The expression $\frac{{\left( {6x + 4y} \right)}}{{\left( {6x - 4y} \right)}} = \frac{8}{6}$

Calculation

The expression $\frac{{\left( {6x + 4y} \right)}}{{\left( {6x - 4y} \right)}} = \frac{8}{6}$

Cross multiply,

$36x + 24y = 48x - 32y$

$24y + 32y = 12x$

$56y = 12x$

$\frac{x}{y} = \frac{{56}}{{12}}$

$\frac{x}{y} = \frac{{14}}{3}$

Then \[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{y^2}}} = \frac{{{{\left( {14} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( 3 \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{196}}{9}\]

Therefore, the value of $\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{y^2}}}$ is $\frac{{196}}{9}.$

Question: A lump of clay of mass 10 g travelling with a velocity of 10 cm<sup>–1</sup> towards east collides head on with another lump of clay of mass 10 g travelling with velocity of 20 cm<sup>–1</sup> towards west. If the two lumps coalesce after collision, what is its velocity, if no external forces act on the system ?
Solution:
Let us assume East is and West is

\[v = \frac{{{m_1}{v_1} + {m_2}{v_2}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}} = \frac{{\left( {0.01} \right)\left( {10} \right) + \left( {0.01} \right)\left( { - 20} \right)}}{{0.02}} = - 5\,m{s^{ - 1}}\]

\[ = 5\,m{s^{ - 1}}\]towards West

Question: The driving side belt has a tension of 1600 N and the slack side has 500 N tension. The belt turns a pulley 40 cm in radius at a rate of 300 rpm. This pulley drives a dynamo having 90% efficiency. How many kilowatts are being delivered by the dynamo ?
Solution:
Work done on pulley = Net work done by torque of 1600N and 500N

\[W = \int {\tau d\theta = {\tau _{net}}\left( \theta \right)} \]

\[Power = \frac{{dW}}{{dt}} = {\tau _{net}}\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}} = {F_{net}}r\omega \]

\[ = (1600 - 500)\left( {0.4} \right)\frac{{\left( {300} \right)}}{{60}} \times 2\pi \]

\[ = 13.8 \times {10^3}Watt\]

\[\therefore \]The efficiency is 90%

Power delivered =0.9 (13.8)kW

=12.4 kW.

Question: The motion of a particle is given x = A sin ωt + B cos ωt. The motion of the particle is
Options: simple harmonic with amplitude A + B
Solution:
$x = A\sin \omega t + B\cos \omega t$
$A = D\cos \phi {\text{ }}...\left( 1 \right)$

$B = D\sin \phi {\text{ }}...\left( 2 \right)$

$x = D\cos \phi \sin \omega t + D\sin \phi \sin \omega t$

$x = D\sin \left( {\omega t + \phi } \right)$

From (1) and (2)

${A^2} + {B^2} = {D^2}{\cos ^2}\phi + {D^2}{\sin ^2}\phi $

${D^2} = {A^2} + {B^2}$

$D = \sqrt {{A^2} + {B^2}} $

Question: An ideal choke draws a current of 8 A when connected to an AC supply of 100 V, 50 Hz. A pure resistor draws a current of 10 A when connected to the same source. The ideal choke and the resistor are connected in series and then connected to the AC source of 150 V, 40 Hz. The current in the circuit becomes
Solution:
Resistance, \[R = \frac{{100}}{{10}} = 10\Omega \]
Inductive reactance, XL = 2πfL
\[\frac{{100}}{8} = 2\pi \times 50 \times L\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,L = \frac{1}{{8\pi }}H\]
\[{X'_L} = 2\pi f'L\]
\[ = 2\pi \times 40 \times \frac{1}{{8\pi }} = 10\Omega \]
Impedance of the circuit,
\[Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + X_L^2}  = \sqrt {{{\left( {10} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {10} \right)}^2}}  = 10\sqrt 2 {\mkern 1mu} \Omega \]
Current in the circuit,
\[i = \frac{V}{Z} = \frac{{150}}{{10\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{15}}{{\sqrt 2 }}A\]

Question: A coach of a uniformly moving train is suddenly detached from the train and stops after covering some distance. The distance covered by the coach and distance covered by the train in the same time has relation (assume constant acceleration)
Options: First will be half of second
Solution:
Let a be the retardation of coach then distance covered by it be s.
If u is the initial velocity of coach after detaching from train (i.e., uniform speed of train), then we have

\[{v^2} = {u^2} + 2as\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,0 = {u^2} - 2a{s_b}\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,{s_b} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2a}}\]

time take by coach to stop

\[ \Rightarrow \,\,v = u + at\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,0 = u - at\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,t = \frac{u}{a}\]

In this time t distance travelled by train is

\[{s_t} = ut = \frac{{{u^2}}}{a}\]

Hence, the ratio is

\[\frac{{{s_b}}}{{{s_t}}} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{{{u^2}}}{{2a}}} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{{{u^2}}}{a}} \right)}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,{s_b} = 0.5{s_t}\]

Thus, the distance covered by the coach will be half of the distance covered by train.

Question: Two particles A and B start moving due to their mutual interaction only. If at any time ‘t’\[{\overrightarrow a _A}\]and \[{\overrightarrow a _B}\]are their respective accelerations, \[{\overrightarrow v _A}\]and \[{\overrightarrow v _B}\]are their respective velocities, and up to that time W<sub>A</sub> and W<sub>B</sub> are the work done on A and B, respectively by the mutual force, m<sub>A</sub> and m<sub>B</sub> are their masses, respectively, then which of the following is always correct.
Solution:
$\because \,\,{\overrightarrow F _{net}}\,$system = 0

\[\because {m_A}\overrightarrow {{a_A}} + {m_B}\overrightarrow {{a_B}} = 0\]

${\overrightarrow P _{_{system}}}$= conserved

Initially both are at rest

\[\therefore {M_{system}}\overrightarrow {{V_{cm}}} = 0\]

\[\therefore \overrightarrow {{V_{cm}}} = 0\]

Hence

\[\frac{{{m_A}\overrightarrow {{v_A}} + {m_B}\overrightarrow {{v_B}} }}{{{m_A} + {m_B}}} = 0\]

\[{m_A}\overrightarrow {{v_A}} + {m_B}\overrightarrow {{v_B}} = 0\]

\[\frac{{{m_A}\overrightarrow {{v_A}} + {m_B}\overrightarrow {{v_B}} }}{{{m_A} + {m_B}}} = 0\]

\[{m_A}\overrightarrow {{v_A}} + {m_B}\overrightarrow {{v_B}} = 0\]

Question: Charges 4Q, q and Q are placed along x-axis at position $$x = 0,x = l/2\,\,and\,x = l,$$ respectively. Find the value of q, so that force on charge Q is zero.
Solution:
From Coulomb’s law, the force acting between two charges (q1, q2) separated at a distance r is given by
$$F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}$$
Total force acting on charge Q is
$$F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{qQ}}{{{{\left( {l/2} \right)}^2}}} + \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{4Q \cdot Q}}{{{{\left( l \right)}^2}}}$$
According to question, F = 0
$$\eqalign{
  & \therefore \,\,\,\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{qQ}}{{{{\left( {l/2} \right)}^2}}} + \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{4{Q^2}}}{{{{\left( l \right)}^2}}} = 0 \cr
  & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,q = - Q \cr} $$

Question: If reactive impedance is \[\sqrt 3 \] times of resistance in a series RLC circuit, then find phase difference.
Solution:
From the relation,\[\tan \phi = \frac{{{X_L} - {X_C}}}{R}\]
where, XLC is the net impedance of the circuit

\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\phi = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{{X_L} - {X_C}}}{R}} \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{Z}{R}} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\phi = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sqrt 3 R/R} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\phi = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sqrt 3 } \right) = 60^\circ \]

Question: A boat man could row his boat with a speed 10 m/sec. He wants to take his boat from P to a point Q just opposite on the other bank of the river flowing at a speed 4 m/sec. He should row his boat
Options: At right angle to the stream
At an angle of \[{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{2}{5}} \right)\] with PQ up the stream
At an angle of \[{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{2}{5}} \right)\] with PQ down the stream
At an angle of \[{\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{2}{5}} \right)\] with PQ up the stream
Solution:
Let \[{v_{b,r}}\] be the velocity of the boat w.r.t the river

\[\sin \theta = \frac{{{v_s}}}{{{v_{b,r}}}}\]

\[\theta = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{4}{{10}}} \right)\]

\[\theta = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{2}{5}} \right)\] with PQ up the stream.

Question: In a full wave rectifier, the rms value of AC component of the waves
Solution:
Due to barrier voltage of p-n junction used as a full wave rectifier, its r.m.s value of a.c. component of wave is more than d.c. value.
We musk know that DC value of a full wave rectifier
Given as,
${V_{DC}} = \frac{{2{V_{peak}}}}{\pi } = 0.637{V_{peak}}$
And, the rms voltage of a full wave rectifier is given as,
${V_{rms}} = \frac{{{V_{peak}}}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 0.71{V_{peak}}$
So, we can easily understand that the rms voltage of full wave rectifier is more than the DC value of the same team.
