Question:
Assertion: The voltage and current in LCR circuit is in the same phase at resonance.
Reason: At resonance, inductive reactance and capacitive reactance has zero value.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Phase angle is given by $\tan \phi \,\, = \,\,\frac{{{V_L}\,\, - \,\,{V_C}}}{{{V_R}}}\,\, = \,\,\frac{{{X_L}\,\, - \,\,{X_C}}}{R};$ at resonance, ${X_L}\,\, = \,\,{X_C};$so $\tan \phi \,\, = \,\,0$ and voltage and current become in same phase.

Question:
Assertion: Two parallel beams of electrons moving with normal velocities in vacuum repel.
Reason: Electrical repulsive force is stronger than magnetic force between currents.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution:
If two charges q1 and q2 are moving with velocities v1 and v2, respectively, and at any instant the distance between them is r, then

Magnetic force between two charges is $${F_m}\,\, = \,\,\frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\,.\,\,\frac{{{q_1}{q_2}{v_1}{v_2}}}{{{r^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$$

and Electric force between two charges is $${F_e}\,\, = \,\,\frac{1}{{4\pi \,{\varepsilon _0}}}\,.\,\,\frac{{{q_1}\,{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}\,\,\left( 2 \right)$$

where c is the velocity of light in vacuum. Therefore,

 $$\frac{{{F_m}}}{{{F_e}}}\,\, = \,\,{\left( {\frac{v}{c}} \right)^2}.$$

As $$v\,\, < \,\,c,\,\,so\,\,{F_m}\,\, < \,\,{F_e}$$. The nature of force between two parallel charge beams decided by electric force, as it is dominator.

Question: An L-C-R series circuit consists of a resistance of 10 Ω, a capacitor of reactance 60 Ω and an inductor coil. The circuit is found to resonate when put across a 300 V, 100 Hz supply. The inductance of coil is (Take, π = 3 )

ω0 = 2 × 3 × 100 \[\left( {\because \,\,\pi = 3} \right)\]
= 600 rad/s
Further \[{\omega _0} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {LC} }}\]…(i)
Also, \[{X_C} = \frac{1}{{C{\omega _0}}} = 60\Omega = {X_L}\]
\[{\omega _0}L = 60\Omega \]
\[L = \frac{{60}}{{600}} = 0.1H\]

Question: Which of the following is largest, when the height attained by the projectile is the largest
Solution:
Height attained by a projectile is given by;
\[ \Rightarrow H = \frac{{{u^2}\sin \theta }}{{2g}}\]

Thus, H is maximum, only when angle of projection is\[90^\circ \].

Range for \[\theta = 90^\circ ;\]

\[ \Rightarrow R = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 2\theta }}{g} = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 180^\circ }}{g} = 0\]

Time of flight (T) for \[\theta = 90^\circ ;\]

\[ \Rightarrow T = \frac{{2u\sin \theta }}{g} = \frac{{2u}}{g}\]

Thus, time of flight (T) will be maximum

Question: A wire $ PQR $ is bent as shown in figure and is placed in a region of uniform magnetic field $ B $ . The length of $ PQ = QR = l $ . A current $ I $ ampere flows through the wire as shown. The magnitude of the force on $ PQ $ and $ QR $ will be
Solution:
$ \vec F = I\left( {\vec l \times \vec B} \right) $ or, $ \vec F = IBl\sin \theta $
Since $ PQ $ is parallel to the direction of magnetic field, $ \theta = 0^\circ $

 $ {F_{PQ}} = IlB\sin {0^ \circ } = 0 $

Since $ QR $ is perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field, $ \therefore $ $ \theta = 90^\circ $

 $ {F_{QR}} = IlB\sin 90^\circ = BIl $

Question: An oil drop having a mass 4.8×10-10 g and charge 2.4×10-18C stands still between two charged horizontal plates separated by a distance of 1 cm. If now the polarity of the plates is changed, instantaneous acceleration of the drop is (g = 10ms-2)
Solution:
As drop is in equilibrium,$$mg = qE$$
$$ \Rightarrow E = \frac{{mg}}{q}$$
$$E = \frac{{4.8 \times {{10}^{ - 10}} \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 10}}{{2.4 \times {{10}^{ - 18}}}} = 2 \times {10^6}\,\,V/m$$
Now, when polarity is changed, force from electric field also comes in the direction of mg.
So, $${F_{net}} = mg + qE = ma$$
$$ \Rightarrow a = g + \frac{q}{m}E$$
$$a = 10 + \frac{{2.4 \times {{10}^{ - 18}} \times 2 \times {{10}^6}}}{{4.8 \times {{10}^{ - 10}} \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = 20\,m{s^{ - 2}}$$

Question: The force of cohesion is :
Options: same in solid, liquid and gas
Solution:
The strong force of attraction which occurs between the same molecules is the cohesion force. Water’s unified force is due to the bond of hydrogen between water molecules. It requires isolation to be avoided by the liquid molecules. In order to create surface stress on water, cohesion binds hydrogen bonds together. The hydrogen bond with the hydrogen atom is formed by electronegative oxygen.
Order of Force solid > liquid > gas

Question: A resistance of 20Ω is connected to a source of an alternating potential\[V = 220sin\left( {100\pi t} \right)\]. The time taken by the current to change from the peak value to rms value, is
Solution:
The resistance will not create any phase difference
\[\omega = 100\pi \]
\[T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega } = \frac{{2\pi }}{{100\pi }} = \frac{1}{{50}}{\text{ }}s\]
Time taken to reach ${i_{rms}}$
\[{t_{rms}} = \frac{T}{8}\]
Similarly, \[{t_{peak}} = \frac{T}{4}\]
\[\therefore \Delta T = {t_{peak}} - {t_{rms}} = \frac{T}{4} - \frac{T}{8} = \frac{T}{8}\]
\[\therefore \Delta T = \frac{1}{{50 \times 8}} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{ }}s\]

Question: In the connections shown in the given figure below, the equivalent capacity between A and B will be
Solution:
Here, $${C_2},{C_4}$$ and $${C_5}$$ are in parallel combination.
So, $${C_2} + {C_4} + {C_5} = 2 + 2 + 2$$
$$ = 6 = {C_{2eq}}$$
Now, $${C_1},{C_{2eq}}$$ and $${C_3}$$ are in series.
So, $$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{{{C_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_{2eq}}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_3}}}$$
 $$ = 1 + \frac{1}{6} + 1 = \frac{{6 + 1 + 6}}{6} = \frac{{13}}{6}$$
$$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,C = \frac{6}{{13}}$$

Question: Which of the following graphs correctly represents the variation of $b =  - \frac{{\left( {\frac{{dV}}{{dP}}} \right)}}{V}$with P for an ideal gas at constant temperature
Solution:
For isothermal process,
PV = constant
$p\left( {dV} \right) + V\left( {dP} \right) = 0$
$\beta = - \frac{1}{V}\left( {\frac{{dV}}{{dP}}} \right) = \frac{1}{P}$
So graph between $\beta $and P will be hyperbola.

Question: Two blocks A and B of masses 5 kg and 3 kg respectively rest on a smooth horizontal surface with B over A. The coefficient of friction between A and B is 0.5. The maximum horizontal force (in kg wt.) that can be applied to A, so that there will be motion of A and B without relative slipping, is
Two equal forces (F each) act a point inclined to each other at an angle of 120°. The magnitude of their resultant is
Solution:
\[\left| {\overrightarrow R } \right| = \sqrt {{F^2} + {F^2} + 2{F^2}\cos \theta } = \sqrt {2{F^2} + 2{F^2}\left( { - \frac{1}{2}} \right)} \]

\[\left\{ {\because \cos 120^\circ = - \frac{1}{2}} \right\}\]

\[ \Rightarrow R = F\]

Question: Two bodies having masses m<sub>1</sub> and m<sub>2 </sub>and velocity  \[{\vec u_1}\,and\,\,{\vec u_2}\]collide and form a composite system. If \[{m_1}{\vec u_1} + {m_2}{\vec u_2} = 0\left( {{m_1} \ne {m_2}} \right),\]the velocity of composite system will be :
Solution:
\[{\vec P_{system}} = {m_1}{\vec u_1} + {m_2}{\vec u_2}\]
\[{\vec P_{system}} = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow {M_{system}} \cdot {\vec V_{cm}} = 0\]

Question: A wire of length 1 m placed in x-z plane carries a current of 1 ampere. The coefficient of friction between the wire and the surface is 0.2 and mass of the wire is 1 kg .The magnetic field of strength 2 T exists along positive y - axis . Then choose the correct option.
Options: wire will not move at all
Solution:
Magnetic force on wire $\overrightarrow F = i\left( {\overrightarrow l \times \overrightarrow B } \right)$
As wire is in x-z plane and B is along y-axis.
$\therefore F = ilB\sin 90^\circ $
$\therefore F = 1 \times 1 \times 2 \times 1$
$F = 2{\text{ }}N$
Maximum frictional force $fe = \mu N$
$ = 0.2 \times 1 \times 10 = 2{\text{ N}}$
Therefore wire will not move.

Question: A light source is at a distance d from a photoelectric cell, then the number of photoelectrons emitted from the cell is n. If the distance of light source and cell is reduced to half, then the number of photoelectrons emitted will become
Solution:
The intensity of photoelectrons is directly proportional to the number of photons incident on the surface.
Therefore, ${\text{Intensity for point source}} \propto \frac{1}{{{\text{distanc}}{{\text{e}}^2}}}$
If the distance of light source and cell is reduced to half, then the number of photoelectrons emitted will become 4 times.

Question:
Using componendo and dividendo find out the value of x.
$\frac{{\sqrt {3x + 4} + \sqrt {3x - 5} }}{{\sqrt {3x + 4} - \sqrt {3x - 5} }} = 9$
Solution:
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{\left( {\sqrt {3x + 4} + \sqrt {3x - 5} } \right) + \left( {\sqrt {3x + 4} - \sqrt {3x - 5} } \right)}}{{\left( {\sqrt {3x + 4} + \sqrt {3x - 5} } \right) - \left( {\sqrt {3x + 4} - \sqrt {3x - 5} } \right)}}$

$ = \frac{{9 + 1}}{{9 - 1}}$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{2\sqrt {3x + 4} }}{{2\sqrt {3x - 5} }} = \frac{{10}}{8} = \frac{5}{4}$

⇒ Simplifying and squaring both sides

$\frac{{3x + 4}}{{3x - 5}} = \frac{{25}}{{16}}$

$48x + 64 = 75x - 125$

$64 + 125 = 75x - 48x$

$189 = 27x$

$x = 7$

Question: The capacity of a parallel plate condenser increases four times if the air between the plate is replaced by glass. The permittivity of glass will be

Velocity and acceleration of a particle initially are \[\vec v = (3\hat i + 4\hat j)m/s\] and \[\vec a =  - \left( {6\hat i + 8\hat j} \right)m/{s^2}\] respectively. Initially particle is at origin. Maximum x–coordinate of particle will be:
Solution:
\[{{\text{u}}_x} = 3\;{\text{m}}/{\text{s}},{{\text{a}}_x} = - 6\;{\text{m}}/{\text{s}}\]
\[x = {{\text{u}}_x}{\text{t}} + \frac{1}{2}{{\text{a}}_x}{{\text{t}}^2}\]

For

\[{\text{x}} \to {\text{ maximum,}}\]

\[\frac{{{\text{d}}x}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = {{\text{V}}_x} = {{\text{u}}_x} + {{\text{a}}_x}{\text{t}} = 0\]

\[{\text{t}} = - \frac{{{{\text{u}}_x}}}{{{{\text{a}}_x}}} = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}\]

\[\therefore {x_{\max }} = 3 \times \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} \times ( - 6){\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {x_{\max }} = \frac{3}{2} - \frac{3}{4} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75\;{\text{m}}\]

Question:
Statement–1 : It is not possible to know details of final motion just by knowing details of initial motion and without knowing the forces which are changing the motion of the system.
Statement–2 : \[{\vec F_{ext}} = \frac{{d\vec P}}{{dt}}\]
Options: Both Statement-1 and Statement-2 are true and Statement-2 is the correct explanation for Statement-1.
Both Statement-1 and Statement-2 are true but Statement-2 is not the correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement-1 is true and the Statement-2 is false.
Statement-1 is false and the Statement-2 is true.
Solution:
\[{F_{ext}} = \frac{{d\vec P}}{{dt}}\], where the acceleration of the object is, \[\vec P = m\vec V\]. The \[\vec P\]denotes the direction of the motion of the system with respect to time.

The object changes its position over time is called motion and the force causes it to change the system velocity or acceleration.

Now, according to the question; It is not possible to know the details of final motion just by knowing details of initial motion without forces, which causes the change in direction of the motion of the system.

So, both the statements are correct but the second statement is not the correct explanation of the first statement.

Question:
In the nuclear decay given below
\[_Z^AX\xrightarrow[{}]{}\,_{z + 1}^{\,\,\,\,A}Y\xrightarrow[{}]{}\,_{Z - 1}^{A - 4}{B^*}\xrightarrow[{}]{}\,_{Z - 1}^{A - 4}B,\]
 the particles emitted in the sequence are
Solution:
Nuclear decay reaction,
${}_Z^AX\xrightarrow{\beta }{}_{Z + 1}^AY + {}_{ - 1}^0\beta \xrightarrow{\alpha }{}_{Z - 1}^{A - 4}{B^*} + {}_2^4\alpha \xrightarrow{\gamma }{}_{Z - 1}^{A - 4}B + {}_0^0\gamma $$* \to {\text{excited state}}$
The emission of the beta particle increases the atomic number by one unit and the emission of the alpha particle reduces the mass number by 4 units and atomic number by 2 units, which causes the product element reaches to the excited state to emit gamma particle with no charge and mass number.

Question:
Assertion: If a body is thrown upwards, the distance covered by it in the last second of upward motion is about 5 m irrespective of its initial speed.
Reason: The distance covered in the last second of upward motion is equal to that covered in the first second of downward motion when the particle is dropped.
Options: If both assertion and reason are correct and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false
If assertion is false but reason is true
Solution:
Let’s assume that the speed of upward projection is u. Particle will have zero velocity when it reaches the highest point at time t, so final velocity (v) = 0 m/sec. We know that;
$ \Rightarrow v = u + \left( { - g} \right)t$

$ \Rightarrow 0 = u + \left( { - g} \right)t$

$ \Rightarrow u = gt$

Particle’s height from ground at t second;

$ \Rightarrow {s_t} = ut - \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}$

Particle’s height from ground at (t - 1) second;

$ \Rightarrow {s_{t - 1}} = u\left( {t - 1} \right) - \frac{1}{2}g{\left( {t - 1} \right)^2}$

Subtracting these equations to find distance covered in the last second of upward motion.

$ \Rightarrow {s_t} - {s_{t - 1}} = u - gt + \frac{g}{2}$

We know that u = gt (solved above);

$ \Rightarrow {s_t} - {s_{t - 1}} = gt - gt + \frac{g}{2}$

$ \Rightarrow {s_t} - {s_{t - 1}} = \frac{g}{2}$ , which is a constant value.

$ \Rightarrow {s_t} - {s_{t - 1}} = 5{\text{ }}m$

Thus, distance covered in the last second of the upward motion is not dependent on the initial velocity, and it is a constant (5 m). Thus, the assertion is correct.

In case of downward motion, we will also get distance covered in the first second independent of initial velocity, and it will be equal to the distance covered in the last second in case of upward motion.

Thus, both assertion and reason are correct, and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.

Question: The gap between the valence and conduction bands of a certain semiconductor is 0.85 eV. When this semiconductor is used to form a light emitting diode, the wavelength of the light emitted.
Options: is in a range above 1.5×10<sup>–6</sup> m
is in a range below 1.5×10<sup>–6</sup> m
is in a range centered on 1.5×10<sup>–6</sup> m.
Solution:
\[{E_g}(Energy\,{\text{Gap}}) = \frac{{12400}}{{\lambda \left( {{\text{in }}{{\text{A}}^0}} \right)}}{\text{ }}eV\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  (Threshold\,wavelength){\text{ }}{\lambda _0} = \frac{{12400}}{{0.85}} = 15000{\text{ }}{{\text{A}}^0} \\
   \Rightarrow {\lambda _0} = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 6}}{\text{ }}m \\
\end{gathered} \]
Only signals having wavelength less than threshold wavelength will be detected.
\[\lambda < {\lambda _0}\]
Hence, wavelength of light emitted is in the range below \[1.5 \times {10^{ - 6}}m\]

Question: A disc of moment of inertia I<sub>1</sub> is rotating freely with angular velocity ω<sub>1</sub> when a second, non-rotating disc with moment of inertia I<sub>2</sub> is dropped on it gently the two then rotate as a unit. Then the total angular speed is :

Taking both the disk as a system, there is no external torque and thus angular momentum remains conserved.
\[{I_i}{\omega _i} = {I_f}{\omega _f}\]

\[0 + {I_1}{\omega _1} = \left( {{I_1} + {I_2}} \right)\omega \]

\[ \Rightarrow \omega = \frac{{{I_1}{\omega _1}}}{{\left( {{I_1} + {I_2}} \right)}}\]

Question: In an L-C-R series, AC circuit at resonance
Options: The capacitive reactance is more than the inductive reactance
The capacitive reactance equals the inductive reactance
The capacitive reactance is less than the inductive reactance
Solution:
The impedance of a series L-C-R circuit is
\[Z = \sqrt {{{\left( {{X_L} - {X_C}} \right)}^2} + {R^2}} \]
At resonance (series resonant circuit),
XL = XC

Question:
Assertion: Electric dipole in non-uniform electric field may have translatory and rotational motion both.
Reason: Net force is always zero on dipole in non-uniform electric field.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: When an electric dipole is placed in a non-uniform electric field, the two charges of dipole experiences unequal forces, therefore, the net force on the dipole is not equal to zero. Due to two unequal forces, a torque is produced which rotate the dipole so as to align it in the direction of the field. When the dipole gets aligned with the field, the torque becomes zero and then the unbalanced force acts on the dipole and the dipole then moves linearly along the direction of field from weaker portion of the field to the stronger portion of the field. So in non-uniform electric field, motion of the dipole is translatory and rotatory both.

Question: How long will the passenger sitting at the window of a train travelling at a speed of 54 kmh<sup>-1</sup> see a train, passing by in the opposite directions with a speed of 36 kmh<sup>-1</sup>, if the length of the second train is 150 m?

\[ = 90 \times \frac{5}{{18}}m{s^{ - 1}}\]

\[ = 25m{s^{ - 1}}\]

Time duration to pass second train

\[ = \frac{{150}}{{25}} = 6s\]

\[\therefore N = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r},{f_\ell } = \frac{{\mu m{v^2}}}{r}\]

Question: Which of the following pairs of physical quantities does not have same dimensional formula?

Question: The resultant of two vectors \[\vec A\,\,and\,\,\vec B\] is perpendicular   to the vector\[\vec A\]  and its magnitude is equal to half of the magnitude of vector \[\vec B.\] Then the angle between \[\vec A\] and \[\vec B\] is
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow R = \overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B \,;\,\,\overrightarrow R \,\,{\text{is}}\,\,{\text{perpendicular}}\,\,{\text{to}}\,\,\overrightarrow A \]

\[ \Rightarrow \tan \alpha = \frac{{B\sin \theta }}{{A + B\cos \theta }} \Rightarrow A + B\cos \theta = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{{ - A}}{B}\]

\[R = \frac{B}{2} \Rightarrow {R^2} = {A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{B^2}}}{4} = {A^2} + {B^2} - 2{A^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {A^2} = \frac{{3{B^2}}}{4} \Rightarrow \frac{A}{B} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{{ - \sqrt 3 }}{2} \Rightarrow \theta = {150^0}\]

Question: A uniform rod is kept on a frictionless horizontal table and two forces F1 and F2 are acted as shown in figure. The line of action of force \[{F_{{R_1}}}\] (which produces same torque) is at a perpendicular distance ‘C’ from O. Now F1 and F2 are interchanged and F1 is reversed. The new forces \[{F_{{R_2}}}\] (which produces torque of same magnitude in the present case) has its line of action at a distance \[\frac{C}{2}\] from O. If the \[{F_{{R_1}}}\] : \[{F_{{R_2}}}\] in the ratio 2:1, then a: b is (assume \[{F_2}a > {F_1}b):\]
Solution:
Torque by ${F_{{R_1}}}$ = Net Torque by \[{{\text{F}}_{\text{1}}}\], and \[{{\text{F}}_2}\].

\[{F_{{R_1}}}(C) = {{\text{F}}_1}{\text{a}} + {{\text{F}}_2}{\text{b}} \ldots \ldots \ldots (1)\]


\[{{\text{F}}_1}\] and \[{{\text{F}}_2}\] are interchange d and \[{F_1}\] is reversed. Torque = Net torque by \[{{\text{F}}_1}\] and \[{{\text{F}}_2}\].

\[{{\text{F}}_{{{\text{R}}_{\text{2}}}}}\left( {\frac{{\text{C}}}{{\text{2}}}} \right) = {{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{a}} - {{\text{F}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{b}} \ldots \ldots \ldots ..(2)\]

On dividing and (1) and (2)

\[\frac{{{{\text{F}}_{{{\text{R}}_{\text{1}}}}}}}{{{{\text{F}}_{{{\text{R}}_{\text{2}}}}}}}(2) = \frac{{{{\text{F}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{a + }}{{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{b}}}}{{{{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{a - }}{{\text{F}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{b}}}} = 4\;\;\;{\kern 1pt} \left( {\because {{\text{F}}_{{{\text{R}}_{\text{1}}}}}:{{\text{F}}_{{{\text{R}}_{\text{2}}}}} = 2:1} \right)\]

\[ \Rightarrow {{\text{F}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{a + }}{{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{b = 4}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{a - 4}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{b}}\]

\[\left( {{\text{4}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ - }}{{\text{F}}_{\text{1}}}} \right){\text{a = }}\left( {{{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + 4}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{1}}}} \right){\text{b}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{\text{a}}}{{\text{b}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + 4}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{\text{4}}{{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ - }}{{\text{F}}_{\text{1}}}}}\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

Question: Graph of force per unit length between two long parallel currents carrying conductor and the distance between them is
Solution:
The force per unit length between two parallel wires carrying currents i1 and i2 separated by a distance R is given by
\[\frac{F}{l} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }}\,\frac{{{I_1}{I_2}}}{R}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{F}{l} \propto \frac{l}{R}\]
Hence, graph between force per unit length and distance between wires is a rectangular hyperbola.

Question: The space between two plates of a condenser is filled with two dielectric media of thickness t1 and t2 and dielectric constant k1 and k2 respectively. The capacity of the condenser is given by
A nucleus <sub>n</sub>X<sup>m</sup> emits one α and two β–particles. The resulting nucleus is
Solution:
The emission of $\alpha - $decay decreases the mass number by 4 units and the atomic number by 2 units, and the emission of $\beta - $decay increases the atomic number by one unit but the mass number remains the same.
Hence, the decay will be,
$X_n^m\xrightarrow{\alpha }{}_{n - 2}^{m - 4}Y\xrightarrow{{2\beta }}{}_{n - 2 + 2}^{m - 4}Z$

Question: In an adiabatic process the quantity which remains constant is:
Solution: Adiabatic process is a thermodynamic process in which total heat of the system remains constant. Thus, there is no transaction of heat between system and surrounding.

Question: When germanium is doped with phosphorus, the doped material has :
Solution: Phosphorous is a pentavalent impurity, hence the doped material has more negative current carriers.

Question:
A spring balance is attached to the ceiling of a lift. A man hangs his bag on the spring and the spring reads 49 N, when the lift is stationary. If the lift moves downward with
an acceleration of $5\,m/{s^2}$, the reading of the spring balance will be
Solution:
$m = \frac{{49}}{{9.8}} = 5\,kg$
$R = m\left( {g - a} \right) = 5 \times \left( {9.8 - 5} \right) = 24N$

Question: A block B of mass 5 kg is placed on another block A of mass 10 kg, which rests on a smooth horizontal surface. If $\mu $ = 0.4 between A and B and a force F = 40 N is applied on block B, the acceleration of A is :
Solution:
Mass of block A, 10kg
Mass of block B, 5kg
Frictional coefficient between block A and B, $\mu = 0.4$
Frictional coefficient between block A and B,
$ \Rightarrow f = \mu {m_B}g = 0.4 \times 5 \times 10 = 20\;{\text{N}}$
Because block A rests on a smooth surface, so only frictional force will make it move, so we can write;
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow f = {m_A} \times a \\
   \Rightarrow 20 = 10 \times a \\
   \Rightarrow a = 2\;{\text{m}}/{{\text{s}}^2} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A uniform rope of mass m hangs freely from a ceiling. A bird of mass M climbs up the rope with an acceleration a. The force exerted by the rope on the ceiling is :
Options: dependent on the position of bird on the rope
Solution:
When bird climbs up the rope with acceleration a then
T – Mg = Ma
⇒ T = M(g + a)
The force exerted by rope on the ceiling is = T + mg = M (g + a) + mg

Question: A block of mass 3 kg is placed on a rough horizontal surface $\left( {\mu  = {\text{ }}0.4} \right)$. A force of 8.7 N is applied on the block. The force of friction between the block and floor is?
Solution:
${f_{ms}} = {\mu _s}mg = 0.4 \times 3 \times 10\;{\text{N}} = 12\;{\text{N}}$
Since the applied force is less than 12 N therefore the force of friction is equal to the applied force.
$f = 8.7N$

Question: Two masses m1 and m2 are attached to a string which passes over a frictionless smooth pulley. When m1 = 10 kg, m2 = 6 kg, the acceleration of masses is
Solution:
Let the common tension in the string is T and common acceleration of blocks is ‘a’ as shown in figure.
For 6 kg block T - 6g = 6a ...(1)
For 10 kg block 10g – T =10a ...(2)
Adding (1) and (2)
$\begin{gathered}
  T - 6g + 10g - T = 6a + 10a \\
   \Rightarrow 4\;{\text{g}} = 16{\text{a}} \Rightarrow {\text{a}} = \frac{{4g}}{{16}} \\
   \Rightarrow a = \frac{g}{4} = \frac{{10}}{4} = 2.5\;{\text{m}}/{{\text{s}}^2} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Pushing force making an angle θ to the horizontal is applied on a block of weight W placed on a horizontal table. If the angle of friction be \[\phi \], the magnitude of force required to move the body is equal to :
Solution:
Angle of friction is $\phi $, so frictional coefficient will be;
$ \Rightarrow \mu = \tan \phi $
As shown in the above diagram, we balance the forces acting in horizontal and vertical directions.
Forces in vertical direction;
$ \Rightarrow N = W + F\sin \theta $
Forces in horizontal direction;
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow F\cos \theta = \mu N \\
   \Rightarrow F\cos \theta = (\tan \phi )(W + F\sin \theta ) \\
   \Rightarrow F\cos \theta - F\sin \theta \tan \phi = W\tan \phi \\
   \Rightarrow F\cos \theta \cos \phi - F\sin \theta \sin \phi = W\sin \phi \\
   \Rightarrow F = \frac{{W\sin \phi }}{{\cos (\theta + \phi )}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question:
A person used force (F), shown in the figure to move a load with a constant velocity on a given surface.

Identify the correct surface profile:
Solution: In figure no. (a) and (c), a constant force equal to mg sinq is required. After reaching the highest point, in case of figure (c), no force is required but in case of figure (a), body travels on its own. In this way, figure (a) represents the given F- x curve.

Question: A body of mass m rests on horizontal surface. The coefficient of friction between the body and the surface is m. If the mass is pulled by a force P as shown in the figure, the limiting friction between body and surface will be :
Solution:
Limiting friction force is given by;
$ \Rightarrow f = \mu N$
Where, N is the normal force , and m is the frictional coefficient.
We need to balance forces acting on the block in vertical direction;
$ \Rightarrow P\sin 30^\circ + N = mg$
$ \Rightarrow N = mg - P \times \frac{1}{2}$
So, limiting frictional force will be;
$ \Rightarrow f = \mu N = \mu \left( {mg - P \times \frac{1}{2}} \right)$

Question: If a body loses half of its velocity on penetrating 3 cm in a wooden block, then how much will it penetrate more before coming to rest?
Solution:
Let the acceleration due to retarding force exerted by
wooden block is $a = \frac{{ - F}}{m}$uniform.
Let, initial velocity = u and on travelling S1 cm = 3 final
velocity become $\frac{u}{2}$due to retardation
So,
$\begin{gathered}
  \because {v^2} = {u^2} + 2as \\
   \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{u}{2}} \right)^2} = {u^2} - 2a{S_1} \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{{{u^2}}}{4} - {u^2} = - 2a{S_1} \Rightarrow - \frac{3}{4}{u^2} = - 2a{S_1} \\
   \Rightarrow {S_1} = \frac{3}{8}\frac{{{u^2}}}{a} \\
\end{gathered} $
When it stops completely final velocity = 0
So,
02 = u2 - 2aS2
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow {S_2} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2a}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right) \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{{{S_2}}}{{{S_1}}} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{8}{3} = \frac{4}{3} \Rightarrow {S_2} = \frac{4}{3}\,{S_1} = \frac{4}{3} \times 3\;{\text{cm}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right) \\
   \Rightarrow {S_2} = 4\,cm \\
\end{gathered} $
So option (a) is correct as required distance is
${S_2} = {S_1} = 1cm.$

Question: Maximum force of friction is called
Solution: Limiting friction is maximum force of friction

Question: With what acceleration ‘a’ should be box of figure moving up so that the block of mass M exerts a force 7 Mg/4 on the floor of the box?
Solution:
FBD of M : If M exerts force F = 7 Mg/4 on floor, then from third law floor also exerts force F on box in upward direction.

F - Mg = Ma
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{7Mg}}{4} - Mg = Ma \Rightarrow a = \frac{{3g}}{4}$

Question: Three forces acting on a body are shown in the figure. To have the resultant force only along the y–direction, the magnitude of the minimum additional force needed is
Solution:
Forces that are acting along ox
$\begin{gathered}
  \sum {{F_x}} = 0 \\
  1\cos 60 + 2\cos 60 - 4\cos 60 + F = 0 \\
  0 = \frac{1}{2} + 2 \times \frac{1}{2} - 4\frac{1}{2} + F \\
  F = 0.5\;{\text{N}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A bird is sitting in a large closed cage which is placed on a spring balance, it records a weight of 35 N. The bird (mass = 0.5 kg) flies upward in the cage with an acceleration of 2 m/s<sup>2</sup> . The spring balance will now record a weight of :
Solution:
When bird flies upward in the cage, so normal force required to provide acceleration of 2m/s2 will be;
$ \Rightarrow \,ma = 0.5 \times 2 = 1N$
Equal force will be applied on the cage in the opposite direction (downward).
So the total weight recorded by the spring will be;
$ \Rightarrow \,W = 35 + 1 = 36N$

Question: When a bird of weight W sits on a stretched wire, the tension T in the wire is
Solution:
Considering the equilibrium of bird

$2\;{\text{T}}\cos \theta = {\text{W}} \Rightarrow {\text{T}} = \frac{{\text{W}}}{{2\cos \theta }}$
$\therefore \cos \theta < 1 \Rightarrow {\text{T}} > \frac{{\text{W}}}{2}$

Question: A block of mass 1 kg is placed on a truck which accelerates with acceleration 5 m/s<sup>2 </sup>. The coefficient of static friction between the block and truck is 0.6. The frictional force acting on the block is :
Solution:
Frictional force Fs = $\mu $gm
= 0.6 x 10 x 1 = 6
Now pseudo force, F = ma = 1 x 5 = 5 N
Here we can see that, Fs > F
So our answer will be 5N

Question: Consider the situation shown in the figure. All surfaces are rough. The friction on B due to A in equilibrium
Options: depends on the masses of A and B
Solution: The normal reaction force on the system (comprising of wall and contact surface of A and B) is provided by F. The weight of A and B together is acting in the downward direction. Therefore, the frictional force f <sub>A</sub> and f <sub>BA</sub> (friction on B due to A) is in upward direction.

Question: A block of mass 4 kg is placed on a rough horizontal plane. A time dependent horizontal force F = kt acts on the block, k = 2 N/s. The frictional force between the block and plane at time t = 2s is (m = 0.2)
Solution:
Time dependent applied force, F = kt
In this question first we need to find whether applied force on block has exceeded the frictional force.
Frictional force;
$ \Rightarrow f = \mu {\text{mg}} = 0.2 \times 4 \times 10 = 8\;{\text{N}}$
Applied force at time t = 2 sec;
$ \Rightarrow F = kt = 2 \times 2 = 4N$
Thus, we can observe that applied force has not exceeded 8 N force, so block will be in stationary state, and friction force will be;
$ \Rightarrow f = 4N$

Question: A block is moving up an inclined plane of inclination θ = 30° with a velocity 5 m/s. If it stops after 0.5 s then what is the value of coefficient of friction ($\mu $) ?
Solution:
We know that
$v = u + at$
$0 = 5 + 0.5a$
$a = - 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
Here minus sign shows that it is retardation,
$a = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
\[{F_{{\text{net }}}} = ma\]
\[mg\sin 30^\circ + \mu mg\cos 30^\circ = ma\]
\[g \times \frac{1}{2} + \mu g \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} = 10g + \mu g \times \sqrt 3 = 20\]
\[\mu = 0.601\]

Question: For ordinary terrestrial experiments, the observer in an inertial frame in the following cases is :
Options: a child revolving in a gaint wheel
a driver in a sports car moving with a constant high speed of 200 kmh<sup>–1</sup> on a straight rod
the pilot of an aeroplane which is taking off
a cyclist negotiating a sharp curve
Solution:
A frame is said to be inertial when it is moving with a constant speed or is at rest.
Case A.- Child is revolving in a giant wheel, thus child is experiencing a centripetal acceleration.
Case B. – Car is moving at a constant speed along a straight road. No acceleration is experienced by him.
Case C. – Aero-plane takes off with a linear acceleration.
Case D. – cyclist experiencing a centripetal acceleration which negotiating a sharp curve. Thus correct option is B.

Question: Tension in the rope at the rigid support is (g = 10 m/s2 )
Solution:
For 40 kg
400 – T1 = 40
T1 = 360 N
For 50 kg
500 + T1 – T2 = 0
⇒ T2 = 860 N
For 60 kg
⇒ T3 – 600 – T2 = 60 × 2
T3 = 1580 N
T3 will be the tension at the topmost point on the rigid support

Question: The below figure is the part of a horizontally stretched net. Section AB is stretched with a force of 10 N. The tensions in the sections BC and BF are
Options: Cannot be calculated due to insufficient data
Solution:
As shown in figure

T1 cos 30° = T2 cos 30°
⸫ T1 = T2 = T (let)
Again, T1 sin 30° + T2 sin 30° = 10
And 2T sin 30° = 10
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow 2\;{\text{T}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 10 \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{T}} = 10\;{\text{N}} \\
\end{gathered} $
Thus, the tension in section BC and BF are 10 N and 10 N respectively.

Question: A metal sphere is hung by a string fixed to a wall. The forces acting on the sphere are shown in figure. Which of the following statements is NOT correct?
Solution:
As the body is in equilibrium, so vector sum of all three forces
$\overrightarrow N + \overrightarrow T \,and\,\,\overrightarrow W $should be zero. Hence (a) is correct.
Now
T sin θ = N (1)
T cos θ = W (2)

$(1) \div (2) \Rightarrow \tan \theta = \frac{N}{W}$
⇒ N = w tan ?
Hence (d) is correct. Squaring and adding (1) and (2): T2 = N2 + W2
Hence (b) is correct
Hence (c) is incorrect.
Body is in equilibrium so sum of all forces applied on body is zero.

Question: Two bodies having masses m<sub>1</sub> = 40 g and m<sub>2</sub> = 60 g are attached to the ends of a string of negligible mass and suspended from massless pulley. The acceleration of the bodies is :
Solution:
Tension in the string, T
Masses , m1 = 0.04 kg and m2 = 0.06 kg
Heavy weight (0.06 kg) will move downward with an acceleration a, then lighter weight (0.04 kg) will move upward with equal acceleration a.
So equations of motion;
For mass m2, m2 g – T = m2a
For mass m1, T – m1 g= m1a
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow {m_2}g - {m_2}a - {m_1}g = {m_1}a \\
   \Rightarrow a = \frac{{\left( {{m_2}g - {m_1}g} \right)}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}} \\
   \Rightarrow a = \frac{{0.06 \times 10 - 0.04 \times 10}}{{0.10}} = 2\;{\text{m}}/{{\text{s}}^2} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Figure shows two blocks connected by a light inextensible string as shown in figure. A force of 10 N is applied on the bigger block at 60° with horizontal, then the tension in the string connecting the two masses is
Solution:
Fnet = Ma (10 cos 60°) = (3 + 2) a
a = 1 m/s2

T = 2(1) = 2 N

Question: A man of weight 80 kg is standing in an elevator which is moving with an acceleration of 6 m/s<sup>2</sup> in upward direction. The apparent weight of the man will be : (g = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup> )
Solution:
When a body is on a platform, which is moving with an acceleration a in a certain direction, a pseudo force is felt by this body in the opposite direction. This pseudo force is given by;
 ⇒ f = ma
In the above given question, elevator is moving with an acceleration (6m/s2 ) in upward direction, then pseudo force
(downward) felt by man (80kg) will be given by;
 ⇒ f = 80 $ \times $ 6 = 480 N
Thus apparent weight of the man in downward direction will be;
⇒ W = f + mg = 480 + 800 = 1280 N

Question: What is the acceleration of 3 kg mass when acceleration of 2 kg mass is 2 m/s2 as shown?
Solution:
For 2 kg
10 – T = 2(2)
T = 10 – 4 = 6 N
For 3 kg
T = 3(a)
6 = 3a a = 2 m/s2

Question: The pulleys and strings shown in the figure are smooth and of negligible mass. For the system to remain in equilibrium, the angle q should be :
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\text{For}}\,A,T = mg \\
  {\text{For}}\,B,T = mg \\
\end{gathered} \]
For Equilibrium:
\[2T\cos \theta = \sqrt 2 mg\]
\[2mg\cos \theta = \sqrt 2 mg\]
\[\theta = 45^\circ \]

Question: While walking on ice, one should take small steps to avoid slipping. This is because smaller steps ensure
Solution: Smaller steps ensure greater friction so smaller acceleration and easy walking.

Question: One end of massless rope, which passes over a massless and frictionless pulley P is tied to a hook C while the other end is free. Maximum tension that the rope can bear is 360 N. With what value of maximum safe acceleration (in ms–2) can a man of 60 kg climb down the rope?
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  T \geqslant m\left( {g - a} \right) \\
  360 \geqslant 60\left( {10 - a} \right) \\
  a \geqslant 4m{s^{ - 2}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question:
Assertion: If the net external force on the body is zero then its acceleration is also zero.
Reason: Acceleration does not depend on force.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution:
According to Newton’s second law,
${\text{Acceleration}} = \frac{{{\text{Force}}}}{{{\text{Mass}}}}$
That is, if net external force on the body is zero then acceleration will be zero.

Question: Two blocks (A) 2 kg and (B) 5 kg rest one over the other on a smooth horizontal plane. The coefficient of static and dynamic friction between (A) and (B) is the same and equal to 0.60. The maximum horizontal force F that can be applied to (B) in order that both (A) and (B) do not have any relative motion is
Solution:
Now both should move with same acceleration. So,
Fr = ma = 2a
F – Fr = 5a
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\text{a}} = \frac{{\mu {\text{mg}}}}{{\text{m}}} = \mu {\text{m}} = 6\;{\text{m}}/{{\text{s}}^2} \\
  \;{\text{F}} - 0.6 \times 2 \times 10 = 5 \times 6\; \\
  {\text{F}} = 42\;{\text{N}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Figure shows two blocks system, 4kg block rests on a smooth horizontal surface, upper surface of 4 kg is rough. A block of mass 2 kg is placed on its upper surface. The acceleration of upper block with respect to earth when 4 kg mass is pulled by a force of 30 N, is
Solution:
If both move together
${\text{a}} = \frac{{30}}{{(4 + 2)}} = 5\;{\text{m}}/{{\text{s}}^2}$
2 kg will move due to frictional force
\[{\text{F}} = {\text{ma}} \Rightarrow {\text{f}} = 2(5) = 10\;{\text{N}}\]
And limiting friction
${{\text{f}}_{\text{L}}} = (0.8)(2\;{\text{g}}) = 16$
⇒ Friction is sufficient to move both block together hence
a = 5 m/s2

Question: Two masses as shown in the figure are suspended from a massless pulley. The acceleration of the system when masses are left free is
Solution:
The force equations are

$\begin{gathered}
  {\text{T}} - 5\;{\text{g}} = 5{\text{a}}. \\
  10\;{\text{g}} - {\text{T}} = 10{\text{a}} \\
  {\text{Adding,}}10g - 5g = 15{\text{a}} \\
  Or, \\
  a = \frac{{5\;{\text{g}}}}{{15}} = \frac{{\text{g}}}{3} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A body is placed on an inclined plan and has to be pushed down in order to make it move. The angle made by the normal reaction with the vertical will be:-
Options: Equal to the angle of repose
Less than the angle of repose
More than the angle of repose
Solution:
The angle of repose, or critical angle of repose, of a granular material is the steepest angle of descent or dip relative to the horizontal plane to which a material can be piled without slumping. At this angle, the material on the slope face is on the verge of sliding.
Since the block doesn’t slide down on its own, this angle must be less than the angle of repose.

Question:
Assertion: Sportsman runs some distance before taking a long jump.
Reason: Because of inertia body remains in state of motion or rest.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution:
Because of inertia body will continue with constant velocity or in rest until any force act on it, for example when sportsman runs some distance his body is in motion and it will continue until any force act on it.
So, for taking long jump sportsman need running hence assertion and reason both are correct.

Question: Two blocks, each having a mass M, rest on frictionless surfaces as shown in the figure. If the pulley are light and frictionless, and M on the incline is allowed to move down, then the tension in the string will be :
Solution:
Considering F.B.D. of mass M on the incline
Mg sin θ - T = Ma .......(1)
Also for mass M on horizontal surface
T = Ma ......(2)
(1) + (2)
${\text{Mg}}\sin \theta - T + T = 2Ma\,\,\,$
$ \Rightarrow a = \frac{{{\text{g}}\sin \theta }}{2}$
Tension in string is $T = \frac{{{\text{Mg}}\sin \theta }}{2}$

Question: Five forces $\overrightarrow {{F_1}} ,\overrightarrow {{F_2}} ,\overrightarrow {{F_3}} ,\overrightarrow {{F_4}} \,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\overrightarrow {{F_5}} $are acting on a particle of mass 2.0 kg so that it is moving with 4m / s<sup>2</sup> in east direction. If $\overrightarrow {{F_1}} $ force is removed, then the acceleration becomes 2 7m /s<sup>2</sup> in north, then the acceleration of the block if only $\overrightarrow {{F_1}} $ is acting will be:
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  \overrightarrow {{F_1}} + \overrightarrow {{F_2}} + \overrightarrow {{F_3}} + \overrightarrow {{F_4}} \, + \,\overrightarrow {{F_5}} = 2\left( {4\widehat i} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( i \right) \\
  {\text{and}} \\
  \overrightarrow {{F_2}} + \overrightarrow {{F_3}} + \overrightarrow {{F_4}} \, + \,\overrightarrow {{F_5}} = 2\left( {7\widehat i} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\left( {ii} \right) \\
\end{gathered} $
From (i) and (ii),
$\overrightarrow {{F_1}} = 8\widehat i - 14\widehat j$
$\overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \frac{{\overrightarrow {{F_1}} }}{m} = 4\widehat i - 7\widehat j$
$ \Rightarrow \,{a_1} = \sqrt {16 + 49} = \sqrt {65} \,m/{s^2}$

Question: When a car moves on a road with uniform speed of 30 km/ h, then the net resultant force on the car is :
Options: the driving force that drives the car in the direction of propagation of car
the resistive force that acts opposite to the direction of propagation of car
Solution:
Applied force on a body is given by;
$ \Rightarrow F = ma$
It always involves acceleration term. Since in the above question, car is moving with uniform velocity of 30 km/hr, then car is not moving under any acceleration. So, net resultant force on the car will be zero.

Question: A body of mass 2 kg is moving with a velocity 8 m/s on a smooth surface. If it is to be brought to rest in 4 s. Then the force to be applied is :
Solution:
Mass of body, m = 2kg
Initial velocity, u = 8 m/s
Final velocity, v = 0 m/s
Applying Newton’s equation of motion;
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow v = u - at \\
   \Rightarrow 0 = 8 - a \times 4 \\
   \Rightarrow a = \frac{8}{4} = 2\;{\text{m}}/{{\text{s}}^2} \\
\end{gathered} $
Thus required retardation (a) is 2m/s2 .
So required force to stop the body will be,
$F = ma = 2 \times 2 = 4N$

Question: Same force acts on two bodies of different masses 3 kg and 5 kg initially at rest. The ratio of time required to acquire same final velocity is
Solution:
From Newton’s second law,
\[\begin{gathered}
  F = \frac{{dp}}{{dt}} = \frac{{mv}}{t} \\
   \Rightarrow t = \frac{{mv}}{F} \\
   \Rightarrow t \propto m \\
\end{gathered} \]
(⸪ Final velocity and force are same)
Given,
\[\begin{gathered}
  {{\text{m}}_1} = 3\;{\text{kg}},\; \\
  {{\text{m}}_2} = 5\;{\text{kg}} \\
\end{gathered} \]
Hence
\[\frac{{{{\text{t}}_1}}}{{{{\text{t}}_2}}} = \frac{{{{\text{m}}_1}}}{{\;{{\text{m}}_2}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\text{t}}_1}}}{{{{\text{t}}_2}}} = \frac{3}{5}\]

Question: When a 4 kg rifle is fired, the 10 g bullet receives an acceleration of 3 $ \times $ 10<sup>6</sup> cm/s<sup>2</sup>. The magnitude of the force acting on the rifle (in newton) is
Solution:
Using Newton’s third law, bullet will apply the same force in the opposite direction
So using
\[F = ma = \frac{{10}}{{100}} \times 3 \times {10^6} \times {10^{ - 2}} = 300N\]

Question: When a bus suddenly take a turn, the passengers are thrown outwards because of :
Solution: Correct answer is option b) because of inertia body will continue with constant velocity until any force act on it. For example when bus is moving straight all passenger has velocity in straight line but when bus takes turn, due to inertia passenger will still be moving in straight be-cause no force acts on them.

Question: The average force necessary to stop a bullet of 20 gm at a speed of 250 m/s as it penetrates wood to a distance of 12 cm is :
Solution:
Initial velocity of bullet, u = 250 m/s
Final velocity of bullet, v = 0 m/s
Mass of bullet, m = 20 gm
Penetration depth,
s = 0.12 m Using Newton’s equation;
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow {v^2} = {u^2} - 2as \\
   \Rightarrow 0 = {250^2} - 2 \times 0.12 \times a \\
   \Rightarrow a = 260416.66\;{\text{m}}/{{\text{s}}^2} \\
\end{gathered} $
Thus average necessary force will be;
\[ \Rightarrow F = ma = 20 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 260416.66 = 5.2 \times {10^3}N\]

Question: A ship of mass 3 × 10<sup>7</sup> kg initially at rest is pulled by a force of 5 × 10<sup>4</sup> N through a distance of 3 m. Assuming that the resistance due to water is negligible, what will be the speed of the ship?
Solution:
When ship is pulled by force F then its acceleration
$\begin{gathered}
  a = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{{5 \times {{10}^4}}}{{3 \times {{10}^7}}} \\
   \Rightarrow a = \frac{5}{{3000}} = \frac{1}{{600}}\;{\text{m}}/{{\text{s}}^2} \\
\end{gathered} $
⸪ initial speed = 0, so by
$\begin{gathered}
  \,{v^2} = {u^2} + 2as \\
   \Rightarrow {v^2} = {0^2} + 2 \times \frac{1}{{600}} \times 3 \\
   \Rightarrow {v^2} = \frac{1}{{100}} \Rightarrow v = \frac{1}{{10}}\;{\text{m}}/{\text{s}} \\
   \Rightarrow v = 0.1\;{\text{m}}/{\text{s}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A force vector applied on a mass is represented by $\overrightarrow F  = 6\widehat i - 8\widehat j + 10\widehat k$ and the force accelerates the mass at 1 m/s<sup>2</sup>. What is the mass of the body?
Solution:
By newton’s 2nd law F = ma
\[\begin{gathered}
  \because \vec F = 6\hat i - 8\hat j + 10\hat k \\
   \Rightarrow |\vec F| = \sqrt {{6^2} + {8^2} + {{10}^2}} \\
   \Rightarrow |\vec F| = \sqrt {200} = 10\sqrt 2 N \\
  \because a = 1m/{s^2} \Rightarrow m = \frac{F}{a} = \frac{{10\sqrt 2 }}{1} \\
  \therefore {\text{ mass }} = 10\sqrt 2 \;kg \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A body of mass 60 kg suspended by means of three strings, P, Q and R as shown in the figure is in equilibrium. The tension in the string P is
Solution:
The free body diagram of mass M is shown in figure

\[\begin{gathered}
  {\text{R}}\cos \theta = {\text{Mg}} \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{R}}\cos 60^\circ = {\text{Mg}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...{\text{(i)}} \\
  {\text{And}} \\
  R\sin 60^\circ = {\text{T}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...{\text{(ii)}} \\
  {\text{By}}\,{\text{Eqs}}.(i)\,{\text{and}}\,(ii),{\text{we}}\,{\text{get}} \\

   \Rightarrow {\text{T}} = {\text{Mg}}\tan 60^\circ \,\,{\text{or}} \\
  {\text{T}} = 60 \times {\text{g}} \times \sqrt 3 = 103.9{\text{gN}} \\

Question: A body of mass 0.1 kg attains a velocity of 10 m/s in 0.1 s. The force acting on the body is:
Solution:
Mass of body, m = 0.1kg Initial velocity,
u = 0 m/sec. Final velocity,
v = 10 m/sec. Time taken to reach final velocity,
t = 0.1 sec. So, we can find the acceleration as;
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow v = u + at \\
   \Rightarrow 10 = 0 + a \times 0.1 \\
   \Rightarrow a = 100\;{\text{m}}/{{\text{s}}^2} \\
\end{gathered} $
Thus applied force on the body will be;
\[ \Rightarrow F = ma = 0.1 \times 100 = 10N\]

Question: A disc is rolling on an inclined plane. What is the ratio of its Rotational K.E. to the total K.E.?
Solution:
KETotal = KE(Rotational) + KE(Translational)
KE(Rotational) = ½ Iω 2
= \[\frac{1}{2}\frac{{M{R^2}}}{2}{\omega ^2} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{M{R^2}}}{2}\frac{{{V^2}}}{{{R^2}}}\]
= \[\frac{1}{4}M{V^2}\]
KE(Translational) = \[\frac{1}{2}M{V^2}\]
KETotal = \[\frac{1}{2}M{V^2}\]+ \[\frac{1}{4}M{V^2}\]
\[Ratio = \frac{{\frac{1}{4}M{V^2}}}{{\frac{3}{4}M{V^2}}} = \frac{1}{3}\]

Question: A horizontal platform is rotating with uniform angular velocity around the vertical axis passing through its centre. At some instant of time a viscous fluid of mass m is dropped at the centre and is allowed to spread out and finally fall. The angular velocity during this period
Solution:
When liquid drop is on the rotating platform and spreads out, it increases moment of inertia of whole system;
$ \Rightarrow {I_{new}} = {I_{disc}} + m{d^2}$

As there is no external torque applied, angular momentum will be conserved;

$ \Rightarrow {I_{disc}}{\omega _{old}} = {I_{new}}{\omega _{new}}$

Since ${I_{new}} > {I_{disc}}$

$ \Rightarrow {\omega _{new}} < {\omega _{old}}$

So, the angular velocity of system will decrease.

When drop falls, it will again result in increase in angular velocity.

Question: A particle starting from the origin (0, 0) moves in a straight line in the (x, y) plane. Its coordinates at a later time are \[\left( {\sqrt 3 ,\,\,3} \right)\]. The path of the particle makes with the x-axis an angle of
Solution:
Particle moves in straight line in (x, y) plane passing through coordinates \[\left( {0,0} \right){\text{and }}\left( {\sqrt 3 ,3} \right)\]
Equation of straight line

\[\frac{{y - 0}}{{x - 0}} = \frac{{3 - 0}}{{\sqrt 3 - 0}}\]

\[\frac{y}{x} = \frac{3}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]

\[y = \frac{3}{{\sqrt 3 }}x\]

Compare with \[y = mx = \tan \theta x\]

\[\tan \theta = \frac{3}{{\sqrt 3 }} = \sqrt 3 \]

\[\theta = 60^\circ \]

Question: If the kinetic energy of a body increases by 300%,  by what % will the linear momentum of the body increase?

A long, straight, solid metal wire of radius 2mm carries a current uniformly distributed over its circular cross-section. The magnetic field induction at a distance 2 mm from its axis is B. Then the magnetic field induction at distance 1mm from axis will be
If $\vec A$ and $\vec B$ are two vectors such that \[\left| {\vec A + \vec B} \right| = \left| {\vec A - \vec B} \right|\] the angle between vectors $\vec A$ and $\vec B$ is :
Solution:
We have
$\left| {\vec A + \vec B} \right| = \left| {\vec A - \vec B} \right|$
$ \Rightarrow {\left| {\vec A + \vec B} \right|^2} = {\left| {\vec A - \vec B} \right|^2}$
$ \Rightarrow {\left| {\vec A} \right|^2} + {\left| {\vec B} \right|^2} + 2\vec A.\vec B = {\left| {\vec A} \right|^2} + {\left| {\vec B} \right|^2} - 2\vec A\vec B$
$ \Rightarrow \vec A.\vec B = 0$
$\therefore \vec A \bot \vec B$
$\therefore \theta = 90^\circ $
Hence, angle between two vectors is $90^\circ $.

Question: A bomb is projected with 200m/s at an angle 60° with horizontal. At the highest point, it explodes into three particles of equal masses. Question: A uniform cylinder of mass M and radius R is released from rest on a rough inclined surface of inclination \[\theta \] with the horizontal as shown in figure. As the cylinder rolls down the inclined surface, the maximum elongation in the spring of stiffness k is

Maximum spring potential energy = maximum decrease in gravitational potential energy

\[ \Rightarrow {x_m} = \frac{{2Mg\sin \theta }}{k}\]

Question: An electron is moving along the positive X-axis. You want to apply a magnetic field for a short time so that the electron may reverse its direction and move parallel to the negative X-axis. This can be done by applying the magnetic field along

Question: A body is moving under the action of a force. Suddenly, force is increased to such an extent that its kinetic energy is increased by 100%. The momentum increases by:
Solution:
\[K = \frac{{{p^2}}}{{2m}},p = \sqrt {2mK} \]

\[K' = 2K\;({\text{since}}\;{\text{there}}\;{\text{is}}\,\,100\% \uparrow )\]

\[p' = \sqrt {2mK'} = \sqrt {2m2K} \]

\[\frac{{p'}}{p} = \frac{{\sqrt {2m \cdot 2K} }}{{\sqrt {2mK} }} = \sqrt 2 \;\;\;{\kern 1pt} \therefore \frac{{p' - p}}{p} = \frac{{\sqrt 2 p - p}}{p}\]

\[\% \] is increase \[\;\frac{{\Delta p}}{p} \times 100 = \frac{{\sqrt 2 p - p}}{p} \times 100 \approx 41.4\% \]

Question: Which of the following may be discontinuous across a charged conducting surface?
Solution:
For a conducting sphere Einside = 0

Hence at the boundary, electric intensity $\vec E$ is discontinuous, while ${V_A} - {V_B} = \int {\vec E.\overrightarrow {dr} } $, potential difference between two points on the surface, is an integral of a non-infinite quantity, which cannot be discontinuous.

Question: A U-tube is partially filled with water. Oil, which does not mix with water, is next poured into one side until water rises by 25 cm on the other side. If the density of oil be 0.8 $$g/c{m^3}$$, the oil level will stand higher than the water level by:
Solution:
$${\rho _{oil}} = 0.8{\text{ g/c}}{{\text{m}}^3}$$

$${\rho _{water}} = 1{\text{ g/c}}{{\text{m}}^3}$$

When water goes down by 25 cm on one side of U tube, then it moves up by 25 cm on other tube.

Thus, the total difference in height of water in both side is equal to 50 cm.

$${P_A} = {P_B}$$

$${P_0} + {\rho _{oil}}gh = {P_0} + {\rho _{water}}g\left( {50} \right)$$

$$0.8gh = g\left( {50} \right)$$

$$h = 62.5{\text{ }}cm$$

Thus, the oil stands higher than water by amount

$$\Delta h = h - 50 = 62.5 - 50 = 12.5{\text{ }}cm$$

Question: Regarding a semiconductor which one of the following is wrong?

There are no free electrons at 0 K

Question: If force [F], acceleration [A] and time [T] are chosen as the fundamental physical quantities. Find the dimensions of energy.
Solution:
\[E \propto {F^a}{A^b}{T^c}\]

\[\left[ {{M^1}{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}} \right] \propto {\left[ {{M^1}{L^1}{T^{ - 2}}} \right]^a}{\left[ {L{T^{ - 2}}} \right]^b}{\left[ T \right]^c}\]

\[a = 1\]

\[a + b = 2\]

\[ \Rightarrow \,\,b = 1\]

\[ - 2a - 2b + c = - 2\]

\[ \Rightarrow \,\,c = 2\]

\[a = 1\]

\[b = 1\]

\[c = 2\]

\[E \propto \left[ F \right]\left[ A \right]\left[ {{T^2}} \right]\]

Question: When a system is taken from state i to state f along the path iaf, it is found that Q = 50 cal and W = 20 cal. Along the path ibf Q = 36 cal. W along the path ibf is
Solution:
For path iaf,
Q = 50 cal
W = 20 cal
By first law of thermodynamics,
\[\Delta U = \;Q - \;W\; = \;50\; - \;20\; = \;30\;cal.\]
For path ibf


\[Q'\; = \;36\,cal\]
W = ?
or, \[W'\; = \;Q'\; - \;\Delta U\]
Since, the change in internal energy does not depend on the path, therefore
\[\Delta U'\; = \;30\;cal\]
\[\therefore \;\;W'\; = \;Q'\; - \;\Delta U'\; = \;36 - \;30 = \;6\;cal.\]

Question: If \[\vec P = 4\hat i - 2\hat j + 6\hat k\] and \[\vec Q = \hat i - 2\hat j - 3\hat k\] then the angle which \[\vec P + \vec Q\] makes with x-axis is

\[\overrightarrow Q = \widehat i - 2\widehat j - 3\hat k,\,\,\overrightarrow P + \overrightarrow Q = 5\widehat i - 4\widehat j + 3\hat k\]

⇒ Angle made by a vector with x-axis is given by

\[{\text{cos}}\phi {\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{x}}\,\,{\text{component}}}}{{{\text{magnitute}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{vector}}}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \cos \phi = \frac{5}{{\sqrt {25 + 16 + 9} }}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \phi = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{5}{{\sqrt {50} }}} \right)\]

Question: Between the plates of a parallel plate condenser, a plate of thickness ${t_1}$ and dielectric constant ${k_1}$ is placed. In the rest of the space, there is another plate of thickness ${t_2}$ and dielectric constant ${k_2}$. If the capacitance has charge Q, the potential difference across the condenser will be
Solution:
Potential difference across the condenser
$V = {V_1} + {V_2} = {E_1}{t_1} + {E_2}{t_2} = \frac{\sigma }{{{K_1}{\varepsilon _0}}}{t_1} + \frac{\sigma }{{{K_2}{\varepsilon _0}}}{t_2}$
⇒ $V = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}\left( {\frac{{{t_1}}}{{{K_1}}} + \frac{{{t_2}}}{{{K_2}}}} \right) = \frac{Q}{{A{\varepsilon _0}}}\left( {\frac{{{t_1}}}{{{K_1}}} + \frac{{{t_2}}}{{{K_2}}}} \right)$

Question: The maximum velocity at the lowest point, so that the string just slack at the highest point in a vertical circle of radius $\ell $ is
Solution:
According to the question: 
     Tension in the string at the highest point, T = 0
     Then, $\frac{{m{u^2}}}{\ell } = mg$
     $\therefore u = \sqrt {g\ell } $
     Now, from the energy conservation equation:
     $\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = 2mg\ell + \frac{1}{2}m{u^2}$
     $\therefore v = \sqrt {5g\ell } $

Question: A 0.5 kg ball moving with a speed of 12 m/s strikes a hard wall at an angle of $30^\circ $ with the wall. It is reflected with the same speed and at the same angle, as shown in fig. If the ball is in contact with the wall for 0.25 s, the average force acting on the wall is
Solution:
No change in linear momentum component parallel to wall along y-axis \[\Delta {P_v} = 0\]

Change in linear momentum component \[{ \bot _r}\]to the wall i.e. along x-axis.

\[\Delta {P_x} = - mv\cos 60^\circ - mv\cos 60^\circ \]

\[ = - mv\cos 60^\circ \times 2\]

\[ = - mv \times \frac{1}{2} \times 2\]

\[ = - mv\]

\[ \Rightarrow \left( {\Delta {P_x}} \right) = mv\]

So, average force on the wall in

\[{F_{avg}} = \frac{{\left( {\Delta {P_x}} \right)}}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{{mv}}{t} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{12}}{{\left( {0.25} \right)}}\]

\[{F_{avg}} = 6 \times 4 = 24N\]

So, option (c) is correct.

= 20 erg

Question: The cause of the potential barrier in a p-n diode is :
Options: depletion of positive charges near the junction
concentration of positive charges near the junction
depletion of negative charges near the junction
concentration of positive and negative charges near the junction
Solution: During the formation of a junction diode, holes from p-region diffuse into n-region and electrons from n-region diffuse into p-region. This creates an assimilation of holes and electrons, which upon meeting cancel each other out, resulting in a thin layer at the junction free of any charge carriers. This is called depletion layer. This also creates a potential difference on the two sides of the depletion layer, which is also known as potential barrier.

Question: In an A.C. circuit, voltage applied is $V = 220\sin 100t.$ If the impedance is $110\,\Omega $ and phase difference between current and voltage is $60^\circ ,$ the power consumption is equal to:
Solution:
$V = 220\,\sin 100t$

$\left| Z \right| = 110\Omega $ and $\phi = 60^\circ $
$\therefore $ Power $ = \frac{{{{\left( {{V_{rms}}} \right)}^2}}}{{|Z|}}\cos \phi = \frac{{{{\left( {200} \right)}^2}}}{{2 \times 110}} \times \frac{1}{2} = 110W$

Question: A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of 20 ms<sup>-1</sup> from the top of a multi storey building. The height of the point where the ball is thrown 25 m from the ground. How long will it be before the ball hits the ground? Take g = 10 ms<sup>-2</sup>.
Solution:
The time for the ball in the air (T) will be corresponding to the displacement with respect to the ground that will be 0, y = 0, t = T
Displacement in y direction is given by

\[y = {y_0} + ut - \frac{1}{2}g{t^2}\]

\[y = 0,t = T\]

\[ \Rightarrow 25 + 20 \times T - \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times {T^2} = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow {T^2} - 4T - 5 = 0\]

Solving

either T = 5 or T = -1

Time cannot be -ve, thus

T = 5s

Question: A man sides down a light rope whose breaking strength is $\eta $ times the weight of man $\left( {\eta  < 1} \right).$ The maximum acceleration of the man so that the rope just breaks is:
Solution:
Given that ${T_{\max }} = \eta w$
Using ${F_{net}} = ma$
$\begin{gathered}
  w - {T_{\max }} = \frac{w}{g}a \\
  {T_{\max }} = \eta w \\
\end{gathered} $
So $a = g\left( {1 - \eta } \right)$

Graph is parabola

Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Question: n-p-n transistors are preferred to p-n-p transistors because:
Options: they are capable of handling large power
electrons have high mobility than holes and hence high mobility of energy.
Solution: The majority of charge carrier in the n-p-n transistor are electrons but in p-n-p transistors are holes, and the mobility of holes is slower than the electrons so, n-p-n transistors are preferred to p-n-p transistors.

Question: A straight horizontal wire of mass 10 mg and length 1m carries a current of 2 ampere. What minimum magnetic field B should be applied in the region so that the magnetic force on the wire may balance its weight.

Assertion: Pulling a lawn roller is easier than pushing it.
Reason: Pushing increases the apparent weight and hence the force of friction.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Suppose the roller is pushed as in Fig. (b). The force F is resolved into two components, horizontal component \[{F_H}\] which helps the roller to move forward, and the vertical component acting downwards adds to the weight. Thus, weight is increased. But in the case of machine being pulled, as shown in Fig. (a), the vertical component of the force \[{F_v}\] is in the direction opposite to its weight; thus, weight is reduced. So, pulling is easier than pushing the lawn roller.

A current of $ 5\,\text{A} $ is flowing at $ 220\,\text{V} $ in the primary coil of a transformer. If the voltage produced in the secondary coil is $ 2200\,\text{V} $ and 50% of power is lost, then the current in the secondary coil will be
Solution:

According to data we can write, $ \frac{{{V}_{p}}{{I}_{p}}}{2}={{V}_{S}}{{I}_{S}}\Rightarrow {{I}_{S}}=\frac{{{V}_{p}}}{{{V}_{s}}}\times \frac{{{I}_{p}}}{2}=\frac{220}{2200}\times \frac{5}{2}=0\cdot 25\,\text{A} $

Question: A raft of wood (density 600 g/m3) of mass 120 kg floats in water. How much weight can be put on the raft to make it just sink?
Solution:
Let we add a mass m on the raft to make it just sink. Meaning that the volume of raft if now
completely immersed in water
Let the volume of raft is V
V = (mass of raft)/(density of raft)
V = 0.2m3
Now this volume is completely immersed in water hence the buoyancy prouided by water must
balance the weight of
raft and extra mass we have just added
hence,
1000 × 0.2 × g = (m + 120)g
m = 80 kg.

Question: A coil of resistance 200 ohm and self-inductance 1.0 henry has been connected to an a.c. source of frequency 200/π Hz. The phase difference between voltage and current is

\[\therefore \theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( 2 \right)\]

Options: E must be perpendicular to B

Question: A block of mass m is given an initial downward velocity ${{\text{v}}_{\text{0}}}$ and left on an inclined plane (coefficient of friction = 0.6). The block will:
Options: continue to move down the plane with constant velocity ${{\text{v}}_{\text{0}}}$
Solution:
Kinetic friction on the block is

\[{f_k} = {\mu _k}N = 0.6 \times mg\cos {30^ \circ }\]

\[ = 0.6 \times {\text{mg}} \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}\]

\[{{\text{F}}_{\text{k}}} = {\text{mg}}(0.3 \times \sqrt 3 ) = {\text{mg}} \times 0.3 \times 1.732\]

\[ = 0.52{\text{mg}}\]

\[\because {\text{mg}}\sin {30^ \circ } = \frac{{{\text{mg}}}}{2} = 0.5{\text{mg}}\]

\[\because {{\text{F}}_{\text{k}}}\] is greater than ${\text{mgsin3}}{{\text{0}}^{\text{o}}}$. so, block will decelerate and come to rest.

So, option (c) is correct.

Question: Which one of the following equations of motion represents simple harmonic motion ?
Options: Acceleration = kx where k, k<sub>0</sub>, k<sub>1</sub> and c are allpositive
Solution:
Equation of SHM is of the form $$a = - {\omega ^2}r$$

$$a' = - k\left( {x + a} \right)$$

Considering x = -a as mean position,

we can write $$a' = - kr$$

where r is displacement from x = -a.

Question: Of the following situations, which one is impossible?
Options: A body is having zero velocity and non–zero acceleration
A body is having constant acceleration and variable velocity
A body is having constant speed and variable acceleration
A body is having constant velocity and variable acceleration
Solution:
(a) It is possible to have zero velocity and non-zero acceleration of a body e.g. body at its highest point of vertical motion.

(b) In vertical motion of body velocity changes whereas acceleration remains constant. so this is also possible.

(c) A uniform circular motion is an example of a body having constant speed and variable acceleration.

(d) It is not possible to have constant velocity and variable acceleration.

Question: Two particles are executing simple harmonic motion of the same amplitude A and frequency $$\omega $$ along the x–axis. Their mean position is separated by distance x<sub>0</sub> (x<sub>0</sub> > A). If the maximum separation between them is (x<sub>0</sub> + A), the phase difference between their motion is
Solution:
We know formula for phase difference,
$x = A\sin \left( {\omega t + \phi } \right)$

$\therefore {x_1} = A\sin \left( {\omega t + {\phi _1}} \right)$

${x_2} = A\sin \left( {\omega t + {\phi _2}} \right)$

Now,

${x_1} - {x_2} = A\left( {\sin \left( {\omega t + {\phi _1}} \right) - \sin \left( {\omega t + {\phi _2}} \right)} \right)$

$x' = 2A\left( {\cos \left( {\omega t + \frac{{{\phi _1} + {\phi _2}}}{2}} \right)\sin \left( {\frac{{{\phi _1} - {\phi _2}}}{2}} \right)} \right)$

Here,

$A = 2A\sin \left( {\frac{{{\phi _1} - {\phi _2}}}{2}} \right)$

$\sin \left( {\frac{{{\phi _1} - {\phi _2}}}{2}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}$

$\frac{{{\phi _1} - {\phi _2}}}{2} = \frac{\pi }{6}$

${\phi _1} - {\phi _2} = \frac{\pi }{3}$

Question: Two spherical conductors B and C having equal radii and carrying equal charges on them repel each other with a force F when kept apart at some distance. A third spherical conductor having same radius as that B but uncharged is brought in contact with B. then brought in contact with C and finally removed away from both. The new force of repulsion between B and Cis
Solution:
\[F \propto \frac{{{Q_B}{Q_C}}}{{{x^2}}}\]
X is distance between the spheres. After first operation charge on B is halved i.e., \[\frac{Q}{2}\] and charge on third sphere becomes \[\frac{Q}{2}\]. Now it is touched to C, charge then equally distributes them selves to make potential same, hence charge on C becomes \[\left( {Q + \frac{Q}{2}} \right)\frac{1}{2} = \frac{{3Q}}{4}\]
\[\therefore {F_{new}} \propto \frac{{{Q_C}{Q_B}}}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{{3Q}}{4}} \right)\left( {\frac{Q}{2}} \right)}}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{3}{8}\frac{{{Q^2}}}{{{x^2}}}\]
or \[{F_{new}} = \frac{3}{8}F\]

Question: When a charged particle moving with velocity $ \overrightarrow \upsilon $ is subjected to a magnetic field $ \overrightarrow B $ , the force on it is non-zero. This implies that
Options: angle between  $ v $  and  $ B $   is either zero or  $ 180^\circ  $
angle between  $ v $  and  $ B $   is necessarily  $ 90^\circ  $
angle between  $ v $  and  $ B $   can have any value other than  $ 90^\circ  $
angle between  $ v $  and  $ B $   can have any value other than zero and  $ 180^\circ  $
Solution:
Force on a particle moving with velocity $ \overrightarrow \upsilon $ in a magnetic field $ \overrightarrow B $ is
 $ \overrightarrow F = q\left( {\overrightarrow \upsilon \times \overrightarrow B } \right) $ $ \Rightarrow \,F = qvB\sin \theta $
(i) When $ \theta = 0^\circ ,\,F = qvB\sin 0^\circ = 0 $
(ii) When $ \theta = 90^\circ ,\,F = qvB\sin 90^\circ = qvB $
(iii) When $ \theta = 180^\circ ,\,F = qvB\sin 180^\circ = 0 $
If angle between $ v $ and $ B $ is either zero or $ 180^\circ $ , then value of $ F $ will be zero

Question:

Reason: In free fall, acceleration of lift is equal to acceleration due to gravity.

Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.

Question: A particle of mass 0.1 kg executes SHM under a force F = (–10x) Newton. Speed of particle at mean position is 6 m/s. Then amplitude of oscillations is
Solution:
Since $F = - m{\omega ^2}x$
and at equilibrium position, $$F = - 10x$$

$ \Rightarrow m{\omega ^2} = 10$

$ \Rightarrow 0.1 \times {\omega ^2} = 10$

$ \Rightarrow \omega = 10$

Also, speed at Mean position = max. speed

$ = A\omega $

$ \Rightarrow 6 = A \times 10$

$ \Rightarrow A = 0.6{\text{ m}}$

Question: In series LR circuit, X<sub>L</sub> = 3 R. Now a capacitor with X<sub>C</sub> = R is added in series. The ratio of new to old power factor is
Solution:
Power factor
\[\cos \phi = \frac{R}{z}\]
$ \Rightarrow P \propto \cos \varphi $
For LR circuit
\[{Z_1} = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {3R} \right)}^2}} \]
\[{Z_1} = \sqrt {10} R\]
 For LCR circuit
\[{Z_2} = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {3R - R} \right)}^2}} \]
\[{Z_2} = \sqrt {{R^2} + 4{R^2}} \]
\[{Z_2} = \sqrt 5 R\]
\[\frac{{{{\left( {\cos \phi } \right)}_2}}}{{{{\left( {\cos \phi } \right)}_1}}} = \frac{{{Z_1}}}{{{Z_2}}} = \frac{{\sqrt {10} R}}{{\sqrt 5 R}} = \sqrt 2 \]

Question: For the LR circuit shown in figure, the phase angle if frequency is 100/π is
Solution:
For LR circuit
\[Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {\omega ^2}{L^2}} = \sqrt {25 + 4 \times {{10}^4} \times 625 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \]
\[ = \sqrt {25 + 25} = \sqrt {50} = 5\sqrt 2 \]
\[\cos \phi = \frac{R}{Z} = \frac{5}{{5\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]
\[\therefore \phi = 45^\circ \]

Question: A person with a mass M kg stands in contact against the wall of a cylindrical drum of radius r rotating with an angular velocity ω . If the coefficient of friction between the wall and the man is μ , then the minimum rotational speed of the cylinder which enables the person to remain stuck to the wall when the floor is suddenly removed will be

ƒ = mg, N = mR\[\omega^2 \]
\[\mu mR{\omega^2} = mg\]
\[\omega^2 \]  = \[\frac{g}{{\mu R}}\]
\[\omega\]  = \[\sqrt {\frac{g}{{\mu R}}} \]

Question: A certain body weighs 22.42 gm and has a measured volume of 4.7 cc. The possible error in the measurement of mass and volume are 0.01 gm and 0.1 cc. Then maximum error in the density will be
Solution: \[D = \frac{M}{V}\]\[\therefore \,\,\,\% \frac{{\Delta D}}{D} = \% \frac{{\Delta M}}{M} + \% \frac{{\Delta V}}{V} = \left( {\frac{{0.01}}{{22.42}} + \frac{{0.1}}{{4.7}}} \right) \times 100 = 2\% \]

Question: In SHM for how many times potential energy is equal at kinetic energy during one complete period ?
Solution:
We know, total mechanical energy E is conserved in SHM.
∴ E= U + KE

For U = KE

(or) U = $$\frac{E}{2}$$

⇒ $$\frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}{x^2} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}{A^2}} \right)$$

⇒ $$x = \pm \frac{A}{{\sqrt 2 }}$$

For one complete cycle, particle will pass through these points 4 times.

Question: Alternating voltage V = 400 sin (500 π t) is applied across a resistance of 0.2 k Ω. The r.m.s. value of current will be equal to
Solution:
\[V = 400\sin \left( {500\pi t} \right)\]
\[i = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{{400}}{{0.2 \times {{10}^3}}}\sin \left( {500\pi t} \right)\]
\[i = 2\sin \left( {500\pi t} \right)\]
\[{i_{rms}} = \frac{2}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \sqrt 2 = 1.414{\text{ }}A\]

Question: Ten coins are placed on top of each other on a horizontal table. If the mass of each coin is 10 g and acceleration due to gravity is 10 \[m{s^{ - 2}}\], what is the magnitude and direction of the force on the 7th coin (counted from the bottom) due to all the coins above it?
Solution:
Let us consider upper 3 coins as a system then force exerted by 7th coins on the 8th coin is N.

From F.B.D. of upper 3 blocks.

\[N = 3mg = 3 \times \frac{{10}}{{1000}} \times 10 = 0.3N\]

Hence the reaction force N = 0.3 newton acts on 7th coin in downwards direction.

So. Option (a) is correct.

Question: The angular velocity of a particle moving in a circle of radius 50 cm is increased in 5 min from 100 revolutions per minute to 400 revolutions per minute. Find tangential acceleration of the particle.
Solution:
\[\alpha = \frac{{\omega - {\omega _0}}}{t} = \frac{{\left( {400 - 100} \right) \times 2\pi }}{{60 \times 5 \times 60}}\]
\[ = \frac{{300 \times 2\pi }}{{60 \times 5 \times 60}}\]
\[\therefore {a_T} = \alpha \cdot r = \frac{{2\pi }}{{60}} \times \frac{{50}}{{100}} = \frac{\pi }{{60}}m/{s^2}\]

Question: A cubical block of mass m and edge a slides down a rough inclined plane of inclination\[\theta \]with a uniform velocity. The torque of the normal force on the block about its centre has a magnitude:
Solution:
On a slope, N does not pass through the COM, it shifts in order to balance the torque due to friction about COM.

\[{\tau _N} = {\tau _f}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {\tau _N} = mg\sin \theta \left( {\frac{a}{2}} \right)\]

{$\therefore f = mg\sin \theta ,$ as the velocity is constant $ \Rightarrow $ acceleration = 0}

Question: If a dipole of dipole moment $$\overrightarrow p $$ is placed in a uniform electric field then torque acting on it is given by
Solution:
Dipole moment $$\overrightarrow p {\text{ }} = {\text{ }}q\overrightarrow a $$ (where q is magnitude of charge and a is distance between charges). When a dipole of dipole moment $$\overrightarrow p $$ is placed in a uniform electric field E, the torque is given by the product of either force and the perpendicular distance between the two forces.
That is, $$\tau = qEa\sin \theta $$

This can also be shown as the cross product of dipole moment and electric field,

Question: A force acts on a 3.0g particle in such a way that the position of the particle as a function of time is given by $x = 3t - 4{t^2} + {t^3},$where x is in metre and t is in second. The work done during the first 4s is:
Solution:
$x = 3t - 4{t^2} + {t^3}$
$v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 3 - 8t + 3{t^2}$
$t = 0$
${v_0} = 3 - 8\left( 0 \right) + 3{\left( 0 \right)^2} = 3m/s$
${v_4} = 3 - 8\left( 4 \right) + 3{\left( 4 \right)^2} = 19\,m/s$
Applying work energy theorem
$$W = \frac{1}{2}m\left( {v_4^2 - v_0^2} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{{1000}}\left[ {{{\left( {19} \right)}^2} - {{\left( 3 \right)}^2}} \right]$$
$W = \frac{{528}}{{1000}} = 528\,mJ$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Work Done by Constant and Variable Force

Question:

Reason: If external force acting on one of the two blocks causes same acceleration in of both them, then contact force exists between them.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: If two body moves with the same acceleration then they act as a single system which is possible only when they are bound by a contact force.

Question: Two concentric metallic spherical shells are given unequal positive charges. Then,
Options: the outer sphere is always at a higher potential
the inner sphere is always at a higher potential
both the spheres are at the same potential
no prediction can be made about their potentials unless the actual values of charges and radii are known
Solution: Two concentric metallic spherical shells are given a positive charge. The potential difference depends on the radius of the spherical shells. As the inner sphere has less radius, so the potential will be high. The radius of outer-sphere has a large radius in comparison to the inner sphere, then the potential is less when compared to the inner sphere. Therefore, the inner sphere has more potential than the outer sphere.

Question: Two identical springs are connected in series and parallel as shown in the figure. If fs and fp are frequencies of series and parallel arrangements, what is fs/fp?
Solution:
\[f = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{k}{m}} \Rightarrow f\,\alpha \sqrt {{K_{eff}}} \]
\[{K_s} = \frac{k}{2},{K_p} = 2k\]
\[\frac{{{f_s}}}{{{f_p}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{K_s}}}{{{K_p}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{\frac{k}{2}}}{{2k}}} = \sqrt {\frac{1}{4}} = \frac{1}{2}\]

Question: The charge density on the surface of a conducting sphere is 64 × 10<sup>-7</sup> C/m<sup>2</sup> and the electric intensity at a distance of 2 m from the centre of the sphere is 4π× 10<sup>4</sup> N/C. The radius of the sphere is
Solution:
σ = 64 × 10-7 C/m2 ; E = 4π× 104 N/C
$$\sigma = \frac{q}{{4\pi {R^2}}};E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}.\frac{q}{{{r^2}}}$$
$$\frac{E}{\sigma } = \frac{{{R^2}}}{{{\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}$$
$$ \Rightarrow {R^2} = \frac{E}{\sigma } \times {\varepsilon _0}{r^2} = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^4}}}{{64 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}} \times 8.85 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times 4$$
$$ \Rightarrow R = \sqrt {0.694} = 0.83\,m$$

Question:
Assertion : In order to hit a target at same horizontal height, a man should point his rifle in the same direction as the target.
Reason : The horizontal range of the bullet is independent of the angle of projection with horizontal.
Options: Assertion is True, Reason is True; Reason is a correct explanation for Assertion
Assertion is True, Reason is True; Reason is NOT a correct explanation for Assertion
Assertion is True, Reason is False
Both Assertion and Reason are False
Solution:
Projectile motion experiences acceleration due to gravity in the downward direction. The man should point the rifle higher up than the target since the bullet will suffer a downward acceleration due to gravity.
Also, \[R = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 2\theta }}{g}\] (dependent on projection angle)

Question: A particle is moving along a circular path of radius 6 m with uniform speed of 8 ms<sup>–1</sup>. The average acceleration when the particle completes one half of the revolution is –
Solution:
On completing half of revolution, the velocity changes the direction. It is opposite in direction now.

Average acceleration = change in velocity divided by total time taken
\[a = \frac{{8 - \left( { - 8} \right)}}{t}.....\left( i \right)\]
Time taken to complete half revolution,
\[t = \frac{s}{v} = \frac{{\frac{{2\pi r}}{2}}}{8} = \frac{{\pi r}}{8}\]
Substituting this in equation (i),
\[a = \frac{{16}}{{\frac{{\pi \times 6}}{8}}} = \frac{{64}}{{3\pi }}m/{s^2}\]

z = 1

y + 2z = 0 x – y – z = 0

y = –2

x + 2 – 1 = 0

x = –1

M ⇒ T–1 C–2 h1
