Question: A car travelling north at 20 m/s turns and then travels south at 20 m/s. The change in its velocity is
Solution:
Let north be represented by \[ - \widehat i,\] thus south is represented by \[ + \widehat i\]

Initial velocity \[{\overrightarrow V _i} = - 20\widehat i\,,\] and final velocity

\[{\overrightarrow V _j} = 20\widehat i\]

Change in velocity

\[\Delta \overrightarrow V = {\overrightarrow V _j} - {\overrightarrow V _i} = 20\widehat i - \left( { - 20\widehat i} \right) = + 40\widehat i\]

\[\, \Rightarrow \Delta V = 40{\text{m/s}}\,\;{\text{south}}.\]

Question: In the arrangement as shown, the mass of block A is m. What should be the mass of the block B so that magnitude of acceleration of centre of mass of both the blocks is zero :
Solution:
for \[{\vec a_{cm}} = 0\]
\[{\vec a_A} = - {a_1}\hat i + {a_2}\hat j\]
\[{\vec a_B} = - {a_3}\hat i - {a_4}\hat j\]
So, it is possible only when
\[{a_A} = {a_B} = 0\]
The system is in equilibrium.
For A
\[mg\sin 30^\circ = T\]
\[ \Rightarrow T = \frac{{mg}}{2}\,...\left( i \right)\]
For B
\[{m_B}\sin 60^\circ = T\]
\[ \Rightarrow T = \frac{{{m_B}g\sqrt 3 }}{2}\,...\left( {ii} \right)\]
\[\therefore \] From equation (i) and (ii)
\[\frac{{mg}}{2} = \frac{{{m_B}g\sqrt 3 }}{2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow {m_B} = \frac{m}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]

Question: A $ 2 $ kg block slides on a horizontal floor with a speed of $ 4 $ m/s. It strikes an uncompressed spring, and compresses it till the block is motionless. The kinetic friction force is $ 15\,\,{\text{N}} $ and spring constant is $ 10,000 $ N/m, the spring compresses by
Solution:
Loss in KE $ = $ Work done against friction $ + $ PE stored in the spring $ \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = Fx + \frac{1}{2}\,\,k{x^2} $
 $ \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {\left( 4 \right)^2} = 15x + \frac{1}{2} \times 10,000\,\,{x^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{i}}{\text{.e}}{\text{., 16}} = {\text{15}}x + 5000\,\,{x^2} \approx \,\,\,5000{x^2} $
 $ x = \sqrt {\frac{{16}}{{5000}}} \approx 5.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}{\text{m}} = 5.5\,\,{\text{cm}} $

Question: The ratio of the radii of gyration of a circular disc about a tangential axis in the plane of the disc and of a circular ring of the same radius about a tangential axis in the plane of the ring is:
Solution:
Moment of inertia of a circular disc about a tangential axis in the plane of disc
${I_1} = MK_1^2 = \frac{5}{4}M{R^2},{K_1} = \sqrt {\frac{5}{4}} R$
Moment of inertia of a circular ring of same radius about a tangential axis in the plane of the ring is
${I_2} = MK_2^2 = \frac{3}{2}M{R^2}$
${K_2} = \sqrt {\frac{3}{2}} R$
$\therefore \frac{{{K_1}}}{{{K_2}}} = \frac{{\sqrt {5/4} R}}{{\sqrt {3/2} R}} = \sqrt {\frac{5}{4} \times \frac{2}{3}} = \sqrt {\frac{5}{6}} = \sqrt 5 :\sqrt 6 $

Question: A ship of mass  $ 2 \times {10^7}\,\operatorname{kg}  $  is at rest. On applying a force of  $ 25 \times {10^5}\,{\text{N}} $ , it is displaced through  $ 25{\text{ m}} $ . The speed of the ship after displacement is
An ice cube is kept on an inclined plane of angle \[{30^o}\]. Coefficient of kinetic friction between block and incline plane is \[\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]. What is acceleration of block?
Solution:
Frictional coefficient\[ = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]

Angle of inclination, \[\theta = {30^o}\]
Let’s consider acceleration of the block is a.
Normal force (N) can be found by balancing the equations as shown in the above diagram;
\[ \Rightarrow N = mg\,\,\cos \theta = mg\,\cos {30^o}\]
Friction force will be; \[ \Rightarrow f = \mu N = \mu \,mg\,\cos {30^o}\]
Then;
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow mg\,\sin {30^o} - f = ma \\
   \Rightarrow ma = mg\,\,\sin {30^o} - \mu \,mg\cos {30^o} \\
   \Rightarrow a = g\left( {\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right) = 0\frac{m}{{{s^2}}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A particle is executing SHM along a straight line. Its velocities at distances $${x_1}$$ and $${x_2}$$, from the mean position are $${v_1}$$ and $${v_2}$$, respectively. Its time period is:
Solution:
\[{V_1} = \omega \sqrt {{A^2} - x_1^2} \,\therefore v_1^2 = {\omega ^2}\left( {{A^2} - x_1^2} \right)\]
\[{v_2} = \omega \sqrt {{A^2} - x_2^2} \,\,\therefore v_2^2 = {\omega ^2}\left( {{A^2} - x_2^2} \right)\]
$$\therefore v_1^2 - v_2^2 = {\omega ^2}\left( {x_2^2 - x_1^2} \right){\mkern 1mu} $$
$$\therefore {\omega ^2} = \frac{{v_1^2 - v_2^2}}{{x_2^2 - x_1^2}}$$
\[\therefore \omega = \sqrt {\frac{{v_1^2 - v_2^2}}{{x_2^2 - x_1^2}}} \,\therefore T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega } = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{x_2^2 - x_1^2}}{{v_1^2 - v_2^2}}} \]

Question: Two thin, long, parallel wires, separated by a distance ‘d’ carry a current of ‘I’ A in the same direction. They will

Attract each other with a force of \[{\mu _0}{i^2}/\left( {2\pi d} \right)\]
Attract each other a force of \[{\mu _0}{i^2}/\left( {2\pi {d^2}} \right)\]
Solution: \[\frac{F}{\ell } = \frac{{{\mu _0}{i_1}{i_2}}}{{2\pi d}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{i^2}}}{{2\pi d}}\]

Question: Pressure at a point inside a liquid does not depend on:
Options: the depth of the point below the surface of the liquid
the acceleration due to gravity at that point
the shape of the containing vessel
Solution:

Pressure depends on height, density and gravity. But independent on the shape of the containing liquid.

Question:
Assertion: Value of frictional force as seen from an inertial frame for a pair of solids, may change if it is observed from a non-inertial frame.
Reason: Coefficient of friction$\mu $depends on the frame of reference.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Value of frictional force depends on inertial and non-inertial forces due to difference in net applied force. Coefficient of friction depends only on type and nature of the surfaces in contact.

Question:
Assertion: When a bicycle is in motion, the force of friction exerted by the ground on the two wheels is always in the forward direction.
Reason: The frictional force acts in the direction of motion of the bicycle.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution:
When a bicycle is in motion, two cases may arise:
(i) When the bicycle is being pedalled: In this case, the applied force is communicated to the rear wheel, due to which the rear wheel pushes the ground backwards. Now the force of friction acts in the forward direction on the rear wheel but front wheel moves forward due to inertia, so force of friction works on it in the backward direction.
(ii) When the bicycle is not being pedalled: In this case both the wheels move in the forward direction, due to inertia. Hence, force of friction on both the wheels acts in the backward direction.

Question: The core of a transformer is laminated because
Options: energy losses due to eddy currents may be minimised
the weight of the transformer may be reduced
rusting of the core may be prevented
ratio of voltages in primary and secondary may be increased
Solution:
When magnetic flux linked with a coil changes, induced emf is produced in it and the induced current flows through the wire forming the coil. These currents look like eddies or whirlpools and likewise are known as eddy currents. They are also known as Focault’s current.
These currents oppose the cause of their origin, therefore due to eddy currents, a great amount of energy is wasted in the form of heat energy. If core of transformer is laminated, then their effect can be minimised.

Question: The correct sequence of the increasing wavelength of the given radiation sources is
Options: radioactive sources, X-rays tube, crystal oscillator, sodium vapour lamp
radioactive source, X-rays tube sodium vapour lamp, crystal oscillator
X-ray tube, radioactive source, crystal oscillator, sodium vapour lamp
X-rays tube, crystal oscillator, radioactive source, sodium vapour lamp.
Solution: Increasing order of wave length, Radioactive sources, X-rays, sodium vapour lamp, Crystal oscillator.

Question: A 60 volt–10 watt bulb is operated at 100 volt–60 Hz a.c. The inductance required is

An alternating electric field, of frequency v, is applied across the dees (radius = R) of a cyclotron that is being used to accelerate protons (mass = m). The operating magnetic field (B) used in the cyclotron and the kinetic energy (K) of the proton beam, produced by it, are given by
Solution:
$$T = \frac{1}{v} = \frac{{2\pi m}}{{eB}}$$
$$B = \frac{{2\pi mv}}{e}$$

$$R = \frac{{mv}}{{eB}} = \frac{P}{{eB}}$$

$$P = eBR = e\left( {\frac{{2\pi mv}}{e}} \right)R = 2\pi mvR$$

$$K = \frac{{{P^2}}}{{2m}} = \frac{{{{\left( {2\pi mvR} \right)}^2}}}{{2m}} = 2{\pi ^2}m{v^2}{R^2}$$

Question: A given shaped glass tube having uniform cross-section is filled with water and is mounted on a rotatable shaft as shown in fig. If the tube is rotated with a constant angular velocity ω then:
Options: water levels in both A and B go up
water level in section A goes up and that in B comes down
water level in section A comes down and that in B it goes up
water level remain same in both sections
Solution:
Force acting on the fluid of length dx.
dF = A(dx) ρ w2x
Total force = \[\int_{}^{} {df} = \int\limits_0^L {A\rho {w^2}\,xdx} \]
= \[\frac{{A\rho {w^2}{L^2}}}{2}\]
Pressure at B will have on extra term due to this force.
Hence, water level will rise in column B.
In this problem, water level will rise in both A and B.

Question: A ball of mass 10 kg is moving with a velocity of 10 m/s. It strikes another ball of mass 5 kg, which is moving in the same direction with a velocity of 4 m/s. If the collision is elastic their velocities after collision will be respectively:

A grinding machine whose wheel has radius \[\frac{1}{\pi }\]metre runs at 2.5 rps, when a tool to be sharpened is held against the wheel with a force of \[40\,\,N\]. If the coefficient of friction between the tool and the wheel is 0.2, power developed is
Solution:
\[R = \frac{1}{\pi }m\,\,\,n = 2.5\,\,rps\,\,\,\omega = 2\pi \,\,n = 5\pi \]
\[F = 40N,\,\,\mu = 0.2\]
Torque \[ = \left( {\mu \times N} \right) \times R\]
\[\tau = 0.2 \times 40 \times \frac{1}{\pi } = \frac{8}{\pi }\]
\[P = \tau \times \omega = \frac{8}{\pi } \times 2\pi \times 2.5\,\, = 40W\]

Question: The radius of germanium (Ge) nuclide is measured to be twice the radius of \[_4^9Be\]. The number of nucleons in Ge are
Solution:
The radius of the nucleus is, $R \propto {A^{\frac{1}{3}}}$
Let, the radius of the germanium nucleus be $2r$, and ${}_4^9Be$the nucleus be $r$.
Therefore, $\frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}$where, $A$ is the atomic number
$\frac{r}{{2r}} = {\left( {\frac{9}{{{A_2}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}$
Therefore, the number of nucleons in Germanium nuclei is, $\therefore {A_2} = 72$

Question: A spring of spring constant 1000 N/m is compressed through 5 cm and is used to push a metal ball of mass 0.1 kg. The velocity with which the metal ball moves is
Solution:

\[\Delta {W_{ext\;force}} = \Delta K\]

$\frac{1}{2}k{x^2} = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$

$ \Rightarrow v = \sqrt {\frac{k}{m}} x = \sqrt {\frac{{1000}}{{0.1}}} 0.05 = 5m/s$

Question: The law that governs the force between electric charges is called
Solution: If two stationary point charges are kept at a particular distance, then the force of attraction or repulsion between them is inversely proportional to the square of the distance and directly proportional to the product of magnitudes of charges. This law is nothing but Coulomb’s law.

Question: The mean free path of a gas molecule at 27°C is 2 cm. If the rms velocity of the gas at that temperature is 10 m/s, what is the time interval between two successive collisions ?

\[t = \frac{{2cm}}{{10\,m/s}} = \frac{1}{{500}}s\]

Question: A long solenoid has 800 turns per metre length of solenoid. A current of 1.6 A flows through it. The magnetic induction at the end of the solenoid on its axis is

Question: An engine exerts a force $$\vec F = \left( {20\hat i - 3\hat j + 5\hat k} \right)N$$ and moves with velocity $$\vec v = \left( {6\hat i + 20\hat j - 3\hat k} \right)m/s$$. The power of the engine (in watt) is :
Solution:
Power is equal to the scalar product of force with velocity. Power of the engine,
$$P = \vec F.\vec v\,\,......\left( i \right)$$
Given
$$\vec F = \left( {20\hat i - 3\hat j + 5\hat k} \right)N$$
$$\vec v = \left( {6\hat i + 20\hat j - 3\hat k} \right)m/s$$
Thus, after substituting for $$\vec F$$ and $$\vec v$$ in equation (i), becomes,
$$P = \left( {20\hat i - 3\hat j + 5k} \right).\left( {6\hat i + 20\hat j - 3\hat k} \right)$$
$$ = \left( {20 \times 6} \right)\left( {\hat i\,.\,\hat i} \right) + \left( { - 3 \times 20} \right)\,\left( {\hat j\,.\,\hat j} \right) + \left( {5 \times - 3} \right)\left( {\hat k\,.\,\hat k} \right)$$$$ = 120 - 60 - 15 = 45W$$
Note : In the simplification for power, the dot product of a unit vector with same unit vector gives 1.
The dot product of a unit vector with its orthogonal gives zero. Thus,

$$\hat i.\,\hat i = \hat j\,.\,\hat j = \hat k\,.\,\hat k = 1$$
$$\hat i.\hat j = \hat i.\hat k = \hat j.\hat k = 0$$
So, in above simplification second type of dot product are not shown

Question: A block A of mass 7 kg is placed on a frictionless table. A thread tied to it passes over a frictionless pulley and carries a body B of mass 3 kg at the other end. The magnitude of acceleration of block B is (given g = 10 ms-2)
Solution:
For block A
T = 7a ….(1)
For Block B
3g – T = 3a ….(2)
Adding (1) and (2)
3g – T + T = 7a + 3a
⇒ 3g = 10 a
\[ \Rightarrow a = \frac{{3g}}{{10}} \Rightarrow a = \frac{{30}}{{10}}\]
⇒ a = 3 m/s2

Question: A deuteron of kinetic energy $50\,{\text{keV}}$ is describing a circular orbit of radius $0.5$ metre in a plane perpendicular to the magnetic field $B$. The kinetic energy of the proton that describes a circular orbit of radius $0.5$ metre in the same plane with the same $B$ is
Solution:
For a charged particle orbiting in a circular path in a magnetic field
$\frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = Bqv \Rightarrow v = \frac{{Bqr}}{m}$ or $m{v^2} = Bqvr$
Also, ${E_K} = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{1}{2}Bqvr = Bq\frac{r}{2} \cdot \frac{{Bqr}}{m} = \frac{{{B^2}{q^2}{r^2}}}{{2m}}$
For deuteron, ${E_1} = \frac{{{B^2}{q^2}{r^2}}}{{2 \times 2\,\operatorname{m} }}$
For proton, ${E_2} = \frac{{{B^2}{q^2}{r^2}}}{{2\operatorname{m} }}$
$\frac{{{E_1}}}{{{E_2}}} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{{50\,{\text{keV}}}}{{{E_2}}} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow {E_2} = 100\,{\text{keV}}$

Question: There is magnetic field acting perpendicular to plane of paper inwards. Particles in vacuum move in the plane of paper from left to right as shown. The paths are numbered as 1 to 3.Which statement is wrong.
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}

  F\,\,\,\,\, \propto \,\,\,{\text{charge}} \\

   \to \,\,{\text{Negative charge moves opposite to the forces}}{\text{.}} \\

  \left( a \right)\,\, \to \,\,1{\text{ could be the }}\alpha {\text{ - particle}} \\

  \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( d \right)\,\,\, \to \,\,3{\text{ is a proton because if it is moving in }} \\

  {\text{So}}\,\,\left( a \right),\,\,\left( b \right),\,\,\left( d \right)\,\,is{\text{ correct}}{\text{.}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A particle moves in the x-y plane according to the rule x = a sin ωt and y = a cos ωt. The particle follows:
Options: A straight line path inclined equally to the x and y-axis.
Solution:
\[{\left( {\frac{x}{a}} \right)^2} + {\left( {\frac{y}{a}} \right)^2} = {\sin ^2}\omega t + {\cos ^2}\omega t\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{a^2}}} = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} = {a^2}\]
Hence, it is a circular path.

Question: In above transfer characteristics of an n-p-n transistor in CE configuration; cut-off region, active region, saturation region respectively, are :
Solution:
Transistor used a Switch:-
In the cutoff region, both collector base junction emitter base junction are reverse biased. This will not allow the current to flow from collector to emitter when the base-emitter voltage is low. In this mode switch is off.
In the saturation region, both the emitter base and collector base junctions will be forward biased. Current will flow freely from collector to emitter if the base-emitter voltage is high. In this mode device is completely switched ON.
In the active region, the transition take place. It is attained when collector-base junctions (output region) is reverse biased and base-emitter junctions (input region) is forward biased . In this region, transistors have many applications. This is also called as linear region. A transistor while in this region, acts better as an Amplifier.

Question: A semi-circular arc of radius ‘a’ is charged uniformly and the charge per unit length is λ. The electric field at the centre of this arc is
Solution:
$$dE = \frac{{kdq}}{{{R^2}}}$$

Due to symmetry Ex =0

$$\eqalign{
  & d{E_y} = - dE\sin \theta \cr
  & d{E_y} = \frac{{ - kdq\sin \theta }}{{{R^2}}} \cr
  & d{E_y} = \frac{{ - kR\lambda d\theta \sin \theta }}{{{R^2}}} \cr
  & {E_y} = \frac{{-k\lambda }}{R}\int\limits_0^\pi {\sin \theta d\theta } \cr
  & {E_y} = \frac{{-k\lambda }}{R}\left[ {-\cos } \right]_0^\pi = \frac{{-2k\lambda }}{R} \cr
  & \left| {{E_y}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{2k\lambda }}{R}} \right| = \frac{{2\lambda }}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}R}} \cr
  & \therefore {E_y} = \frac{\lambda }{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}a}}[\because R = a] \cr} $$

Question: Two capacitors marked $10\mu F,$ 400 volt and $4\mu F,$ 100 Volt are connected in series. What is the maximum safe potential that can be applied when these capacitor are joined in series.
Solution:
${Q_1} = 4000\mu C$ ${Q_2} = 400\mu C$
Maximum charge can be provided to both capacitor is $400\mu C$.
${C_{eq}} = \frac{{40}}{{14}}\mu F;Q = {C_{eq}}.V$
$V = \frac{{400 \times 14}}{{40}} = 140V$
$400 = \frac{{40}}{{14}}V$
$ \Rightarrow V = 140V$

Question: An electron is moving along positive x axis. A uniform electric field exists towards negative y axis. What should be the directions of the magnetic field of suitable magnitude so that net force on the electron is zero?

\[\hat j = \hat i \times \left( { - \hat k} \right)\]
Direction of Magnetic field should be suitable so that it null the force due to Electric field.

\[{\vec F_E}\] is along positive y-axis. So, \[{\vec F_B}\] should be along negative y-axis.
(note : Electron has negative charge, hence the direction of force will be opposite the direction of cross product vector)

Question:
An ac voltage is represented by \[E = 220\sqrt 2 \,\cos \,\left( {50\pi } \right)\,t\]
How many time will the current become zero is 1 s ?
Solution:
\[E = {E_0}\cos \omega t\]
\[E = 220\sqrt 2 \cos \left( {50\pi } \right)t\]
\[\omega = 50\pi \]
\[2\pi \gamma = 50\pi \]
\[\gamma = 25Hz\]
So current became zero 50 time in 1 sec.

Question: Hydrogen atom is excited from ground state to another state with principal quantum number equal to 4. Then the number of spectral lines in the emission spectra will be:
Solution:
For ground state, the principal quantum no. $\left( n \right) = 1.$
There is a 3rd excited state for principal quantum number.

The possible number of the spectral lines is given $ = \frac{{n\left( {n - 1} \right)}}{2} = \frac{{4\left( {4 - 1} \right)}}{2} = 6$

$\begin{gathered}

Question: The ratio of work done by an ideal monoatomic gas to the heat supplied to it in an isobaric process is
Solution:
In an isobaric process (P = constant)
Heat supplied $\Delta Q = n{C_P}\Delta T$
[for a monoatomic gas ${C_P} = \frac{J}{2}R$]
$ \Rightarrow \Delta Q = \frac{5}{2}nR\Delta T$
Work done $W = P\Delta V = nRT$
$\frac{{\left( 2 \right)}}{{\left( 1 \right)}} \Rightarrow \frac{W}{{\Delta Q}} = \frac{{nR\Delta T}}{{\frac{5}{2}nR\Delta T}} = \frac{2}{5}$

Question: In the pulley system shown, if radii of the bigger and smaller pulley are 2 m and 1 m respectively and the acceleration of block A is 5 m/s2 in the downward direction, then the acceleration of block B will be:
Solution:
In the given problem, both the pulley will rotate by the same angle in any interval of time.
\[\therefore {\alpha _A} = {\alpha _B}\; \Rightarrow \frac{{{a_A}}}{{{r_A}}} = \frac{{{a_B}}}{{{r_B}}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {a_B} = {a_A}\left( {\frac{{{r_B}}}{{{r_A}}}} \right) = 5\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right) = \frac{5}{2}{\text{ m/}}{{\text{s}}^2}\]

Question: A projectile fired at \[30^\circ \] to the horizontal. The vertical component of its velocity at point of projection is \[80m{s^{ - 1}}\]. Its time of flight is T. What will be the velocity of the projectile at \[t = \frac{T}{2}\]?
Solution:
At half of the time of flight, the position of the projectile will be at the highest point of the parabola and at that position particle possess horizontal component of velocity only.
Given,
\[{u_{vertical}} = u\sin \theta = 80 \Rightarrow u = \frac{{80}}{{\sin 30^\circ }} = 160m/s\]
\[\therefore {u_{horizontal}} = u\cos \theta = 160\cos 30^\circ = 80\sqrt 3 m/s\]

Question: At absolute zero, Si acts as
Solution: Pure silicon, at absolute zero, will contain all the electrons in bounded state. The conduction band will be empty. So, there will be no free electrons (in conduction band) and holes (in valence band) due to thermal agitation. Pure silicon will act as insulator.

Question: Water does not wet an oily glass because
Options: cohesive force of oil > adhesive force between oil and glass
cohesive force of oil > cohesive force of water
cohesive force of water > adhesive force between water and oil molecules
Solution: Cohesive force of water is greater than adhesive force between water and oil hence water does not wet the surface.

Question: If the resultant of the vectors \[\left( {\hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k} \right),\,\,\left( {\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k} \right)\] and $\overrightarrow C $ is a unit vector along the y-direction, then $\overrightarrow C $ is
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow A = \widehat i + 2\widehat j - \hat k;\,\overrightarrow B = \widehat i - \widehat j + 2\hat k\]

Let, \[\overrightarrow C = x\widehat i + y\widehat j + z\hat k\]

\[\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B + \overrightarrow C = 1\widehat j\]

\[ \Rightarrow \left( {2 + x} \right)\widehat i + \left( {1 + y} \right)\widehat j + \left( {1 + z} \right)\widehat k = 1\widehat j\]

Equating components.

\[2 + x = 0 \Rightarrow x = - 2\]

\[1 + y = 1 \Rightarrow y = 0\]

\[1 + z = 0 \Rightarrow z = - 1\]

\[ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow C = - 2\hat i - \hat k\]

Question: In a projectile motion, the velocity
Options: Is always perpendicular to the acceleration
Is never perpendicular to the acceleration
Is perpendicular to the acceleration for one instant only
Is perpendicular to the acceleration for two instants
Solution: In projectile motion velocity is perpendicular to acceleration only at highest point to trajectory. Therefore, velocity is perpendicular to the acceleration for one instant only.

Question: Two rigid boxes containing different ideal gases are placed on a table. Box A contains one mole of nitrogen at temperature T<sub>0</sub>, while box B contains one mole of helium at temperature (7/3)T<sub>0</sub>. The boxes are then put into thermal contact with each other and heat flows between them until the gases reach a common final temperature (Ignore the heat capacity of boxes and heat exchange will happen only between boxes). Then, the final temperature of the gases, T<sub>f</sub> in terms of T<sub>0</sub> is
Solution:
When two gases are mixed together then
Heat lost by the Helium gas = Heat gained by the Nitrogen gas
\[{\mu _B} \times {({C_v})_{He}} \times \left( {\frac{7}{3}{T_0} - {T_f}} \right) = {\mu _A} \times \left( {{C_v}} \right){N_2} \times \left( {{T_f} - {T_0}} \right)\]

\[ \Rightarrow 1 \times \frac{3}{2}R \times \left( {\frac{7}{3}{T_0} - {T_f}} \right) = 1 \times \frac{5}{2}R \times \left( {{T_f} - {T_0}} \right)\]
By solving we get \[{T_f} = \frac{3}{2}{T_0}\]

i.e., B ∝ (1/r) (r > a).
Magnetic field at a point lying inside the conductor is

Question: A man drops a ball downwards from the roof of a tower of height 400 meters. At the same time another ball is thrown upwards with a velocity 50 meter/sec. from the bottom of the tower, then they will meet at which height from the bottom of the tower
Solution:

\[{{\text{h}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{h}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ = 400}}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{\text{g}}{{\text{t}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{ + 50t - }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{\text{g}}{{\text{t}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{ = 400}}\]

Question: A block A kept on an inclined surface just begins to slide if the inclination is 30°. The block is replaced by another block B and it is found that it just begins to slide if the inclination is 40°.
Options: mass of A > mass of B
mass of A < mass of B
mass of A = mass of B
Solution:
if a plane is inclined at some angle say θ and μ which is coefficient of friction between the plane and the object then
\[\mu = \tan \theta \]
Now, according to the question object A starts to slide when θ=300 and at this angle the coefficient of friction has its some value like,
\[\mu = \tan 30 = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]
When the object A is replaced by object B, then the angle required to just sliding of object B were θ=400.
Which leads to different value of coefficient of friction from the case when object A were placed. So, we see that the sliding of an object only depends upon the coefficient of friction between the two bodies and its independent of the mass of the body. So, the mass of body A can either be greater, lesser or even equal to the mass of body B.
Hence, the correct option is D.

Question: Given that P = 12, Q = 5 and R = 13 also \[\vec P + \vec Q = \vec R,\] then the angle between \[\vec P\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec Q\] will be

Question:
Assertion: If there is Coulomb attraction between two bodies, both of them may not be charged.
Reason: They will be oppositely charged.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Coulomb attraction exists even when one body is charged, and the other is uncharged. If both are charged then they must be oppositely charged otherwise any charge can attract any uncharged body. So the reason is false.

Question: An electric dipole of moment $\vec p$ is placed in a uniform electric field $\vec E$, with parallel to $\vec p$. It is then rotated by an angle $\theta$. The work done is
Solution:
$\tau = pE{\text{sin}}\theta $
     Work$ = \smallint \tau \,d\theta $
      $ = \mathop \smallint \limits_0^\theta pE{\text{sin}}\theta = pE\left[ {{\text{cos}}\theta } \right]_\theta ^0$
     $ = pE\left( {1 - {\text{cos}}\theta } \right)$

Question: A body is under the action of two equal and opposite forces, each of 3 N. The body is displaced by 2 m. The work done is:
Solution:
\[{F_{{\text{net}}}} = 0\]

\[W = \vec F \cdot \Delta \vec r = 0.\Delta \vec r = 0\]

Tags.Topic: Work Done by Constant and Variable Force

Question:
Assertion: A reference frame attached to the Earth is an inertial frame of reference.
Reason: The reference frame which has zero acceleration is called a non-inertial frame of reference.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: An inertial frame of reference is one which has zero acceleration and in which law of inertia holds good, that is, Newton’s first law of motion is applicable equally. Since Earth is revolving around the Sun and also rotating about its own axis, there are forces acting on it and hence there will be acceleration of Earth due to these factors. Therefore, Earth cannot be taken as inertial frame of reference.

Question: The point charges Q and –2Q are placed at some distance apart. If the electric field at the location of Q is E, the electric field at the location of –2Q will be:
Solution:
As field on Q is E, force on Q is $$F = QE$$
Let field on –2Q be E’. So, force on –2Q will be

$$F' = - 2QE'$$

Using Coulomb’s law, the forces on both will be equal, i.e., $$F' = F$$or $$QE = - 2QE'$$.

So, $$E' = - \frac{E}{2}$$

VR = 4V

Question: A mass of 1 kg attached to the bottom of a spring has a certain frequency of vibration. The following mass has to be added to it in order to reduce the frequency by half.
Solution:
$f \propto \omega $
$$\frac{{{f_1}}}{{{f_2}}} = \frac{{\sqrt {\frac{k}{{{m_1}}}} }}{{\sqrt {\frac{k}{{{m_2}}}} }} \Rightarrow \frac{f}{{\frac{f}{2}}} = \frac{{\sqrt {\frac{k}{1}} }}{{\sqrt {\frac{k}{{m + 1}}} }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sqrt {m + 1} = 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow m = 3kg$$

Question: A man weighing 60kg lifts a body of mass\[15\,kg\] to the top of a building 10m high in 3minute. His efficiency is

\[M = 60kg,\Delta m = 15kg,\,\,H = 10m,t = 3\min \]
\[\eta = \frac{{{W_{out}}}}{{{W_{in}}}}\]
\[\eta = \frac{{\Delta mgh}}{{\left( {M + \Delta m} \right)gh}} = \frac{{\Delta m}}{{\left( {M + \Delta m} \right)}} = \frac{{15}}{{60 + 15}}\% \]
\[\eta = \frac{1}{5} \times 100 = 20\% \]

Question: Mark the correct statements:

Question: A charge q is placed at (1, 2, 1) and other charge –q is placed at (0, 1, 0) such that they form an electric dipole. There exist a uniform electric field \[\vec E = 2\hat i + 3\hat j\]. What is torque experienced by the dipole.
Solution:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  {\vec \tau = \vec p \times \vec E} \\
  {\vec E = 2\hat i \times 3\hat j} \\
  {\vec p = q(\hat i + \hat j + \hat k)} \\
  {\vec \tau = q( - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k)Nm}
\end{array}$

Question: For a projectile, if the velocity of projection be doubled, keeping the angle of projection  same, then  the maximum height reached by it will:

Question: In the given circuit below, the charge in μ C on the capacitor having capacity of 5μ F
Solution:
$\frac{1}{{C{'_{eff}}}} = \frac{1}{{{C_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_2} + {C_3}}} = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{{2 + 5}} = \frac{{10}}{{21}}$
${C_{eff}} = C{'_{eff}} + 4 = 2.1 + 4 = 6.1{\text{ }}\mu {\text{F}}$
Ceff = 6.1 μ F
QB = Ceff × V = 6.1 × 6
⇒ QB = 36.6C
Q’ = 4 × 10-6 × 6 = 24 μ C
Q” = 2.1 × 6 = 12.6 μ C
Potential difference across 2μ F and 5μ F combination.

Charge on 5μ F (Q3) = C3V2 = 5 × 1.8 = 9μ C

Question: Three capacitors 2.0, 3.0 and 6.0 microfarad are connected in series to a 10 V source. The charge on the 3.0 microfarad capacitor in microcoulomb is
Solution:
Net capacitance \[ = \frac{1}{{\left( {\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{6}} \right)}} = 1\mu F\]

Total charge
= CV = 1μ F × 10V = 10 μ C

Total charge on every capacitor in series system is same. So charge on 3μ F is 10μ C

Question: A metallic chain 1 m long lies on a horizontal surface of a table. The chain starts sliding on the table if 25 cm (or more of it) hangs over the edge of a table. The correct value of the coefficient of friction between the table and the chain is

In an electrical circuit $ R,L,C $ and an $ a.c. $ voltage source are all connected in series. When $ L $ is removed from the circuit, the phase difference between the voltage and the current in the circuit is $ \pi /3. $ If instead, $ C $ is removed from the circuit, the phase difference is again $ \pi /3. $ The power factor of the circuit is
Solution:
when $ L $ is removed from the circuit

 $ \frac{{{X_C}}}{R} = \tan \frac{\pi }{3} $
 $ {X_C} = R\tan \frac{\pi }{3} $ …(1)
When $ C $ is remove from the circuit

 $ \frac{{{X_L}}}{R} = \tan \frac{\pi }{3} $
 $ {X_C} = R\tan \frac{\pi }{3} $ …(2)

net impedance $ Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{({X_L} - {X_C})}^2} = R} $
power factor $ \cos \phi = \frac{R}{Z} = 1 $

Question: In the given figure the capacitors C1, C3, C4 and C5 have a capacitance of $4\mu F$ each. If the capacitor C2 has a capacitance of $10\mu F$, then effective capacitance between A and B is

$${C_{14}} = 2 = {C_{35}}$$
$${C_{AB}} = 2 + 2 = 4\mu F$$

Question: A truck weighing 1000 kg changes its speed from 36 km/h to 72 km/h in 2 minutes. Thus, the work done by the engine on the truck is:
Solution:
\[{\text{M}} = 1000\;{\text{kg}}\]

\[{\kern 1pt} {{\text{v}}_1} = 36\;{\text{km}}/{\text{hr}}\; = {\text{10m/s}}\]

\[{{\text{v}}_2} = 72\;{\text{km}}/{\text{hr}} = 20\;{\text{m}}/{\text{s}}\]

\[\Delta W = \Delta K = \frac{1}{2} \times m{v^2} - \frac{1}{2} \times m \times {u^2}\]

\[\Delta W = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times \left[ {{{20}^2} - {{10}^2}} \right] = 500 \times 300\]

\[ = 1.5 \times {10^5}{\text{J}}\]

Question: The molecules of a given mass of a gas have a r.m.s velocity of 200m/sec at $27^\circ C$and $1.0 \times {10^5}N/{m^2}$pressure. When the temperature is $127^\circ C$and pressure is $0.5 \times {10^5}N/{m^2}$the r.m.s. velocity in m/sec will be
Solution:
r.m.s velocity doesn’t upon pressure, it depends upon temperature only i.e.${v_{rms}} = \sqrt T $
$\frac{{{v_1}}}{{{v_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}}} $
$\frac{{200}}{{{v_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{\left( {273 + 27} \right)}}{{\left( {273 + 127} \right)}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{300}}{{400}}} $
${v_2} = \frac{{400}}{{\sqrt 3 }}m/\sec $

Question: A sphere of solid material of specific gravity 8 has a concentric spherical cavity and just sinks in water. Then, the ratio of the radius of the cavity to the outer radius of the sphere must be
Solution:
Let say r be the radius of cavity and R be the radius of outer sphere. Acc. To law of floatation, a body floats if the weight of the body is equal to the weight of water displaced.
$${W_s} = {W_w}$$

$${V_s}{\rho _s}g = {V_w}{\rho _w}g$$

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi \left( {{R^3} - {r^3}} \right) \times 8{\rho _w} = \frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3} \times {\rho _w}$$

$$8\left( {{R^3} - {r^3}} \right) = {R^3}$$

$$\frac{{{r^3}}}{{{R^3}}} = \frac{7}{8}$$

$$\frac{r}{R} = \frac{{{7^{1/3}}}}{2}$$

Question: A capacitor connected to a 10 V battery collects a charge of 40 microcoulomb with air as dielectric and 100 microcoulomb with oil as dielectric. The dielectric constant of the oil is
Solution:
C (with air in between) \[ = \frac{q}{V} = 4\mu F\]
C (with oil in between) \[ = \frac{{100 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{10}} = 10\mu F\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{C\left( {with\,\,oil\,\,in\,\,between} \right)}}{{C\left( {with\,\,air\,\,in\,\,between} \right)}} = \frac{{10}}{4} = \frac{5}{2} \\
  \frac{{C\left( {with\,\,oil\,\,in\,between} \right)}}{{C\left( {with\,\,air\,\,in\,\,between} \right)}} = \frac{{k\frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}}}{{\frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}}} = \frac{5}{2} \Rightarrow k = 2.5 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A projectile if fired making an angle \[2\theta \] with horizontal with velocity 4 ms<sup>-1</sup>. At any instant it makes an angle \[\theta \]then its velocity is
Solution:
Let v be the velocity when projected with angle \[\theta \], then equating the horizontal velocities in both the cases, we get
(horizontal velocities remains same during projectile)


\[v\cos \theta = u\cos \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow v = \frac{{u\cos 2\theta }}{{\cos \theta }}\]

\[As,\,\sec \theta = \frac{1}{{\cos \theta }}\]

\[\therefore v = u\cos 2\theta \sec \theta \]

\[{\text{Using}}\,\cos 2\theta = 2{\cos ^2}\theta - 1\]

\[{\text{Given}},\,\,u = 4m{s^{ - 1}},\,{\text{we}}\,{\text{get}}\]

\[v = 4\left( {2{{\cos }^2}\theta - 1} \right)\sec \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow v = 4\left( {2\cos \theta - \sec \theta } \right)\]

Question: A capacitor having capacitance 1 μ F with air, is filled with two dielectrics as shown. How many times capacitance will increases?
Solution:
As shown, the two capacitors are connected in parallel. Initially the capacitance of capacitor
\[C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}\]
Where A is area of each plate and dis the separation between the plates.
After filling with dielectrics, we have two capacitors of capacitance
\[{C_1} = \frac{{{K_1}{\varepsilon _0}\left( {A/2} \right)}}{d} = \frac{8}{2}\frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d} = \frac{{4{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}\] and \[{C_2} = \frac{{{K_2}{\varepsilon _0}\left( {A/2} \right)}}{d}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  =\frac{4}{2}\frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d} \\
 = \frac{{2{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d} = 2C \\
\end{gathered} \]
Hence, their equivalent capacitance
C = C1 + C2
= 4C + 2C
= 6C

Question: Charge q is uniformly distributed over a thin half ring of radius R. The electric field at the centre of the ring is
Solution:
From figure, \[dl = Rd\theta ,\]
Charge on \[dl = \lambda Rd\theta \,\,\,\,\,\left( {\therefore \lambda = \frac{q}{{\pi R}}} \right)\]
Electric field at centre due to \[dl\,\,is\,\,dE = K\frac{{\lambda Rd\theta }}{{{R^2}}}\]
We need to consider only the component \[dE\cos \theta \]as the component \[dE\sin \theta \]will cancel out because of the field at C due to the symmetrical element dl’.
Total field at centre \[ = 2\int\limits_0^\pi {dE\cos \theta } \]
\[ = \frac{{2K\lambda }}{R}\int_0^\pi {\cos \theta d\theta } = \frac{{2K\lambda }}{R} = \frac{q}{{2{\pi ^2}{\varepsilon _0}{R^2}}}\]

Question: A meter stick of mass 400 g is pivoted at one end and displaced through an angle 60°. The increases in its potential energy is:
Solution:
\[M = 400gm,\,\,\,\theta = 60^\circ \]

\[\Delta W = \Delta U = mgh\]


Using COM concept,

\[\Delta W = \Delta U = mgl(1 - \cos \theta )\]

\[\Delta W = \frac{{400}}{{1000}} \times 10 \times \frac{{50}}{{100}} \times (1 - \cos 60) = 1\,J\]

Question: A rectangular block of mass m and area of cross-section A floats in a liquid of density $$\rho $$.If it is given a small vertical displacement from equilibrium, it undergoes oscillation with a time period T. Then,
Solution:
Let block is displacement through x metre, then weight displaced water or upthrust (upwards)
$$ = - Ax\rho g\,\,\left[ {\because f = mg = \left( {Ax\rho } \right)g} \right]$$

where, A is area of cross-section of the block and $$\rho $$is its density. This must be equal to force (=ma) applied, where m is mass of the block and a is acceleration.

$$\therefore ma = - Ax\rho g$$

$$ \Rightarrow a = - \frac{{A\rho g}}{m}x = - {\omega ^2}x$$


This is the equation of simple harmonic motion. Time period of oscillation,

$$T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega } = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{m}{{A\rho g}}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow T \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt A }}\left[ {\because T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{displacement}}{{acceleratoin}}} } \right]$$

Question: A stone is shot straight upward with a speed of 20 m/sec from a tower 200 m high. The speed with which it strikes the ground is approximate. (take g = 9.8 m/s<sup>2</sup>)
Solution:
Speed of stone in a vertically upward direction is 20 m/s. So for vertical downward motion we will consider \[u = - 20m/s\]
\[{v^2} = {u^2} + 2gh = {\left( { - 20} \right)^2} + 2 \times 9.8 \times 200 \Rightarrow v = 65.73m/s\]

Question: If $\overrightarrow A  = 2\widehat i + 3\widehat j + 4\widehat k$ and $\overrightarrow B  = 4\widehat i + 3\widehat j + 2\widehat k,$ then angle between $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $ is
Solution:
$\overrightarrow A = 2\widehat i + 3\widehat j + 4\widehat k$ and $\overrightarrow B = 4\widehat i + 3\widehat j + 2\widehat k$

Let angle between $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $ is

$\overrightarrow A \cdot \overrightarrow B = AB\cos \theta $

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\left( {8 + 9 + 8} \right) = \left( {\sqrt {4 + 9 + 16} } \right)$

$\left( {\sqrt {16 + 9 + 4} } \right)\cos \theta $

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\cos \theta = \frac{{25}}{{{{\left( {\sqrt {29} } \right)}^2}}}$

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{25}}{{{{\left( {\sqrt {29} } \right)}^2}}}} \right)$

Question: The displacement-time graph of a moving particle is shown in figure. The instantaneous velocity of the particle is negative at the point:

Question: A bullet of mass 2 g is having a charge of $2\mu C$. Through what potential difference must it be accelerated, starting from rest, to acquire a speed of 10 m/s?

Velocity $$v = \sqrt {\frac{{2qV}}{m}} $$
$$V = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{{2q}} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}\left( {{{10}^2}} \right)}}{{2\left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)}} = 50,000V = 50\,kV$$

Question: Two equal drops of water are falling through air with a steady velocity v. If the drops coalesce, the new velocity will be
Solution:
Let r be the radius of smaller drop and R the radius of bigger drop. By equating the volume we have.
\[2\left( {\frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}} \right) = \frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3}\,\,or\,\,R = {\left( 2 \right)^{1/3}}.\,r\]
Now terminal velocity \[ \propto \] (radius)2
\[\therefore \,\,v' = {\left( 2 \right)^{2/3}}v\]

Question: A physical quantity P is described by the relation P = a<sup>1/2</sup> b<sup>2</sup> c<sup>3</sup> d<sup>–4</sup> If the relative errors in the measurement of a,b,c and d respectively, are 2%, 1%, 3% and 5%, then the relative error in P will be-

= ((1/2) × 2) + (2 × 1) + (3 × 3) + (4 × 5)

Question: If the flux of the electric field through a closed surface is zero then
Options: Electric field must be zero everywhere on the surface

Electric field must be uniform throughout the closed surface
Charge outside the surface must be zero
Solution: If the flux of the electric field through a closed surface is zero then Total charge inside the surface must be zero as flux = qenclosed/ ε0

Question:
Assertion: Newton’s second law holds good in an inertial frame only.
Reason: Newton’s second law is a basic law.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: To apply Newton’s second law in a non-inertial frame, we have to consider a pseudo force and it has to be applied only on inertial frame. Newton’s second law is a basic law because with the help of this law we can derive other two laws.

Question: If for a given angle of projection, the horizontal range is doubled, the time of flight becomes
Solution:
$R = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 2\theta }}{g}$
when \[R \to 2R\]i.e. \[u \to \sqrt 2 u\]

So, \[T = \frac{{2u\sin \theta }}{g}\left( {T \propto u} \right)\]

So, \[T \to \sqrt 2 T\]

Question: In forward biasing of the p-n junction,
Options: the positive terminal of the battery is connected to p-side and the depletion region becomes thin.
the positive terminal of the battery is connected to p -side and the depletion region becomes thick.
the positive terminal of the battery is connected to n-side and the depletion region becomes thin.
the positive terminal of the battery is connected to n-side and the depletion region becomes thick.
Solution:
In forward biasing of the p-n junction, the positive terminal of the battery is connected to p side and negative terminal of the battery is connected to n-side.
When the junction diode is in forward bias, width of depletion layer decreases.

Question: One of the rectangular components of a velocity of 60 kmh<sup>−</sup><sup>1</sup> is 30 km h<sup>−</sup><sup>1</sup>. The other rectangular component is
Solution:
Choose one of the direction as x and other as y.

\[\overrightarrow v = {v_x}\widehat i + {v_y}\widehat j\,;v = 60,{v_x} = 30\,;\]

\[{v^2} = v_x^2 + v_y^2 \Rightarrow {\left( {60} \right)^2} = {\left( {30} \right)^2} + v_y^2\]

$ \Rightarrow {v_y} = 30\sqrt 3 $ km/hr

Question: A mass of  $ 2\,\operatorname{kg}  $  is whirled in a horizontal circle by means of a string at an initial speed of  $ {\text{5}} $  revolutions per minute. Keeping the radius constant the tension in the string is doubled. The new speed is nearly
Solution:
Tension in the string $ T = m{\omega ^2}r = 4{\pi ^2}{n^2}mr $
  $ \therefore \,\,T \propto {n^2} $ $ \Rightarrow \frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{T_2}}}{{{T_1}}}} $
  $ \Rightarrow {n_2} = 5\sqrt {\frac{{2T}}{T}} = 7\,{\text{rpm}} $

Question: Scent sprayer is based on :

According to Bernoulli’s theorem, an increase in the velocity of the fluid co-occurs with a decrease in the pressure or a decrease in the potential energy of the fluid, or an increase in the pressure could be shown to result in a decrease in the velocity of the fluid.

Question:
The magnitude of the magnetic field $ \left( B \right) $ due to loop $ ABCD $ at the origin $ \left( O \right) $ is: (Here, $ {{\mu }_{0}} $ is
permeability of free space)
Solution:
$ O $ is along line $ CD $ and $ AB $ . They do not contribute to the magnetic induction at $ O $ . The field due to $ DA $ is positive $ r $ out of the paper and that due to $ BC $ is into the paper and negative. The total magnetic field due to loop $ ABCD $ at $ O $ is
 $ B=BAB+BCD+BDA $
$\Rightarrow B=0-\frac{{{\mu }_{_{0}}}2\text{I}}{4\pi b}\times \frac{\pi }{6}+O+\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}2\text{I}}{4\pi a}\times \frac{\pi }{6}\Rightarrow B=\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}I}{12ab}\left( b-a \right)$
out of paper or positive.

Question:
Assertion: The value of dynamic friction is less than the limiting friction.
Reason: Once the motion has started, the inertia of rest has been overcome.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: For a body at rest, friction force is static so it has to act against inertia of rest but once the motion starts inertia of rest  has been overcome and friction force reduces. So dynamic friction becomes lesser than static friction.

Question: In an experiment four quantities a,b,c and d are measured with percentage error 1% , 2 % , 3% and 4% respectively. Quantity P is calculated as follows \[P = \frac{{{a^3}{b^2}}}{{cd}}\]. percentage error in P is
Solution:
Given that
\[P = \frac{{{a^3}{b^2}}}{{cd}}\]
\[\therefore \,\,\frac{{\Delta P}}{P} =  \pm \left[ {3\frac{{\Delta a}}{a} + 2\frac{{\Delta b}}{b} + \frac{{\Delta c}}{c} + \frac{{\Delta d}}{d}} \right]\]
\[ \to \]Percentage error in P,
\[\frac{{\Delta P}}{P} \times 100 = \pm \left[ {3 \times 1\% + 2 \times 2\% + 3\% + 4\% } \right]\]
\[ = \pm 14\% \]
Hence, the % error in P is 14%.

Tags.Subject: Physics

Question: Which of the following is not the property of equipotential surfaces?
Options: They do not cross each other
They are concentric spheres for uniform electric field
Rate of change of potential with distance on them is zero
Solution:
Equipotential surfaces are plane surfaces as the uniform field is directed in a fixed direction for all points.
The electric field is always perpendicular to equipotential surface as$$E = - \frac{{dV}}{{dr}}$$.
Now as the surfaces are equipotential, so it must be spaced such that the surfaces have equal differences in potential and are separated by equal distances.
We have $$E = - \frac{{dV}}{{dr}}$$, so it must have decreasing potentials in the direction of the field.

Question: The power dissipated in an AC circuit is zero, if the circuit is
Options: either purely inductive or purely capacitive
Solution:
The power in an AC circuit is given by P = VI cosφ.
where, φ = phase angle between V and I. Thus, power is zero when cosφ = 0
⇒ φ = 90°
For both pure inductive and pure capacitive, the phase between current and voltage is 90°. Thus, they have zero power dissipation.

$ \Rightarrow AC = \sqrt {A{B^2} + B{C^2}} = \sqrt {{{\left( {\pi R} \right)}^2} + {{(2R)}^2}} $

$ \Rightarrow AC = \sqrt {{\pi ^2} + 4} {\text{ }}m$

Question: A circular conducting ring of radius R is connected to two exterior straight wires ending at two ends of a diameter. The current I split into unequal portions while passing through the ring as shown. What is magnetic field induction at the centre of the ring?

${B_0} = {B_2} - {B_1} = \frac{{{\mu _o}I}}{{8R}}$

Question:
What is the number of α and β− particle emitted respectively in the following radioactive decay?
90X200 → 80Y168

Half-lives of two radioactive substances A and B are respectively 20 minutes and 40 minutes. Initially the samples of A and B have equal number of nuclei. After 80 minutes, the ratio of remaining numbers of A and B nuclei is
Solution:
${{\text{T}}_{\text{A}}}{\text{ = 20}}$ Min in 80 min, it has 4 half lives
${{\text{T}}_{\text{B}}}{\text{ = 40}}$ Min in 80 min, it has 2 half lives
So, \[{{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{N}}_{\text{0}}}{\left( {\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}} \right)^{\text{4}}}\]
$\begin{gathered}
  {{\text{N}}_{\text{B}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{N}}_{\text{0}}}{\left( {\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}} \right)^{\text{2}}} \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}}}{{{{\text{N}}_{\text{B}}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\frac{{\text{1}}}{{16}}}}{{\frac{1}{4}}} = \frac{1}{4} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: To determine the Young’s modulus of a wire, the formula is \[Y = \frac{F}{A}.\frac{L}{{\Delta l}};\] where L = length, A = area of cross – section of the wire, ΔL = Change in length of the wire when stretched with a force F. The conversion factor to change it from CGS to MKS system is
Solution:
We know that the dimension of young’s modulus is [ML−1T−2]

C.G.S. unit: gm cm−1 sec−2 and M.K.S. unit: kg. m−1 sec−2.

By using the conversion formula:

\[{n_2} = {n_1}{\left[ {\frac{{{M_1}}}{{{M_2}}}} \right]^1}{\left[ {\frac{{{L_1}}}{{{L_2}}}} \right]^{ - 1}}{\left[ {\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}}} \right]^{ - 2}} = {\left[ {\frac{{gm}}{{kg}}} \right]^1}{\left[ {\frac{{cm}}{{{\text{meter}}}}} \right]^{ - 1}}{\left[ {\frac{{\sec }}{{\sec }}} \right]^{ - 2}}\]∴ conversion factor

\[\frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1}}} = {\left[ {\frac{{gm}}{{{{10}^3}gm}}} \right]^1}{\left[ {\frac{{cm}}{{{{10}^2}cm}}} \right]^{ - 1}}{\left[ {\frac{{\sec }}{{\sec }}} \right]^{ - 2}} = \frac{1}{{10}} = 0.1\]

$U = \frac{6}{2}nRT$
$U = 3nRT\,\,\,n = 1$
$U = 3RT$

Question:
By what velocity, a ball be projected vertically upwards so that the distance covered in 5th second is twice of that covered in 6th second?
(g = 10 m/s2)
Solution:
Given, \[{S_5} = 2{S_6}\]
\[u - \frac{{\left( {10} \right)}}{2}\left( {2 \times 5 - 1} \right) = 2\left[ {u - \frac{{\left( {10} \right)}}{2}\left( {2 \times 6 - 1} \right)} \right]\]

\[u - 45 = 2\left( {u - 55} \right)\]

\[ \Rightarrow u - 45 = 2u - 110\]

\[ \Rightarrow u = 110 - 45\]

\[\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 65m/s\]

Question: A ball is dropped from a height of 1 m. If coefficient of restitution between the surface and the ball is 0.6, the ball rebounds to a height of
Solution:
Speed gain by ball just

Before collision \[ = u = \sqrt {2gh} \]

\[u = \sqrt {2g(1)} \]

Speed of ball just after collision = v

Applying definition of e

\[e = \frac{{{\text{velocity of separation along LOI}}}}{{{\text{velocity of Approach along LOI}}}}\]

\[e = \frac{v}{u}\]

\[0.6 = \frac{v}{{\sqrt {2g} }}\]

\[v = \sqrt {2g} \left( {\frac{3}{5}} \right)\]

\[\therefore \] max height attained is given by

\[{h_{\max }} = \frac{{{v^2}}}{{2g}} = \frac{{{{\left( {\sqrt {2g} \frac{3}{5}} \right)}^2}}}{{2g}}\]

\[{h_{\max }} = \frac{{2g}}{{2g}} = \left( {\frac{9}{{25}}} \right)\]

\[{h_{\max }} = \frac{9}{{25}} = 0.36\;m\]

Question:
Assertion : When an electric lamp connected in series with a  variable capacitor and ac source, its brightness decreases with increase in capacitance.
Reason: Capacitive reactance increases with increase in capacitance of capacitor.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Capacitive reactance \[{X_C} = \frac{1}{{\omega C}}\] when capacitance increases, the capacitive reactance decrease. Due to decrease in its value, the current in the circuit will increase \[\left( {I = \frac{E}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + X_C^2} }}} \right)\] and hence brightness of source (or electric lamp) will also increases.

Question: A number of condensers, each of the capacitance 1μ F and each one of which gets punctured, if a potential difference just exceeding 500 V is applied, are provided. An arrangement suitable for giving capacitance of 2μ F across which 3000 V may be applied requires atleast
Solution:
Minimum number of condensers in each row $$ = \frac{{3000}}{{500}} = 6$$
If CS is capacity of 6 condensers in a row,
$$\frac{1}{{{C_S}}} = \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{1} = 6$$
$$ \Rightarrow {C_S} = \frac{1}{6}\mu F$$
Let there be m such rows in parallel.
Total capacity $$ = m \times {C_S}$$
$$2 = m \times 1/6$$
$$ \Rightarrow $$$$m = 12$$
Total number of capacitors
 $$ = 6 \times 12 = 72$$

Question: Three charges, each of +4μC, are placed at the corners B, C, D of a square ABCD of side 1 m. The electric field at the centre O of the square is
Solution:
$$OA = OB = OC = OD = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}m$$
EOB and EOC will cancel each other because of equal magnitudes and opposite directions.

$$\eqalign{
  & \left| {{{\overrightarrow E }_{Net}}} \right| = \left| {{{\overrightarrow E }_{OD}}} \right| = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{q}{{{{(OD)}^2}}} \cr
  & \Rightarrow \left| {{{\overrightarrow E }_{Net}}} \right|\, = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times 4 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)}^2}}} = 72 \times {10^3}N/C \cr} $$
As clear from the diagram, this field acts towards A.

Question: A conducting sphere of radius R and carrying charge Q is joined to an uncharged conducting sphere of radius 2R. The charge flowing between them would be
Solution:
When joined together

When joined together, let charge flown from first sphere to the next sphere be x. Also, potentials at both spheres can be equated and hence, we have:
$\eqalign{
  & \frac{{k(Q - x)}}{R} = \frac{{kx}}{{2R}} \cr
  & k(Q - x) = x \cr
  & x = \frac{{2Q}}{3} \cr} $

Question:
A child is swinging a swing. Minimum and the maximum heights of swing from earth’s surface are 0.75m and 2m respectively. The maximum velocity of this swing is :
\[\left( {g = m/{s^2}} \right)\]

From energy conservation,

\[v_{_{\max }}^2 = 2 \times 10 \times 1.25\]
\[{v_{\max }} = 5m{s^{ - 1}}\]

Question: A container containing water has a constant acceleration ‘a’ in the horizontal direction. Free surface of water gets sloped with the horizontal at angle:
Solution:
When the water is at rest, angle between free surface and weight of water (= mg) is $$90^\circ $$. When an acceleration ‘a’ is given in the horizontal direction as shown in the figure, pseudo force (= ma) also acts perpendicular to the weight of the water, on any particle being considered. Therefore, the net acceleration at the considered point (liquid particle) is given by:

$${a_{net}} = \sqrt {{a^2} + {g^2}} $$

Clearly, if $$\theta $$ is the angle between g and $${a_{net}}$$ as shown, it is given by

$$\theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{a}{g}} \right)$$

where $$\theta $$ is also the angle between the free surface of water and the horizontal (from properties of triangle).

Question: Two vectors $\overrightarrow a \,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\overrightarrow b $ are at an angle of 60° with each other. Their resultant makes an angle of 45° with $\overrightarrow a $. If $\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|$= 2 unit, then $\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|$is
Solution:
θ = 60°, a = ?, b = 2, α = 45°
$\begin{gathered}
  tan\alpha = \frac{{b\sin \theta }}{{a + b\cos \theta }} \\
   \Rightarrow b\sin \theta = a + b\cos \theta \\
   \Rightarrow 2\left\{ {\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right\} = a + 2\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right) \\
   \Rightarrow a = \sqrt 3 - 1 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A small sphere carrying a charge ‘q’ is hanging in between two parallel uncharged plates by a string of length L. Time period of the pendulum is T0. When the parallel plates are charged, creates electric field E between the plates, the time period changes to T. The ratio T/T0is equal to
Solution:
$$\eqalign{
  & {T_0} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} \cr
  & g' = g + \frac{{qE}}{m}\,\,\, \cr} $$
$$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{g'}}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{\left( {g + \frac{{qE}}{m}} \right)}}} \,\,\,\,$$
$$\frac{T}{{{T_0}}} = \sqrt {\frac{g}{{g + \frac{{qE}}{m}}}} \,\,\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\frac{T}{{{T_0}}} = {\left[ {\frac{g}{{g + \frac{{qE}}{m}}}} \right]^{\frac{1}{2}}}$$

Question: Two simple pendulums of length 0.5 m and 2.0 m respectively are given small linear displacement in one direction at the same time. They will again be in the same phase when the pendulum of shorter length has completed oscillations :
Solution:
Time period of oscillation of a simple pendulum is given by :
T = 2π \[\sqrt {\frac{1}{g}} \propto \sqrt 1 \]
Thus, the ratio of their time period is \[\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = \]
\[\sqrt {\frac{{0.5}}{2}} = \frac{1}{2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow {T_2} = 2{T_1}\]
Thus, the shorter pendulum would have completed exactly 2 oscillations when the longer pendulum would have completed the first oscillation.

Question: If pressure and temperature of an ideal gas are doubled and volume is halved, the number of molecules of the gas
Solution:
PV = nRT
$\begin{gathered}
  \left( {2P} \right) \times \left( {\frac{V}{2}} \right) = {n_1}R \times \left( {2T} \right) \\
  \frac{{{n_1}}}{n} = \frac{1}{2} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: When a ball of mass = 5 kg hits a bat with a velocity =3 m/s, in positive direction and it moves back with a velocity = 4 m/s, find the impulse in SI units:
Solution:
Impulse = change in Linear momentum = $\Delta \overrightarrow p $

\[\Delta p = {\vec p_f} - {\vec p_i}\]

\[\Delta p = 5\left( 4 \right) - \left( 3 \right)5\]

\[\Delta p = 20 + 15\]

\[\Delta p = 35\,kg\,m/s\]

\[\therefore \,{\text{Impulse}} = 35\,kg\,m/s\]

Question: A cylinder containing an ideal gas is in vertical position and has a piston of mass M that is able to move up or down without friction (figure). If the temperature is increased
Options: Both p and V of the gas will change
Only p will increase according to Charles’ law
V will change but not p
p will change but not V
Solution:
Consider the diagram where an ideal gas is contained in a cylinder, having a piston of mass M. Friction is absent.

The pressure inside the gas will be
$$p = {p_a} + Mg/A$$
where, $${p_a} = $$ atmospheric pressure
$$A = $$ area of cross-section of the piston.
$$Mg = $$ weight of piston
Hence, $$p = $$ constant.
When temperature increases
as $$pV = nRT \Rightarrow $$ volume (V) increases at constant pressure.

Question: If each capacitor of $$9\mu F$$ is arranged as shown in figure below, then the equivalent capacitance between the points A and B is
Solution:
The arrangement can be redrawn as

$$\therefore \,{C_{13}} = {C_1} + {C_3} = 9 + 9 = 18\,\mu F$$
$${C_{2 - 13}} = \frac{{{C_2} \times {C_{13}}}}{{{C_2} + {C_{13}}}} = \frac{{9 \times 18}}{{9 + 18}} = 6\mu F$$
$$\therefore \,C = {C_{2 - 13}} + {C_4}\, = 6 + 9 = 15\mu F$$

Question: Which statement is correct?
Options: n-type germanium is negatively charged, and p-type germanium is positively charged
both n-type and p-type germanium are neutrals
n-type germanium is positively charged, and p-type germanium is negatively charged
both n-type and p-type germanium are negatively charged
Solution: If a pentavalent atom is doped to a pure semiconductor to produce a n-type semiconductor, it will donate one free electron to the crystal. This will leave a positive charge on the donor atom, the donated electron will be in the crystal in free state. Because of the charge on the donor ion is similar and opposite to the charge of the electron, the crystal itself remains electrically neutral. In the same way electrical neutrality of P type can also be found. Therefore, both n-type and p-type germanium are neutral.

Question: An electron enters a region where electrostatic field is 20N/C and magnetic field is 5T. If electron passes undeflected through the region, then velocity of electron will be [Assume$$\overrightarrow E ,\overrightarrow B \,\,and\,\overrightarrow v $$are mutually perpendicular.]
Solution:
If electron passes undeflected through a region of crossed field.
Then $q\overrightarrow E = - q\left( {\overrightarrow V \times \overrightarrow B } \right)$
$E = VB$
$V = \frac{E}{B}$
$ = \frac{{20}}{5}$
$V = 4m/s$

Question: A charge particle is free to move in an electron field. It will travel

Question: Projection of \[\vec P\] on is \[\vec Q\]
Solution:
Projection of \[\overrightarrow P \] on \[\overrightarrow Q = P\cos \theta \]

\[\left\{ {\theta \,{\text{ = }}\,{\text{angle}}\,\,{\text{between}}\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\overrightarrow P \,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\overrightarrow Q } \right\}\]

\[\because \vec P \cdot \vec Q = PQ\cos \theta \]

\[P\cos \theta = \frac{{\vec P \cdot \vec Q}}{{\left| {\vec Q} \right|}} = \vec P \cdot \hat Q\]

Question:
Assertion: Airplanes always fly at low altitudes.
Reason: According to Newton’s third law of motion, for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: The wings of the airplane push the external air backwards and the airplane moves forward by reaction of pushed air. At low altitudes, density of air is high and so the airplane gets sufficient force to move forward.

= ML2T–2/M–1L3T–2

= [M2L–1T0]

Question: Body A starts from rest with an acceleration a<sub>1</sub>. After 2s another body B starts from rest with an acceleration a<sub>2</sub>. If they travel equal distances in 5 sec after the start of A, the ratio a<sub>1</sub> : a<sub>2</sub> will be equal to :
Solution:
\[{\text{S}} = \frac{1}{2}{{\text{a}}_1}{(5)^2} = \;\frac{1}{2}{{\text{a}}_2}{(5 - 2)^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {\text{ }}\frac{1}{2}{{\text{a}}_1}{(5)^2} = \frac{1}{2}{{\text{a}}_2}{(3)^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\text{a}}_1}}}{{{{\text{a}}_2}}} = \frac{{{3^2}}}{{{5^2}}} = \frac{9}{{25}}\]

Question: A point charge of value 10<sup>-7</sup> C is situated at the centre of cube of 1 m side. The electric flux through its total surface area is
A mechanic can open a nut by applying a force of 150N while using a lever handle of length 40 cm. How long is the handle required if he wants to open it by applying a force of only 50 N?
Solution:
In the first case, F = 150 N, r = 40 cm = 0.4 m
The moment of force needed to open the nut

\[ = {\text{ }}150{\text{ }}N \times 0.4{\text{ }}m = 60{\text{ }}N - m{\text{ }} \ldots \left( i \right)\]

In the second case, F = 50 N,

If he uses the handle of length L m, then

Moment of force \[ = 50 \times L = 50L{\text{ }}...\left( {ii} \right)\]

From eq. (i) and (ii),

\[50L = 60\]

\[ \Rightarrow L = \frac{{60}}{{50}} = 1.2m\]

Question:

Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Question: Radio wave has a maximum magnetic field induction of 10<sup>–4</sup> T on arrival at a receiving antenna. The maximum electric field intensity of such a wave is

Number of particles is given by \[n =  - D\frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{{{x_2} - {x_1}}}\] crossing a unit area perpendicular to X-axis in unit time, where n<sub>1</sub> and n<sub>2</sub> are number of particles per unit volume for the value of x meant to be x<sub>2</sub> and x<sub>1</sub> respectively. Find dimensions of D called as diffusion constant
Solution:
(n) = Number of particle passing from unit area in unit time \[ = \frac{{{\text{No}}{\text{.}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{particle}}}}{{A \times t}} = \frac{{\left[ {{M^0}{L^0}{T^0}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{L^2}} \right]\left[ T \right]}} = \left[ {{L^{ - 2}}{T^{ - 1}}} \right]\]

\[\left[ {{n_1}} \right] = \left[ {{n_2}} \right] = \]No. of particle in unit volume \[ = \left[ {{L^{ - 3}}} \right]\]

Now from the given formula \[\left[ D \right] = \frac{{\left[ n \right]\left[ {{x_2} - {x_1}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{n_2} - {n_1}} \right]}} = \frac{{\left[ {{L^{ - 2}}{T^{ - 1}}} \right]\left[ L \right]}}{{\left[ {{L^{ - 3}}} \right]}} = \left[ {{L^2}{T^{ - 1}}} \right].\]

Question: The (x, y, z) co-ordinate of two points A and B are given respectively as $\left( {0,3, - 1} \right)$ and $\left( { - 2,6,4} \right)$. The displacement vector from A to B is given by

$s = {r_B} - {r_A}$

Question: A man traveling in a bus moves from west to east with a speed of 40 km/hr. He observes that the raindrops are falling vertically down. To another man standing on the ground, the rain will appear
Options: to be falling at an angle going from east to west
to be falling at an angle going from west to east
insufficient information is given to determine the direction of the rain
Solution:
A main is sitting in a bus and travelling from west to east, and the rain drops are appears falling vertically down.
${V_m} = $ Velocity of man
${V_r} = $ Actual velocity of rain which is falling at an angle $\theta $ with vertical
${V_m} = $ velocity of rain w.r.t moving man
If the another man observe the rain then he will find that actually rain falling with velocity ${V_r}$ at an angle going from west to west.

Question: What vector must be added to the sum of two vectors \[2\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k\] and \[3\hat i - 2\hat j - 2\hat k\] so that the resultant is a unit vector along Z-axis
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow A = 2\widehat i - \widehat j + 3\widehat k;\overrightarrow B = 3\widehat i - 2\widehat j - 2\widehat k\]

Let a vector

\[\overrightarrow C = x\widehat i + y\widehat j + z\widehat k\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,{\text{added}}\,\,\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B + \overrightarrow C = \widehat k\]

\[ \Rightarrow \left( {5 + x} \right)\widehat i + \left( {y - 3} \right)\widehat j + \left( {1 + z} \right)\hat k = \hat k\]

Equating components.

\[5 + x = 0 \Rightarrow x = - 5\]

\[y - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow y = 3\]

\[1 + z = 1 \Rightarrow z = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow C = - 5\hat i + 3\hat j\]

Question: Which of the following statements is true for collisions-
Options: Momentum is conserved in elastic collisions but not in inelastic collisions
Total kinetic energy is conserved in elastic collisions but momentum is not conserved
Total kinetic energy is not conserved in inelastic collisions but momentum is conserved
Total kinetic energy and momentum both are conserved in all types of collisions
Solution:
In inelastic collision kinetic energy is not conserved, momentum is conserved.

Total momentum and total energy of a system always remain conserved. In inelastic collisions, some of the kinetic energy goes into the heat energy, with total energy and momentum still being conserved.

Question: A particle of mass m, charge Q and kinetic energy T enters a transverse uniform magnetic field of induction B. After 3 s the kinetic energy of the particle will be
Solution: Work done by magnetic force is zero hence no change in kinetic energy.

Question: A body weighs 40 g in air. If its volume is 10 cc, in water it will weigh:
Solution:
According to Archimedes principle
mass in air $$ = 40{\text{ }}gm$$

volume $$ = 10{\text{ }}c{m^3}$$

So, when immersed in water displaces 10 gm of water.

It also experiences 0.1 N of buoyant force.

Hence, it loses 10 gm of its mass. Now,

mass in water = mass in air - weight loss in water

$$ \Rightarrow 40-10 = 30{\text{ }}gm$$

Question: Eight drops of water each of radius 2 mm are falling through air at a terminal velocity of 8 cm/s, if they coalesce to form a single drop. The terminal velocity of the combined drop is:
Solution:
Let the radius of bigger drop is R and smaller drop is r then
\[\frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3} = 8 \times \frac{4}{3} \times \pi {r^3}\]
or R = 2r …(i)
Terminal velocity,
\[\begin{gathered}
  {v_t} = \frac{2}{9}\frac{{{r^2}\left( {\rho - \sigma } \right)g}}{\eta } \\
  {v_t} \propto {r^2} \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\frac{{v'}}{v} = \frac{{{R^2}}}{{{r^2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{2r}}{r}} \right)^2} = 4\] (Using (i))
or \[v' = 4v = 4 \times 8 = 32\,cm{s^{ - 1}}\]

$ \Rightarrow \tau = $ constant

Question: For the phenomenon of electrostatic induction, mark out the correct statement.

The magnitude of net induced charge is equal and opposite to the inducing charge
The mass of the body changes slightly

Question: In the following figure the masses of the blocks A and B are same and each equal to m. The tensions in the strings OA and AB are\[{T_2}\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,{T_1}\] respectively. The system is in equilibrium with a constant horizontal force mg on B. The ${{\text{T}}_{\text{1}}}$ is
Solution:
Net force on block A and B is zero. Consider the F.B.D. of A and B respectively




From A, \[{T_2}\cos {\theta _2} = mg + {T_1}\cos {\theta _1}\,\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)\]

\[{T_1}\sin {\theta _1} = {T_2}\sin {\theta _2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,......\left( 2 \right)\]

 From B,\[{T_1}\cos {\theta _1} = mg\,\,\,.....\left( 3 \right)\]

 \[{T_1}\sin {\theta _1} = mg......(4)\]

  

Squaring and adding (3) and (4)

\[T_1^2 = {(mg)^2} + {(mg)^2}\]

\[{T_1} = \sqrt 2 mg\]

So, option (b) is correct.

Question: A bullet fired into a fixed target loses half of its velocity after penetrating 3 cm. How much further it will penetrate before coming to rest assuming that it faces constant resistance to motion?
Solution:
Let initial velocity of the bullet = u
After penetrating 3 cm its velocity becomes $\frac{u}{2}$
From ${v^2} = {u^2} - 2as$
${\left( {\frac{u}{2}} \right)^2} = {u^2} - 2a\,(3)$

 ⇒ $6a = \frac{{3{u^2}}}{4}$ ⇒ $a = \frac{{{u^2}}}{8}$

Let further it will penetrate through distance x and stops at point C.

For distance BC, $v = 0,\,u = u/2,\,s = x,\,a = {u^2}/8$

From ${v^2} = {u^2} - 2as$⇒$0 = {\left( {\frac{u}{2}} \right)^2} - 2\left( {\frac{{{u^2}}}{8}} \right)\,.\,x$⇒$x = 1$ cm.

Question: A step-up transformer operates on a 230 V line and supplies a load of 2 A. The ratio of the primary and secondary windings is\[1:25\]. The current in the primary is:

Operating voltage, $V = 230{\text{ V}}$
And the supply load,
${I_2} = 2{\text{ amp}}$
${n_1}:{n_2} = 1:25$.
The ratio of current in the primary and secondary
windings,
$\frac{{{I_1}}}{{{I_2}}} = \frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{I_1}}}{2} = 25$
$\therefore {I_1} = 50{\text{ A}}$

Question: Two particles are projected from the same point with the same speed u such that they have the same range R; but different maximum heights ${h_1}$ and ${h_2}$. Which of the following is correct?
Solution:
Key Idea: Same range for two particles thrown with some initial speed may occurs when they are projected at complementary angle.
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\text{Range}}(R) = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 2\theta }}{g} = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 2({{90}^ \circ } - \theta )}}{g} \\
  {\text{Maximumheight}}({h_1}) = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{2g}} \\
  {\text{Maximumheight}}({h_2}) = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}({{90}^ \circ } - \theta )}}{{2g}} = \frac{{{u^2}{{\cos }^2}(\theta )}}{{2g}} \\
  {h_1}{h_2} = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}(\theta )}}{{2g}}.\frac{{{u^2}{{\cos }^2}(\theta )}}{{2g}} \\
\end{gathered} \]
Now Multiplying and dividing by 4
\[\begin{gathered}
  {h_1}{h_2} = [\frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}(\theta )}}{{2g}}.\frac{{{u^2}{{\cos }^2}(\theta )}}{{2g}}] \times \frac{4}{4} \\
  {h_1}{h_2} = [\frac{{2{u^2}\sin (\theta )\cos (\theta )}}{g}.\frac{{2{u^2}\sin (\theta )\cos (\theta )}}{g}] \times \frac{1}{{16}} \\
  {R^2} = 16{h_1}{h_2} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A man projects a coin upwards from the gate of a uniformly moving train. The path of coin for the man will be
Solution: Because horizontal velocity is same for coin and the observer. So relative horizontal displacement will be zero.

Question: A ball is thrown up at an angle with the horizontal. Then the total change of momentum by the instant, it returns to ground is:
Options: acceleration due to gravity × total time of flight
weight of the ball × half the time of flight
weight of the ball × total time of flight
weight of the ball × horizontal range
Solution:
Formula for momentum in upward direction is $mv\sin \theta $
And in downward direction, it is: -$mv\sin \theta $

Now change in momentum,

\[\left| {\vec P} \right| = \;\left| { - mv\sin \theta - mv\sin \theta } \right|\]

\[\left| {\vec P} \right| = 2mv\sin \theta \]

Now let’s multiply and divide by g

\[ = mg \times \frac{{2v\sin \theta }}{g}\]

\[ = W \times T\]

So, from above equation we can tell that change in momentum is multiplication of weight of the ball and total time of flight.

Question: A rain drop of radius \[0.3\;mm\] falling vertically downwards in air has a terminal velocity of\[1\;m/s\]. The viscosity of air is\[18 \times {10^{ - 5}}\;poise\]. The viscous force on the drop is
Solution:
Here, $$r = 0.3\,{\text{mm}} = 0.03\,{\text{cm}}$$

$$v = 1\,{\text{m}}{{\text{s}}^{ - 1}} = 100\,{\text{cm}}{{\text{s}}^{ - 1}}$$

$$\eta  = 18 \times {10^{ - 5}}{\text{poise}}.$$

According to stokes law, force of viscosity on rain drop is 

$$F = 6\pi \eta rv$$

$$ = 6 \times 3.142 \times 18 \times {10^{ - 5}} \times 0.03 \times 100\,{\text{dyne}}$$

$$ = 1.018 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{\text{dyne}}$$

Question: A cart is moving horizontally along a straight line with constant speed 30 m/s. A projectile is to be fired from the moving cart in such a way that it will return to the cart after the cart has moved 80 m. At  what  speed  (relative to the cart) must the projectile be fired? (Take = 1 0 m/s<sup>2</sup>)
Solution:
Firstly, let’s find time
\[t = \frac{{{\text{Distance}}}}{{{\text{velocity}}}}\]

\[ = \frac{{80}}{{30}}\]

\[t = \frac{8}{3}\sec \]

Formula for time of flight,

$t = \frac{{2u}}{g}$

$\frac{8}{3} = \frac{{2u}}{{10}}$

$u = \frac{{40}}{3}\,m/s$

Question: In the reaction \[_1^2H + \,_1^3H \to \,_2^4He + \,_0^1n,\] if the binding energies of\[_1^2H\],\[\,_1^3H\] and \[_2^4He\] are respectively a, b and c (in MeV), then the energy (in MeV) released in this reaction is
Solution:
The reaction is, ${}_1^2H + {}_1^3H \to {}_2^4He + {}_0^1n$
Now, the mass defect,
$\Delta m = {\text{ mass of product}} - {\text{ mass of reactant}}$
Then, the total energy released, $E = 931\Delta m{\text{ MeV}}$
Now, the binding energy of $\left( {{}_1^2H + {}_1^3H} \right) = a + b$, and ${}_2^4He = c$
As we know that, in a nuclear reaction the resultant nucleus is more stable than the parent nucleus.
Therefore, binding energy E=$c - \left( {a + b} \right)$
$\therefore c - a - b$

Question: The work done to break up a drop of a liquid of radius R and surface tension σ into eight drops, all of the same size, is
Solution:
By volume conservation
$$\frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3} = 8\left( {\frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}} \right) \Rightarrow R = 2r$$

$$W = \sigma \left( {\Delta s} \right)$$

$$ = \sigma \left[ {8\left( {4\pi {r^2}} \right) - 4\pi {R^2}} \right]$$

$$ = \sigma \left[ {8\pi {R^2} - 4\pi {R^2}} \right] = 4\pi {R^2}\sigma $$

Question: Water flows steadily through a horizontal pipe of a variable cross-section. If the pressure of water is p at a point where the velocity of flow is \[v\], what is the pressure at another point where the velocity of flow is 2\[v\], $\rho $ being the density of water?
Solution:
According to Bernoulli’s theorem, for a horizontal flow at a height h from ground level,
${p_1} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho gh = {p_2\,} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho gh$
or \[{p_2} = {p_1} + \frac{1}{2}\rho\left( {v_1^2 - v_2^2} \right)\]
$ = p + \frac{1}{2}\rho\left\{ {{v^2} - {{\left( {2v} \right)}^2}} \right\}$
$ = p - \frac{3}{2}\rho {v^2}$

Question: The area of the acceleration-displacement curve of a body gives:
Options: change in momentum per unit mass
change in KE per unit mass
Solution:
We know Newton’s 3rd equation of motion.
\[ \Rightarrow {v^2} = {u^2} + 2as\]

We can further write it as:

\[ \Rightarrow as = \frac{1}{2}\left( {{v^2} - {u^2}} \right)\]

Thus, area under the acceleration-displacement curve gives the change in kinetic energy per unit mass.

Question: Binding energy per nucleon plot against the mass number for stable nuclei is shown in the figure. Which curve is correct
Solution: For light nuclei, B.E. increases with A while for heavy nuclei, it decreases.

Question: A metal ball of mass 2 kg moving with a velocity of 36 km/h has a head on collision with a stationary ball of mass 3 kg. If after the collision, the two balls move together, the loss in kinetic energy due to collision is
Solution:
$v = 36\,km/h = 10\,m/s$
By law of conservation of momentum
$2 \times 10 = (2 + 3)\,V$ ⇒ $V = 4\,m/s$
Loss in K.E.$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {(10)^2} - \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times {(4)^2} = 60\,J$

Question:
The displacement of a particle executing simple harmonic motion is given by
y = A0 + A sin ωt + B cos ωt.

Then the amplitude of its oscillation is given by :
Solution:
$$y = {A_0} + A\sin \omega \,t + B\cos \omega \,t$$
$$y' = y - {A_o} = A\sin \omega \,t + B\cos \omega \,t$$

$${R_{amplitude}} = \sqrt {{A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos 90^\circ } = \sqrt {{A^2} + {B^2}} $$

Question: A capacitor is charged by using a battery which is then disconnected. A dielectric slab is then slipped between the plates, which results in
Options: Reduction of charge on the plates and increase of potential difference across the plates
Increase in the potential difference across the plate, reduction in stored energy, but no change in the charge on the plates
Decrease in the potential difference across the plates, reduction in the stored energy, but no change in the charge on the plates
Solution: Battery is disconnected so Q will be constant as\[C \propto K\]. So with introduction of dielectric slab capacitance will increase using Q = CV, V will decrease and using\[U = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{2C}}\]energy will decrease.

Question: The magnitude of a given vector with end points (4 - 4, 0) and (-2, -2, 0) must be
In thermal equilibrium, the average speed of gas molecules is
Solution:
Average speed of the gas is given by:

${v_{average}} = \sqrt {\frac{{8RT}}{{\pi M}}} $

Hence average molecular speed is directly proportional to $\sqrt T $

Solution: $$Q = 8\left( {7.06} \right) - 7\left( {5.6} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ {_1^1H\,{\text{has no BE}}} \right] = 17.28MeV$$

Question: When an ideal diatomic gas is heated at constant pressure the fraction of the heat energy supplied which increases the internal energy of the gas is
Solution:
The desired fraction is
\[f = \frac{{\Delta U}}{{\Delta Q}} = \frac{{n{C_V}\Delta T}}{{n{C_p}\Delta T}} = \frac{{{C_V}}}{{{C_p}}} = \frac{1}{\gamma }\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,f = \frac{5}{7}\]\[\left( {{\text{as}}\,\,\gamma = \frac{7}{5}} \right)\]

Question: Two bodies of mass 1 kg and 3 kg have position vectors \[\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k\] and \[ - 3\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k\], respectively. The centre of mass of this system has a position vector
Solution:
\[{\vec r_{cm}} = \frac{{{m_1}{{\vec r}_1} + {m_2}{{\vec r}_2}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}\]
\[ = \frac{{1 \times \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k} \right) + 3\left( { - 3\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k} \right)}}{{1 + 3}}\]
\[ = - 2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k\]
