Question: Two cars of masses $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{2}$ are moving along the circular path of radius $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$. They take one round in the same time. The ratio of angular velocities of the two cars will be
A particle of mass 0.1 kg is subjected to a force which varies with distance as shown in the figure. It it starts its journey with distance from rest at x = 0, its velocity at x = 12 m is

Two ice skaters A and B approach each other at right angles. Skater A has a mass 30 kg and velocity 1 m/s skater B has mass 20 kg and velocity 2 m/s. They meet and cling together. The final velocity of the couple is :
Solution:
\[{\vec P_i} = {\vec P_f}\]

\[30\left( 1 \right)\hat i + 20\left( 2 \right)\hat j = \left( {30 + 20} \right)\vec v\]

\[\vec v = \frac{3}{5}\hat i + \frac{4}{5}\hat j\]

\[\therefore \left| {\vec v} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{3}{5}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{4}{5}} \right)}^2}} \]

\[\left| {\vec v} \right| = \sqrt {\frac{9}{{25}} + \frac{{16}}{{25}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{25}}{{25}}} \]

\[\left| {\vec v} \right| = 1m/s\]

Question: A hydrogen atom and a $L{i^{2 + }}$ ion are both in the second excited state. If ${l_H}\,{\text{and}}\,{l_{Li}}$ are their respective electronic angular momenta, and ${E_H}\,{\text{and}}\,{E_{Li}}$ their respective energies, then
Solution:
In second excited state n = 3,
So, ${l_H} = {l_{Li}} = 3\left( {\frac{h}{{2\pi }}} \right)$
while $E \propto \,{Z^2}$
and ${Z_H} = 1,{Z_{Li}} = 3$
So, $\left| {{E_{Li}}} \right| = 9\left| {{E_H}} \right|$
or $\left| {{E_H}} \right| < \left| {{E_{Li}}} \right|$

Question: A body is just floating on the surface of a liquid. The density of the body is same as that of the liquid. The body is slightly pushed down. What will happen to the body
Options: It will slowly come back to its earlier position
It will remain submerged, where it is left
Solution:
$$\eqalign{
  & Mg = {\rho _B}Vg \cr
  & {F_B} = {\rho _\omega }Vg \cr
  & Given{\rho _B} = {\rho _\omega } \cr
  & \Rightarrow Mg = {F_B} \cr} $$
This implies that buoyant force will always be equal to the force of gravity which means, it will be in a state of equilibrium even when completely submerged.

Even when it is slightly pushed down, it will still remain in equilibrium. The buoyant force can never be greater than mg, therefore, the block will just remain there.

Question: A mass is suspended separately by two different springs in successive order then time periods is \[{t_1}\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\,\,{t_2}\] respectively. If it is connected by both springs as shown in figure then time period is \[{t_0},\] the correct relation is
Solution:
The time period of a spring mass system as shown in figure 1 is given by \[T = 2\pi \sqrt {m/k} ,\] where k is the spring constant.

\[\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \,\,{t_1} = 2\pi \sqrt {m/{k_1}} .......\left( i \right) \\
  {\text{and}}\,\,{t_2} = 2\pi \sqrt {m/{k_2}} ........\left( {ii} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]

Now, when they are connected in parallel as shown in figure 2(a), the system can be replaced by a single of spring constant, \[{k_{eff}} = {k_1} + {k_2}.\]
[Since\[mg = {k_1}x + {k_2}x = {k_{eff}}x\]]
\[\therefore \,\,{t_0} = 2\pi \sqrt {m/{k_{eff}}} = 2\pi \sqrt {m/\left( {{k_1} + {k_2}} \right)} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,........\left( {iii} \right)\]
From \[\left( i \right),\frac{1}{{t_1^2}} = \frac{1}{{4{\pi ^2}}} \times \frac{{{k_1}}}{m}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,.....\left( {iv} \right)\]
From \[\left( {ii} \right),\frac{1}{{t_2^2}} = \frac{1}{{4{\pi ^2}}} \times \frac{{{k_2}}}{m}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,.....\left( v \right)\]
From \[\left( {iii} \right),\frac{1}{{t_0^2}} = \frac{1}{{4{\pi ^2}}} \times \frac{{{k_2} + {k_2}}}{m}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,.....\left( {vi} \right)\]
\[\left( {iv} \right) + \left( v \right) = \frac{1}{{t_1^{\,2}}} + \frac{1}{{t_1^{\,2}}} = \frac{1}{{4{\pi ^2}m}}\left( {{k_1} + {k_2}} \right) = \frac{1}{{t_0^{\,2}}}\]
\[\therefore \,\,t_0^{ - 2} = t_1^{ - 2} + t_2^{ - 2}.\]

Question: Two charged particles having charges 1 μ C   and –1 μ C and of mass 50 g each are held at rest while their separation is 2 m. Now the charges are released. Find the speed of the particles when their separation is 1 m.
Solution:
Since they are released from rest initial kinetic energy is zero and finally both are moving with same speed v.
Apply conservation of energy,
\[ \Rightarrow \,\frac{{k{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r_1}}} - \,\frac{{k{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r_2}}} = 2 \times \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{ - 9 \times {{10}^9} \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{2} + \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{1} = 50 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times {v^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \,\,v = \sqrt {\frac{9}{{100}}}  = \frac{3}{{10}}\,m/s\]

Question:
A wire carrying current I and other carrying 2I in the same direction produces a magnetic field B at the mid point.
What will be the field when 2I wire is switched off
Solution:
When two parallel conductors carrying current I and 2I in same direction, then magnetic field at the midpoint is
\[B = \frac{{{\mu _0}2I}}{{2\pi r}}-\frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi r}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi r}}\]
When current 2I is switched off then magnetic field due to conductor carrying current I is \[B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi r}}.\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Magnetic Field Due to Current Element

Question: In a fission reaction the building energy per nucleon of X and Y is 8.5 MeV whereas of <sup>236</sup>U is 7.6 MeV. The total energy liberated will be about
Solution:
Binding energy
\[\begin{gathered}
   = 117 \times 8.5 + 117 \times 8.5 - 236 \times 7.6 \\
   = 234 \times 8.5 - 236 \times 7.6 \\
   = 1989 - 1793.6 = 200\,MeV \\
\end{gathered} \]
Thus, in per fission of Uranium nearly 200 MeV energy is liberated

Question: The height y and the distance x along the horizontal plane of a projectile on a certain planet (with no surrounding atmosphere) are given by y = (8t – 5t2) meter and x = 6t meter, where t is in second. The velocity with which the projectile is projected is
A lift of mass 1000 kg is moving upward with an acceleration of 1 m/s<sup>2</sup>. The tension developed in the string, which is connected to lift is (g = 9.8 m/s<sup>2</sup>)
Solution:
Key Idea: The tension in the string during upward motion increases from weight of lift due to its upward acceleration.
T – mg = ma
or T = m (g + a)
Thus, T = 1000 (9.8 + 1)
= 1000 x 10.8
= 10800 N

Question: Two projectiles are projected with the same velocity. If one is projected at an angle of 30º and the other at 45º to the horizontal, the ratio of maximum heights reached is
Solution:
For maximum height
\[H = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\alpha }}{{2g}}\]

\[{H_1} = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}30^o }}{{2g}}\] 

\[{H_2} = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}45^o }}{{2g}}\]

\[\frac{H_1}{H_2}=\frac{{{\sin}^2}30}{{{\sin}^2}45} =\frac{1}{2}\]

Question:
Assertion: Charge is invariant.
Reason: Charge does not depend on speed of frame of reference.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Any physical quantity which is independent of reference frame is known as invariant. Since charge is intrinsic property so it remains invariant.

Question: The engine of a motorcycle can produce a maximum acceleration of 5 m/s2. Its brakes can produce a maximum retardation of 10 m/s2. What is the minimum time in which it can cover a distance of 1.5 km (The engine is starting from the rest and comes to the rest finally.)
Solution:
If a body starts from rest with acceleration \[\alpha \]and then retards with retardation \[\beta \] and comes to rest. The total time taken for this journey is t and distance covered is S then
\[S = \frac{{\alpha \beta {t^2}}}{{2\left( {\alpha + \beta } \right)}} = \frac{{15 \times 10}}{{2\left( {5 + 10} \right)}} \times {t^2} \Rightarrow t = 30\sec \]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Kinematic Equations for Uniformly Accelerated Motion

Question:
Assertion: Displacement of a body is the vector sum of the area under velocity– time graph.
Reason: Displacement is a vector quantity.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Both Assertion and Reason are false
Solution:
According to definition, displacement
= velocity × time. Since displacement is a vector quantity, its value is equal to the vector sum of the area under the velocity-time graph

Question: In a transistor in CE configuration, the ratio of power gain to voltage gain is
Solution:
For CE configuration voltage gain $ = \beta \times {R_L}/{R_i}$
Power gain $ = {\beta ^2} \times {R_L}/{R_i}$ ⇒ $\frac{{{\text{Power gain}}}}{{{\text{Voltage gain}}}} = \beta $

Tags.Subject: Physics

Question: In the figure shown, if coefficient of friction is $\mu $, then m2 will start moving upwards if:
Solution:
\[{{\text{m}}_{\text{2}}}\] will start moving upwards if

\[{m_1}g > {m_2}g\sin \theta + \mu {m_2}g\cos \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{m_1}}}{{{m_2}}} > \sin \theta + \mu \cos \theta \]

So, option (b) is correct.

Question: A bullet of mass 20 g and moving with $600\,{\text{m}}{{\,\text{s}}^{ - 1}}$ collides with a block of mass 4 kg hanging with the string of length 0.4 m. What is velocity of bullet when it comes out of block, if block rises to height 0.2 m after collision?
Solution:
Velocity of block just after collision $ = \sqrt {2gh} $
$ = \sqrt {2 \times 10 \times 0.2} $
$ = 2\,{\text{m}}{{\,\text{s}}^{ - 1}}$
Now applying conservation of linear momentum just before and just after collision.
$0.02 \times 600 = 4 \times 2 + 0.02 \times v$
$v = 200\,{\text{m}}{{\,\text{s}}^{ - 1}}$

Question: A transformer with efficiency 80% works at 4 kW and 100 V. If the secondary voltage is 200 V, then the primary and secondary currents are respectively,
Solution:
Efficiency, \[\eta = \frac{{output\,power}}{{input\,power}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\eta = \frac{{{V_s}{I_s}}}{{{V_p}{I_p}}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{80}}{{100}} = \frac{{200 \times {I_s}}}{{4000}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,{I_s} = 16\,A\]
Also, VpIp = 4000
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,{I_p} = \frac{{4000}}{{100}} = 40\,A\]

Question: A source of an alternating voltage $E = 200\sqrt 2 \sin (100\,t)$ is connected to a 1 microfarad capacitor through an ac ammeter. The reading of the ammeter shall be:
Solution:
Reading of ammeter $ = {i_{rms}} = \frac{{{V_{rms}}}}{{{X_C}}} = \frac{{{V_0}\omega C}}{{\sqrt 2 }}$
$ = \frac{{200\sqrt 2 \times 100 \times (1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}})}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 2 \times {10^{ - 2}}A = 20\;mA$

Question: Two Carnot engines A and B are operated in succession. The first one A receives heat from a source at T<sub>1</sub> = 800 K and rejects to sink at T<sub>2 </sub>K. The second engine B receives heat rejected by the first engine and rejects to another sink at T<sub>3</sub> = 300 K. If the work output of two engines are equal, then value of T<sub>2</sub> is
Solution:
Let, $${Q_1} = $$ heat absorbed by first engine, 

$${Q_2} = $$ Heat released by first engine = heat absorbed by second engine,

$${Q_3} = $$ Heat released by second engine,

$${T_1} = $$ Source temperature of first engine, 

$${T_2} = $$ Sink temperature of first engine = Source temperature of second engine, 

$${T_3} = $$ Sink temperature of second engine

For the 2 Carnot Engines, 

$$\frac{{{Q_2}}}{{{Q_1}}} = \frac{{{T_2}}}{{{T_1}}}$$    &    $$\frac{{{Q_3}}}{{{Q_2}}} = \frac{{{T_3}}}{{{T_2}}}$$

The condition given is, $${W_1} = {W_2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {Q_2} - {Q_1} = {Q_3} - {Q_2} \Rightarrow 2{Q_2} = {Q_1} + {Q_3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{T_2} = {T_1} + {T_3} \Rightarrow 2{T_2} = 300 + 800 = 1100$$

Hence required temperature is 550 K.

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Kinetic Theory of an Ideal Gas

Question: A long straight wire of radius a carries a steady current i. The current is uniformly distributed across its cross section. The ratio of the magnetic field at a/2 and 2a is
Solution:
Here, current is uniformly distributed across the cross section of the wire, therefore, current enclosed in the amperean path formed at a distance ${r_1}\left( { = \frac{a}{2}} \right)$

$ = \left( {\frac{{\pi r_1^2}}{{\pi {a^2}}}} \right) \times I,$ where I is total current
$\therefore $ Magnetic field at P1 is
${B_1} = \frac{{{\mu _0} \times {\text{current}}\,{\text{enclosed}}}}{{{\text{Path}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {B_1} = \frac{{{\mu _0} \times \left( {\frac{{\pi r_1^2}}{{\pi {a^2}}}} \right) \times I}}{{2\pi {r_1}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0} \times I{r_1}}}{{2\pi {a^2}}}$
Now, magnetic field at point P2,
${B_2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }}.\frac{I}{{\left( {2a} \right)}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4\pi a}}.$
$\therefore $ Required ratio $ = \frac{{{B_1}}}{{{B_2}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}I{r_1}}}{{2\pi {a^2}}} \times \frac{{4\pi a}}{{{\mu _0}I}}$
$ = \frac{{2{r_1}}}{a} = \frac{{2\frac{a}{2}}}{a} = 1.$

Question: Positive and negative point charges of equal magnitude are kept at (0, 0, a/2) and (0, 0, -a/2), respectively. The work done by the electric field when another positive point charge in moved from (-a, 0, 0) to (0, a, 0) is
Options: Depends on the path connecting the initial and final positions
Solution:

Question: A sinusoidal voltage of peak value 300 V and an angular frequency $\omega  = 400\,{\text{rad}}{{\text{s}}^{ - 1}}$is applied to series L-C-R circuit, in which $R = 3\Omega ,L = 20\,mH$and $C = 625\,\mu F$. The peak current in the circuit is
Solution:
The impedance of the circuit is
$Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {{X_L} - {X_C}} \right)}^2}} $
${X_L} = \omega L = 400 \times 20 \times {10^{ - 3}} = 8H$
\[{X_C} = \frac{1}{{\omega C}} = \frac{1}{{400 \times 625 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = 4F\]
$Z = \sqrt {{{\left( 3 \right)}^2} + {{\left( {8 - 4} \right)}^2}} = 5$
$I = \frac{E}{Z} = \frac{{300}}{5} = 60\,A$

Question: A sphere is suspended by a thread of length l. What minimum horizontal velocity has to be imparted the ball for it to reach the height of the suspension

Kinetic energy given to a sphere at lowest point = potential energy at the height of suspension

\[\therefore \;\;v\; = \;\sqrt {2gl} \]

Question: The magnetic induction at point P which is at a distance of 4 cm from a long current carrying wire is \[{10^{ - 3}}T\]. The field of induction at a distance of 12 cm from the same current carrying wire will be
Solution:
Strength of magnetic field at a point near infinitely long straight wire is inversely proportional to the distance of the point from the wire
\[\frac{{{B_1}}}{{{B_2}}} = \frac{{{r_2}}}{{{r_1}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{{B_2}}} = \frac{{12}}{4} \Rightarrow {B_2} = \frac{1}{3} \times {10^{ - 3}}T\]
\[{B_2} = \frac{{10}}{3} \times {10^{ - 4}}T = 3.33 \times {10^{ - 4}}T\]

Question: An ideal gas is initially at temperature T and volume V. Its volume is increased by $$\Delta V$$ due to an increase in temperature$$\Delta T$$, pressure remaining constant. The physical quantity $$\delta  = \frac{{\Delta V}}{{V\Delta T}}$$ varies with temperature as

Dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq. (i), we get
$$\frac{{\Delta V}}{V} = \frac{{\Delta T}}{T}$$
$$\frac{{\Delta V}}{{V\Delta T}} = \frac{1}{T} = \delta \quad \left[ {\because \delta = \frac{{\Delta V}}{{V\Delta T}}} \right]$$
So, the graph between $$\delta $$and T will be

Question: Figure shown in water filled in symmetrical container. Four pistons of equal area A are used at the four openings to keep the water in equilibrium. Now an additional force F is applied at each piston. The increase in the pressure at the centre of container due to this addition is
Solution:
If pressure is increased at one point, same pressure is increased without being diminished. (Pascal’s law)
Hence due increases in pressure $\left( { = \frac{F}{A}} \right)$ will need to the balanced by additional force on remaining piston.
Hence additional force liquid $ = PA = \frac{F}{A} \times A = F$
Hence only increase in pressure = F/A

Question: A car travels first half the distance between two places with a speed of 30 km/h and the remaining half with a speed of 50 km/h. The average speed of the car is :
Solution:
Average speed is taken as ratio of total distance to total time. Let total distance be AB = x, and C be the midpoint, then AC = \[\frac{x}{2}\]and AB = \[\frac{x}{2}\]

We know that time \[ = \frac{{{\text{distance}}}}{{{\text{speed}}}}\]
For distance AC, \[{t_1} = \frac{{\frac{x}{2}}}{{30}} = \frac{x}{{60}}h\]
For distance CB, \[{t_2} = \frac{{\frac{x}{2}}}{{50}} = \frac{x}{{100}}h\]
Average speed of the car \[ = \frac{{{\text{Total distance}}}}{{{\text{Total time}}}}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
   = \frac{x}{{\frac{x}{{60}} + \frac{x}{{100}}}} \\
  \frac{{300}}{8} = 37.5\,\,km/h \\
\end{gathered} \]

\[ = 1 - {\left( {\frac{{1 - 2}}{{1 + 2}}} \right)^2}\] \[ = 1 - {\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right)^2} = 1 - \frac{1}{9} = \frac{8}{9}\]

\[\therefore {{\text{v}}_x} = 3 + 0.4 \times 10 = 3 + 4 = 7m{s^{ - 1}}\]
and \[{v_y} = 4 + 0.3 \times 10 = 4 + 3 = 7m{s^{ - 1}}\]
\[\therefore v = \sqrt {v_x^2 + v_y^2} = 7\sqrt 2 m{s^{ - 1}}\]

Question: A particle moving along x-axis has acceleration f, at time t, given by \[f = {f_0}\left( {1 - \frac{t}{T}} \right)\], where f<sub>0</sub> and T are constants. The particle at t = 0 has zero velocity. At the instant when f = 0, the particle’s velocity (v) is
Solution:
When acceleration is zero,

\[f = {f_0}\left( {1 - \frac{t}{T}} \right)\]

\[0 = {f_0}\left( {1 - \frac{t}{T}} \right)\]

\[t = T\]

\[f = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = {f_0}\left( {1 - \frac{t}{T}} \right)\]

\[\int\limits_0^v {dv} = \int\limits_0^T {{f_0}\left( {1 - \frac{t}{T}} \right)dt} \]

\[\left[ v \right]_0^v = \left[ {{f_0}t - {f_0}\left( {\frac{{{t^2}}}{{2T}}} \right)} \right]_0^T\]

\[v - 0 = {f_0}T - \frac{{{f_0}T}}{2}\]

\[v = \frac{{{f_0}T}}{2}\]

Question: A body of mass 100 g is rotating in a circular path of radius r with constant velocity. The work done in one complete revolution is :
Solution:
Key Idea : Direction of force is perpendicular to the direction of motion. If force is making an angle of θ with the direction of displacement (Δs) of the body, then work done is

W = Fcosθ × Δs
In one complete motion, velocity and force are perpendicular to each other that is angle θ is 900 , hence cos 900 = 0
∴ W = F × 0 × Δs = 0
i.e., work done is zero
Alternative : Whenever a force acting on a body produces a change in the position of the body , work is said to be done, by the force. If there is no change in the position of the body, work is not done. Since the displacement in this case is zero, work done is also zero.

Question: The particle executing simple harmonic motion has a kinetic energy $${K_0}{\cos ^2}\omega t$$. The maximum values of the potential energy and the total energy are respectively:
Solution:
In simple harmonic motion, the total energy of the particle is constant at all instants which is totally kinetic when particle is passing through the mean position and is totally potential when particle is passing through the extreme positions.
The variation of PE and KE with time is shown in figure by dotted parabolic curve and solid parabolic curve, respectively.

Figure indicates that maximum values of total energy, KE and PE of SHM are equal.


Now, $${E_K} = {K_0}{\cos ^2}\omega t \Rightarrow {\left( {{E_K}} \right)_{\max }} = {K_0}$$.

$$So,\,\,{\left( {{E_P}} \right)_{\max }} = {K_0}\,\,and\,\,{\left( E \right)_{Total}} = {K_{\max }} + {P_{\min }} = {K_0}$$.

Question: If a spring of stiffness k is cut into two parts A and B of length ${\ell _A}:{\ell _B} = 2:3,$ thenthe stiffness of spring A is given by
Solution:
If total length is $\ell $then,

${l_A} = \frac{{2l}}{5}$

We know that

$k \times l = {k_A} \times {l_A} = {k_B} \times {l_B}$

$\therefore \,k \times l = {k_A} \times {l_A}$

${k_A} = \frac{{k \times l}}{{{l_A}}}$

${k_A} = \frac{{k \times l}}{{\frac{{2l}}{5}}}$

${k_A} = \frac{5}{2}k$

Question: A bullet fired at an angle of 30° with the horizontal hits the ground 3 km away. By adjusting its angle of projection, can one hope to hits a target 5 km away. Assume the muzzle speed to be same and the air resistance is negligible:
Options: possible to hit a target 5 km away
not possible to hit a target 5 km away
Solution:
Formula for range R is,
$R = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 2\theta }}{g}$

Now at $30^\circ $, Range R=3km

$R = \frac{{{u^2}\sin {{60}^ \circ }}}{g}$

$3 = \frac{{{u^2}\sqrt 3 }}{{2g}}$

$\frac{{{u^2}}}{g} = 2\sqrt 3 $

Now for maximum range, angle is 45,

${R_{\max }} = \frac{{{u^2}\{ \sin 2({{45}^ \circ })\} }}{g}$

${R_{\max }} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{g}$

Comparing both the equations we get,

${R_{\max }} = 2\sqrt 3 $

$ = 2 \times 1.732$

${R_{\max }} = 3.46{\text{ }}km$

Hence maximum range is 3.46 km that means bullet will not hit target that is 5 km away

Question: A particle is projected up an inclined plane with initial speed v = 20 m/s at an angle \[\theta  = 30^\circ \]with a plane. The component of its velocity perpendicular to plane when it strikes the plane is

\[x\]\[y\]

\[{a_x} = - g\sin 30^\circ \]\[{a_y} = - g\cos 30^\circ \]




Resolving velocity and parallel and perpendicular to the inline.

Displacement perpendicular to plane is \[0 = u\sin 30^\circ t - \cos 30^\circ \frac{{g{t^2}}}{2}\]

\[ = 20 \times \frac{1}{2} - 10 \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} \times \frac{4}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]

Question:
A bullet of mass 10 gm leaves a rifle at an initial velocity of 1000 m/sec and strikes the
earth at the same level with a velocity of 500 m/sec. The work done in overcoming the resistance
of air will be:
Solution:
By work energy theorem

Work done in overcoming the air resistance = Loss in energy

Initial $$K.E. = 0.5 \times m \times v_1^2 = 0.5 \times 0.01 \times {10^6} = 5000J$$

Final $$K.E. = 0.5 \times m \times v_2^2 = 0.5 \times 0.01 \times 25 \times {10^4} = 1250J$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ Work done in overcoming the air resistance = 5000 - 1250 = 3750

Loss of potential energy = gain in K.E
\[mgh = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}\]
\[U = \sqrt {2 \times 9.8 \times 19.6} = 19.6m/s\]

Question: The coefficient of static friction μs between block A of mass 2 kg and the table, as shown in the figure, is 0.2. What would be the maximum mass of block B so that the two blocks do not move? (The string and the pulley are assumed to be smooth and massless.) (g = 10 m/s2)
Solution:
The tension in the string is equal to static frictional force between block A and the surface.
Let the mass of the lock B be M.

In equilibrium
T – Mg = 0
$ \Rightarrow T = mg$ …(i)
If blocks do not move, then
$T = {f_s}$
where ${f_s} = $ frictional force $ = {\mu _s}R = {\mu _s}mg$
$\therefore T = {\mu _s}mg$ …(ii)
Thus, from eqs. (i) and (ii). We have
\[Mg = {\mu _s}mg\]
or $M = {\mu _s} = m$
Given: ${\mu _s} = 0.2,m = 2{\text{kg}}$
$\therefore M = 0.2 \times 2 = 0.4{\text{kg}}$

Question: The work function of a substance is 4.0 eV. The longest wavelength of light that can cause photoelectron emission from this substance is approximately
Solution:

For photoelectron emission photon must have

E ≥ Work function

\[E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }\]

\[\frac{{hc}}{\lambda } \geqslant 6.4 \times {10^{ - 19}}\]

\[\frac{{6.626 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}(3 \times {{10}^8})}}{\lambda } \geqslant 6.4 \times {10^{ - 19}}\]

\[\lambda \leqslant \frac{{6.626 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{6.4 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}\]

\[\lambda \leqslant 3.10 \times {10^{ - 7}}m = 310\,nm\]
\[\lambda \leqslant 310\,\,nm\]

Question: The turns ratio of a transformer is given as 2 : 3 . If the current through the primary coil is 3 A, thus calculate the current through load resistance.
Solution:
If is and ip be the currents in the primary and secondary coils at any instant and the energy losses be zero, then Power in secondary coil = Power in primary coil
\[\begin{gathered}
  {V_s} \times {i_s} = {V_p} \times {i_p} \\
    \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\frac{{{i_p}}}{{{i_s}}} = \frac{{{V_s}}}{{{V_p}}} = \frac{{{N_s}}}{{{N_p}}} = transformer\,ratio\]
Given, \[\frac{{{N_p}}}{{{N_s}}} = \frac{2}{3},{i_p} = 3A\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{i_s} = \frac{{{N_p}}}{{{N_s}}}{i_p} = \frac{2}{3} \times 3 = 2A\]

Question: Two particles A and B are projected simultaneously from a point situated on a horizontal plane. The particle A is projected vertically up with a velocity v<sub>A</sub> while the particle B is projected up at an angle of 30<sup>0</sup> with horizontal with a velocity v<sub>B</sub>. After 5 s particles were observed moving mutual perpendicular to each other. The velocity of projection of the particle v<sub>A</sub> and v<sub>B</sub> respectively at
Options: v<sub>A</sub> can have any value greater than 25 ms<sup>-1</sup>, 100 ms<sup>-1</sup>
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

After 5s velocity of B should be horizontal.
$\frac{T}{2} = 5$ or $T = 10$
$\therefore \,\,T = \frac{{2{v_B}\sin \theta }}{g} = 10$
$\therefore \,\,\,\frac{{2{v_B}\sin 30^\circ }}{{10}} = 10$
${v_B} = 100\,m/s$
Time of flight of A should be greater than 5s
$\frac{{2{v_A}\sin 90^\circ }}{g} > 5$
${v_A} > 25\,m/s$

Question: A gas is compressed at a constant pressure of 50 N/m<sup>2</sup> from a volume of 10m<sup>3</sup> to a volume of 4 m<sup>3</sup>. Energy of 100 J is then added to the gas by heating. Its internal energy is:
Solution:
$\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W$
$\begin{gathered}
  \Delta U = \Delta Q - \Delta W \\
   = 100{\text{ J}} - P\Delta V \\
   = 100{\text{ J}} - \left[ {50 \times \left( { - 6} \right)} \right] \\
   = 400{\text{ J}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A $500\,\mu F$ capacitor is charged at the steady rate of $100\,\mu C/s.$ How long will it take to raise the potential difference between the plates of the capacitor to 10 V?
Solution:
Total final charge Q which will be acquired at 10 V is 
Q = CV = $500\,\mu F\, \times \,10V = 5 \times {10^{ - 3}}C$
Now, Q = qt where q is charge given per unit time
 $t = \,\frac{Q}{q}$
or $t = \,\frac{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{100 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = \frac{1}{{20}} \times 1000s = 50s$

Question: If the length of a second's pendulum is increased by 2%, then in a day the pendulum
Solution:
\[T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{\ell }{g}} \]
\[\frac{{\Delta T}}{T} \times 100 = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{\Delta L}}{L} \times 100\]
\[\frac{{\Delta T}}{T} \times 100 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 = 1\% \]
\[\frac{{\Delta T}}{T} \times 100 = 1\% \]
Total T in sec for 1 day
= 24 × 60 × 60 = 86400 sec.
1% of 86400 = \[\frac{1}{{100}} \times 86400 = 864\,\sec \,(loss)\]

Question: A cubic vessel (with face horizontal + vertical) contains an ideal gas at NTP. The vessel is being carried by a rocket which is moving at a speed of 500 m/s in vertical direction. The pressure of the gas inside the vessel as observed by us on the ground
Options: remains the same because 500 m/s is very much smaller than V<sub>rms</sub> of the gas
remains the same because motion of the vessel as a whole does not affect the relative motion of the gas molecules and the walls
will increase by a factor equal to \[\frac{{\left( {V_{rms}^2 + {{\left( {500} \right)}^2}} \right)}}{{V_{rms}^2}}\] where V<sub>rms</sub> was the original mean square velocity of the gas
will be different on the top wall and bottom wall of the vessel
Solution: As the motion of the vessel as a whole does not affect the relative motion of the gas molecules with respect to the walls of the vessel, hence pressure of the gas inside the vessel, as observed by us, on the ground remains the same.

Question: An equilateral prism of mass m rests on a rough horizontal surface with coefficient of friction \[\mu \]. A horizontal force F is applied on the prism as shown in the figure. If the coefficient of friction is sufficiently high so that the prism does not slide before toppling, then the minimum force required to topple the prism is:
Solution:
The block will topple about right corner A and will only topple if the torque by F is more than the torque by mg.

\[{\tau _F} \geqslant {\tau _{mg}}\]

\[F\frac{{a\sqrt 3 }}{2} \geqslant mg\frac{a}{2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow F \geqslant \frac{{mg}}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]

Question: When a ceiling fan is switched off, its angular velocity falls to half while it makes 36 rotations. How many more rotations will it make before coming to rest?
Solution:
Assuming, a constant avg. angular retardation.
\[{\omega _o} = \omega \]

\[{\omega _1} = \frac{\omega }{2}\]

\[\theta = 36\left( {2\pi } \right)\]

\[ \Rightarrow \omega _1^2 = \omega _0^2 + 2\alpha \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow - \frac{{3{\omega ^2}}}{4} = 2\alpha \left( {36} \right)\left( {2\pi } \right)\]

\[ \Rightarrow \alpha = - \frac{{{\omega ^2}}}{{192\pi }}\]

Now, from this point to the moment, the fan stops.

\[{\omega _1} = \frac{\omega }{2},{\omega _f} = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow 0 = \frac{{{\omega ^2}}}{4} + 2\alpha \theta \]

\[ \Rightarrow \theta = - \frac{{{\omega ^2}}}{{8\alpha }} = \frac{{{\omega ^2} \times 192\pi }}{{8{\omega ^2}}} = 24\pi \]

\[ \Rightarrow {\text{ No}}{\text{. of revolutions }} = \frac{{24\pi }}{{2\pi }} = 12\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
Tags.Topic: Kinematics of Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis

Question: A wire of length 0.5m and carrying the current of 1.2 amp is placed in a uniform magnetic field of induction 2 tesla. If the magnetic field is perpendicular to the length of the wire, then the force on the wire will be
The electric field due to a uniformly charged non-conducting sphere of radius R as a function of the distance from its centre is represented graphically by
Solution: Inside a uniformly charged non-conducting sphere, charge is uniformly distributed. So, the field is there. In such cases, the electric field is directly proportional to the distance from the centre. Outside the sphere, field is inversely proportional to the square of the distance. So, the graph flows as shown.

Question: The X and Y components of vector \[\vec A\] have numerical values 6 and 6 respectively and that of \[\left( {\vec A + \vec B} \right)\] have   numerical values 10 and 9. What is the numerical value of \[\vec B\]?
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow A = 6\widehat i + 6\widehat j\,;\,\,{\text{Let}}\,\overrightarrow B = x\widehat i + y\widehat j\]

\[\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B = 10\widehat i + 9\widehat j \Rightarrow \left( {6 + x} \right)\widehat i + \left( {6 + y} \right)\widehat j = 10\widehat i + 9\widehat j\]

Equating the components

\[ \Rightarrow 6 + x = 10\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,6 + y = 9\]

\[ \Rightarrow x = 4\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,y = 3\]

\[ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow B = 4\widehat i + 3\widehat j\]

\[ \Rightarrow \left| {\overrightarrow B } \right| = \sqrt {{4^2} + {3^2}} = 5\]

Question: A fan is making 600 revolutions per minute. If after some time it makes 1200 revolutions per minute, then increase in its angular velocity is
Solution:
Initial revolution, N1 = 600 rpm ;
Final revolution, N2 = 1200 rpm
Now, Increment in angular velocity
\[\omega = 2\pi \left( {{N_2} - {N_1}} \right)\]rad/min
\[ \Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi \left( {1200 - 600} \right)rad/\min \]
\[ \Rightarrow \omega = \frac{{1200\pi }}{{60}}rad/\sec = 20\pi \,rad/\sec \]

Question: A heavy small-sized sphere is suspended by a string of Length l. The sphere rotates uniformly in a horizontal circle with the string making an angle θ with the vertical. Then, the time-period of this conical pendulum is
Solution:
Radius of circular path in the horizontal plane
$r = l\sin \theta $

Resolving T along the vertical and horizontal directions, we get
\[T = \cos \theta = Mg..\left( i \right)\]
$T\sin \theta \; = Mr{\omega ^2} = M\left( {l\sin \theta } \right){\omega ^2}$
${\text{Or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} T = Ml{\omega ^2}...\left( {{\text{ii}}} \right)$
Dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq.(i), we get
$\frac{1}{{\cos \theta }} = \frac{{l{\omega ^2}}}{g}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\omega ^2} = \frac{g}{{l\cos \theta }}$
$\therefore $ Time period
$t = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega } = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{l\cos \theta }}{g}} $

Question: ABC is a right angled triangular plate of uniform thickness. $${I_1},{I_2}$$ and $${I_3}$$ are moments of inertia about AB, BC and AC respectively. Then which of the following relation is correct?
Solution:
AC > BC > AB
The moment of inertia of a body depends on the distribution of mass from the axis of rotation. The extent of mass distribution is the farthest from the axis AB and so, $I_1$ is the highest. Mass distribution is closest with respect to the axis AC. So, $${I_3}$$ is minimum or $${I_3} < {I_2} < {I_1}.$$

Question: If the surface tension of soap solution is σ , what is the work done in blowing soap bubble of radius r ?
Solution:
$${\text{Work done }}\sigma \left( {\Delta s} \right)$$
$${\text{Where }}\sigma {\text{ = surface tension}}$$

$$\Delta {\text{s = change in surface tension}}$$

$${\text{So }}W = \sigma \left[ {2\left( {4\pi {R^2}} \right) - 0} \right]$$

[As soap bubble has two surfaces]

W$$ = 8\pi {r^2}\sigma $$

Question: The reading of a spring balance when a block is suspended from it in air is 60 N. This reading is changed to 40 N when the block is submerged in water. The specific gravity of the block must therefore be:
Solution:
Let m be mass of block ; D be the density of mass;
V be the volume of the mass; d be the density of water.

Clearly,

$$m = DV$$

$$mg{\text{ }} = DVg = 60{\text{ }}...(i)$$

Now, when the block is fully submerged in water,

$$(D - d)Vg = 40{\text{ }}...(ii)$$

Dividing (ii) by (i),

$$\frac{{D - d}}{D} = \frac{{40}}{{60}} = \frac{2}{3} \Rightarrow 1 - \frac{d}{D} = \frac{2}{3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\text{Specific gravity, }}\frac{D}{d} = 3$$

Question: A particle of mass m moves in the XY plane with a velocity v along the straight-line AB. If the angular momentum of the particle with respect to origin O is LA when it is at A and LB when it is at B, then
Options: the relationship between L<sub>A</sub> and L<sub>B</sub> depends upon the slope of the line AB
Solution:
Angular momentum is defined as;

\[ \Rightarrow \vec L = \vec r \times m\vec v = \left( {r\sin \theta } \right)mv( - \widehat k)\]

Here $r\sin \theta $ is the perpendicular distance from origin to velocity vector.

Since both particles are moving in the same direction, so v will be in the same direction, and also perpendicular distance from origin to their velocity vector will be same.

Thus;

$ \Rightarrow {L_A} = {L_B}$

\[\therefore {\lambda _{\max }} = 3000\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} \]

Question: If a star can convert all the He nuclei completely into oxygen nuclei, the energy released per oxygen nuclei is: (Mass of the helium nucleus is 4.0026 amu and mass of oxygen nucleus is 15.9994 amu)

Question: A particle is moving in a uniform circular path of radius a under the action of an attractive potential $U = - \frac{k}{{2{r^2}}}.$ Its total energy is
Solution:
$U = - \frac{{k}}{{2{r^2}}}$
$F = - \frac{{dU}}{{dr}} = + \frac{K}{2}\left( { - 2} \right){r^{ - 2 - 1}}$
$ = - \frac{K}{{{r^3}}}$
According to Ques.
$\frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = \frac{K}{{{r^3}}}$
$\therefore \,\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{K}{{2{r^2}}}$
Now, TE = KE + PE
$ \Rightarrow \,TE = - \frac{K}{{2{r^2}}} + \frac{K}{{2{r^2}}} = 0$

Question: A particle is moving along a circular path of radius 5 m with a uniform speed $5\,{\text{m}}{{\,\text{s}}^{ - 1}}$. What will be the average acceleration when the particle completes half revolution?
Solution:
\[{{\vec a}_{avg}} = \frac{{\Delta \vec v}}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{{{{\vec v}_f} - {{\vec v}_i}}}{t}\]

\[{a_{avg}} = \frac{1}{{\Delta t}}\sqrt {v_i^2 + v_f^2 - 2{v_i}{v_f}\cos \theta } \]

\[\theta  = 180^\circ ,\,\,{v_i} = {v_f} = v\]

\[{a_{avg}} = \frac{{2v}}{t}\]

Time (t) for half revolution

\[t = \frac{{\pi r}}{v} = \frac{{5\pi }}{5} = \pi \sec \,\]   \[\left( {v = 5\,m/s,\,\,r = 5m} \right)\]

\[{a_{avg}} = \frac{{2 \times 5}}{\pi } = \frac{{10}}{\pi }\,m/{s^2}\]

Question: A particle moves a distance x in time t according to equation x = (t + 5)<sup>-1</sup>. The acceleration of particle is proportional to -
Solution:
\[x = \frac{1}{{t + 5}} \Rightarrow v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = - \frac{1}{{{{\left( {t + 5} \right)}^2}}}\]
Acceleration, a = \[\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = + \frac{2}{{{{\left( {t + 5} \right)}^3}}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow a \propto \] (velocity)3/2

Question: A boat is moving with a velocity of ($3\hat i + 4\hat j$ ) $$m/s$$ in river with respect to ground. River flows with a velocity of ($ - 3\hat i - 4\hat j$) $m/s$  with respect to ground. Relative velocity (in m/s) of boat with respect to river is:
Solution:
The relative velocity of boat w.r.t. water
$ = {v_{boat}} - {v_{water}}$$ = (3\;\hat i + 4\;\hat j) - ( - 3\;\hat i - 4\;\hat j)$$ = 6\;\hat i + 8\;\hat j$

Question: The displacement equation of a particle is x = 3 sin 2t + 4 cos 2t. The amplitude and maximum velocity will be respectively
Solution:
x = 3 sin 2t + 4 cos 2t. From given equation
${a_1} = 3,\,{a_2} = 4\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\phi = \frac{\pi }{2}$
$\therefore \,\,a = \sqrt {a_1^2 + a_2^2} = \sqrt {{3^2} + {4^2}} = 5$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,{v_{\max }} = a\omega = 5 \times 2 = 10$

Question: The coefficient of performance for a refrigerator can be:
Solution:
We know that a refrigerator is a power absorbing device.
COP is nothing but the efficiency in different words.
$COP = \frac{{Output}}{{Input}} = $(Desired effect/work Input)
For a refrigerator working between two temperature limits, ${T_1}$(higher) and ${T_2}$(lower)
Refrigerators will be throwing the heat taking from the ${T_2}$(Storage space) to ${T_1}$(Room or any exhaust at higher temperature).
COP = (Heat taken from the storage space)/(work Input) = ${Q_2}/W$, where W in work Input.
As, ${Q_1} = {Q_2} + W$
So, $COP = \frac{{{Q_2}}}{{\left( {{Q_1} - {Q_2}} \right)}}$
Applying the reversible refrigerator theory, Q can be replaced by temperature (100% second law Efficiency)
$COP = \frac{{{T_2}}}{{\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right)}}$
As ${T_1} > {T_2}$
So, $COP > 1$
Generally,${T_1}$and ${T_2}$difference is less than ${T_2}$so efficiency is always greater than 1.
Hence the correct answer is option (A).

Question: A constant torque of 31.4 Nm is exerted on a pivoted wheel. If the angular acceleration of the wheel is 4π rad/s<sup>2</sup>, then the moment of inertia, will be :

\[ \Rightarrow I = \frac{{31.4}}{{4 \times 3.14}} = \frac{{10}}{4} = 2.5kg - {m^2}\]

Question: The probability of electrons to be found in the conduction band of an intrinsic semiconductor at a finite temperature
Options: Decrease exponentially with increasing band gap
Increase exponentially with increasing band gap
is independent of the temperature and the band gap
Solution: Electrons pump from valence band to conduction band; Initial electrons are in valence bond. Probability of finding the electron decreases exponentially with band gap because more energy is revised to overcome the band gap to pump to conduction band as here it is mentioned at finite temperature as no external energy is given the electrons.

Question: A boy carries a fish in one hand and a bucket of water in the other hand; if he places the fish in the bucket, the weight now carried by him:
Solution: The bucket will be heavier by the weight of the fish, so one hand will have more load. But the other hand will have less load since the weight of the fish is removed. Hence, there is no change in total load. If the bucket is completely full up to the brim and if water spills out when the fish is added, then the load will drop by the weight of water that spills out. But no one carries a bucket completely full up to the brim.

Question: A shell of mass m moving with velocity u suddenly breaks into 2 pieces. The part having mass m/4 remains stationary. The velocity of other shell will be:
Solution:
By conservation of linear momentum Pi = Pf
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,mu = \frac{m}{4} \times 0 + \frac{{3m}}{4} \times v\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,v = \frac{{4u}}{3}\]

Question: The block of mass M moving on the frictionless horizontal surface collides with the spring of spring constant k and compresses it by length L. The maximum momentum of the block after collision is
Solution:
Elastic energy stored in spring $ = \frac{1}{2}K{L^2}$
$\therefore $ Kinetic energy of block $E = \frac{1}{2}K{L^2}$
Since ${p^2} = 2ME$
$\therefore p = \sqrt {2ME} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{2M \times K{L^2}}}{2}} = \sqrt {MK} L$

Question: A proton is moving in a uniform magnetic field B in a circular path of radius a in a direction perpendicular to Z-axis along which field B exists. Calculate the angular momentum if the charge on proton is e.

\[ herefore \,\, rac{{m{ u ^2}}}{a} = e u B\]

\[or\, u  = rac{{aeB}}{m}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( i ight)\]

Now, angular momentum, $$\overrightarrow J = \overrightarrow r imes \overrightarrow p $$
$$Here,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,J = a imes mv\,\sin {90^0}$$
Putting the value of $$ u \,\,from\,\,Eq.\,\,\,\left( i ight),$$
We get 

\[J = a imes m\left( { rac{{aeB}}{m}} ight) = {a^2}eB\]

Question: The graph between the resistive force F acting on a body and the distance covered by the body is shown in the figure. The mass of the body is 25kg and initial velocity is 2 m/s. When the distance covered by the body is 4m, its kinetic energy would be

Reason: Some energy disappears in the form of heat, sparking etc.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Charge is always conserved but energy is lost in the terms of heat etc.

Question: The number of turns in primary and secondary of a transformer are 5 and 10 and mutual inductance of transformer is 25 H. Now, if the number of turns in primary and secondary are 10 and 5, the new mutual inductance will be
Solution:
Mutual inductance of transformer
\[\,M \propto {N_1}{N_2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{{M_1}}}{{{M_2}}} = \frac{{{N_1}{N_2}}}{{N{'_1}N{'_2}}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{25}}{{{M_2}}} = \frac{{5 \times 10}}{{10 \times 5}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,{M_2} = 25\,H\]

Question: A cubical block of wood of specific gravity 0.5 and chunk of concrete of specific gravity 2.5 are fastened together. The ratio of mass of concrete to the mass of wood which makes the combination to float its entire volume submerged in water is
Solution:
${m_1} + {m_2} = m$    (total mass)
$V_T=V_1+V_2$               (total volume)
Weight of the system(chunk+wooden block) = buoyant force on the wooden piece with chunk 
 ($\because$ it is given whole system is submerged in water)
$mg=V_T\rho g$
${\rho _1}{V_1} + {\rho _2}{V_2} = \rho \left( {{V_1} + {V_2}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow 2.5{V_1} + 0.5{V_2} = {V_1} + {V_2}$
$1.5{V_1} = 0.5{V_2}$
$\frac{{{V_1}}}{{{V_2}}} = \frac{1}{3}$
$\frac{{{m_1}}}{{{m_2}}} = \frac{{{\rho _1}{V_1}}}{{{\rho _2}{V_2}}} = \frac{{2.5}}{{0.5}} \times \frac{1}{3} = \frac{5}{3}$

Question: A motor - cycle is moving in a vertical circular path. At what stage will the speed of the motor cycle be maximum ?

At the lowest point of the path
At the mid height of the path
At all the points in the path
Solution: The speed is maximum at the lowest point as the P.E. gets converted into K.E.

Question: Four capacitors of each of capacitance $3\mu F$ are connected as shown in the adjoining figure. The ratio of equivalent capacitance between $A$ and $B$and between $A$ and $C$ will be:
The moment of inertia of a metre stick of mass 300 gm, about an axis at right angles to the stick and located at 30 cm mark, is:
Solution:
Since, it’s a meter stick, the mark is 20cm from the center.

\[{I_{Mark}} = {I_{cm}} + M{d^2}\]

\[ = \frac{{300{{\left( {100} \right)}^2}}}{{12}} + 300{\left( {20} \right)^2}\]

\[ = 25 \times {10^4} + 12 \times {10^4}\]

\[ = 37 \times {10^4}{\text{ g - c}}{{\text{m}}^2}\]

Question:
.Assertion: Choke coil is preferred over a resistor to adjust current in an ac circuit.
Reason: Power factor for inductance is zero.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: If resistor is used in controlling ac supply, electrical energy will be wasted in the form of heat energy across the resistance wire. However, ac supply can be controlled with choke without any wastage of energy. This is because, power factor ($\cos \,\,\phi $) for resistance is unity and is zero for an inductance. [ $P\, = \,EI\,\cos \,\phi $].

Question: A block is kept on an inclined plane of inclination θ and of length l. If the block slides down, velocity of the block at the bottom of the inclined plane is (the coefficient of friction is μ )

\[v = \sqrt {2as}  = \sqrt {2gl\left( {\sin \theta  - \mu \cos \theta } \right)} \]

Question: Eight spherical rain drops of the same mass and radius are falling down with a terminal speed of $6\,{\text{cm}}\,{s^{ - 1}}.$If they coalesce to form one big drop, what will be its terminal speed? Neglect the buoyancy due to air.
Solution:
Let r be the radius of each small drop. The volume of each drop $ = \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}$.
Let R be the radius of the big drop formed when 8 small drops coalesce. The volume of the big drop will be 8 times that of the small drop. Hence
$\frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3} = 8 \times 4\pi \frac{{{r^3}}}{3}$
or ${R^3} = 8{r^3}$
or $R = 2r$
i.e. the radius of the big drop is twice the radius of each small drop. Now, if the buoyancy of the drop due to air is neglected, the terminal speed of the small drop is given by
\[{\upsilon _t} = \frac{2}{9}\frac{{\rho {r^2}g}}{\eta }\] …… (i)
The terminal speed of the big drop will be
${V_t} = \frac{2}{9}\frac{{\rho {R^2}g}}{\eta }$ ……. (ii)
Dividing (i) and (ii), we have
$\frac{{{V_t}}}{{{\upsilon _t}}} = \frac{{{R^2}}}{{{r^2}}} = \frac{{{{\left( {2r} \right)}^2}}}{{{r^2}}} = 4$
or ${V_t} = 4{\upsilon _t} = 4 \times 6 = 24\,{\text{cm}}\,{{\text{s}}^{ - 1}}$

Question: A particle of mass m oscillates with simple harmonic motion between points x<sub>1</sub> and x<sub>2</sub>, the equilibrium position being O. Its potential energy is plotted. It will be as given below in the graph :
Solution: $$PE = U = \frac{1}{2}k{x^2}$$which is of the form$$y = {x^2}$$

Question: The function ${\sin ^2}\left( {\omega t} \right)$ represents
Options: a periodic, but not simple harmonic motion with a period $\frac{\pi }{\omega }$
a periodic, but not simple harmonic motion with a period $\frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }$
a simple harmonic motion with a period $\frac{\pi }{\omega }$
a simple harmonic motion with a period $\frac{{2\pi }}{\omega }$
Solution:
Clearly ${\sin ^2}\omega t$is a periodic function as $\sin \omega t$as is periodic with period $\frac{\pi }{\omega }$

For SHM $\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{t^2}}} \propto - y$
$\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 2\omega \sin \omega t\cos \omega t = \omega \sin 2\omega t$
$\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{t^2}}} = 2{\omega ^2}\cos 2\omega t$ which is not proportional to –y.
Hence, it is not in SHM.

The horizontal distance covered by bomb

Question: There is an electric field $E$ in X-direction. If the work done on moving a charge $0.2\,C$ through a distance of $2\,m$ along a line making an angle $60^\circ $ with the X-axis is 4.0 J, what is the value of E?

Question: A steamer moves with velocity 3 km/h in and against the direction of river water whose velocity is 2 km/h. Calculate the total time for total journey if the boat travels 2 km in the direction of steam and then back to its place:
Solution:
\[{\text{P}} \to {\text{Q}}\]

\[{\vec V_B}^\prime = {\vec V_{BR}} + {\vec V_R}\]

\[\mathop {\text{P}}\limits_. \,\xrightarrow{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{R}}}}}\mathop {\text{Q}}\limits_. \]

\[ \leftarrow 2{\text{km}} \to \]

\[{\text{Q}} \to {\text{P}}\]

\[{\vec V_B}^\prime = {\vec V_{BR}} + {\vec V_R}\]

\[ = - 3 + 2 = - 1\,kph\]

\[{{\text{t}}_{{\text{QP}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{2}}}{{\text{1}}}{\text{hr}}\]

\[{\text{T}} = {{\text{t}}_{{\text{PQ}}}} + {{\text{t}}_{{\text{QP}}}} = \frac{2}{5} + 2 = 2.4{\text{hrs}}\]

Question: In an ac circuit, the current lags behind the voltage by $\pi /3$. The components in the circuit are
Solution:
Since, current lags behind the voltage in phase by a constant angle, then circuit must contain R and L.
We find that in RL circuit, voltage leads the current by a phase angle \[\phi \], where
\[\begin{gathered}
  \tan \phi = \frac{{AK}}{{OA}} = \frac{{OL}}{{OA}} \\
   = \frac{{{V_L}}}{{{V_R}}} = \frac{{{I_0}{X_L}}}{{{I_0}R}} \\
  \therefore \,\,\tan \phi = \frac{{{X_L}}}{R} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A Carnot engine absorbs 1000 J of heat energy from a reservoir at 127 C° and rejects 600 J of heat energy during each cycle. The efficiency of engine and temperature of sink will be
Solution:
Work done per cycle by engine
$W = {Q_1} - {Q_2}$
$ = 1000 - 600 = 400J$
Efficiency of engine
$\eta = \frac{W}{{{Q_1}}} \times 100 = \frac{{400}}{{1000}} \times 100 = 40\% $
In Carnot engine ${Q_1}$ and ${Q_2}$ are proportional to ${T_1}$ and ${T_2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{Q_1}}}{{{Q_2}}} = \frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{1000}}{{600}} = \frac{{400}}{{{T_2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {T_2} = 240K = - 33^\circ C$

     Moment of inertia about a parallel axis through its ends is

Question: A nucleus \[_Z^AX\] has mass represented by m (A, Z). If m<sub>p</sub> and m<sub>n</sub> denotes the mass of proton and neutron respectively and BE the binding energy (in MeV), then
Options: BE = [m (A, Z) – Zm<sub>p</sub> – (A – Z) m<sub>n</sub>] c<sup>2</sup>
BE = [Zm<sub>p</sub> + (A – Z) m<sub>n</sub> – m (A, Z)] c<sup>2</sup>
Be = m (A, Z) – Zm<sub>p</sub> – (A – Z) m<sub>n</sub>
Solution:
${m_p}{\text{ and }}{m_n}$ be the mass of the proton and neutron, respectively. The mass of the nucleus$m\left( {A,Z} \right)$.
The mass defect of the nucleus,
$\Delta m = \left[ {Z{m_p} + \left( {A - Z} \right){m_n} - m\left( {A,Z} \right)} \right]$
Now, the binding energy of a nucleus containing Nneutrons and $Z$ protons, $BE = \Delta m{c^2}$
Therefore,
$BE = \Delta m = \left[ {Z{m_p} + \left( {A - Z} \right){m_n} - m\left( {A,Z} \right)} \right]{c^2}$

Question: During a nuclear fusion reaction,
Options: a heavy nucleus breaks into two fragments by itself
a light nucleus bombarded by thermal neutrons breaks up
a heavy nucleus bombarded by thermal neutrons breaks up
two light nuclei combine to give a heavier nucleus and possibly other products.
Solution: During a nuclear fusion reaction, two or more lighter nuclei fuse together to form one or more different heavier nuclei and release an enormous amount of energy and subatomic particle also. The difference in product and reactant mass is manifested as the release or absorption of energy.

Question: If the banking angle of curved road is given by \[{\tan ^{ - 1}}\frac{3}{5}\] and the radius of curvature of the road is 6 m, then the safe driving speed is : (g = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup>)

\[ \Rightarrow v = 6 \times \frac{{18}}{5}\,km/h\]
\[ \Rightarrow v = 21.6\,km/h\]

Question: A gramophone record is revolving with an angular velocity \[\omega \] A coin is placed at a distance r from the centre of the record. The static coefficient of friction is μ. The coin will revolve with the record if-

\[\frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} \leqslant \mu mg\]
\[\therefore r \geqslant \frac{{{v^2}}}{{\mu g}}\]
\[or,\,r \geqslant \frac{{{\omega ^2}{r^2}}}{{\mu g}}or,\,r \leqslant \frac{{\mu g}}{{{\omega ^2}}}\]

Question: The ratio of specific heats $\left( {\frac{{{C_p}}}{{{C_V}}}} \right)$ in terms of degree of freedom (f) is given by:

\[{C_v} = \frac{f}{2}R\]

f = degree of freedom

\[{C_p} = R + {C_v} = R + \frac{f}{2}R = R\left( {1 + \frac{f}{2}} \right)\]


\[ = \frac{2}{f}\left( {1 + \frac{f}{2}} \right) = \left( {1 + \frac{2}{f}} \right)\]

Question: During the adiabatic expansion of two moles of a gas the internal energy of a gas is found to decrease by 2 J. The work done during the process on gas will be equal to:
Solution:
In adiabatic process heat transfer between system and surrounding doesn’t take place, so;
$ \Rightarrow Q = 0$
And change in internal energy, $\Delta U = - 2J$
Putting this value in thermodynamic first law;
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \Delta U = Q - W \\
   \Rightarrow W = 0 - \left( { - 2} \right) = 2J \\
\end{gathered} $
Hence, work done by the system is 2J.

Question: Two stones are projected with the same speed but making different angles with the horizontal. Their horizontal ranges are equal. The angle of projection of one is \[\pi /3\] and the maximum height reached by it is 102 m. Then the maximum height reached by the other in metres is:
Solution:
For first stone, angle is \[\frac{\pi }{3} = 60^\circ \]
Clearly, other stone angle is\[\left( {90^\circ - \theta } \right) = \left( {90^\circ - 60^\circ } \right) = 30^\circ \]

For first stone,

\[{H_1} = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{2g}}\]

\[102 = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\left( {60^\circ } \right)}}{{20}}\]

\[u = 52.2{\text{ }}m{s^{ - 1}}\]

Now height formula for second stone,

\[{H_2} = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{2g}} = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\left( {30^\circ } \right)}}{{20}} = \frac{{{{\left( {52.2} \right)}^2}\left( {\frac{1}{4}} \right)}}{{20}}\]

\[{H_2} = 34{\text{ }}m\]

Height reached by other stone is 34 m.

Question: Resonance frequency of LCR series a.c. circuit is \[{f_0}.\] Now the capacitance is made 4 times, then the new resonance frequency will become
Solution:
In LCR series circuit, resonance frequency \[{f_0}\] is given by
\[L\omega = \;\frac{1}{{C\omega }}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;{\omega ^2} = \;\frac{1}{{LC}}\]
\[\therefore \;\;\omega = \;\sqrt {\frac{1}{{LC}}} \; = \;2\pi {f_0}\]
\[\therefore \;\;{f_0} = \;\frac{1}{{2\pi \sqrt {LC} }}\;\;or\;\;{f_0}\;\alpha \;\frac{1}{{\sqrt C }}\]
When the capacitance of the circuit is made 4 times, its resonant frequency become \[{f_0}\]
\[\therefore \;\;\frac{{{{f'}_0}}}{{{f_0}}}\;\; = \;\frac{{\sqrt C }}{{\sqrt {4C} }}\;\;or\;{f'_0}\;\; = \;\frac{{{f_0}}}{2}\]

Question: A car when passes through a convex bridge exerts a force on it (when at highest point) which is equal to
Solution:
Let us suppose that the body exerts a force N on the surface when it travels through the convex surface.
The forces on the car is mg downwards and N upwards.
Hence the total force mg – N will provide the essential centripetal acceleration to the car.
Hence \[mg - N = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{R}\]
Hence \[N = mg - \frac{{m{v^2}}}{R}\]

Question: A metallic sphere floats in an immiscible mixture of water (ρ w = 103 kg/m3) and a liquid (ρ l = 13.5 × 103 kg/m3) such that its $$\frac{4}{5}th$$portion is in water and $$\frac{1}{5}th$$portion is in liquid. Density of the metal is:
Solution:
Since the metallic sphere rest in a liquid mixture the gravitational force is balanced by buoyant force.
Weight of metallic sphere = buoyant force of water + buoyant of liquid

$$mg = {m_1}g + {m_2}g$$

$$v\rho g = \frac{{4v}}{5} \times {10^3}g + \frac{v}{5} \times 13.5 \times {10^3}g$$

$$\rho = 3.5 \times {10^3}{\text{ }}kg/{m^3}$$

Question: A body is vibrating in simple harmonic motion with an amplitude of 0.06 m and frequency of 15 Hz. The maximum velocity and acceleration of the body’s
A body is placed on a rough inclined plane of inclination θ. As the angle θ is increased from 0° to 90° the net contact force between the block and the plane
Solution:
Till sliding starts i.e., θ < tan–1(μ), contact force (FC) and force of gravity (mg) are equal and opposite. Once the body starts sliding i.e., θ > tan–1(μ), net contact force becomes
     \[{F_C} = \sqrt {{{\left( {mg\cos \theta } \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\mu mg\cos \theta } \right)}^2}} \]
or \[{F_C} = mg\cos \theta \sqrt {1 + {\mu ^2}} \]
Here, cos θ decreases as θ increases.
Hence, the contact force starts decreasing and finally it becomes zero at θ = 90°.

Question: Two water droplets coalesce to form a large drop. In this process,
Options: energy is neither liberated nor absorbed
a small amount of mass is converted into energy in accordance with Einstein’s  mass- energy equivalence
Solution: The molecules on the surface experience an inward force and hence the surface tries to contract. Since it cannot contract beyond a point the surface remains under tension. This tension is in the form of surface energy. When two water droplets combine to form a large and a single drop, the net surface area decreases because the sum of areas of two drops is certainly bigger than a single drop. Hence total surface energy decreases. The remaining energy is liberated.

Solution:

The angle between \[\overrightarrow \tau \] and \[\overrightarrow r \] is 90o and the angle between \[\overrightarrow \tau \] and \[\overrightarrow F \] is also 90o. We also know that the dot product of two vectors which have an angle of 90o between them is zero. Therefore (d) is correct option.

Question: The X-ray beam coming from an X-ray tube will be
Options: Having all wavelengths smaller than a certain maximum wavelength
Having all wavelengths larger than a certain minimum wavelength
Having all wavelengths lying between a minimum and maximum wavelength.
Solution:
The X-ray beam coming out from an X-ray tube contains more than one wavelength, so it not monochromatic.
The maximum kinetic energy of the X-rays photons is equal to the maximum energy of the electron incident.
Therefore, $\frac{{hc}}{\lambda } = eV$
Therefore, the minimum wavelength of the X-ray beam, $\lambda = \frac{{hc}}{{eV}}$
The minimum kinetic energy of the X-rays photons is zero, the maximum wavelength of the X-ray beam is infinite
Hence, the wavelength for X-ray beam, ${\lambda _{\min }} \leqslant \lambda \leqslant \infty $

Tags.Subject: Physics

Question: The shortest wavelength of X- rays emitted from an X-ray tube depends upon
Options: nature of the gas in the tube
the voltage applied to the tube
nature of the target of the tube
Solution:
The shortest wavelength of X-rays emitted from an X-ray tube depends primarily on the voltage applied to the tube and the nature of the target material within the tube.
1. Voltage applied to the tube: The wavelength of X-rays produced in an X-ray tube is inversely proportional to the voltage applied across the tube. As the voltage increases, the wavelength of the X-rays decreases, resulting in shorter wavelengths (higher energy X-rays).
2. Nature of the target material: When high-energy electrons produced by the acceleration of electrons in the X-ray tube strike the target material, they undergo rapid deceleration, producing X-rays through bremsstrahlung radiation. A higher atomic number target material results in more efficient production of higher energy X-rays, which have shorter wavelengths.
3. The nature of the gas in the tube and the current in the tube do not directly affect the shortest wavelength of X-rays emitted from an X-ray tube. The gas in the tube is typically used to create a vacuum environment for efficient electron acceleration, and the current in the tube controls the number of electrons flowing through it, affecting the intensity of X-ray emission but not the wavelength.

Tags.Subject: Physics

Question: Three particles, each of mass m situated at the vertices of an equilateral triangle ABC of side l (as shown in the figure). The moment of inertia of the system about a line AX perpendicular to AB and in the plane of ABC, in will be :
Solution:
Moment of inertia of a system is defined as;

\[ \Rightarrow I = \sum {m_i}r_i^2\]

Perpendicular distance of mass at A from AX axis, \[{r_1} = 0\]

Perpendicular distance of mass at B from AX axis, ${r_2} = l$

Perpendicular distance of mass at C from AX axis,

${r_3} = l\cos 60^\circ = \frac{l}{2}$

Thus, moment of inertia of the system;

$ \Rightarrow I = {m_1}r_1^2 + {m_2}r_2^2 + {m_3}r_3^2$

$ \Rightarrow I = 0 + m{\left( l \right)^2} + m{\left( {\frac{l}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{5}{4}m{l^2}$

Consider a two particle system with particles having masses m<sub>1</sub> and m<sub>2</sub>. If the first particle is pushed towards the centre of mass through a distance d, by what distance should the second particle is moved, so as to keep the centre of mass at the same position?
Solution:
Initially,

\[0 = \frac{{{m_1}\left( { - {x_1}} \right) + {m_2}{x_2}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}} \Rightarrow \,\,{m_1}{x_1} = {m_2}{x_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 1 \right)\]
Finally,
The centre of mass is at the origin

\[\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \,\,0 = \frac{{{m_1}\left( {d - {x_1}} \right) + {m_2}\left( {{x_2} - d'} \right)}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}} \\
   \Rightarrow 0 = {m_1}d - {m_1}{x_1} + {m_2}{x_2} - {m_2}d' \\
   \Rightarrow \,d' = \frac{{{m_1}}}{{{m_2}}}d \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A charged capacitor when filled with a dielectric K =3 has charge ${Q_0},$ voltage ${V_0}$ and field ${E_0}$. If the dielectric is replaced with another one having K = 9, the new values of charge, voltage and field will be respectively
Solution:
When battery is disconnected then charge remains constant, V becomes $\frac{1}{K}$times and E becomes $\frac{1}{K}$times.
So, new value of charge is ${Q_0}$.
New value of $V = \frac{{{V_0} \times 3}}{9} = \frac{{{V_0}}}{3}$
New value of $E = \frac{{{E_0} \times 3}}{9} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{3}$

∴ Fe = qE = -16 x 10-18 x 104(- j)
  Fe=16 x 10-14k
 Fm = -16 x 10-18 (10i × Bj)
 Fm = -16 x 10-17 x B(+k)
  Fm = -16 x 10-17Bk
Since, the particle will continue to move alone +X -axis, so resultant force is equal to zero.
Fe + Fm = 0
 16 x 10-14 - 16 x 10-17 B = 0
 16 x 10-14 = 16 x 10-17 B
\[ \Rightarrow B = \frac{{16 \times 1{0^{ - 14}}}}{{16 \times 1{0^{ - 17}}}} = 1{0^3}\]
 B =103Wb/m2
Hence, the correct option is (a).

Question: A block of mass 10 kg is suspended by three strings as shown in the figure. The tension T2 is
Solution:
$${T_3}\cos 30^\circ  = {T_2}\cos 60^\circ $$

$${T_3}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right) = {T_2}\frac{1}{2}$$

$${T_3} = \frac{{{T_2}}}{{\sqrt 3 }}$$

$${T_2}\sin 60^\circ  + {T_3}\sin 30^\circ  = 100$$

$${T_2} \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} + \frac{{{T_2}}}{{\sqrt 3 }} \times \frac{1}{2} = 100$$

$$\frac{{\sqrt 3 {T_2}}}{2} + \frac{{{T_2}}}{{2\sqrt 3 }} = 100$$

$${T_2} = 50\sqrt 3 $$

Question: An electron is moving round the nucleus of a hydrogen atom in a circular orbit of radius r. The coulomb force $\overrightarrow F $ between the two is (Where $K = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}$)
Solution: $\overrightarrow {F\,}  =  - \,k\frac{{{e^2}}}{{{r^2}}}\hat r =  - \,k.\frac{{{e^2}}}{{{r^3}}}\overrightarrow {r\,} $                 $\left( {\because \,\,\hat r = \frac{{\overrightarrow {r\,} }}{r}} \right)$

Question: The moment of inertia of a triangular lamina of mass M about edge AB is equal to
Solution:
\[{I_{AB}} = M{h^2}/6\]

\[h = a\sin 30^\circ = a/2\]
\[{I_{AB}} = \left( {\frac{{M{a^2}}}{{24}}} \right)\]

Question: A particle moves in a circular orbit under the action of a central attractive force inversely proportional to the distance 'r'. The speed of the particle is
Solution: As centripetal force is \[F = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}\] and force is proportional to \[\frac{1}{r}\] hence r will get cancelled out from both sides and v is independent of r.

Question: A stone projected vertically up with a velocity u reaches a maximum height h. when it at a height of 3h/4 from the ground, the ratio of KE and PE at that point is: (consider PE = 0 at the point of projection)
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{1}{2}m{u^2} = mgh \Rightarrow \,{u^2} = 2gh\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \\
  mg\left( {\frac{{3h}}{4}} \right) + K.E. = mgh \\
  K.E. = \frac{{mgh}}{4} \\
  \frac{{K.E.}}{{P.E.}} = \frac{{mgh/4}}{{3mgh/4}} = \frac{1}{3} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A hollow metal sphere is filled with water through a small hole in it. It is hung by a long thread and is made to oscillate. Water slowly flows out of the hole at the bottom. Select the correct variation of its time period
Options: The period will go on increasing till the sphere is empty
The period will go on decreasing till the sphere is empty
The period will not be affected at all
The period will increase first, then decrease to initial value till the sphere is empty
Solution:
As the water level goes down, the distance of C.O.M
From point of oscillation P keeps increasing
Since \[T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} \]
The period will increase first, then decrease to initial value till the sphere is empty.

Question:
Carbon, silicon and germanium have four valence electrons each. These are characterised by valence and conduction bands separated by energy band gap respectively equal to ${\left( {{E_g}} \right)_C},{\left( {{E_g}} \right)_{Si}}$and ${\left( {{E_g}} \right)_{Ge}}$.
Which of the following statements is true?
Solution:
${\left( {{E_g}} \right)_{Ge}} < {\left( {{E_g}} \right)_{Si}} < {\left( {{E_g}} \right)_C}$
$\left[ {\because \,{{\left( {{E_g}} \right)}_{Ge}} = 0.7\,{\text{eV,}}\,{{\left( {{E_g}} \right)}_{Si}} = 1.1\,eV,{{\left( {{E_g}} \right)}_C} = 5.5\,{\text{eV}}} \right]$

Question: If two balls each of mass 0.06 kg, moving in opposite direction with speed of 4 m/s collide and rebound with same speed then impulse imparted to each ball due to other will be
Solution:
Impulse  \[\overrightarrow {\Delta p}  = \overrightarrow {{p_f}}  - \overrightarrow {{p_i}}  = m\overrightarrow v  - \left( { - m\overrightarrow v } \right) = 2m\overrightarrow v \]
\[\Delta p = 2mv\]
$ = 2 \times 0.06 \times 4$
= $0.48\,kg\,m/s$

Question: A shell of mass $ 20\,{\text{kg}} $ at rest explodes into two fragments whose masses are in the ratio $ 2:3 $ . The smaller fragment moves with a velocity of $ 6\,{\text{m}}\,{{\text{s}}^{ - 1}} $ . The kinetic energy of the larger fragments is
Solution:
Before explosion, momentum of the shell $ = 0 $
After explosion, the masses of the fragments are 8 kg and 12 kg,
Let $ {v_1} $ and $ {v_2} $ denote the velocities of the respective fragments.
 $ {m_1} = 8\,{\text{kg,}}\,\,\,\,{u_1} = 0,\,\,\,{m_2} = 12\,\,{\text{kg,}}\,\,\,{u_2} = 0 $
 $ {v_1} = 6\,\operatorname{m} \,{s^{ - 1}},{v_2} = {v_2} $
 $ {m_1}{u_1} + {m_2}{u_2} = {m_1}{v_1} + {m_2}{v_2} $
 $ 8\left( 0 \right) + 12\left( 0 \right) = 8\left( 6 \right) + 12{v_2} $
 $ = 48 + 12{v_2} $
Since momentum is conserved
